Bid and Offer Swap Rates
Once again, when we talk about swaps we almost always focus on the ﬁxed
side exclusively. And as mentioned previously, the bid side swap rate is de-
ﬁned as the ﬁxed rate at which a swap dealer is willing to pay ﬁxed, and
the oﬀer side swap rate is deﬁned as the ﬁxed rate at which a swap dealer is
willing to receive ﬁxed. When a client is receiving ﬁxed, he wants to receive
as high of a ﬁxed rate as possible, and of course a dealer is incentivized to
pay as low of a rate as possible. Similarly, when a client is paying ﬁxed, he
wants to pay as low of a ﬁxed rate as possible, and the dealer is incentivized
to receive as high of a rate as possible. As a convention, most composite
pages show the bid and oﬀer semi bond swap rates as the bid and oﬀer swap
spread over the mid-market Treasury. Although this serves as a very reason-
able indication of where the swaps market is, it is often more appropriate
to take the relevant bid or oﬀer side Treasury yield into account. If the size
of the trade is small enough to get done “on screen” (meaning at the levels
that appear on the composite page), then
Bid Side Swap Rate
= Oﬀer Side Treasury + Bid Side Swap Spread
(1.11)
Oﬀer Side Swap Rate
= Bid Side Treasury + Oﬀer Side Swap Spread.
(1.12)
18

<!-- Page 42 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
This may seem a bit mysterious, so let’s try to shed some light on it. Suppose
that a dealer is asked to pay ﬁxed in a swap by a client. Let’s forget about
the ﬂoating side right now and just focus on the ﬁxed side.29 If the dealer is
paying ﬁxed, thereby agreeing to pay out ﬁxed coupons over the life of the
swap, it is kind of like the dealer is selling a ﬁxed rate bond to the client.
Think about it: the client is receiving those ﬁxed coupons. Therefore, the
client’s position, looking at the ﬁxed leg of the swap in isolation, is very
similar to having purchased a ﬁxed rate bond from the dealer.30
Let’s remind ourselves now of a few facts about ﬁxed rate bonds. If we
own a ﬁxed rate bond, and the bond market rallies (meaning rates go down),
then the value of our bond goes up. Conversely, if we own a ﬁxed rate bond
and the bond market sells oﬀ(meaning rates go up), then the value of our
bond goes down. Of course, if we are instead short the bond, then we beneﬁt
when the market sells oﬀand are hurt when the market rallies.
Let’s get back to the swap dealer who just paid ﬁxed in a swap. As we
suggested, by paying ﬁxed it is as if the dealer has just sold a ﬁxed rate
bond. In fact, after entering into the swap and paying ﬁxed, the dealer will
be underwater on its position if the market rallies and will be making money
if the market sells oﬀ. The dealer may want this exposure, or it may look to
hedge itself to immunize against changes in interest rates.
One way the swap dealer could hedge himself is to buy Treasuries in the
market.31 Let’s keep in mind that swap dealers are market makers in swaps.
They are not market makers in Treasuries. Thus, when the swap dealer goes
to the Treasury market, he is a customer of that market and has to buy
Treasuries on the oﬀer side and sell Treasuries on the bid side. Since the
swap dealer would have to buy Treasuries on the oﬀer side if he went in to
hedge against his paying ﬁxed, it is more customary to regard the bid side
swap spread as being spread over the oﬀer side yield corresponding to the
oﬀer side price at which he could actually buy those Treasuries.
Figure 1.4 shows how this works. Let’s suppose a swap dealer agrees to
pay ﬁxed in a 5-year swap to a client and goes to the Treasury market and
29It turns out, as we will soon see, that we often ignore the ﬂoating side when we look
at valuing swaps, so get used to it now. The ﬂoating side of the swap for purposes of
valuation is like Rodney Dangerﬁeld: it don’t get no respect.
30Obviously, one big diﬀerence is that a bond has principal that the holder is entitled
to, but let’s ignore that for now.
31It is not the case that the dealer is buying Treasuries in order to use coupon payments
to have cash necessary to make the ﬁxed coupon payments in the swap. The Treasuries
are bought by the dealer in order to give him a position that has similar, but opposite,
interest rate sensitivity to the swap. The Treasuries that are bought by the swap dealer
will go up in value if the market rallies and down in value if the market sells oﬀ, which
is the opposite of the change in the value of the swap due to a movement in rates, as we
will see.
19

<!-- Page 43 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.4: Mechanics Behind a Swap Dealer Paying Fixed and Buying Trea-
suries to Hedge
buys 5-year Treasury notes from a Treasury dealer at the oﬀer side price of
101-16. The yield corresponding to this price is 1.436%. The ﬁxed rate in the
swap is, in accordance with Equation 1.11, the oﬀer side yield of 1.436% plus
the bid side swap spread of 17 bps, giving a bid side swap rate of 1.606%.
What’s the big deal? This number is only a tenth of a bp diﬀerent from
what was shown on the composite page for the bid side swap rate. Well,
suppose that the notional on the swap was really big like $500mm, in which
case the swap dealer would have to buy about $500mm Treasuries to hedge
himself.32 A $500mm Treasury trade is a big trade. Maybe the price on the
screen of 101-16 is good for $25mm Treasuries. Suppose that if the dealer
goes in to the Treasury market to buy another $50mm, this will push the
32The swap dealer would not necessarily have to buy exactly $500mm Treasuries to
hedge himself since the interest rate sensitivity of a swap (which we haven’t talked about
yet) per million notional is not generally equal to the interest rate sensitivity of a Treasury
of comparable maturity per million principal.
20

<!-- Page 44 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
price up to 101-16+. Maybe he can get another $100mm there, but the next
$50mm he has to pay 101-17. In order to set the rate in the swap for a large
trade like this, a swap dealer is going to estimate a weighted average dollar
price that he is going to have to pay for the Treasury hedge. He will need
to take into consideration that as he tries to accumulate enough Treasuries
to hedge he will likely push the price up. Suppose he estimates that, given
market conditions, he is going to have to pay a weighted average price of 101-
19. The yield corresponding to this price is 1.417%. If the dealer agrees to pay
ﬁxed at 17 bps over where he can buy Treasuries, then in this case the bid
side swap rate would be 1.417% + 0.17% = 1.587%. Notice here that the
eﬀect is to lower the swap rate at which the dealer is willing to pay. If the size
of the trade was small and could get done “at screen levels,” then the rate
the dealer would be willing to pay is 1.606%. But for this larger trade, when
we incorporate where the swap dealer can actually buy his Treasury hedge,
the rate the dealer would be willing to pay is nearly 2 bps lower than the
level on the screen. As we will be able to show later in Chapter 2, the present
value of this rate diﬀerential is on the order of magnitude of $400,000.
Repo
This story (the one of a swap dealer who pays ﬁxed and buys Treasuries to
hedge) isn’t quite over yet. Suppose a swap dealer pays ﬁxed on $100mm
5-year swaps and is able to buy $100mm 5-year Treasuries at the price on
the screen of 101-16 to hedge his position. The dealer is going to have to pay
over $100mm to the Treasury dealer for these Treasuries. Where is the swap
dealer going to get this money? He probably doesn’t have $100mm lying
around. What he will do is enter into a repurchase agreement (also known
as a repo) in the repo market. A repo is similar to a secured loan. It is the
sale of a security with the simultaneous agreement to repurchase the security
at a predetermined price at some time in the future. Implicit in a repo is a
repo rate, which is used to determine the price at which the securities will
be repurchased. The counterparty on the other side of the trade who takes
in the security in exchange for cash is known to have done a reverse repo.
Repo markets exist for a variety of security types, including Treasuries and
mortgages.33
The length of a repo can be overnight, for term (meaning a speciﬁed
period of time of more than one day), or open (meaning a nonspeciﬁed period
of time of more than one day).34 Term repos typically do not exceed one year.
33For more information about the repo markets, see, for example, Stigum (2007),
Fabozzi (2001), Sundaresan (2009), or Tuckman (2002).
34In an open repo, there is no predetermined price at which the security will be repur-
chased, since the length of the contract has not been speciﬁed.
21

<!-- Page 45 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
A popular time frame for a term repo of an on-the-run Treasury security is to-
the-date, or to the settlement date of the next Treasury of the same maturity
series that will be issued. If the securities should pay any coupons over the
life of a repo agreement, the counterparty who delivered the securities in
exchange for cash in the repo is entitled to the coupon interest.
Whereas an investor will often enter into a repo in order to fund a secu-
rity purchase, his counterpart who enters into a reverse repo is doing so in
order to earn interest on cash he has on hand. Oftentimes those who take in
securities for cash in a reverse repo are indiﬀerent as to the particular Trea-
sury securities they take in. When the particular Treasury securities that are
delivered in a repo are not speciﬁed, the securities are referred to as general
collateral. Certain investors, such as swap desks who need to fund particular
Treasuries that they have purchased, need to specify particular Treasuries
in a repo. Such repos are referred to as special. The repo rate for specials is
no greater than the repo rate on general collateral and is often signiﬁcantly
lower. When the availability of a particular security is relatively low, and the
demand to take delivery of the particular security in a reverse repo is rel-
atively high, this imbalance manifests itself by putting downward pressure
on repo rates. Such Treasuries are said to be trading special. On-the-run
Treasury securities typically trade special in the repo market.
Let’s continue with our example of the swap dealer who pays ﬁxed on
$100mm 5-year swaps and hedges the trade by buying $100mm on-the-run
5-year notes at 101-16. This is the oﬀer side price shown on the composite
page in Table 1.1. The market data on this page is from August 11, 2010.
The on-the-run 5-year Treasury on this date was the 1.75% of July 31, 2015.
Treasuries purchased on this date would settle reg, or T + 1, on August
12, 2010. The total invoice price or dirty price paid by the buyer on the
settlement date includes accrued interest. In this case, the dirty price for the
bond is35
$100mm × (101.5% + 0.05706522%) = $101, 557, 065.22.
(1.13)
If the swap dealer goes to the repo market to enter into a repo on August
12, 2010 to fund this position for n days, then the swap dealer will deliver
35For Treasuries, accrued interest is calculated on an Act/Act basis. In the case of
the 1.75% of July 31, 2015 for settlement August 12, 2010, there are 12 days of accrued
interest (the number of days between July 31, 2010, and August 12, 2010) and 184 days
in the accrual period (i.e., the number of days between July 31, 2010, and January 31,
2011). Thus, the amount of accrued interest as a percentage as par is given by
0.875% × 12/184 = 0.05706522%.
22

<!-- Page 46 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
$100mm face of the security and will receive $101,557,065.22.36 When the
repo matures, the swap dealer will take delivery of the securities and owe a
total of
$101, 557, 065.22 ×

1 + n
360 × repo rate

,
(1.14)
where the repo rate is the n-day rate of interest charged by a repo dealer
for the use of its funds. For instance, if the repo is overnight (in which case
n = 1) and if the repo rate is 20 bps, then the swap dealer will owe
$101, 557, 065.22 ×

1 +
1
360 × 0.20%

= $101, 577, 629.43.
When the swap dealer goes into the repo market to do a repo, it is a
customer of the repo market and will have to transact at the bid side repo
rate when taking in cash and delivering securities and at the oﬀer side repo
rate when providing cash and taking in securities.37 Figure 1.4 summarizes
the position of the swap dealer who pays ﬁxed in a swap, buys Treasuries to
hedge, and enters into an overnight repo to ﬁnance the Treasury purchase
for a day.
Resets and Payments
If we transact a USD swap, a spot starting transaction has an eﬀective date
(the date from which payments begin to accrue), which is “T + 2,” or two
New York and London Business Days after the trade date.38 Suppose we
transact a swap on a Tuesday. Then if Wednesday and Thursday are both
Business Days (sometimes referred to as “good days”) in New York and Lon-
don, then the eﬀective date of the swap will be that Thursday. Suppose now
we transact a swap on a Thursday. If Friday and Monday are good days, then
the eﬀective date of the swap will be Monday since Saturdays and Sundays
are never good days. If, however, Monday is not a good day in either New
York or London, then the eﬀective date would be Tuesday.
The resets and payments made on the LIBOR leg of the swap typically
occur with “resets in advance, payments in arrears.” This is demonstrated in
36Often repos are subject to haircuts by dealers, which stipulate that the borrower
receives an amount of cash lower than the value of the securities delivered as collateral.
This protects the dealer (to some extent) in case the borrower defaults and the value of
the pledged securities should fall, in which case liquidating the bonds would not make the
dealer whole. We shall ignore haircuts for our purposes here.
37Repo desks are market makers in repo and proﬁt from the bid/oﬀer spread between
the bid side repo rate and the oﬀer side repo rate. The bid side rate (at which repo desks
earn interest) is higher than the oﬀer side rate (at which they pay interest).
38A Business Day for a particular city is deﬁned as a day upon which commercial banks
and foreign exchange markets are generally open to settle payments in the speciﬁed city.
23

<!-- Page 47 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.5: Resets and Payments on the Floating Side
Figure 1.5. Suppose that we transact a spot starting swap on June 23, and it
has an eﬀective date of June 25. The ﬁrst 3-month LIBOR setting will be on
June 25, and that rate will accrue for the quarter, and payment will be made
on September 25, assuming that is a good day. On September 25, LIBOR
will be set for the next quarter, and that rate will accrue for the quarter, and
that payment would normally be made on December 25. Since December 25
is a holiday in New York and London (i.e., neither commercial banks nor
foreign exchange markets are open to settle payments on this day), neither a
payment nor a LIBOR reset will occur on that day. This payment date falls
on a “bad day” (i.e., a weekend or holiday). So when is this payment made?
Payments in swaps can be made on a Preceding (meaning payments are made
on the ﬁrst good Business Day prior to the bad day), a Following (payments
are made on the ﬁrst good day after the bad day), or a Modiﬁed Following
(payments are made on the ﬁrst good day after the bad day, provided that
does not bring us into the next month; if it does bring us to the next month,
then that particular payment is made the ﬁrst good day prior to the bad day)
basis, which determines when a payment that would otherwise fall on a bad
day is paid. For a USD swap, the standard convention is Modiﬁed Following.
Exactly how much should be paid when we need to move the payment
date from a bad day depends on whether payments are adjusted (in which
case the number of days that are accrued is altered in accordance with the
payment date, and hence the magnitude of the payment for that period
changes) or unadjusted (in which case the number of days that a rate is
accrued for does not change despite changing the payment date). For a USD
swap, the standard is that payments are adjusted. Appendix C goes into a lot
24

<!-- Page 48 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
more detail about how these conventions come into play and gives an example
demonstrating why one should be careful about which conventions apply.
One other slight technicality to be aware of: in a standard swap the
LIBOR rate that is set on each reset date is actually the LIBOR setting
determined by the BBA two London Business Days prior to each reset date.
Thus, if we transact a swap on June 23 and it has an eﬀective date of June 25,
the LIBOR setting for the initial period uses the rate two London Business
Days prior to June 25, which is June 23. Thus, for a spot starting swap, the
initial LIBOR set for the ﬁrst three-month period is typically known with
certainty when entering into the trade. All other LIBOR rates will not be
known until we get to the respective rate setting dates.
Sample Term Sheet
It used to be commonplace that a dealer would send a client a sample term
sheet (or vice versa) prior to executing a swap in order to make sure that
each counterparty was in agreement with the terms of the trade. These days,
a term sheet is rarely sent prior to executing a relatively simple transaction
but may be sent in advance of more complicated or exotic transactions.
It is, however, very common today to send a quick term sheet (perhaps
more aptly called a trade recap) soon after a swap has been transacted
in order to review the terms to which the two counterparties have agreed.
Whether or not a term sheet is used, a more formal conﬁrmation is sent
by one counterparty to the other after the trade has been executed. It is
signed by both counterparties and is a legally binding agreement of the
swap.39 Figure 1.6 shows a sample term sheet. The last line of the conﬁrm
39What happens when the terms of the written conﬁrmation do not match the terms
of the trade that has been executed? This is not that uncommon, actually. Most often
the two people who execute the trade are not the two people who draft the conﬁrm,
and sometimes, like a game of telephone, the written conﬁrmation does not accurately
reﬂect the trade that was executed. Assuming both counterparties agree that the signed
conﬁrmation does not accurately reﬂect the terms of the trade as executed, a revised
written conﬁrmation is signed by both counterparties.
But what if one of the counterparties says that the signed conﬁrmation does not accu-
rately reﬂect the terms of the trade as executed, but the other counterparty says the signed
conﬁrmation is accurate? That gets complicated. In this case, the two counterparties may
try to reach a compromise. In the event that the two counterparties are unable to reach
a compromise, a lawsuit may be necessary to settle the matter. The area of law that is
relevant here is referred to as the statute of frauds. In the event that a voice trade and a
written conﬁrmation do not match, the voice trade is the trade (i.e., the oral contract is
the binding contract). A counterparty who believes that a signed conﬁrmation does not
accurately reﬂect the terms of a trade at the point of execution will likely need to prove
that is the case. In such cases, electronic records of the trades (e.g., telephone recordings)
may provide such evidence, provided that, at a minimum, such recordings are legal. See,
for example, Gooch and Klein (2002).
25

<!-- Page 49 -->
Figure 1.6: A Sample Term Sheet

<!-- Page 50 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
indicates that an ISDA will govern the trade. ISDA is an organization that,
among other things, has created a legal document, known as an ISDA Master
Agreement, that two counterparties who enter into swaps have typically
signed in advance of transacting any OTC derivatives together. The Master
Agreement carefully spells out certain legal conditions that govern swaps
and gives deﬁnitions to many of the terms the counterparties may use in a
conﬁrmation.
OIS Swaps
An Overnight Index Swap (OIS) is a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap in which the ﬂoating
leg is tied to a reference overnight rate. In the USD swaps market, that
overnight reference rate is the Federal Funds eﬀective rate. The ﬁxed rate
in an OIS is referred to as the OIS rate. In an OIS, one counterparty will
pay the OIS rate, and the other will pay an average of the Federal Funds
eﬀective rate over the term of the swap. The OIS rate for a given maturity
is often thought of as the expected Federal Funds rate over the term of the
swap.
It has become common in the market to look at the LIBOR-OIS spread,
the diﬀerence between a LIBOR rate and an OIS rate of the same maturity.
Many in the market regard this spread as a proxy for the health of the ﬁ-
nancial system. The Fed Funds eﬀective rate is the rate that banks lend to
one another overnight in the Fed Funds market. The LIBOR-OIS spread is
thought to measure the risk premium that banks charge to lend to one an-
other above and beyond the funds rate. This interpretation comes from the
fact that a bank could either roll funds over at the overnight rate for some
period of time, say, three months, or, alternatively, lock in a rate at 3-month
LIBOR. In times of stress, there may be a signiﬁcant premium attached to
lending funds for three months, compared to lending overnight in the Fed
Funds market.
The LIBOR-OIS spread had been stable for many years but saw a tremen-
dous widening at the height of the crisis. For example, as shown in Figure 1.7,
the spread between 3-month LIBOR and 3-month OIS traded consistently
around 10 bps prior to the crisis but widened out dramatically when Lehman
fell, trading in excess of 300 bps at the height of the crisis. When LIBOR
and OIS tracked each other very closely, there was not a tremendous amount
of interest in OIS swaps. But since the time of the crisis, trading in OIS has
picked up signiﬁcantly. Investors have used the product to hedge against or
take a view on unanticipated policy changes from the Fed.
27

<!-- Page 51 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.7: Historical LIBOR-OIS Spread
1.2.2
Basis Swaps
In a basis swap, both streams of payment are ﬂoating in nature. Whereas
the ﬁxed coupon in a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap is set such that the trade has zero
value today, in a basis swap a spread is added to one of the ﬂoating legs in
order to make it an NPV = 0 transaction.
As an example, consider the swap in Figure 1.8 that shows a 1s3s (pro-
nounced “ones threes”) basis swap, a swap in which one counterparty pays
1-month LIBOR, and the other pays 3-month LIBOR. Notice that the dealer
is agreeing to receive 1-month LIBOR plus 5 bps monthly money and pay
3-month LIBOR quarterly money for two years. In a 1s3s basis swap, when
the dealer receives 1-month LIBOR plus a spread, it is referred to as the
oﬀer side of the basis swap. The bid side of the basis swap would have the
dealer paying 1-month LIBOR plus a (lower) spread monthly money. Since
the dealer is the one making a market in the basis swap, the bid side will
have a spread that is lower than the oﬀer side. Thus, for example, the dealer
may be willing to pay 1-month LIBOR plus 3 bps versus receiving 3-month
LIBOR for two years (on the bid side) and may be willing to receive 1-month
LIBOR plus 5 bps versus paying 3-month LIBOR for two years (on the oﬀer
side). Simply put, the 1s3s basis swap is “bid at 3, oﬀered at 5.” The mid-
28

<!-- Page 52 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.8: A “1s3s” Basis Swap
market in the basis swap would be deﬁned as the average of the bid and the
oﬀer, or a spread of 4 bps in this case.
Why is there a spread at all in this basis swap? That is, why isn’t the
dealer willing to receive 1-month LIBOR ﬂat, or plus 0 bps, versus 3-month
LIBOR? The single biggest determinant of the spread in the 1s3s basis swap
is supply and demand: as investors want to pay 1-month LIBOR in the basis
swap, that will increase the spread, and vice versa. But aside from this, there
are other diﬀerences in the two legs of the basis swap that would suggest the
spread should be nonzero. First, assuming the basis swap is spot starting, the
initial 1-month and 3-month LIBOR settings are known with certainty and
are typically not the same. For example, in an upward-sloping yield curve
environment, it is usually the case that 3-month LIBOR is greater than 1-
month LIBOR, suggesting a factor that would make the spread positive.
These are of course only the ﬁrst settings in the swap (of the 24 settings on
the 1-month LIBOR side and the 8 settings on the 3-month LIBOR side). If
the market’s expectation is that 1-month LIBOR will be lower than 3-month
LIBOR over the next two years, that would also suggest that the spread in
the basis swap should be positive. Finally, payments on the 1-month LIBOR
side occur monthly, whereas payments on the 3-month LIBOR side occur
quarterly. Since it is always better to receive money sooner rather than
later, this factor in isolation would actually suggest that the spread should
be negative. Taking into account all of these factors, the mid-market spread
for a 1s3s basis swap for two years is 4 bps.
Figure 1.9 shows a history of the 1s3s basis swap for a maturity of two
years. As the ﬁgure demonstrates, the basis swap spread was not particu-
larly volatile prior to the ﬁnancial crisis. But during the ﬁnancial crisis, the
basis swap spread experienced tremendous volatility and widened out signif-
icantly. As the crisis was mounting, so were concerns about liquidity. There
29

<!-- Page 53 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.9: History of “1s3s” Basis Swap, 2-Year Maturity
were concerns abounding that ﬁnancial institutions were reluctant to lend,
particularly for longer periods of time. Many in the market expected that
ﬁnancial institutions would be less willing to lend for, say, three months than
to lend for, say, one month. This would manifest itself in a wider diﬀeren-
tial between 3-month LIBOR and 1-month LIBOR. The expectation in the
market was that this wider diﬀerential would persist for some time, and this
was reﬂected by a widening of the basis swap spread.
Example
Let’s look back at Table 1.1 and suppose that a swap dealer is willing to
make a two-way market in 2-year swaps as indicated on the screen.40 That
is, the dealer is willing to pay 0.665% semi bond versus threes and is willing
to receive 0.705% semi bond versus threes. Suppose that at the same time
40In the swaps market, it was common etiquette for many years that if a client called
a dealer and wanted to do a swap, the client would indicate ﬁrst if he were interested in
paying ﬁxed or receiving ﬁxed prior to asking for a swap rate from a dealer. Thus, a client
might say something like, “Where can I pay ﬁxed on $100mm 5-year swaps?” For many
years, asking for a two-way market (i.e., asking a dealer to simultaneously show a bid side
and oﬀer side swap rate and expecting the dealer to be prepared to deal at either level)
was considered a huge breach of etiquette. In more recent years, it has become acceptable
to ask a dealer to show a two-way.
30

<!-- Page 54 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
the dealer is a 3 bid and a 5 oﬀer in a 1s3s basis swap for two years. That
is, the dealer is willing to pay 1-month LIBOR + 3 bps monthly money ver-
sus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money and is also willing to receive 1-month
LIBOR + 5 bps monthly money versus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money,
both for a term of two years. If a client is interested in receiving ﬁxed semi
bond versus 1-month LIBOR paid monthly money for two years, what ﬁxed
rate should he expect to see from the dealer?
Let’s look at this trade from the point of view of the swap dealer. The
dealer is being asked to pay ﬁxed for two years. We know that the dealer
is willing to pay ﬁxed at 0.665% semi bond. However, this is the rate the
dealer is willing to pay versus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money. The dealer
is, however, being asked to receive 1-month LIBOR monthly money instead.
As we have discussed, any change to the ﬂoating leg from the standard 3-
month LIBOR implies that the ﬁxed rate in the swap will change. The swap
dealer is not indiﬀerent between receiving 3-month LIBOR quarterly money
for two years and receiving 1-month LIBOR ﬂat (meaning plus a spread of
0 bps) monthly money for two years. We know this from the basis swap:
in particular, we know that if the dealer is receiving 1-month LIBOR, he
will want to receive 1-month LIBOR + 5 bps monthly money versus paying
3-month LIBOR quarterly money for two years.
As shown in Figure 1.10, the implication of this is that the dealer will
be willing to pay 0.615% semi bond versus 1-month LIBOR monthly money.
We can think about decomposing a swap in which a dealer pays ﬁxed semi
bond versus ones into two swaps: a standard swap in which a dealer pays
ﬁxed versus threes and a 1s3s basis swap in which a dealer receives 1-month
LIBOR and pays 3-month LIBOR. We know that the dealer is willing to pay
0.665% versus threes, and we also know that a dealer is willing to receive 1-
month LIBOR + 5 monthly money versus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money.
Each of these two transactions is shown in the decomposition of the swap into
two pieces. Notice that the two 3-month LIBOR legs in the decomposition
are oﬀsetting and thus cancel each other out. We are left with the dealer
paying ﬁxed 0.665% versus receiving 1-month LIBOR + 5 monthly money.
The only problem is that the client wants to pay 1-month LIBOR ﬂat, not
1-month LIBOR + 5, in the terms of the trade. However, if we “subtract”
5 bps from each leg of the swap, we see that the dealer is willing to receive
0.665% −0.05% = 0.615% versus 1-month LIBOR.41
2
41We are free to “subtract” 5 bps from each leg of the swap because doing so does not
change the value of the swap. We should be careful, however, because the 5 bps in the 1s3s
basis swap is quoted on a monthly money basis, whereas the ﬁxed leg of the swap is semi
bond. Thus, subtracting the 5 bps from the ﬁxed rate is technically wrong. We should
convert the 5 bps paid monthly money to an appropriate number of bps paid semi bond.
The magnitude of this error is so small as to be safely ignored for our purposes here.
31

<!-- Page 55 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.10: Decomposing a Fixed-Floating Swap Versus 1-Month LIBOR
Let’s consider now another type of basis swap as shown in Figure 1.11.
The ﬁrst thing to note about this transaction is that it is a forward starting
swap, meaning it is a swap transacted at one point in time with an eﬀective
date beyond the normal T + 2 settlement. In this case, the swap is a “2x5”
(pronounced “2 by 5”), meaning a 3-year swap, two years forward. The swap
itself will last a total of three years. The eﬀective date of the swap is two
years hence. First payments on each side are in 2.25 years, and ﬁnal payments
are ﬁve years from today, which is three years after the eﬀective date of the
swap.
In this basis swap, the dealer is receiving 2-year CMS, or Constant Ma-
turity Swap. The 2-year CMS rate is a ﬂoating index that is set equal to the
2-year swap rate each day at exactly 11:00 a.m. in New York. CMS rates
for other maturities are similarly deﬁned. The convention in the market is
that derivative transactions that depend on various CMS rates refer to a
data source known as ISDAFIX (which was created by ISDA). ISDAFIX
displays swap rates of various maturities for a variety of currencies each day.
These rates are also displayed in the Federal Reserve’s Statistical Release
H.15.42
42For USD swaps, ISDAFIX actually makes rates available from a mid-day polling at
11:00 a.m. New York time as well as an end-of-day polling at 3:00 p.m. New York time. A
number of dealers are polled and asked to submit the mean of where the dealer would bid
32

<!-- Page 56 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.11: A CMS-CMT Basis Swap
The dealer is paying a ﬂoating rate of interest equal to 2-year CMT, or
Constant Maturity Treasury, plus 30 bps. Constant Maturity Treasury rates
are yields on nominal Treasury issues adjusted to constant maturities, as
posted in the Federal Reserve Statistical Release H.15. We can think of
2-year CMT as the daily rate on a hypothetical Treasury issue with 2 years
until maturity.43 On each of the 12 quarterly resets (the ﬁrst of which is in
two years), levels for 2-year CMT and 2-year CMS are set. The spread of 30
is added to the 2-year CMT setting, which is paid by the dealer at the end
of each quarter in exchange for the 2-year CMS setting.
How do we interpret this trade? You may recall from Section 1.2.1 that a
swap spread is the diﬀerence between the swap rate of a given maturity and
the yield of the respective on-the-run Treasury. Since this basis swap has no
upfront cash ﬂows, one (loose) interpretation of this trade is that the 2-year
swap spread over the three-year period commencing two years hence is, on
average, expected to be 30 bps.44 Which side of this trade would we want
and oﬀer a swap of a particular maturity for a notional of $50mm. Of the quotes obtained,
the four highest and four smallest are tossed, and a simple average of the remaining quotes
constitutes the rate setting. See http://www.isda.org/ﬁx/isdaﬁx.html for more details.
43The 2-year CMT rate does not, in general, exactly equal the yield of the on-the-
run 2-year Treasury. Whereas the 2-year swap rate observed each day has a maturity
of exactly two years, the 2-year on-the-run Treasury does not have two years maturity
in general. A methodology is employed to adjust the yields on nominal Treasuries to a
constant maturity of two years, and this yield is the 2-year CMT rate.
44Due to some technicalities that we need not concern ourselves with here, this loose
interpretation is not entirely accurate. The risk proﬁle of this trade is more complicated
than this. In addition, as we will see in Chapter 6, trades like these have some option-like
features to them.
33

<!-- Page 57 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Figure 1.12: A Cross-Currency Swap
to take if we thought that 2-year swap spreads in the “2 by 5” period were
going to be, on average, less than 30 bps?45
1.2.3
Cross-Currency Swaps
A cross-currency swap is a swap in which the streams of payment over the
life of the trade are made in two diﬀerent currencies. The streams of payment
can both be ﬁxed, both be ﬂoating (in which case the swap is a cross-
currency basis swap) or consist of one ﬁxed stream and one ﬂoating stream.
The two counterparties in a cross-currency swap typically exchange speciﬁc
amounts of the two diﬀerent currencies at the beginning and end of the trade.
Those amounts are determined by the spot exchange rate between the two
currencies.
Example
Suppose that the spot $/yen exchange rate is 85.3, which is to say that U.S.
$1 can be exchanged for 85.3 Japanese yen (JPY). Consider the transaction
shown in Figure 1.12.
45We would want to take the position of the client. Let’s say that 2-year swap spreads
each quarter during the “2x5” period were 20 bps. Then the client would wind up receiving
2-year CMT + 30 and paying a stream of payments that is (roughly) 10 bps lower.
34

<!-- Page 58 -->
A N I N T R O D U C T I O N T O S WA P S
Notice that at the spot exchange rate of 85.3, $100mm can be exchanged
for 8.53bn JPY. These are the amounts that are exchanged not only on the
eﬀective date of the swap but also on the maturity date. Regardless of where
$/yen exchange rates may be at maturity, the ﬁnal exchange of principal is
based solely on where the exchange rate is when the trade is executed.
One way to interpret this trade is as follows: the dealer is receiving
$100mm on the eﬀective date and is ﬁnancing this “dollar loan” over the
life of the trade by paying USD LIBOR + x bps. Simultaneously, the client
is receiving 8.53bn yen on the eﬀective date and is ﬁnancing this “yen loan”
by paying JPY LIBOR + y bps. The quantities x and y are set such that
the trade has zero net present value at the inception of the trade.
2
35

These basis swap spreads are quoted as a spread to the non–3-month LIBOR
side of the basis swap. Thus, for example, the swap dealer is willing to pay
Prime minus 276 bps in order to receive 3-month LIBOR for ﬁve years, and
the dealer is also willing to receive Prime minus 272 bps in order to pay
3-month LIBOR for ﬁve years.
In a 1s3s basis swap, the standard convention is that 1-month LIBOR resets
once at the beginning of each month, accrues, and is paid at the end of the
month on a monthly Act/360 basis. In a Prime-LIBOR basis swap, Prime,
which is the interest rate that banks charge to its middle-market customers,
is reset daily during each day during a quarter. At the end of a quarter, these
daily Prime rates observed during each day of the quarter are averaged, and
that average is used for the quarterly rate set on the Prime side.46 That
average is then paid on a quarterly Act/360 basis. In all cases, 3-month LI-
BOR is reset once at the beginning of each quarter, accrues, and is paid at
the end of the quarter on an Act/360 basis, as is standard.
Suppose further that the following information about swaps rates is made
available from a swap dealer (these rates are quoted semi 30/360 versus
3-month LIBOR):
46If you want to get really technical, Prime is computed on a daily weighted average,
meaning that a rate setting on Friday counts for Saturday and Sunday as well.

Chapter 2
The Risk Characteristics and
the Traditional Uses of Swaps
In this chapter we will consider two things: the risks inherent when one
enters into a swap and the traditional ways in which investors use swaps.
When an investor enters into a swap, there are several sources of risk that
he faces. In particular, there are three primary sources of risk in a ﬁxed-
ﬂoating swap: interest rate risk, spread risk, and counterparty risk. Cross-
currency swaps also have currency risk. We shall discuss each of these in this
chapter.
It would seem an unlikely pairing, then, to discuss some of the traditional
ways in which investors have used swaps in this chapter. But it is precisely
these sources of risk (interest rate risk, spread risk, and currency risk, but not
counterparty risk) that are the reasons that investors put on these swaps in
the ﬁrst place. Movements in interest rates, swap spreads, and/or exchange
rates will change the value of a swap, which makes swaps an eﬀective hedge
for many assets and liabilities.
2.1
Interest Rate Risk
As we mentioned in Chapter 1 (and will discuss in great detail in Chapter 3),
the ﬁxed rate in a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap is set in such a way when the trade
is executed that the swap has a net present value of zero (which we will
denote NPV = 0). Although a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap is an NPV = 0 transaction

when ﬁrst executed, its value changes as swap rates move.1 Let us now
consider how the value of $100mm swaps of various maturities changes with
an instantaneous parallel shift in the swap curve shown in Table 2.1. The
swap curve refers to the relationship between maturity and swap rates. It is
analogous to the Treasury yield curve, with the exception that swap rates
replace Treasury yields. The interest rate risk inherent in these swaps is
demonstrated in Table 2.2.
These numbers were computed using a proprietary swap pricing model.2
It is important to note two things before we look at any of these computa-
tions. First, the numerical results presented here depend very much on the
particular swap model used; if we were to use a diﬀerent model, we would
likely obtain slightly diﬀerent results. Diﬀerent models employ slightly dif-
ferent methodologies and will compute diﬀerent values for these types of
sensitivities. That being said, the diﬀerences computed by diﬀerent models
that employ mainstream methods to price swaps are typically pretty small.
Second, and more important, the computations presented in Table 2.2 de-
pend very much on the particular swap curve that was in eﬀect at the time.
As swap rates move around, these numbers can vary signiﬁcantly.
Referring to Table 2.2, we see that if we enter into a $100mm 5-year swap
(and the ﬁxed coupon has been set), if there is an instantaneous parallel shift
in the swap curve downward 100 bps, then the value of the swap is $4.185mm
in favor of the ﬁxed rate receiver. If instead swap rates were to shift up 100
bps, then the value of the swap is $3.963mm against the ﬁxed rate receiver.
1Actually, in general the value of a swap will change with the passage of time even if
swap rates do not move. This characteristic is quantiﬁed by a measure known as roll-down,
which we shall examine further in Chapter 8.
2We will look at developing a swap pricing model of our own in Chapter 3.

Notice that the ﬁxed rate receiver makes more in a rally than he loses in a
sell-oﬀof equal magnitude.
As we alluded to in Chapter 1, the position of the ﬁxed rate receiver is
somewhat analogous to someone who owns a typical ﬁxed rate bond. The
numbers in Table 2.2 lend further credence to this interpretation; the owner
of a ﬁxed rate bond beneﬁts as the market rallies and is hurt as the market
sells oﬀ. Moreover, for rallies and sell-oﬀs of equal magnitude, the owner of
a ﬁxed rate bond makes more in the rally than he is hurt in a sell-oﬀ. More
formally, these phenomena are quantiﬁed in the analysis of ﬁxed rate bonds
as modiﬁed duration and convexity.3
Consider now a ﬁxed rate bond whose dirty price, P, can be regarded as
a function of its yield to maturity. The modiﬁed duration of the bond, Dm,
is given by
dP = −Dm × P × dy,
(2.1)
which relates the change in the value of the bond dP to its modiﬁed duration
Dm, its dirty price P, and a change in its yield to maturity dy. The modiﬁed
duration of a ﬁxed rate bond is not constant but depends on the level of its
yield to maturity. A bond that exhibits positive convexity has the property
that its modiﬁed duration increases as its yield to maturity decreases. Such
a bond exhibits the characteristic that its value increases by more in a rally
than it declines in a sell-oﬀof equal magnitude. A bond with positive mod-
3For a brief review of modiﬁed duration and convexity, as well as other concepts often
employed in ﬁxed income analysis, see Appendix B.
42

<!-- Page 66 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.1: Price-Yield Relationship of a Bond with Positive Modiﬁed Du-
ration and Positive Convexity
iﬁed duration and positive convexity has a price-yield relationship as shown
in Figure 2.1.
However, for swaps it is not common to employ the concept of modiﬁed
duration when analyzing interest rate sensitivity. There are a couple of rea-
sons for this. First, as we will show in Chapter 3, the valuation of swaps does
not lend itself well to the concept of a yield to maturity but rather depends
on a sequence of discount factors. This makes it diﬃcult to employ the def-
inition of modiﬁed duration as given in Equation 2.1. Second, the deﬁnition
of duration as given in Equation 2.1 depends on the price of the bond, P.
Yet a swap when initiated is an NPV = 0 transaction, meaning its “price” is
zero. Although it is possible to overcome this second issue, the industry has
nonetheless gravitated in a diﬀerent direction to quantify the interest rate
risk inherent in swaps.
2.1.1
PV01
We deﬁne the present value of a basis point, denoted PV01, of a swap to
be the change in the value of a swap due to a 1 basis point parallel shift of
the swap curve. We will say that a swap has a positive PV01 if the value of
the swap increases when the swap curve is shifted downward by 1 bp. We
will say that a swap has negative PV01 if the value of the swap decreases
when the swap curve is shifted downward by 1 bp. Thus, a swap in which we

Table 2.1. Assumed Notional of $100mm in Each Case.
are receiving ﬁxed has a positive PV01, and a swap in which we are paying
ﬁxed has a negative PV01. The PV01s for swaps of various maturities were
computed using a proprietary swap model and are shown in Table 2.3. The
column labeled “PV01” provides these PV01 calculations and assumes that
the ﬁrst LIBOR has set in the swap, and thus a parallel shift in the swap
curve does not aﬀect this setting. This measure is the most popular measure
for quantifying the interest rate sensitivity of swaps.
This table contains another set of interest rate sensitivity numbers that
are sometimes employed and that we refer to as “PV01 without LIBOR
set.” These calculations (artiﬁcially) assume that the initial LIBOR setting
in these spot starting swaps has not ﬁxed yet. Thus, under this assumption
a parallel shift in the swap curve will aﬀect the initial LIBOR setting. Some-
times people prefer to look at these PV01 numbers because it allows them to
isolate the interest rate risk inherent on the ﬁxed side of the swap separately
from the risk associated with the ﬂoating side. In comparison, a PV01 num-
ber (with LIBOR set) presents the net exposure of the ﬁxed side and the
ﬂoating side, incorporating the fact that the initial LIBOR set creates (de
minimis) interest rate sensitivity on the ﬂoating side that minimally oﬀsets
some of the interest rate sensitivity inherent in the ﬁxed leg of the swap.
In addition to these two metrics of interest rate sensitivity, this table also
contains numbers called “PV Coupon 01.” For a given swap curve, these
numbers indicate how much the value of the swap changes if we move the
ﬁxed coupon in the swap by 1 bp. Here we adopt the convention that a
positive (negative) PV Coupon 01 indicates that if we increase the coupon by
1 bp, then the value of the swap increases (decreases) to us. Thus, a positive
PV Coupon 01 is associated with a swap in which we are receiving ﬁxed.
44

<!-- Page 68 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
PV01 and PV Coupon 01 are two very diﬀerent things conceptually: the
PV01 is calculated by moving the swap curve 1 bp and asking how this
changes the value of the swap. The PV Coupon 01 is calculated by holding
the swap curve unchanged, moving the ﬁxed coupon 1 bp, and asking how
this changes the value of the swap. People sometimes use these two numbers
interchangeably, which is deﬁnitely incorrect. But as Table 2.3 shows, they
are pretty close to one another.
Once again, it is important to note that the numbers in Table 2.3 depend
very much on both the particular swap model used and the level of swap
rates. In a diﬀerent rate environment, these numbers would be diﬀerent. If
swap rates were lower, all of these numbers would be higher, and vice versa.4
PV01 in bps Upfront Instead of Dollars
People will often equivalently speak about any of these PV01 metrics in terms
of bps upfront, instead of in dollars. For example, on $100mm 10-year swaps,
the PV Coupon 01 is $75,007; $75,007 is approximately 7.5 bps upfront (i.e.,
7.5 bps × $100mm ≈$75,007). Thus, we will say that 1 bp running (meaning
a 1 bp change in the coupon of a swap) is worth 7.5 bps upfront. In this way,
there is no need to carry around a notional when speaking; one can always
apply the relevant notional of the swap to translate into dollars. For example,
on a $50mm 10-year swap, the PV Coupon 01 is also worth 7.5 bps upfront,
or 7.5 bps × $50mm ≈$37,504, half that of a $100mm swap.
Approximating PV01
The calculation of any of the PV01 numbers we just discussed requires a
swap model. In the absence of a model, it is possible to come up with an
approximation to the PV Coupon 01 using a ﬁnancial calculator. To this
end, consider an annuity of 1 bp over the life of the swap, discounted using
the relevant swap rate. The frequency of the annuity payments should con-
cur with the frequency of the payments on the ﬁxed side of the swap. The
present value of this annuity, using the swap rate as the discount rate, is
an approximation to the PV Coupon 01. It approximates moving the ﬁxed
coupon in the swap by 1 bp and asking how this aﬀects the value of the
swap. The approximation manifests itself from using the swap rate to dis-
count, instead of using a sequence of distinct discount factors (which, as we
shall discuss in Chapter 3, is most appropriate).
4Recall that the duration of a ﬁxed rate bond is not constant but is a function of the
level of rates: as rates go lower, duration goes up, and vice versa. An analogous result
holds for PV01s of swaps: the lower the swap curve, the higher the PV01 of a swap, and
vice versa.
45

<!-- Page 69 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
For example, suppose the 10-year semi bond swap rate is 5.90%, as given
in Table 2.1. Set
PMT = 1 bp/2
n = 20
i = 5.90%/2,
where payment (PMT) represents the annuity of 1 bp paid in semiannual
installments, n is the number of semiannual periods in the swap, and i is
the swap rate set as discount rate, adjusted for the frequency of the ﬁxed
payments. Using a ﬁnancial calculator, the present value of this annuity is
approximately 7.5 bps. On a notional of $100mm, this PV01 in dollars is
$100mm × 7.5 bps = $75,000,
very close to the PV Coupon 01 number for a 10-year swap given in Table 2.3.
Examples
Let’s work through a few examples to demonstrate how these various PV01
numbers can be used. In each of the following examples, we refer to the PV01
numbers in Table 2.3 that are based on the swap curve in Table 2.1.
• Suppose we enter into a $100mm 10-year swap and receive at the par
swap rate of 5.90%. If the swap curve were to rally 1 bp across the
curve, we would expect the value of the swap to be $69,253 in our
favor. The change in the value of just the ﬁxed leg of the swap in
isolation given this move would be $71,789 in our favor.
• Suppose we pay ﬁxed on a $100mm 5-year swap at a rate of 5.60%. If
the swap curve were to rally 1 bp across the curve, we would expect
the value of the swap to be $40,237 against us. If the swap curve were
to rally 10 bps across the curve, we would expect the value of the swap
to be 10 × $40,237 = $402,370 against us. This will serve as a good
approximation. However, inasmuch as the PV01s of a swap is not con-
stant but changes with the level of swap rates, this amount will not
be precise. For small perturbations of the swap curve the error will be
modest, but for larger changes it can be signiﬁcant. For example, if the
swap curve were to rally 100 bps, we would estimate that the value of
the swap would be 100 × $40,237 = $4,023,700 against us. However,
from Table 2.2, we know that the value of the swap given a 100 bp
rally will be $4,185,000 against us. The diﬀerence between these two
numbers is due to the fact that PV01s do not remain constant as swap
rates move.
46

<!-- Page 70 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
• Suppose that we receive on $50mm 30-year swaps at a rate of 6.20%.
If the swap curve were to rally 1 bp across the curve, the value of the
swap will be 0.5 × $112,636 = $56,318 in our favor. That is, PV01s
are scalable in accordance with the notional of the swap.
• Suppose we receive on a $100mm 5-year swap, but instead of receiving
at the par swap rate of 5.60%, we receive at the rate of 5.59%. In lieu
of receiving the par rate and instead receiving 1 bp lower, we should
expect to be compensated for this. In fact, using the PV Coupon 01
number for a 5-year swap, we see that if we receive 5.59%, we should
expect to be paid an upfront fee of $43,321. Note that the 5-year swap
with the coupon of 5.59% is an oﬀ-market transaction (i.e., it is not
an NPV = 0 transaction in and of itself). However, the combination
of the upfront fee and the oﬀ-market swap constitutes an NPV = 0
transaction.
• Suppose we pay ﬁxed on a 2-year swap on a notional of $1bn. Suppose
we would like to be paid an upfront fee of $1mm in the terms of the
swap and will adjust the ﬁxed rate accordingly. We should pay an above
market coupon in the swap. Since the PV Coupon 01 on $100mm
of a 2-year swap is $18,858, this means that the PV Coupon 01 on
$1bn of a 2-year swap is 10 × $18,858 = $188,580. Thus, we should
pay $1,000,000/$188,580 ≈5.3 bps above the par 2-year swap rate, or
5.11% + 0.053% = 5.163%.
Let’s consider now one ﬁnal example that makes use of the approximation
to the PV01 of a swap:
• Suppose that the 30-year semi bond swap rate is 3.583%, as depicted
in Table 1.1. Suppose further that a client is interested in paying on
$100mm of a 30-year swap in which the ﬁxed rate is quoted annual
money. As discussed in Section 1.2.1, the annual money equivalent is
3.563%. If the client and dealer make a mistake and forget to convert
the rate and instead they deal at 3.583% annual money instead of
3.563% annual money, then the client will have paid a coupon that
is 3.583% −3.563% = 2.0 bps too high. We can approximate the PV
Coupon 01 in this swap by setting
PMT = 1 bp
n = 30
i = 3.563%
and computing a present value of approximately 18.25 bps, or around
$182,500 on a notional of $100mm. Since the coupon in this trade

T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
is oﬀby 2 bps, the value of the error is around 2 × $182,500 =
$365,000.5
2
It should be noted that PV01 is not a perfect concept; the swap curve
typically does not move in a parallel fashion (this is one of the motivating
factors why some investors prefer to decouple the ﬁxed leg from the ﬂoating
leg of a swap and look at the PV01 without LIBOR set). Some investors will
look at their exposure to a shift in individual swap rates for more granular
information on their swap or portfolio of swaps.
2.2
Spread Risk
In the previous section, we considered the risk inherent in a swap given a
shift in swap rates. Note that when a swap rate moves 1 bp, this can happen
in any number of ways. For example, if the 5-year swap rate were to go up
1 bp, this could occur if the 5-year Treasury yield increased 1 bp, or if the
5-year swap spread increased 1 bp, or if the 5-year Treasury yield decreased
1 bp and at the same time the 5-year swap spread increased 2 bps, etc.
Despite the fact that a movement in swap spreads is subsumed in a movement
in swap rates, it is customary to consider the sensitivity that swaps have to
the movement of swap spreads in isolation.
Consider now the market as depicted in Table 1.1 and a $100mm 5-year
swap in which a client received at a rate of 1.606% = T5 +17. That is, at the
time this swap was executed, the bid side swap spread was 17 bps, and the
5-year note was yielding 1.436%. Suppose that a moment after the swap is
executed, 5-year Treasury yields remain the same, but 5-year swap spreads
fall 1 bp to 16. In this case, the client has received ﬁxed at 1.606%, and the
5-year swap rate is now only 1.596%. The swap is now in-the-money to the
client. The client will have recognized a mark-to-market gain on the swap.
How much is this worth to the client? According to the PV01 of a 5-year
swap found in Table 2.3, the client will be up $40,237.6
Alternatively, suppose that after executing at 1.606%, 5-year Treasury
yields are unchanged, but 5-year swap spreads increase 1 bp to 18. Now the
client ﬁnds that he has received at 1.606% and 5-year swap rates are 1.616%.
In this case, the swap is out-of-the-money to the client. And once again, the
5In Section 1.2.1, we said the value of this mistake was around $365,000, and now we
see why.
6This is not exactly what the PV01 of a swap tells us. PV01 considers a parallel shift
in the curve. Here we have considered only a movement in the 5-year swap spread. The
two numbers are practically the same, however. That is, the contribution to the PV01 of
an n-year swap comes almost entirely from the movement in the n-year swap rate and
very little from the movement in other rates.
48

<!-- Page 72 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
PV01 of the swap will tell us that the client is down $40,237.7 As these
examples indicate, swaps have spread risk, meaning that their value is af-
fected even by movements in swap spreads alone. That is, even when the
relevant Treasury yield is unchanged, the value of a swap changes when
swap spreads alone move.
We can actually take this realization a step further. If we pay ﬁxed in
a swap and buy Treasuries to hedge, the net position is such that we are
not exposed to movements in Treasuries but are only exposed to movements
in swap spreads. Let’s go back and take a look again at Figure 1.4, which
shows the position of a dealer who pays ﬁxed and hedges himself by buying
Treasuries. The dealer has paid ﬁxed at T5 + 17 = 1.436% + 17 bps = 1.606%
and has purchased Treasuries at a price of 101-16. Equivalently, we can say
that the dealer purchased Treasuries at a yield of 1.436%. Let’s look at
the value of the swap plus the hedge both in the case in which the 5-year
Treasury yield, T5, moves and in the case in which the 5-year swap spread,
denoted ss5, moves.
As shown in Table 2.4, a change in the Treasury yield by 1 bp changes the
value of the swap and the value of the Treasury hedge in equal but opposite
directions. Thus, the value of the net position is unchanged given a change
in Treasury yields. This will be true for any movement in Treasury yields.
However, a move in swap spreads changes the value of the net position: when
spreads go up, the trade moves in favor of the dealer, and when spreads go
down, the trade moves against the dealer.
This type of position in which one enters into a swap and trades Trea-
suries in order to hedge the interest rate risk associated with a ﬁxed-ﬂoating
swap is referred to as a spread trade.8 If someone receives ﬁxed and sells
Treasuries to hedge, this is referred to as being short spreads or having sold
spreads. In this trade, the value of the position will go up if spreads go down
and go down if spreads go up. Movements in Treasury yields do not aﬀect
the value of the position.
If one pays ﬁxed and buys Treasuries to hedge, this is known as being
long spreads or having bought spreads. In this case, an increase in spreads
7As mentioned previously, PV01s are not constant but depend upon the level of rates.
Thus, for even a slight move of a single basis point in the swap rate, the PV01 will not be
the same in both directions. However, the magnitude of the diﬀerence is so small that it
can be safely ignored for our purposes here.
8There are many common trades in other areas of the ﬁxed income markets in which
the interest rate risk inherent in a position is hedged with Treasuries. For example, consider
an investor who purchases a ﬁxed rate corporate bond. The investor is exposed to both
changes in interest rates and changes in the underlying credit. Oftentimes investors just
want to take a view on the underlying credit but not on interest rates. In this case the
investor who bought the ﬁxed rate corporate bond will sell Treasuries of a comparable
maturity. The investor is immunized against changes in the level of rates and is only
exposed to changes in the underlying corporate spread.
49
