means that the value of the position will go up, and a decrease in spreads
will mean a loss. A movement in the Treasury yields does not aﬀect the
value of the position. Swap spreads are a very popular thing to trade in
the swaps market. People put on positions expressing a view on (or hedging
against) movements in swap spreads. Those who wish to express the view
that spreads will widen will buy spreads, and those who wish to express the
view that spreads will tighten will short spreads.
2.2.1
A Closer Look at Swap Spreads
Figure 2.2 shows historical mid-market (deﬁned to be the average of the bid
and oﬀer) swap spreads for 2-year and 10-year swaps. Once again, the 2-
year swap spread is the spread in basis points added to the yield of the
on-the-run 2-year Treasury to come up with the 2-year swap rate. The 10-
year swap spread is deﬁned analogously. Notice that 2-year spreads are typi-
cally less than 10-year spreads.9 Swap spreads were fairly stable in the early
to mid-1990s. As we approached the end of the millennium, and Treasury
supply was diminishing and concerns about Y2K (year 2000) were ram-
pant, swaps spreads were generally increasing. Since the year 2000 up until
the ﬁnancial crisis, swaps spreads have generally trended lower. The market
saw a dramatic widening of 2-year spreads during the crisis, but since that
time spreads have generally tightened. Following the phenomenon of 30-year
9In late 1994, 2-year swap spreads traded at levels above 10-year spreads for several
days when it had been announced that Orange County’s investment portfolio had gone
bankrupt. Orange County had invested heavily in structured notes, bonds whose coupons
are linked to underlying derivative transactions. As we will see in Chapter 7, the market
correctly responded to this news with an immediate increase in 2-year, but not 10-year,
swap spreads.
50

<!-- Page 74 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.2: Historical Swap Spreads
spreads trading in negative territory (see Chapter 1), 10-year spreads ﬁrst
traded in negative territory in the spring of 2010.
There are many competing factors that explain either the equilibrium
level of swap spreads or movements in swap spreads from one day to the
next. Invariably, one of these factors takes center stage at any given time,
receiving the attention of market participants as the leading explanation of
the primary driver of spreads. Let’s ﬁrst start with an explanation suggesting
the equilibrium level of swap spreads:
• The GC-LIBOR Argument. Let’s go back to Figure 1.4, which
shows what happens when a dealer pays ﬁxed and hedges himself by
buying Treasuries and enters into a repo transaction to ﬁnance the
Treasury position. But let’s redraw this ﬁgure, focusing only on the
rates that are either paid or received over the life of the swap, as
well as the yield on Treasuries that is earned throughout time, and
the repo rate that is paid out over time as the dealer ﬁnances the
Treasury purchase. In other words, let’s redraw Figure 1.4, excluding
the initial purchase of Treasuries and payment for those Treasuries, and
just concentrate on the ﬂows that occur over the life of the trade. This
is demonstrated in Figure 2.3. By buying 5-year Treasuries, the dealer
is earning the yield T5. He needs to ﬁnance this purchase through a
repo and pays a rate of interest equal to the GC (general collateral)
51

<!-- Page 75 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.3: The GC-LIBOR Explanation of Swap Spreads
repo rate.10 The repo typically lasts for a relatively short period of
time, either overnight or for term (i.e., more than one day), but the
point here is that the repo rate is not locked in over the full ﬁve years of
the trade and is likely to be renegotiated from time to time, depending
on then-prevailing repo rates.11 In the swap itself, the dealer of course
pays a ﬁxed rate equal to T5 + 17 and receives LIBOR, which will
change every three months.
So let’s put this all together: from the top two arrows, we see that
the dealer is earning T5 on the Treasuries and paying out T5 + 17,
for a net position of paying out the swap spread of 17 bps. From the
bottom two arrows, we see the dealer is earning a net spread equal to
the diﬀerence between LIBOR and GC repo rates, which is typically a
positive number.12
A dealer who enters into this trade should on average expect to
break even, and thus the GC-LIBOR explanation of swap spreads sug-
gests that the swap spread should be equal to the expected diﬀerential
between LIBOR and GC rates over time. Of course, LIBOR is likely
to change over time, and GC repo rates are likely to change, and their
diﬀerence is likely to change as well. If we were absolutely certain that
the diﬀerence between LIBOR and GC rates were to remain constant
over time, then this diﬀerential should be equal to the swap spread.
Now let’s look at factors that don’t necessarily suggest the equilibrium
level of swap spreads but deﬁnitely cause swap spreads to move from one
10Or possibly a lower rate if the Treasuries are trading special. We will ignore that
possibility in this discussion.
11Moreover, when hedging this swap, many dealers would choose not to maintain a
long position in this particular Treasury the entire ﬁve years but would rather roll into
the on-the-run whenever a new issue settles in order to maintain a position in the most
liquid issue. We will ignore this possibility as well.
12Recall that repo rates are generally lower than LIBOR rates of comparable maturities:
repos are secured loans, whereas LIBOR represents the rate of interest banks charge other
banks for unsecured loans.
52

<!-- Page 76 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
day to the next, or even one minute to the next minute. It is important to
note that these factors may not occur in isolation, and ferreting out which
factor is the dominating eﬀect as it pertains to movements in spreads is often
diﬃcult.
• Supply and Demand. This is the biggest factor that determines
swap spread movement from one moment to the next. If investors in
aggregate want to pay ﬁxed to dealers, this will increase swap spreads,
and as they want to receive ﬁxed in aggregate, this will tend to decrease
swap spreads.13
• Treasury Supply. Depending on the funding needs of the U.S. gov-
ernment, the U.S. Treasury may either increase or decrease future
issuance of Treasuries. Even the expectation of a shift in the supply of
Treasuries can have a dramatic eﬀect on the market. If investors believe
for some reason that the supply of newly issued Treasuries is going to
be lower than had been previously expected, this is going to raise the
demand for existing Treasuries, raising their prices, and lowering their
yields. This is an example of a technical bid in the market for Trea-
suries: Treasuries prices may increase and yields may decrease not due
to some fundamental outlook on the economy but rather on supply and
demand considerations. As Treasuries become a scarce resource, the
demand for them will increase. Although Treasury yields will naturally
decline in this scenario, this should not necessarily have any bearing on
swap rates. Swap rates are a function of LIBOR rates. Inasmuch as a
decrease in Treasury yields due to the diminishing supply of Treasuries
may not aﬀect (current or future anticipated) LIBOR rates, this would
suggest that swap spreads may widen as Treasury supply decreases.
• The Shape of the Yield Curve. The steeper the yield curve (i.e.,
the greater the diﬀerential between long-term rates and short-term
rates), the more incentive investors will have to receive ﬁxed in, say, a
10-year swap (a long-term rate) and pay 3-month LIBOR (a short-term
rate). As we will see later in this chapter, this dynamic is a key driver
inducing corporate issuers to receive ﬁxed in swaps in order to swap
ﬁxed rate issuance into a synthetic ﬂoating rate issuance. As discussed
previously, if investors on aggregate want to receive ﬁxed in swaps, this
13The dynamic described here is similar to, say, the market for a stock. As investors
in general want to buy a stock, this will tend to push the price up. Similarly, as investors
want to pay ﬁxed, this will tend to push up swap spreads, as dealers will want to receive
a higher ﬁxed rate (which is composed of the Treasury yield plus the swap spread) for
additional swaps that they trade.
53

<!-- Page 77 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
will tend to cause swap spreads to tighten. Thus, as the yield curve
steepens, swap spreads often tend to tighten.14
• The Level of Rates. The conventional wisdom is that swap spreads
should tighten as the bond market rallies (provided the rally is not a
ﬂight to quality) and widen as the bond market sells oﬀ. As the bond
market rallies, mortgage homeowners have an increased incentive to re-
ﬁnance their mortgages. Holders of mortgage securities therefore tend
to experience prepayment of their mortgages as the market rallies. In
order to hedge this risk, those who hedge their mortgage holdings will
tend to receive ﬁxed as the market rallies. Conversely, as the mar-
ket sells oﬀand mortgage securities prepay slower than expected and
therefore extend, holders of mortgage securities will often pay ﬁxed in
swaps to hedge.15
• Systematic Credit and Liquidity Concerns. A shock to the ﬁnan-
cial system of almost any sort (e.g., Russia defaulting on its sovereign
debt, the near collapse of Long-Term Capital, terror threats, Y2K con-
cerns, etc.) is typically accompanied by a “ﬂight to quality” in which
investors shed assets with credit risk and buy risk-free Treasuries.
LIBOR rates, which represent the rates that banks lend to one an-
other, embody some component of credit risk and as such tend to rise
as perceived systemic risk to the ﬁnancial system is heightened. Thus,
in such an event, Treasury yields decline, LIBOR rates rise, and this
tends to increase swap spreads.
• Repo Rates. Look back at Figure 1.4, which shows the position of
a swap dealer paying ﬁxed, buying Treasuries to hedge, and entering
into a repo to ﬁnance the Treasuries. Suppose that ceteris paribus
repo rates decline. This could occur if, say, the on-the-run ﬁve-year
Treasury were to go special. In this case, the dealer is saving on his
interest expense in the repo and would be able to aﬀord (i.e., would
still break even) if he were to pay a higher swap spread. Thus, as repo
rates go down, swap spreads tend to go up, and vice versa.
The magnitude of this eﬀect tends to be quite small and dissipates
as the maturity of the swap increases. This is due to the fact that a
change in an overnight repo rate is an eﬀect that has an impact for
only one day, whereas any change in a swap spread impacts over the
life of the swap.
14This is not to suggest that swap spreads always tighten when the curve steepens.
Other market forces may be at work that cause spreads to widen when the curve is
steepening.
15For a quick review on mortgages, see Appendix D.
54

<!-- Page 78 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Example
Consider the example depicted in Figure 1.4 and assume a swap dealer
paid ﬁxed on $100mm 5-year swaps at a rate of 1.606% and hedged
the position by buying $100mm 5-year notes at 100-16. As shown in
Equation 1.13, the dirty price of the bond is $101,557,065.22, and based
upon Equation 1.14, under the assumption that the overnight repo rate
was 20 bps, the swap dealer would owe $101,557,629.43 upon ﬁnancing
the position for one day. If instead the repo rate were 0 bps, the swap
dealer would instead owe $101,557,065.22 after ﬁnancing the position
for a day (i.e., the amount owed is equal to the amount borrowed when
the repo rate is zero). Thus, a 20 bps change in the repo rate reduces
the cost of ﬁnancing by $101,557,629.43 −$101,557,065.22 = $564.21.
Moreover, assume that it is anticipated that the repo rate on this se-
curity will fall from 20 bps to 0 bps until August 31, 2010 (a total of 19
days), the settlement date of the next newly issued 5-year note. In this
case, the ﬁnancing costs of the dealer will be expected to be reduced
by $564.21 × 19 = $10,719.99. Suppose this savings to the swap dealer
were to be reﬂected in an increased swap spread paid by the dealer.16
The PV Coupon 01 in this swap can be approximated by putting
PMT = 1 bp/2
n = 10
i = 1.606%/2
and getting PV = 4.8 bps, or around $48,000 on the notional of $100mm.
Thus, the reduced ﬁnancing costs of $10,719.99 translated into a 5-year
swap spread increase of
$10,719.99
$48,000 per bp ≈0.2 bps.
2
The Summer of 2003
The summer of 2003 was a particularly volatile time in the ﬁxed income
markets and was a period in which supply and demand factors greatly in-
ﬂuenced the level of Treasury yields and swap spreads. Figure 2.4 shows
16The forces of competition will incentivize a swap dealer to increase the swap spread
he is willing to pay when repo rates decline. If repo rates in the market drop, then all swap
dealers who are paying ﬁxed and buying Treasuries to hedge would beneﬁt from reduced
ﬁnancing costs. In order to win more business in this scenario, swap dealers would be
inclined to increase the spread they are willing to pay, without aﬀecting their P&L (proﬁt
and loss).
55

<!-- Page 79 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
movements in 10-year swap spreads superimposed on movements in 10-year
Treasury yields during this period. Over the course of six weeks, the yield
of the on-the-run 10-year Treasury increased nearly 150 bps.17 As yields on
Treasuries began to rise, the duration of mortgage securities increased as
well.18 This meant that these securities were becoming even more sensitive
to changes in interest rates, causing large mortgage holders to rebalance their
portfolios.19 Some estimate that over this six-week period, the aggregate du-
ration of the mortgage market increased by $800bn 10-year equivalents (i.e.,
loosely speaking, it was as if mortgage investors in aggregate had gotten long
an extra $800bn 10-year Treasuries during a six-week period in which the
market had sold oﬀabout 150 bps).20 Investors who had huge portfolios of
mortgages, needing to manage their interest rate risk, found it useful to do
so either by selling Treasuries or by paying ﬁxed in swaps.21 As more and
more mortgage holders rebalanced their portfolios by selling Treasuries or by
paying ﬁxed in swaps, this actually fed on itself, causing rates to rise further,
which triggered additional selling.22 As swap dealers were asked to receive
ﬁxed more and more, swaps spreads started to rise—and did so rapidly.
Both the vast selling of Treasuries and the paying of ﬁxed in swaps caused
Treasury yields and swap spreads to rise dramatically. After a number of
weeks, the selling died down, as did the need for mortgage investors to pay
17There were a number of reasons for the dramatic sell-oﬀin the market, but to many
it is believed that the initial catalyst was the announcement by the Fed that they were not
going to purchase Treasury securities in the open market as an unconventional method of
maintaining low interest rates in order to stimulate the economy. There had been much
speculation that the Fed would resort to this unconventional method, and investors who
had held Treasuries in part anticipating that the Fed would be forced to buy Treasuries
in this manner were quick to sell them upon the announcement by the Fed, thus causing
Treasury yields to begin to rise rapidly.
18The value of mortgage securities is sensitive to the level of interest rates. Mortgage
securities are subject to prepayment, meaning that the underlying loans can be prepaid
by the borrower. As interest rates fall, the economic incentive for borrowers to reﬁnance
increases, which is to say that their incentive to prepay their existing mortgages increases.
As rates rise, it becomes less likely that the loans will be prepaid, and hence the average life
of a mortgage, and its duration, increases as well. Once again, for a review of mortgages,
see Appendix D.
19This is the characteristic negative convexity of mortgages: as rates rise, mortgages
increase in duration. Thus, if we hold mortgage securities in our portfolio, our portfolio
becomes “longer the market” just when we don’t want that to occur.
20The movements in Treasury yields during this time were not uniformly in one direc-
tion; the market experienced signiﬁcant oscillation over this period, sometimes rallying
and sometimes selling oﬀbefore ﬁnally reaching equilibrium levels at much higher yields
some weeks later.
21Remember, we said that paying ﬁxed in a swap is kind of like selling a ﬁxed rate bond.
22The adage “selling begets selling” deﬁnitely applies.
56

<!-- Page 80 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.4: Swap Spreads Versus Treasury Yields During the Summer of 2003
ﬁxed.23 As the smoke cleared, the swaps market had found that 10-year swap
spreads had been through one of their most dramatic increases anyone had
ever seen. At this point, speculators came into the market and put on posi-
tions that expressed their view that swap spreads would soon decrease back
within historical norms. As Figure 2.4 shows, spreads did return to lower
levels shortly thereafter.
2.3
Currency Risk
Cross-currency swaps have currency (or exchange rate) risk. The easiest way
to see this is to recall that the initial and ﬁnal exchanges of principal are
made at the spot exchange rate. If exchange rates move dramatically over the
life of the transaction, the oﬀ-marketness of the ﬁnal payments at maturity
can be huge.
23The selling died down, as did the need for mortgage investors to pay ﬁxed once the
rate of mortgage extension slowed signiﬁcantly. That is, after the market had sold oﬀto
the extent that it had, any further increase in rates would not cause mortgage durations
to increase substantively. To put this another way, given the large backup in rates, the
embedded options to prepay found in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) were so far out-
of-the-money that the negative convexity in such securities was of little consequence.
57

<!-- Page 81 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
2.4
Counterparty Risk
That leaves one source of risk that we haven’t touched upon yet: counter-
party credit risk. Each counterparty in a swap enters into the transaction and
expects that the other will be able to make the payments that he has com-
mitted to make over the life of the transaction. But what if a counterparty,
say, the ﬁxed rate payer, should go bankrupt and not have the funds available
to make payments in the swap? This would constitute a default on the part
of the ﬁxed rate payer. If at the time of default the swap were in favor of the
ﬂoating rate payer, then the ﬂoating rate payer would have a loss if he had to
write oﬀthe value of the swap due to the insolvency of the ﬁxed rate payer.
Counterparty credit exposure is a term that refers to the amount that some-
one could lose on a swap (or a portfolio of swaps) if its counterparty defaults.
Counterparty credit exposure can be quite signiﬁcant. Table 2.2 gave us an
idea of how much a swap’s value can change. Of course, in this table we only
considered an instantaneous change in the value of swaps for a given interest
rate shift. A credit risk oﬃcer will be interested in knowing how much the
value of a swap may move over time. One metric that might be proposed is
looking at, say, a 95% conﬁdence interval of the value of a swap for each day
during the life of the swap. That is, given some model of interest rates that
accounts for movements in rates over time, we could come up with a number
for each day during the life of the swap for which there is a 95% chance that
the value of the swap will not exceed this number. The relationship between
the passage of time and the magnitude of the 95% conﬁdence interval is re-
ferred to as the exposure proﬁle of the swap. Figure 2.5 shows what a typical
exposure proﬁle for an interest rate swap looks like.24
Note that the exposure proﬁle is such that when the swap is ﬁrst executed,
it has zero exposure. There are two eﬀects that cause the exposure proﬁle to
look the way it does as time goes on. First, as time passes, it is more likely
that rates move a lot, and this tends to increase the maximum exposure of
the swap. Second, as time goes on, the swap is paying oﬀcoupons, and even
though rates may be moving a lot from where they were initially, we just
don’t get a lot of bang for our buck with the now-short maturity swap.25
24The modeling, management, and pricing of credit exposures resulting from OTC
derivative trades are complex. Some institutions will actively manage their counterparty
credit exposure to a particular counterparty or counterparties. The cost of hedging coun-
terparty credit exposure (i.e., the market value of the counterparty credit exposure) is
referred to in the industry as Credit Valuation Adjustment (CVA). For further informa-
tion on these topics, see, for example, Flavell (2010), Cesari et al. (2009), and Ammann
(2001).
25Coupons that have come and gone in a swap no longer ﬁgure into its existing val-
uation, much as the dividends already paid by a stock do not contribute to the current
value of the stock.
58

<!-- Page 82 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.5: Interest Rate Swap Exposure Proﬁle
That is, as time goes on, the remaining swap has a lower PV01. Eventually
the second eﬀect dominates the ﬁrst, and the exposure proﬁle starts coming
down. As the swap approaches maturity, the exposure approaches zero.
Cross-currency swaps have a much diﬀerent exposure proﬁle, as demon-
strated in Figure 2.6. Cross-currency swaps have an exchange of principal
on the ﬁnal day of the swap. The spot exchange rate when the swap is ﬁrst
executed is used to set the principal amounts that will be exchanged at ma-
turity. Inasmuch as exchange rates may vary signiﬁcantly over the life of the
swap, the exposure proﬁle on a cross-currency swap remains high even as
the swap approaches maturity.
In order to reduce the counterparty risk inherent in swaps, counterparties
will often put in place a Credit Support Annex (CSA) in addition to an ISDA
Master Agreement. The CSA will provide for the transfer of collateral, either
cash or securities, from one counterparty to the other in order to immunize
against changes in a portfolio of OTC derivatives, including swaps. Under
the terms of a CSA, a counterparty, say, a client, who is out-of-the-money on
its derivatives portfolio with another counterparty, say, a dealer, will have
to post collateral to the latter to protect against the possibility of default.
In the event of a default, the dealer could then sell the collateral in the mar-
ket in an attempt to make himself whole. The terms of a CSA between two
counterparties will, among other things, specify the types of collateral that
are acceptable, thresholds in the mark-to-market of the derivatives portfolio
below which no collateral is posted, and minimum transfer amounts of col-
59

<!-- Page 83 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.6: Currency Swap Exposure Proﬁle
lateral (preventing nuisance transfer amounts for de minimis changes in the
mark-to-market of the derivatives portfolio).26
The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act
The Dodd-Frank Act, signed into law in July 2010 in response to the ﬁnan-
cial crisis, provides for sweeping changes to the ﬁnancial system. Although
the Act covers a wide range of reform, our focus here is on Title VII, which
pertains to the OTC derivatives markets. The goals of the Act as it per-
tains to derivatives are to increase transparency and reduce systemic risk.
The collapse of Lehman and the near-collapse of AIG prompted lawmak-
26One of the most famous examples of the workings of CSAs is found in the bailout of
the American International Group (AIG). AIG, with its high credit rating, had enjoyed
very advantageous terms (regarding the collateral it was required to post) in the CSAs it
had negotiated with many dealers. But many of these CSAs required AIG to start posting
additional collateral based on the downgrading of its credit.
AIG was known to have sold credit default swaps on a variety of mortgage-related under-
lyings. At the height of the crisis, many of these underlyings deteriorated signiﬁcantly, and
the mark-to-market on AIG’s credit derivative positions moved sharply against the ﬁrm.
The downgrades of AIG in conjunction with the continued deterioration of its derivative
positions during the crisis triggered collateral calls by many dealers, ultimately leading to
a government bailout of AIG in September 2008. See Sorkin (2009) for more details.
60

<!-- Page 84 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
ers to implement changes aimed at reducing the risk of systemic failure
of the ﬁnancial system. The concern is that the inability of an insolvent
ﬁnancial institution to perform on its OTC derivatives positions with its
various counterparties could cause a ripple eﬀect throughout the ﬁnancial
system.
Title VII of the Act contains three broad mandates. The ﬁrst of these,
known as the clearing mandate, requires that certain derivative transactions
must be centrally cleared through clearing houses. The U.S. Commodity
Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and the U.S. Securities and Exchange
Commission (SEC) are jointly responsible for determining which swaps are
to be cleared.27
The second mandate is referred to as the trading mandate and requires
that any swap that is required to be centrally cleared must be traded on
either an exchange or a swap execution facility, presuming that the swap is
made available for trade. A swap execution facility is deﬁned in Section 721
of the Act as a “trading system or platform in which multiple participants
have the ability to execute or trade swaps by accepting bids and oﬀers made
by multiple participants in the facility or system.”
The third mandate, the registration mandate, requires that certain mar-
ket participants, known as swap dealers and major swap participants, register
with the CFTC and/or the SEC. A swap dealer is deﬁned in the Act to be
any person (i.e., an entity) who holds itself out as a dealer in swaps, makes
a market in swaps, regularly enters into swaps with counterparties as an
ordinary course of business for its own account, or engages in any activity
causing the person (i.e., an entity) to be commonly known in the trade as
a dealer or market maker in swaps. A major swap participant is deﬁned in
the Act as any person who is not a swap dealer and maintains a substantial
position in swaps, whose outstanding swaps create substantial counterparty
exposure that could have serious adverse eﬀects on the ﬁnancial stability of
the U.S. banking system or ﬁnancial markets or is a ﬁnancial entity that is
highly leveraged relative to the amount of capital it holds.28
It is important to draw a distinction between the clearing of derivative
transactions under Dodd-Frank and the trading of derivative transactions
27The CFTC is broadly responsible for the regulation of derivative transactions known
as “swaps” in the Act, and the SEC is responsible for “security-based swaps.” A third
category of trades known as “mixed swaps” is subject to joint regulation by the CFTC
and the SEC. Interest rate swaps in particular fall in the category of “swaps” and as such
will be regulated by the CFTC. For our purposes here, we will not distinguish between
the various categories.
28For more details on the Dodd-Frank Act, see The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Re-
form and Consumer Protection Act, downloaded from http://docs.house.gov/rules/ﬁnserv
/111 hr4173 ﬁnsrvcr.pdf. For a brief summary of the Act, see Davis Polk & Wardwell
(2010).
61

<!-- Page 85 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.7: The Nature of Counterparty Credit Exposure When Counterpar-
ties Face Each Other Bilaterally and Under Central Clearing
Source: Figure adapted from Duﬃe, Li, and Lubke (2010).
under Dodd-Frank. The clearing mandate will imply a change from the bi-
lateral nature of counterparty credit risk, which has been synonymous with
the OTC derivative markets. This change is depicted in Figure 2.7, which is
adapted from a ﬁgure presented in Duﬃe, Li, and Lubke (2010).
The move to central clearing implies that a trade, which may be executed
bilaterally or on an exchange, will not have the two counterparties to the
trade facing one another directly, but, rather, each counterparty will face a
central clearing house.29 The belief held by some is that the existence of cen-
tral counterparties will mitigate counterparty credit risk and limit the possi-
bility of ﬁnancial contagion throughout the ﬁnancial system. In the event of
29As Duﬃe, Li, and Lubke (2010) point out, Figure 2.7 should not be interpreted
as a literal depiction of the move from bilateral counterparties to central clearing. For
example, if the counterparties A through F depicted in this ﬁgure represent both buy-side
institutions and swap dealers, then the left-hand portion of the ﬁgure is inaccurate since
buy-side institutions typically do not face one another in OTC derivative trades. Moreover,
most buy-side institutions will not be clearing members of a clearing house themselves
and thus will not face the clearing house directly as depicted on the right-hand portion
of the ﬁgure. Instead, most buy-side institutions will utilize a clearing member or futures
commission merchant (FCM) to clear their trades.
62

<!-- Page 86 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
the insolvency of a large player in the derivative markets, it is proposed by
some that central clearing would reduce the risk of triggering a ripple eﬀect.
If a clearing member should fail to meet its obligations under its derivative
contracts, the belief is that the central clearing house would absorb the losses
itself and continue to make payments on derivative transactions to its other
members, thereby insulating against a ripple eﬀect.30
Whereas a move of the swaps market to an exchange for purposes of
trading would be a radical departure from the market structure currently
synonymous with the OTC market, trading swaps on a swap execution fa-
cility has the ability to maintain an OTC marketplace. In particular, a swap
execution facility may allow counterparties to continue to transact deriva-
tives bilaterally, albeit on a regulated trading system.
2.5
Traditional Uses of Swaps
Having now looked at the risk characteristics of swaps, we will take a look
now at a number of traditional ways in which investors use swaps.31 In
particular, we will examine the use of swaps when corporations issue bonds
and want to change the characteristics of the cash ﬂows of the bond. We will
also look at asset swaps in which investors buy or sell bonds and use swaps
to change the nature of the cash ﬂows of the bond. Finally, we will look at
how swaps are used as a part of balance sheet management by a variety of
institutions.
2.5.1
New Issue Hedging
Suppose that a corporation wishes to raise $500mm to undertake an invest-
ment project by issuing ﬁve-year debt. Given its asset and liability mix, the
corporation would like to fund itself on a ﬂoating rate basis.32 The corpora-
tion works closely with its investment bankers and determines that there is
signiﬁcant investor demand for the corporation to issue one of two alternative
bonds. The corporation could issue $500mm of a ﬁve-year ﬂoating rate note
30For a more detailed discussion of these and other issues, see Duﬃe, Li, and Lubke
(2010) or Pirrong (2008–2009).
31As mentioned in Section 1.1, there are many nontraditional uses of swaps that will
be discussed in the chapters ahead.
32Many corporations choose to fund themselves on a ﬂoating rate basis. Doing so pre-
vents a lot of mark-to-market change in the value of debt, which is often desirable. If a
corporation has ﬁxed rate liabilities, the value of that liability can move substantially,
given a move in rates. When a corporation has a ﬂoating rate liability, the value of the lia-
bility does not vary tremendously with movements in rates; as rates move, the (expected)
coupons on the liability go up or down accordingly, and the value of the liability does not
move signiﬁcantly.
63

<!-- Page 87 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.8: New Issue Hedging
with a coupon of 3-month LIBOR + 37 bps. Alternatively, the corporation
could issue $500mm of a ﬁve-year ﬁxed rate bond with a coupon of T5 + 50
bps paid semiannually. What should the corporation do if it wants to have
ﬂoating rate funding and to obtain the most attractive funding possible?
Although the corporation wants to fund itself on a ﬂoating rate basis,
this does not mean that it should disregard the opportunity to issue a ﬁxed
rate bond. Let’s go back to Table 1.1. Notice that if a client wants to receive
ﬁxed in ﬁve years, a dealer is willing to pay T5 + 17. That is, the bid side
swap spread in ﬁve years is 17 bps, indicating that a dealer will pay a ﬁxed
coupon that is 17 bps over the yield of the ﬁve-year on-the-run Treasury in
exchange for 3-month LIBOR in a ﬁve-year swap.
If the corporation were to issue a ﬁxed rate bond with a coupon of T5 +
50 and enter into a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap in which it receives ﬁxed from a
swap dealer at T5 + 17, then the corporation would create synthetic ﬂoating
rate funding of 3-month LIBOR + 33 bps. This strategy would save the
corporation 4 bps running (i.e., over the life of the transaction) as compared
to issuing a ﬂoating rate bond with a coupon of 3-month LIBOR + 37 bps.
This opportunity is shown in Figure 2.8.
How do we ﬁgure out that the corporation would create synthetic ﬂoat-
ing rate funding of LIBOR + 33 bps if they issue a ﬁxed rate bond and
swap to ﬂoating? In Figure 2.8, the ﬁxed semiannual 30/360 coupon pay-
ments of T5 + 50 that the corporation is paying to the bondholders more
or less “cancel out” with the ﬁxed semiannual 30/360 coupon payments of
T5 + 17.33 These ﬂows more or less cancel out because if you do the math of
33Recall that in a spread trade the Treasuries are traded not to exactly oﬀset the ﬁxed
cash ﬂows in the swap but rather to immunize against changes in the value of the swap
64

<!-- Page 88 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
these two ﬂows in Figure 2.8, the “outﬂow” of T5 + 50 partially nets against
the “inﬂow” of T5 + 17, giving −(T5 + 50) + T5 + 17 = −33 bps. That is,
the net of these two ﬂows leaves the corporation paying out an annuity of
33 bps semiannually over the life of the trade. Thus, the corporation is left
paying 3-month LIBOR quarterly Act/360 and also paying an annuity of 33
bps, or more simply, 3-month LIBOR + 33 bps.34
So if the corporation issues a ﬁxed rate bond and swaps to ﬂoating, it cre-
ates synthetic ﬂoating rate funding of 3-month LIBOR + 33 bps. Recall that
if the corporation had issued a ﬂoater, it would have to pay coupons equal to
3-month LIBOR + 37 bps. By issuing ﬁxed and swapping, the corporation
is saving itself 4 bps running, or over the life of the transaction. The value
of this savings can be computed by approximating the PV Coupon 01 of a
5-year swap. The PV Coupon 01 for $100mm 5-year swaps is approximated
by putting
PMT = 1 bp/2
n = 10
i = 1.606%/2
and getting PV = 4.8 bps, or around $48,000. Thus, the present value of the
savings from the synthetic ﬂoating rate funding is around35
4 × $48,000 × 5 = $960,000.
The factor of 4 in the preceding equation comes from the fact that we are
saving 4 bps, and the factor of 5 comes from the fact that the notional on the
swap is $500mm, whereas the PV Coupon 01 was computed on an assumed
notional of $100mm.
The corporation has accomplished a few other things by issuing ﬁxed rate
bonds and swapping into ﬂoating. First, by transforming the nature of their
that result from movement in rates. In new issuance hedging, this is not the case. Most
often the ﬁxed cash ﬂows in the swap are set to exactly oﬀset the cash ﬂows from the bond
being issued. Corporations often choose to do this for a number of reasons, not the least
of which is to qualify for certain (simpliﬁed) accounting treatment of the swap as a hedge.
34It’s not so simple, actually. To be technically correct, we can’t just move the 33 bps,
which are paid semi bond to the ﬂoating side that is paid quarterly money. There would
be a small adjustment to account for the fact that one leg pays semi bond and the other
leg pays quarterly money. In other words, there is an adjustment made both for the change
in the frequency and for the change in the day count for the spread. We will ignore this
adjustment for our purposes here.
35This calculation is not entirely accurate. The PV Coupon 01 is calculated assuming a
1 bp change in the ﬁxed leg of a swap, which pays semi bond. The 4 bps saving here is on the
ﬂoating side, which pays quarterly money. Thus, the savings given here is approximately
correct.
65

<!-- Page 89 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
liability from ﬁxed to ﬂoating, the duration of their liability moves to (near)
zero. That is, the value of their liability is not going to change signiﬁcantly
when interest rates change. This is not the case with a ﬁxed rate liability;
as rates go down, the value of a ﬁxed rate liability increases, and vice versa.
Second, if the yield curve is upward sloping, as it often is, then the corpo-
ration’s interest expense in the current period may be signiﬁcantly reduced
by swapping from ﬁxed to ﬂoating. Of course, if the corporation issues a
ﬁxed rate bond and swaps to ﬂoating, it is exposed if rates subsequently
move higher. In this case the interest expense the corporation faces could be
higher than if it had maintained its ﬁxed rate liability.
New Issue Hedging Using Cross-Currency Swaps
Let’s consider now a second example that shows how a cross-currency swap
may be used to hedge an issuance in one currency to create synthetic ex-
posure in another currency. Suppose there is a Japanese corporation that
would like to raise 8.53bn yen in order to undertake an investment project.
The corporation has decided that it would like to fund itself on a ﬂoating
rate basis denominated in yen. The term of the investment project is ﬁve
years.
The corporation works with its investment bankers who inform the cor-
poration that there is demand from investors for the corporation to issue a
ﬁve-year ﬂoater denominated in yen. The bonds would be issued at par and
would have a coupon of JPY LIBOR + 25. Alternatively, there is also suﬃ-
cient demand from investors for the Japanese corporation to issue a ﬁve-year
ﬂoater denominated in USD. The principal amount on this bond would be
$100mm, the equivalent of 8.53bn yen at the assumed spot exchange rate of
85.3. This bond would also be issued at par and would have a coupon of
USD LIBOR + 27. Which of the two alternatives should the corporation
pursue?
The answer is, it depends. Although the corporation wishes to fund itself
on a ﬂoating basis denominated in yen, it should not dismiss the possibility
of issuing in dollars. The corporation can issue in dollars and use a cross-
currency swap in order to swap the coupons to ﬂoating yen, creating syn-
thetic ﬂoating rate funding denominated in yen. The determination of which
alternative is more advantageous depends on the pricing implicit in the cross-
currency swap. If the corporation issues a dollar-denominated ﬂoater, it will
pay coupons of USD LIBOR + 27. Suppose that the corporation contacts a
swap dealer and indicates it may have interest in entering into a dollar-yen
cross-currency basis swap. The corporation indicates that the terms of the
swap would be as follows:
66

<!-- Page 90 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.9: Japanese Corporation Issuing in Dollars and Entering into a
Cross-Currency Swap in Order to Create Synthetic Floating Rate Funding
Denominated in Yen
1. Initial Exchange: The corporation would pay $100mm to and receive
8.53bn yen from the dealer.
2. Interest Payments: The corporation would receive USD LIBOR + 27
and would pay JPY LIBOR + x to the dealer.
3. Final Exchange: The corporation would receive $100mm from and pay
8.53bn yen to the dealer.
The spread x will be set such that the cross-currency swap is an NPV = 0
transaction. Suppose that the dealer prices up the swap and determines that
x = 22 bps. In this case, the corporation is better oﬀissuing in dollars and
67

<!-- Page 91 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
entering into the cross-currency swap. If it does so, it will create synthetic
ﬂoating rate funding of JPY LIBOR + 22 bps, a savings of 3 bps running
compared to the direct issuance of a yen ﬂoater with a coupon of JPY LIBOR
+ 25 bps. Figure 2.9 shows the mechanics faced by the corporation if it issues
in dollars and enters into the cross-currency swap. At the time the bond is
issued, the corporation receives $100mm from the bondholders, but it deliv-
ers this amount to the dealer in exchange for 8.53bn yen. Over the life of the
transaction, the dealer pays USD LIBOR + 27 coupons to the corporation,
which it uses to pay the bondholders. The corporation is left paying JPY
LIBOR + 22. And at maturity, the dealer pays $100mm principal to the
corporation, which it uses to return the principal to the bondholders, and it
pays the 8.53bn yen to the dealer.
As shown in this ﬁgure, by issuing in dollars and entering into the cross-
currency swap, the corporation synthetically raises 8.53bn yen, funds it-
self at JPY LIBOR + 22, and returns 8.53bn yen at maturity. Notice that
even though the margin on the dollar issuance was higher than on the yen-
denominated issuance (27 bps versus 25 bps), it was nonetheless advan-
tageous for the corporate to issue in dollars and swap due to the pricing
inherent in the basis swap.
2.5.2
Asset Swaps
An asset swap is a popular trade in which an investor simultaneously pur-
chases a bond and uses a swap to transform the nature of the asset from,
say, a ﬁxed one to a ﬂoating one.36 Figure 2.10 shows how an investor who
has purchased a ﬁxed rate bond asset swaps the bond into a ﬂoater. There
are a variety of diﬀerent methodologies that are used to asset swap bonds.
In the 1990s, a common type of asset swap transaction was known as a par
asset swap. An investor would buy a ﬁxed rate bond and enter into a swap
in which he would pay ﬁxed. The terms of the swap would be such that
there would be an upfront payment made in the swap such that the dirty
price of the bond, which is the quoted price plus accrued, plus the upfront
amount would equal par, or the face amount of the bond. Thus, if the dirty
price of the bond were less than par, the swap would include a payment from
the investor to the dealer, and vice versa.37 The terms of the swap would
be such that the investor would pay ﬁxed coupons in the swap in the exact
amount and on the exact dates in which the bond payments were received.
36Most asset swaps involve the purchase of a ﬁxed rate bond, although some involve
the purchase of a ﬂoating rate bond and use of a swap to transform the coupons to
ﬁxed.
37Note that once the upfront payment has been made at the outset of the swap, it is
no longer an NPV = 0 transaction. Suppose that in the terms of the asset swap the ﬁxed
68

<!-- Page 92 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
Figure 2.10: An Asset Swap
In return, the investor would receive LIBOR plus or minus a spread. This
spread is known as the asset swap spread. An investor doing a par asset
swap would want to receive as high of a spread to LIBOR as possible; a high
spread would indicate that the bond was “cheap on a LIBOR basis,” and a
low spread would indicate that the bond was “rich on a LIBOR basis.”
More recently, the variety of asset swap most often employed results in a
much simpler transaction. It is now more common for an investor to buy the
bond and pay ﬁxed in a swap that has a maturity date equal to the maturity
of the bond. In this type of transaction there is no upfront payment, and the
ﬁxed cash ﬂows in the swap do not in general exactly match the coupons
in the bond. The diﬀerence between the swap rate that matures when the
bond matures and the yield of the bond is referred to as the yield/yield asset
swap spread. As in the case of a par asset swap, the higher the asset swap
spread, the cheaper the bond is considered, and vice versa.
We’ll have more to say about asset swaps in Chapter 8. For now, here
are a few takeaways to keep in mind. First, when an investor buys a ﬁxed
rate payer makes an upfront payment of $100,000 to its counterparty. Then after that
upfront payment in the swap has been made, the value of the remaining cash ﬂows in the
swap is $100,000 in favor of the ﬁxed rate payer.
69

<!-- Page 93 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
rate bond and asset swaps it, he no longer has material duration exposure
on the bond since he is transforming it to a ﬂoater. That is, the package of
the bond and the swap together have no (or little) sensitivity to movements
in interest rates. Second, the investor still has exposure to the underlying
credit he is buying; if the credit spread on the bond should tighten, he will
beneﬁt, and vice versa. Third (and this should not necessarily be clear at
this point in time), whereas an investor who buys the bond and asset swaps
it is going to beneﬁt if the credit spread of the bond tightens, he will also
beneﬁt if swaps spreads in the oﬀsetting swap widen. Again, we will look at
these dynamics much more closely in Chapter 8.
2.5.3
Balance Sheet Management
In Section 2.2.1, we alluded to the fact that swaps are used for balance sheet
management purposes. Remember how swap spreads widened dramatically
in the summer of 2003 because of all of those mortgage portfolios who were
paying ﬁxed? As the market sold oﬀ, mortgage securities were extending,
which increased the duration of mortgage portfolios. Due to the negative
convexity of mortgages, mortgage portfolios were getting longer and longer
Figure 2.11: Mortgage Portfolio Paying Fixed to Manage Duration Risk
70

<!-- Page 94 -->
T H E R I S K C H A R AC T ER I S T I C S A N D T H E T R A D I T I O NA L U S E S O F S WA P S
the market as rates rose. This is of course a bad thing for those portfolios,
because as the market sells oﬀ, the last thing you want is to essentially have
more ﬁxed rate bonds in your portfolio than you started with, which are de-
creasing in value as rates rise. But this is the plight of those whose portfolios
exhibit negative convexity. And during the summer of 2003, many mortgage
portfolios managed this increase in the duration of their portfolios by paying
ﬁxed in swaps. This dynamic is depicted in Figure 2.11.
71

<!-- Page 95 -->

38Technically, this is not entirely accurate. We are not quite free to “add or subtract”

<!-- Page 99 -->
Chapter 3
The Pricing of Swaps
We spent a considerable amount of time in Chapter 1 discussing the funda-
mentals of the swaps market. In Chapter 2 we focused on the risk charac-
teristics of swaps. In this chapter we are going to be focused on the pricing
of swaps.
When we speak of pricing a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap when it is ﬁrst exe-
cuted, this amounts to determining the ﬁxed coupon that makes the trade an
NPV=0 transaction. At other times we will be interested in valuing a ﬁxed-
ﬂoating swap that has been in existence for some time. In these cases, the
pricing of a swap refers to determining the mark-to-market value of a swap
whose ﬁxed coupon was previously set. Prior to addressing both of these
questions, we will examine the relationship between swap rates and Euro-
dollar futures.
3.1
Where Do Swap Rates Come From?
There is an intimate relationship between swap rates and Eurodollar futures
contracts.1 In order to understand this relationship, we will ﬁrst go through
the very basics of Eurodollar futures. Eurodollars are time deposits made
1This is true for swaps with maturities of ﬁve years and less. Beyond ﬁve years, Euro-
dollar futures contracts are not considered very liquid by many traders, and as a result
they are typically not used to price swaps with a maturity of greater than ﬁve years. We
will implicitly restrict our attention, then, to the pricing of swaps on the short end of the
curve in this section.

<!-- Page 100 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
in U.S. dollars but are actually held at banks outside of the United States.
The rates of interest implicit in these deposits have long been a standard
benchmark in the market. A Eurodollar futures contract is traded on the
Chicago Mercantile Exchange and2
• Is a commitment to deliver a $1mm Eurodollar time deposit with a
3-month maturity;
• Has a price that is quoted as an index value equal to 100 minus the
Eurodollar interest rate (thus, e.g., a rate of 3% has a price quote of
97.00);
• Has contracts months that are March, June, September, and December
for a total of 10 years (40 contracts);3
• Has as its last trading day two London business days before the third
Wednesday of the contract month;
• Has a cash settlement price equal to 100 minus the BBA survey of
3-month USD LIBOR on the last trading day;
• Has $25 in value for a 1 bp move in the underlying rate (a “tick”);
contracts may trade in half-tick increments, with the exception of the
nearest-term contract, which can trade in quarter-tick increments.
Although Eurodollar futures are indeed futures and thus have daily variation
margin, we will for our purposes here ignore this feature and regard them as
forwards.4
2For more detail on Eurodollar futures, see Burghardt (2003) or Hull (2008), for
example; or for information from the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, go to http://www
.cmegroup.com/trading/interest-rates/stir/eurodollar.html.
3In addition, the four nearest serial months that are not of this cycle also trade.
4A forward contract is an agreement to buy or sell some underlying asset at a speciﬁed
time (which we will refer to as the settlement date) at a price that is speciﬁed today. That
price that is speciﬁed today is known as the forward price. No cash exchanges hands today,
and in fact the only time cash changes hands is at the settlement of the contract. A futures
contract is similar to a forward contract, except there is a daily marking-to-market of
the contract. Counterparties in a futures contract are subject to daily variation margin,
reﬂecting changes in the value of the contract. Forwards and futures are very similar
except that futures have cash ﬂows that occur over the life of the contract. The price at
which the underlying asset will be bought or sold in a futures contract is referred to as
the futures price.
Are forward prices equal to futures prices? This is a question that has been analyzed
extensively. Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross (1981) and Jarrow and Oldﬁeld (1981) both deter-
mined that futures prices and forward prices can diﬀer under the assumption that interest
rates are stochastic. If interest rates are stochastic, then futures prices will be greater
than forward prices, provided that futures prices are positively correlated with interest
77

<!-- Page 101 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.1: Hedging with a Eurodollar Future
By trading in Eurodollar futures, an investor is able to lock in the rate
implied by the contract. For example, suppose that on September 13, 2010,
an investor buys 100 of the Dec 10 Eurodollar contract (whose last trad-
ing day is Monday, December 13, 2010) at 99.595. If the investor holds the
contract to expiration, he will lock in an interest rate for the 90-day period
between December 15, 2010 and March 15, 2011 of 100 −99.595 = 0.405%
and protect himself from a decline in the then-prevailing 3-month LIBOR
rate below the level of 0.405% (see Figure 3.1). On December 13, the in-
vestor may enter into a Eurodollar investment at the then-prevailing 3-month
LIBOR rate. Any proﬁts or losses in the futures contract will exactly oﬀset
any changes in 3-month LIBOR from what the market is currently predict-
ing. For example, suppose that on December 13, 3-month LIBOR sets at
0.305%. In this case the investor will earn interest of
$100mm × 0.305% × 90/360 = $76,250.
(3.1)
In addition, the investor will make money on his position in futures. Since
LIBOR settles at 0.305% at the settlement of the contract, the contract
closes at 99.695. Thus, the position in futures earns
rates. To see this, assume futures prices are positively correlated with interest rates and
consider someone who is long a futures contract. When interest rates go up, futures prices
will tend to go up, and the long will receive cash ﬂow in the form of daily variation margin.
When interest rates go down, futures prices will tend to go down, and the long will have
to pay out cash. Thus, the long will be able to reinvest cash ﬂow coming in from variation
margin when rates are high and borrow to fulﬁll variation margin at low rates. This is
an advantageous position for the long to be in, certainly as compared to the long in a
comparable forward contract. Thus, in order to make a long indiﬀerent between the two
contracts, the futures price must be greater than the forward price. Sundaresan (1991) has
looked at these issues extensively for the case of forwards and futures on interest rates.
In summary, the assumption that we are making in this section (i.e., futures are the same
as forwards) is not entirely innocuous. Nevertheless, it is suitable for our purposes here.
78

<!-- Page 102 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
100 × $25 × (9969.5 −9959.5) = $25,000.
(3.2)
Thus, the total earned is $76,250 + $25,000 = $101,250. Notice that if the
investor had been able to invest at a rate of 0.405% instead of the then-
prevailing rate of 0.305%, he would have earned interest of
$100mm × 0.405% × 90/360 = $101,250,
(3.3)
the same amount obtained by investing at the lower rate of 0.305% and
beneﬁting from the position in futures (i.e., the sum of the quantities in
Equations 3.1 and 3.2).
Conversely, if rates had gone up to, say, 0.505%, the investor would have
again locked in a rate of 0.405% if he had hedged with futures. In this case
the investor, upon investing his funds in December at a rate of 0.505%, will
earn interest of
$100mm × 0.505% × 90/360 = $126,250.
(3.4)
But this time the investor will lose money on his position in futures. Since
LIBOR settles at 0.505% at the settlement of the contract, the contract
closes at 99.495. Thus, the position in futures earns
100 × $25 × (9949.5 −9959.5) = −$25,000,
(3.5)
a loss for the investor. Thus, the total earned is $126,250 −$25,000 =
$101,250, the sum of the quantities in Equation 3.4 and 3.5. This is the
same amount shown in Equation 3.3, as if the investor had been able to in-
vest at a rate of 0.405% instead of the then-prevailing rate of 0.505%. Thus,
in the case of either rates going up or rates going down, the investor locks
in a rate of 0.405%, the rate implied by the Eurodollar contract.5
3.1.1
The Link Between Swap Rates and Eurodollar Futures
Following Sundaresan (2009), let’s consider another (somewhat contrived)
example now. Suppose it is Monday, September 13, 2010, and a company
5We should be a little careful here. Although the nominal cash ﬂows obtained by
hedging and investing at the then-prevailing rate are the same as if we had invested at the
rate implied by the Eurodollar futures, the timing of the cash ﬂows diﬀers. In our example,
any investment in a Eurodollar time deposit will generate cash ﬂows in March. But any
position in futures earns daily variation margin from when we ﬁrst enter the contract in
September until the settlement of the contract in December. Even if we pretended the
futures were forwards and paid out only at settlement, they would in that case pay in
December, whereas interest in the Eurodollar deposit will pay in March. We will ignore
such timing diﬀerences for our purposes here.

has a ﬂoating rate liability over the next year that resets quarterly indexed
to 3-month LIBOR on a face amount of $100mm. The liability commences
today based on today’s 3-month LIBOR setting, which is 0.29%, and the
remaining three reset dates correspond to the settlement dates of the Dec 10,
Mar 11, and Jun 11 futures contracts. We will assume, for simplicity, that the
liability for each quarter will accrue for 90 days (even though each quarter
may not have exactly 90 days). The company is expecting an increase in
LIBOR over the coming year and wants to lock in its liabilities from ﬂoating
to ﬁxed using Eurodollar futures. Table 3.1 provides information regarding
futures prices for the three contracts as of September 13, 2010.
Can the company do anything to hedge its ﬁrst three-month liability
based oﬀof today’s 3-month LIBOR setting?6 What can the company do
to hedge its remaining exposure for the other three quarters? The company
can sell a strip of 100 Eurodollar futures contracts to hedge against a rise in
rates. By selling 100 of each of the Dec 10, Mar 11, and Jun 11 Eurodollar
futures, the company will be locking in the rate implied in the respective
futures contracts and will thereby protect itself against a rise in rates during
each of the three subsequent quarters in which it is exposed. Assuming the
company does hedge by selling the strip of Eurodollar futures, the cash ﬂows
it will lock in at the end of each quarter are shown in Table 3.2.7
That is, the ﬁnal column in Table 3.2 shows the net cash ﬂow that occurs
from the combination of the liability and the futures position. More impor-
tant, it shows the eﬀective rate that the company has paid each quarter.
For example, for the three-month payment that is due in June 2011, the
company will pay a net amount of $117,500, and it is as if the company has
paid a rate of 0.47% for the quarter. That is, the company was able to lock
in the rate implied by the Mar 11 futures contract for the quarter.
6The answer is no. The rate has already set, and the cash ﬂow that the company will
owe for this quarter is already determined.
7Once again, there is actually a time mismatch between when the futures settle and
when the company pays the cash on its quarterly liability, but we will ignore that here.

So let’s take a step back now. The company had a ﬂoating rate liability
and, without spending any cash today, was able to transform its liability from
a ﬂoating one to a ﬁxed one. The ﬁxed rate of course varies from period to
period (in accordance with the rates implied by the futures contracts), but
nevertheless it is a ﬁxed rate.
Can we ﬁnd a single ﬁxed rate such that when that rate is paid on the
same dates as the ﬂoating payments, it has the same value as the rates im-
plied by the futures contracts? Does such a rate exist? Sure it does. Now let’s
go about ﬁnding it. To start, let’s compute the discount factors correspond-
ing to each of the quarterly payment dates. The discount factor for time t,
denoted D(0, t), is that number that is multiplied by cash ﬂow received at
time t to compute its present value.8 They are implied by today’s LIBOR and
the futures contracts. We will compute discount factors for times t1, . . . , t4,
as deﬁned in Table 3.2. The discount factors are computed as follows: the
ﬁrst discount factor for cash ﬂow received on December 13, 2010, is given by
D(0, t1) =
1
1 + 89
360 × 0.29% = 0.999284,
since there are 89 days between September 15, 2010, and December 13, 2010.
The second discount factor is given by
D(0, t2) =
1
1 + 91
360 × 0.405% × D(0, t1) = 0.998262,
since there are 91 days between December 13, 2010 and March 14, 2011. The
remaining two discount factors are computed in a similar fashion, as shown
in Table 3.3.
8We will adopt the convention that discount rates are calculated so that the present
value of cash ﬂow is calculated as of the spot date of September 15, 2010, not the trade
date of September 13, 2010.

Figure 3.2: Solving for the Fixed Rate
Now remember, we are looking for a single ﬁxed rate that when paid on
the same dates as the ﬂoating payments has the same present value as the
four diﬀerent ﬁxed rates the company was able to lock in using the futures
contracts. That’s easy to ﬁnd when we use a spreadsheet like the one in
Figure 3.2. In this ﬁgure, we have iterated for a single ﬁxed coupon (which
turned out to be 0.424%) such that the discounted cash ﬂows associated with
this single ﬁxed coupon have the same present value as the four distinct ﬁxed
coupons that the company could lock in by trading in the futures.9
9Here’s some detail regarding some of the calculations in Figure 3.2. The implied rates
and discount factors are taken from Table 3.3. The locked-in ﬂows are the nominal cash
ﬂows we obtain by accruing the locked-in rate for 90 days on a notional of $100mm. These
ﬂows were actually determined previously in Table 3.2. We then seek to determine the
single ﬁxed rate such that we are indiﬀerent between paying that ﬁxed rate or paying
82

<!-- Page 106 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
But then we’re done. We found that the company was able to transform
the nature of its liability from a ﬂoating one based upon LIBOR to a ﬁxed
one, in which the ﬁxed rate that the company pays every quarter is the same.
The company was able to do this by using futures and did not need to take
any money out of its pocket today. The present value of the ﬁxed ﬂows based
on a rate of 0.424% is equal to the present value of the ﬂoating ﬂows based
on LIBOR that the company is contracted to pay.
This gets us extremely close to the notion of computing the 1-year swap
rate. After all, the 1-year swap rate is, by deﬁnition, the ﬁxed rate in a 1-year
swap such that swap is an NPV=0 transaction to both sides. In other words,
the 1-year swap rate is the ﬁxed rate in a 1-year swap such that the present
value of the ﬁxed cash ﬂows is equal to the present value of the ﬂoating cash
ﬂows. As we just showed in the example of our corporation, on September
13, 2010 the present value of the ﬂoating cash ﬂows that the company is
due to pay is equal to the present value of the ﬁxed cash ﬂows based on a
rate of 0.424%. Is it fair to say that the 1-year swap rate must be this same
0.424%?
Not exactly. Suppose it is once again September 13, 2010, and consider
now a spot starting 1-year swap in which both sides pay quarterly. The
swap will be eﬀective September 15, 2011, and in accordance with standard
conventions will make quarterly payments on the dates shown in Table 3.4.
But if we compare the payment dates in a standard 1-year swap to the dates
on which the corporation made its payments, we see that they are not the
same. The payment dates for the corporation were chosen to concur with the
settlement date of the Eurodollar futures. The payment dates in a 1-year
swap are slightly diﬀerent. Moreover, the dates upon which we reset LIBOR
in the swap diﬀer from the reset dates of the corporation’s ﬂoating liability,
the latter of which were chosen to match up with the settlement dates of the
Eurodollar futures contracts.
This means that the rate of 0.424% that we calculated will not exactly
equal the 1-year swap rate. Again, the 1-year swap rate has LIBOR reset and
payment dates that do not concur with the settlement dates of the Eurodol-
lar futures contracts. This means that the calculation of the 1-year swap rate
will not be as “clean” as was the example of the corporation transforming
the individual locked-in rates that we obtained by using futures. To say we are indiﬀerent
means that the present value of both streams is the same. As indicated, we solve for a ﬁxed
rate of 0.424%. Given this ﬁxed rate, the nominal ﬁxed cash ﬂows are equal to $100mm ×
0.424%/4 = $105,908. The present value of each nominal cash ﬂow is found by multiplying
by the respective discount factor. For example, the present value of $105,908 to be received
on June 13, 2011 is equal to $105,908 × 0.997077 = $105,598. The ﬁxed rate of 0.424%
is such that the sum of the present value of the locked-in ﬂows is equal to the sum of the
present value of the ﬁxed ﬂows.

Table 3.4: LIBOR Reset and Payment Dates in a Standard Spot Starting

the nature of its ﬂoating rate liability. For instance, what rate should we use
to “transform” the ﬂoating rate in the swap that sets on March 11, 2011?
Inasmuch as this date does not concur with the settlement date of the March
11 future, we will need to resort to using some sort of interpolated rate using
implied rates from multiple futures. Similarly, we will need to adjust our dis-
count factors since our payment dates do not concur with settlement dates of
the Eurodollar futures. Using a proprietary swap model, the 1-year swap rate
on September 13, 2010 (quoted quarterly bond versus 3-month LIBOR quar-
terly money priced using market data inputs as given in Table 3.1) is 0.443%.
The building of a swap model that incorporates these features requires
much work and is a hot topic of discussion throughout the industry. In gen-
eral, most swap models use a combination of deposit rates (much like we
used the spot LIBOR setting of 0.29%), a series of Eurodollar futures and
implied rates, and (due to the limitations of Eurodollars in terms of both
liquidity and availability) swap rates that trade in the market. Much care is
spent on interpolation techniques, and the creation of robust swap models
is often considered as much an art as a science. Many general techniques
used are well known throughout the industry, but many ﬁrms have devel-
oped proprietary techniques as well. A further discussion of these issues is
beyond the scope of this discussion and our needs, but the interested reader
is referred to Hagan (2003), Ron (2000), Zhou (2002), and Flavell (2010).
3.1.2
The Futures Convexity Bias
As we discussed in Section 3.1.1, Eurodollar futures are often used to price
ﬁxed-ﬂoating swaps. Swap dealers will often use these futures to hedge the
interest rate exposure implicit in a swap, selling futures when they received
ﬁxed in a swap or buying futures when they pay ﬁxed in a swap.10 It turns
10In Section 2.2 we discussed that a dealer will sometimes use Treasuries to hedge a
swap, and now we are indicating that a dealer may use Eurodollar futures to hedge. What’s
84

<!-- Page 108 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.3: The Futures Convexity Bias
out, however, that the swap rates implied by pricing from Eurodollar fu-
tures are not equal to the swap rates observed in the market (even after
adjusting for reset and payment mismatches between swaps and futures).
The diﬀerence in these two rates is due to the futures convexity bias.
In order to gain insight into the nature of this bias, consider the following:
in Section 2.1, we mentioned that receiving ﬁxed in a swap is a positively
convex position, since for a shift in swap rates up or down of equal magnitude
the ﬁxed rate receiver beneﬁts more in a rally than he is hurt in a sell-oﬀ.
But Eurodollar futures are nonconvex instruments: the change in value of a
Eurodollar futures contract for a bp move in rates is always a constant $25,
regardless of the level of rates. These relationships are displayed in Figure 3.3.
This convexity bias implies that there is a systematic beneﬁt to receiving
ﬁxed in swaps and hedging by selling Eurodollar futures. If we receive ﬁxed
the diﬀerence? A dealer who uses Treasuries to hedge a swap will be left with a spread
position: a dealer who pays ﬁxed and buys Treasuries will be long swap spreads, and one
who receives ﬁxed and sells Treasuries will be short spreads. On the other hand, a dealer
who uses Eurodollar futures to hedge will not have a spread position. A dealer will make
a choice of which instrument to use to hedge based on whether or not he wants a residual
position in spreads as well as on the relative liquidity in Treasuries and Eurodollars at the
time.
85

<!-- Page 109 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
in swaps and can hedge our risk by selling futures, then we would beneﬁt
by being long a position that is positively convex and being short a position
that is nonconvex. As Figure 3.3 demonstrates, the more volatile rates are,
the greater the value of the convexity mismatch (i.e., the more the curve and
the line diverge from one another).
The punch line here is that swap rates, as implied by Eurodollar futures,
are too high. Since there is a systematic beneﬁt from receiving ﬁxed in a swap
and selling futures, this means that the ﬁxed rate receiver should be willing,
everything else the same, to receive a lower ﬁxed rate to compensate for
the convexity beneﬁt he receives (and which the ﬁxed rate payer gives up).
How much lower should the ﬁxed rate be? In order to answer this question
theoretically, we would need to employ a model of interest rate movements
to put a value on this convexity. This is beyond the scope of our discussion;
however, the interested reader is referred to Kirikos and Novak (1997) and
Gupta and Subrahmanyam (2000). In general, the bias is an increasing func-
tion of the volatility of interest rates and of the maturity of the swap (longer
maturity swaps exhibit greater convexity just like ﬁxed rate bonds).
The conventional wisdom held by many in the market is that the convex-
ity bias was ignored (actually not recognized, sotto voce) by swap traders in
the 1980s and even early 1990s. An early analysis of the convexity bias in
swaps was conducted by Burghardt and Hoskins (1995). They claim that al-
though the swaps market had by the mid-1990s recognized the bias, swap
rates trading in the market did not fully reﬂect the value of the convexity
bias. In a later study, Gupta and Subrahmanyam (2000) indicate that in
the period 1987–1990 the diﬀerential between swap rates in the market and
rates implied by futures were insigniﬁcantly diﬀerent from zero. They further
indicate that during the period 1991–1993, the diﬀerential between the rates
was signiﬁcant (from a statistical point of view) and was very signiﬁcant
in the period 1994–1996. Their ﬁndings are in general agreement with the
notion that the bias was ignored by traders in the early years of the swaps
market and recognized in later years. They ﬁnd, for example, that the value
of the rate diﬀerential during the period 1991–1993 was averaging 8–10 bps
for swaps of diﬀerent maturities.
3.2
Moving On: Bootstrapping the Curve and
Creating a Swap Model
In this section we are going to put the link between swaps and Eurodollar
futures in our rearview mirror. From this point on, we are going to take it
for granted that all of the swap rates (at least those on the short end of the
86

<!-- Page 110 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
curve) we see in the market on a composite screen like the one in Table 1.1
have a connection to Eurodollar futures prices.11 We will from this point on
regard swap rates as primitive objects. We will develop a technique known
as bootstrapping in order to obtain zero coupon and forward rates implicit
in the swap curve. Use of these rates will enable us to price a wide variety
of trades.
Let’s start by reminding ourselves of the following: we’ve previously al-
luded to the fact that we can think of receiving ﬁxed in a swap as a long
position in a ﬁxed rate bond (whose value is denoted Bﬁxed) and a short
position in a ﬂoating rate bond (whose value is denoted Bﬂoating). The value
of the swap is therefore
Swap = Bﬁxed −Bﬂoating.
This interpretation is valid if we assume that the two counterparties ex-
change principal at maturity. Although the counterparties do not literally
exchange principal at maturity, the value of the swap will not change if we
assume that each counterparty both pays and receives an amount equal to
the notional at maturity. The ﬁctitious notional payment at maturity in
conjunction with the ﬁxed coupons constitutes a ﬁxed rate bond. And the
ﬁctitious notional payment at maturity in conjunction with the ﬂoating rate
coupons constitutes a ﬂoating rate bond.
Let’s consider now this ﬂoating rate bond, which pays coupons equal to
LIBOR. We want to compute the present value of these cash ﬂows, using the
swap curve for discounting purposes. Very loosely speaking, we are discount-
ing these cash ﬂows using the forward LIBOR curve. Hopefully this suggests
that the present value of the bond is therefore equal to par, or the amount
of the notional. We will show that this is true later on in this chapter, but
we will accept it as fact for now.
This is a really important fact, so let’s emphasize it one more time: in
a spot starting swap that is executed today, the present value of the ﬂoating
side of the swap, including principal at maturity, is par. Moreover, on any
of the dates on which LIBOR is reset, the value of the ﬂoating leg must also
be worth par.12
Since a swap is an NPV = 0 transaction when we ﬁrst enter into it, and
since the value of the ﬂoating side is worth par when we enter into it, this
11As mentioned previously, Eurodollar futures contracts don’t trade past 10 years and
thus don’t explain swap rates beyond 10 years. Moreover, some traders indicate that they
do not use Eurodollar futures beyond a few years to price swaps. Nevertheless, we will
proceed taking the swap rates that trade in the market as a given. The forces of supply
and demand ultimately determine where swap rates trade in the market.
12If we are not on a reset date, but rather in the middle of a period, the value of the
ﬂoating side need not be par. Again, we will show this is the case later in this chapter.
87

<!-- Page 111 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
must mean that the value of the ﬁxed side must also be par at the inception
of the trade. That is, the swap rate is the ﬁxed rate that makes the value of
the ﬁxed rate bond par when using the swap curve to discount cash ﬂows.
The implications of this realization are extremely important. This means
that the mid-market swap rates on a composite page like the one shown in
Table 1.1 deﬁne a sequence of bonds that are each worth par, and the swap
curve is therefore referred to as being a par yield curve.13
One nice aspect of par yield curves is that they lend themselves well to
bootstrapping. This technique can be used in order to determine zero coupon
rates and forward rates for any maturity that are implicit in the curve:
• Zero coupon rates are used to discount a single cash ﬂow received at
some point in time in the future back to today. Assuming annual com-
pounding, if zc(t) is the zero coupon rate for cash ﬂow received at time
t, then the present value of $1 received at time t is
$1
[1 + zc(t)]t .
• Forward rates of interest are rates that can be locked in for future time
periods, as determined by zero coupon rates. In the case of a swap, the
forward rates of interest calculated are LIBOR forwards. It is common
in the swaps market for people to think of forward rates as expressing
the market’s expectation of where LIBOR will be in the future, and
we will employ this interpretation as well.14
Once again assuming annual compounding, we will denote by f(t, t+
1) the forward LIBOR rate between times t and t + 1. When we say
that a forward rate is a rate of interest that can be locked in for future
periods, this means that $1 to be received at time t + 1 is worth the
same as
$1
1 + f(t, t + 1)
at time t.
We will use zero coupon and forward rates to value every swap that we en-
counter from here on out.
13If we really wanted to be a stickler, we could say that a swap transacted on either
the bid side or the oﬀer side is not worth zero, since the dealer has presumably made some
money on the transaction. So to avoid this criticism we’ll just talk about a swap with a
mid-market swap rate as having zero value.
14Empirical evidence may refute the validity of the assumption that a forward rate is
equal to the expectation of the future spot rate. Fama (1984a, 1984b) wrote two early
works examining this question in the interest rate markets. Despite raising doubts about
the appropriateness of forward rates as expected future spots rates, most market partici-
pants fully adopt this usage, and we will too.
88

<!-- Page 112 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
3.2.1
A Stylized Example
We are going to work with a particular stylized example in which we assume
for simplicity that we live in a world in which the standard convention in
swaps is that the ﬁxed side pays annually 30/360, and the ﬂoating side
is 1-year LIBOR, which pays annual 30/360 as well. We will also assume
that yields on Treasuries are quoted annual bond and that swap spreads are
quoted on the same basis. These assumptions are only made for simplicity; we
could deﬁnitely assume that the standard semi bond ﬁxed side and quarterly
money ﬂoating side conventions apply, but that would just give us more
numbers to calculate for a minimal pickup in modeling ﬂexibility.
Suppose we walk in one morning and look on a composite page depicting
our stylized example and observe the mid-market rates and spreads shown
in Table 3.5. Each of the swap rates in the ﬁnal column of Table 3.5 deﬁnes
a ﬁctitious bond that is worth par today. The cash ﬂows of each of the four
ﬁctitious bonds (with an assumed principal of $100) are depicted in Table 3.6.
The present value of each of these bonds must be par, or $100, today.
Now let’s remember the big picture here: we are going to use these swap
rates (and the par bonds they imply) to determine zero coupon rates and
then forwards.
