T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Finding Zero Coupon Rates
To ﬁnd the zero coupon rates, we proceed iteratively: we solve for one zero
coupon rate at a time, then iterate to get the next one. Here’s how it goes:
• The 1-Year Zero Coupon Rate is simply equal to the 1-Year Swap Rate:
– The 1-year bond that pays $105 in a year must be worth $100
today. This implies that the 1-year zero coupon rate, zc(1), must
satisfy
$100 =
$105
1 + zc(1).
That is, the 1-year zero coupon rate, zc(1), is 5%.15
• To ﬁnd the 2-Year Zero Coupon Rate:
– Working now with the 2-year bond that has a coupon of 5.11%,
use the 1-year zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the
ﬁrst-year coupon, which is $5.11/1.05 = $4.867.
– Since the 2-year bond is worth par today, and we know the present
value of the ﬁrst-year cash ﬂow must be $4.867, this implies the
present value of the cash ﬂow occurring in the second year must
equal $100 −$4.867 = $95.133.
– Thus, the 2-year zero coupon rate must satisfy
$95.133 =
$105.11
[1 + zc(2)]2 ,
or zc(2) = 5.113%.
• To ﬁnd the 3-Year Zero Coupon Rate:
15Note that in our (relatively simplistic) stylized example with annual 30/360 coupons
on both sides, we could have concluded that the 1-year zero coupon rate was 5% in a
diﬀerent manner. Note that it must be the case that 1-year LIBOR today is 5%. By
deﬁnition, the 1-year swap rate is the ﬁxed rate that someone is willing to pay or receive
versus LIBOR in a 1-year swap. That is, the 1-year swap rate is the ﬁxed rate such that a
1-year swap is an NPV = 0 transaction. Since the 1-year swap rate is 5%, and since each
side of the swap pays annual 30/360 coupons, we must conclude that 1-year LIBOR today
is also 5%. Since 1-year LIBOR today is 5%, this means that $1 invested today at LIBOR
for a year grows to $1.05 at the end of year. Equivalently, this means that the present
value of $1 to be received in a year is $1/1.05, which is to say that the 1-year zero coupon
rate is 5%.
90

<!-- Page 114 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
– Working now with the 3-year bond that has a coupon of 5.30%,
use the 1-year zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the
ﬁrst-year coupon, which is $5.30/1.05 = $5.048, and use the 2-
year zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the second-year
coupon, which is $5.30/(1.05113)2 = $4.797.
– The cash ﬂows in the 3-year bond occurring in year 3 must have
a present value equal to $100 −$5.048 −$4.797 = $90.155.
– The 3-year zero coupon rate must satisfy
$90.155 =
$105.30
[1 + zc(3)]3 ,
or zc(3) = 5.312%.
• To ﬁnd the 4-Year Zero Coupon Rate:
– Working now with the 4-year bond that has a coupon of 5.47%,
use the 1-year zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the
ﬁrst-year coupon, which is $5.47/1.05 = $5.210; use the 2-year
zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the second-year
coupon, which is $5.47/(1.05113)2 = $4.951; and use the 3-year
zero coupon rate to ﬁnd the present value of the third-year coupon,
which is $5.47/(1.05312)3 = $4.683.
– The cash ﬂows in the 4-year bond occurring in year 4 must have a
present value equal to $100 −$5.210 −$4.951 −$4.683 = $85.156.
– The 4-year zero coupon rate must satisfy
$85.156 =
$105.47
[1 + zc(4)]4 ,
or zc(4) = 5.494%.
From Zero Coupon Rates to Forward Rates
Once we have found the zero coupon rates, ﬁnding forward rates is a piece
of cake. Let f(t, T) denote the T −t year forward rate from time t to time
T. Then the forward rate f(t, T) must satisfy
[1 + zc(t)]t × [1 + f(t, T)]T −t = [1 + zc(T)]T .
(3.6)
For our purposes here we will only need to solve for 1-year forward rates,
and thus we will make use of Equation 3.6 in the following form:
[1 + zc(t)]t × [1 + f(t, t + 1)]1 = [1 + zc(t + 1)]t+1 .
(3.7)
91

Table 3.7: Zero Coupon Rates and Forwards Obtained from Bootstrapping
For example, since the 1-year zero coupon rate is 5% and the 2-year zero
coupon rate is 5.113%, the 1-year forward in one year, f(1, 2), must satisfy
(1.05)1 × [1 + f(1, 2)]1 = (1.05113)2,
or f(1, 2) = 5.226%. The other 1-year forwards are found similarly making
use of Equation 3.7. The results of this bootstrapping technique are shown
in Table 3.7. Although we just worked through an example that was four
years long, this technique can be used to ﬁnd zero coupon and forward rates
from swap curves with longer maturities as well. By the way, how did we
come up with f(0, 1), that ﬁrst 1-year forward, the 1-year forward starting
today, as being 5%?16
So how do we interpret each of these 1-year forwards that we just calcu-
lated? These are forward rates of interest that one could lock in and they are
implicit in the swap curve. As is customary in the industry, we will regard
these forwards as the expectation of where 1-year LIBOR rates will set each
year in the future. For example, since f(2, 3) = 5.712%, we will say that the
market expects 1-year LIBOR to be 5.712% two years from now.
Figure 3.4 shows graphically the relationship between the zero coupon
rates and the forward rates that we derived from the swap curve in Table 3.5.
As shown in this ﬁgure, the 1-year forward rates increase in each successive
year. We should not allow this particular example to lull us into the belief
that the forward rates are always a monotonically increasing function of time
when the swap curve is upward sloping. Consider, for example, the swap
curve in Table 3.8. One thing to note is that this curve does not contain a
16A forward rate out of today is just a spot rate, which in this case is today’s 1-year
LIBOR rate of 5%.

rates that do not appear explictly in this curve. Thus, for example, the 6-year
swap rate is assumed to be 2.63% (i.e., an 80% weighting of the 5-year swap
rate and a 20% weighting of the 10-year swap rate), the 7-year swap rate is

<!-- Page 117 -->

Figure 3.5: Forward Rates Obtained from Bootstrapping the Swap Curve in
Table 3.8
assumed to be 2.66%, etc.17 Using the same bootstrapping technique we have
just developed, the forward rates for this swap curve are shown in Figure 3.5.
The resultant curve of 1-year forwards is not “pleasing.” These forwards
appear “well behaved” for the ﬁrst ﬁve years, but the jagged proﬁle that fol-
lows with signiﬁcant oscillation thereafter is deemed undesirable by many in
the industry. More sophisticated techniques have been adapted throughout
the industry to create a smoother pattern of forward LIBOR rates, which is
generally considered more desirable.18 Yet even with more advanced meth-
ods, given an upward-sloping swap curve such as the one in Table 3.8, the
17Suppose sL and sH are swap rates of maturity L and H, respectively, and we wish
to compute an M-year swap rate, sM, where L < M < H, by linear interpolation. Then
sM is given by
sM = sL +
M −L

× (sH −sL).
H −L
18The issue of appropriate and adequate swap curve construction is a big one of the
industry. Many diﬀerent methods are employed. See, for example, Hagan (2003), Ron
(2000), and Zhou (2002). While generic methodologies abound, many institutions have
spent considerable time developing proprietary methods and systems for curve construc-
tion.
94

<!-- Page 118 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
usual pattern of forward LIBOR rates that are obtained start out increasing
but eventually decrease at some point in the future.
Creating a Swap Model
We are going to use this bootstrapping methodology to create a swap model.
This methodology is very important and will serve as the basis of pricing
most of the trades we will look at in this and subsequent chapters. It is
important to note at the outset that the swap model we will develop here,
while a powerful tool, will not be a fully robust model. Any comprehensive
swap model would have to have the capability of modeling swap rates (and
zero coupon rates and forward rates) to any particular date. Our model will
only have the capacity to consider annual cash ﬂows. A more realistic model
that allowed for the ﬂexibility of incorporating swap cash ﬂows on any arbi-
trarily speciﬁed date would use a similar bootstrapping methodology as we
have developed, albeit with more sophisticated techniques for interpolating
rates beyond a set of benchmark par swap rates such as given in Table 3.8.
Despite these shortcomings, our model will allow us to price a great variety
of trades common in the swaps market.
Let’s return now to our stylized example and consider the swap curve given
in Table 3.5. We will use the zero coupon and forward rates derived from
bootstrapping to price up a $100mm 4-year swap in which we are receiving
ﬁxed. To price up a swap in this context means to ﬁgure out what the ﬁxed
coupon in the trade should be. Remember, the swap rate is the ﬁxed rate
that makes the present value of the ﬁxed side equal to the present value of
the ﬂoating side. We use the zero coupon rates to compute present values,
and we use the forwards to determine the market’s expectation of 1-year
LIBOR over time.
Figure 3.6 shows a swap model used to price up the 4-year swap. Each row
corresponds to a given year in the swap, and contains the appropriate zero
coupon rate that we use to discount cash ﬂows that occur at the end of that
year and also contains the forward rate applicable for that year. For example,
let’s look at the last row in the model that refers to cash ﬂow received in
year 4. The 4-year zero coupon rate, zc(4), of 5.494% is provided and is
used to discount all cash ﬂows received in year 4. This row also contains
f(3, 4) = 6.041%, the 1-year forward LIBOR in the fourth year. We can
think of this as the market’s expectation of where 1-year LIBOR will reset
at year 3, for accrual and payment at year 4. Based on this forward rate, we
see that the ﬂoating cash ﬂow that we expect in the ﬁnal year of the swap is
$100mm × f(3, 4) = $6,041,364. The present value of this expected ﬂoating
ﬂow is
$100mm × f(3, 4)
[1 + zc(4)]4
= $4,877,792.
95

<!-- Page 119 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.6: A Swap Model
The other nominal ﬂoating cash ﬂows are modeled, and the present values
of these cash ﬂows are calculated in a similar manner.19 The sum of the
present values of each of the ﬂoating coupons is $19,260,092. Our job, then,
is to iterate and solve for that single ﬁxed coupon such that the sum of
the present value of the ﬁxed cash ﬂows is also equal to $19,260,092. In
Figure 3.6, we have solved for the ﬁxed coupon that makes the present value
of the ﬁxed side equal to the present value of the ﬂoating side. That ﬁxed
coupon is 5.47%.
With a ﬁxed coupon of 5.47%, the nominal cash ﬂows on the ﬁxed side
of the swap are $100mm × 5.47% = $5, 470, 000 each year. The zero coupon
rates are used to compute the present value of these nominal ﬁxed ﬂows. For
example, the present value of the ﬁxed coupon received at the end of year 3 is
$100mm × 5.47%
[1 + zc(3)]3
= $4,683,288.
Adding up the present values of each of the ﬁxed coupons of 5.47% gives us
$19,260,092, the same present value as the ﬂoating side of the swap. Thus,
the 4-year swap rate is indeed 5.47%.
19The ﬁrst ﬂoating cash ﬂow is calculated oﬀof today’s spot LIBOR setting. As we ar-
gued earlier, in our stylized example with annual bond coupons on both sides of the swap,
the spot 1-year LIBOR rate is equal to the 1-year swap rate. This happens to be 5% in this
numerical example. And as discussed before, the spot 1-year LIBOR is the (degenerate)
1-year forward that sets today, f(0, 1).
96

<!-- Page 120 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
So what does this tell us? It’s like that Seinfeld episode: nothing, ab-
solutely nothing.20 We just found using our swap model that the 4-year
swap rate is 5.47%. But recall that when we started this whole thing, we
were given a swap curve in Table 3.5 in which it was given that the 4-year
swap rate is 5.47%. So we went through a lot of work just to show that
something was exactly what we knew it to be. This would seem, then, to be
a big waste of time. But although the answer to this particular problem was
readily available before we started modeling, the technology that we have
just developed will be crucial for pricing a variety of swaps, as we will soon
see. So perhaps it’s not that big a waste of time after all.
Relationship Between Swap Rate and Forwards
Figure 3.7 shows the relationship between the 4-year swap rate that we just
solved for and the forwards. As this ﬁgure demonstrates, the forward curve
(the relationship between successive 1-year LIBOR forwards and time) is up-
ward sloping, and the swap rate that we solved for lies somewhere in the
middle of the forwards. It might be tempting to say that the swap rate is a
simple average of the forwards, but this is not the case. However, it is ac-
curate to say that the swap rate that we computed is a speciﬁc weighted
average of the forwards. In fact, deﬁne
DF(0, t) =
1
[1 + zc(t)]t ,
the discount factor for time t, and further deﬁne
4
A =
t=1
DF(0, t),
the sum of the discount factors. This quantity A is the annuity factor; it
is the number that, when multipled by $1, gives then present value of a
$1 annuity received over the life of the swap. Now deﬁne weights wt, for
t = 1, 2, 3, and 4 by
wt = DF(0, t)
A
.
It is easy to show that these weights sum to one. Note that wt is the pro-
portion of the present value of the annuity of $1 that is associated with a
20Reference to Episodes 63 and 64, “The Pilot (1)” and “The Pilot (2),” respectively, in
the sitcom Seinfeld. In these episodes, the characters George Costanza (played by Jason
Alexander) and Jerry Seinfeld (played by himself) get permission from NBC to go ahead
with their idea for a new sitcom that is about nothing.
97

<!-- Page 121 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.7: The Relationship Between the 4-Year Swap Rate and the For-
wards
payment at time t. The 4-year swap rate, s4, that we solved for is equal to
4
s4 =
t=1
wt × f(t −1, t)
and is therefore a weighted average of the forward rates.
Figure 3.7 also demonstrates that in the case of an upward-sloping for-
ward curve, the swap rate of a given maturity is greater than the initial
forward rate, which is of course today’s LIBOR setting. This is to say that
the ﬁxed rate receiver in this swap will earn positive cash ﬂow in the initial
period. This is commonly referred to as positive carry. Thus, this swap has
no cash ﬂows when it is initiated (it is an NPV = 0 transaction) and will
have positive cash ﬂow for the ﬁxed rate recipient at the end of the ﬁrst year.
Of course, this in no way means that there is an arbitrage. According to the
forwards, the ﬁxed rate receiver is expected to earn positive carry at the
beginning of the trade and negative carry (or negative cash ﬂow) after some
point. The ﬁxed rate in the swap is such that the present value of the positive
carry that is expected exactly oﬀsets the present value of the negative carry.
It is important to remember that these forwards may or may not (and
98

<!-- Page 122 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.8: The Swap Model Assuming the Fixed Side Pays Annual Money
they usually won’t) come to fruition. It is only the ﬁrst LIBOR setting in a
spot starting swap that is known with certainty, and all subsequent LIBOR
settings in the swap will not be known until they occur. Inasmuch as the ac-
tual LIBOR settings diﬀer from the rates implied by the forwards, one may
have either positive or negative proﬁts from holding on to the swap until
maturity.
A Change in Day Count
By the way, what is the swap rate if we assume the ﬁxed side pays an-
nual money instead of annual bond (and assume the ﬂoating side still pays
1-year LIBOR annual bond)? If we use Equation 1.9, given a swap rate
of 5.47% annual bond, we see that the annual money ﬁxed rate should be
approximately
5.47% × 360/365 = 5.395%.
(3.8)
But in Section 1.2.1 we emphasized that the only way to answer a question
like this precisely is to use a swap model. Refer now to Figure 3.8, which
shows the swap modeled up assuming the ﬁxed side pays annual money. In
this case, the nominal value of each of the ﬁxed ﬂows is
Fixed Rate × Act
360 × Notional.
It is assumed that the fourth year in this swap is a leap year and thus
accrues for 366 days. Note that the ﬂoating side, which still pays 1-year
LIBOR annual bond, is unchanged from the swap we modeled in Figure 3.6.
99

<!-- Page 123 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
As shown in Figure 3.8, the ﬁxed rate assuming the ﬁxed side pays annual
money is 5.392%. Our approximation of 5.395% was only three-tenths of a
bp oﬀfrom the answer provided by our model.
One More Question
What is the present value of $100mm received at the end of year 4? Well,
there are a couple of ways to go about this. We could use the 4-year zero
coupon rate and compute the present value as
$100mm
(1.0494)4 = $80,739,908.
How else could we solve for this? Remember that on any reset date, the
present value of the ﬁctitious ﬂoating rate bond is par.21 In Figure 3.6,
21We did say earlier in this chapter that we would show that this is true. To this end,
consider a $100 swap that has T years and pays annual coupons on both sides at times
1, 2, 3, . . . , T. Denote by f(t, t+1) the forward rate between time t and t+1. Now suppose
we are standing at time T −1. We know that in one year at time T we will receive the
principal of $100 and the last ﬂoating coupon. The forwards indicate that the expected
coupon to be received is f(T −1, T)×$100. Using the forward f(T −1, T), we can discount
the cash ﬂows at time T to time T −1 as follows:
[$100 + f(T −1, T) × $100]
1 + f(T −1, T)
= $100.
Now take another step back one year to time T −2. In a year from time T −2 we expect
to receive the ﬂoating coupon f(T −2, T −1) × $100, and we will also have all other cash
ﬂows to come, which we found was worth $100 at time T −1. We can discount this back
to time T −2 using f(T −2, T −1) and ﬁnd when we are standing at time T −2 the value
of all remaining cash ﬂows is:
[$100 + f(T −2, T −1) × $100]
1 + f(T −2, T −1)
= $100.
There is a pattern here. When we are standing at any time t, we know that in a year
from now we are expecting to get the coupon $100 × f(t, t + 1) plus all remaining cash
ﬂows in the swap that are worth, as we just showed, $100 at time t + 1. Now, going all
the way back to time 0, the eﬀective date of the swap, we know that in a year at time
1 we are going to receive the ﬁxed coupon $100 × f(0, 1) and all future cash ﬂows in the
swap, which have a value as of time 1 of $100. Discounting back to time 0 gives a value of
par.
So what if today is not a LIBOR reset date in the swap? Is the value of the ﬂoating side
still par? The answer is not necessarily. Let’s consider, without loss of generality, the case in
which we are currently in the middle of the ﬁrst year of the swap. The eﬀective date (and
ﬁrst LIBOR reset date) of the swap is time 0, the ﬁrst payment date will be at time 1, and
the current time is denoted by τ, where 0 < τ < 1. For example, if the eﬀective date of
the swap was nine months ago, then τ = 0.75. Denote by L(0,1) the ﬁrst LIBOR setting.
This is the LIBOR that set at time 0 and will be paid at time 1.
100

<!-- Page 124 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.9: The Swap Assuming Principal Exchanged
we see that the present value of the ﬂoating coupons is $19,260,092. So
this implies that the present value of the principal received at maturity is
$100,000,000 −$19,260,092 = $80,739,908.
In order to verify that the present value of the ﬁctitious ﬂoating rate
bond is indeed par, take a look at Figure 3.9. This is the same swap that
we just modeled; however, we are now making the artiﬁcial assumption that
the two counterparties exchange principal at maturity. Of course, the two
counterparties do not literally exchange principal at maturity, but including
ﬁctitious principal on each side does not change the value of the swap at all.
The swap rate is as we found before. Moreover, note that the present value
of the ﬂoating side, including principal, is par. In addition, since the swap
is an NPV = 0 transaction, this means that the ﬁxed side is also worth par,
as we see is the case in Figure 3.9. It’s like that song by Radiohead about
everything being in its right place.
A very similar argument to the one above can be used to “bring back” all of the
outstanding cash ﬂows on the ﬂoating side to time 1. The present value as of time τ of all
cash ﬂows of the ﬂoating rate bond is
[$100 + L(0, 1) × $100]
1 + (1 −τ)L(τ, 1)
,
where L(τ, 1) is the spot LIBOR rate from time τ to time 1, and the factor 1−τ preceding
this term adjusts for the appropriate day count. In general, this expression does not equal
par, and thus it is not the case that the value of the ﬁctitious ﬂoating rate bond is par on
a date that is not a reset date.
101

Table 3.9: PV01s and PV Coupon 01 in a $100mm Swap (Value in Dollars)
Given the Swap Curve in Table 3.5
3.2.2
PV01s in Our Stylized Example
In Chapter 2, we introduced the notions of PV01 (both with and without the
ﬁrst LIBOR set) as well as PV Coupon 01. We can apply the same concepts
here in our stylized example in order to measure interest rate sensitivity of
swaps. Table 3.9 provides these computations.
Note that the relatively large diﬀerential between PV01 numbers with
and without the LIBOR set is a function of the assumption of 1-year LIBOR
in our stylized example. Setting the ﬁrst LIBOR in our stylized example
(which sets a rate for an entire year) has a greater impact in reducing PV01s
than in Table 2.3 in which a LIBOR setting is only for three months. In
the case of a 1-year swap in which LIBOR has been set, we see that the
degenerate nature of our stylized example results in a zero PV01.
3.3
Moving On: Pricing Up Nonstandard Swaps
We have barely gotten oﬀthe ground yet. In Section 3.2 we saw that we could
regard the swap curve as a par bond curve and exploited this fact to derive
zero coupon rates and LIBOR forwards. We then used these zero coupon
rates and forwards to model and price up (i.e., solve for the ﬁxed coupon in)
a 4-year swap. And we found the following completely unspectacular result:
the 4-year swap rate that we solved for in our model was equal to the 4-year
swap rate. That is to say, we started out with a swap curve as given on a
composite page, and from our swap model we found that the 4-year swap
rate was equal to the 4-year swap rate that we saw on the composite page.
As suggested, this exercise was not a waste of our time. The bootstrap-
ping technology that we have just developed is going to be the underpinning
of virtually everything else that we price from here on out. Obviously, there
102

<!-- Page 126 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
was no need for us to model up a simple 4-year swap, since we could observe
the 4-year swap rate on the composite page. But sometimes it is nice when
working on something new that things turn out the way we would expect
them to.
Handchecks
Since the swaps that we are going to look at now are not going to have prices
that we observe on a screen, we are going to emphasize, where possible,
something called a “handcheck.” A handcheck is a quick and dirty way for
us to price up a swap on a ﬁnancial calculator. The handcheck will not be
exact and is no replacement for calculating the answer using a model, but it
is a good way to check that the answers we get make sense.
We actually already worked through one example of a handcheck. In
Section 3.2.1, we modeled up a 4-year swap assuming the ﬁxed side paid
annual money instead of annual bond. In Figure 3.8, we found that the ﬁxed
rate annual money was 5.392%. But whereas the 4-year swap rate quoted
annual bond could be found by looking on the composite page, we could not
look there to ﬁnd the rate quoted annual money. It was therefore comforting
when our approximation of 5.395%, as computed in Equation 3.8, came to
within a few tenths of a bp of the answer our model gave. Our model may
as well be regarded as a random number generator unless we have some way
of justifying the answers that it produces.22 This gave us conﬁrmation that
we modeled the trade correctly.
We are still going to rely on the bootstrapping model to price up a wide
variety of swaps. But learning to handcheck a trade is an invaluable tool.
We want to make sure the answer that our model spits out is in the realm
of reality. We should focus as much on the handchecks that we will develop
as on the pricing our model gives us. Otherwise, it will be hard to know if
we have made a modeling mistake or accidentally suﬀered from hitting the
wrong key on our keyboard. It will be nice to have a way to make sure that
the answer we’re getting from our model is somewhere in the ballpark.
Let’s keep in mind handchecks are only approximations. They are back-
of-the-envelope calculations, making simplifying assumptions in order to get
quick veriﬁcation that a model has given us the right number. Not every
trade can be handchecked, but for those that can, it’s very reassuring when
the results of a pricing model agree with a handcheck.
22Many in the swap industry are taught to never say “done” on a trade unless either
it is possible to justify the number that a model came up with using a handcheck or a
colleague independently veriﬁes the pricing.
103

<!-- Page 127 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
3.3.1
Mark-to-Markets
In this section we will look at pricing swaps that have been in existence for
some time. When a swap is ﬁrst executed, the ﬁxed rate is such that the
swap is an NPV = 0 transaction. Yet as we saw in Chapter 2, as swap rates
move around, the value of a swap that was previously executed will usually
not remain zero.23 That is precisely why investors enter into swaps in the
ﬁrst place. An investor who enters into a swap in order to hedge an asset or
liability or to take a view on the market is counting on the change in value
of the swap, given the passage of time and the change in swap rates. To
price an existing swap in this context means to determine its current market
value, or mark-to-market. Let’s work through a few examples now.
Example 1
Referring to our stylized example and the swap curve in Table 3.5, suppose
that three years ago a client entered into a $100mm 7-year swap with a
dealer in which the client received ﬁxed at 7.00% annual bond and paid
1-year LIBOR annual bond. What is the value of this swap today?
The ﬁrst thing to consider is whether or not the ﬁrst three coupon
payments on each side of the swap that have come and gone should be
considered when valuing the swap today. These coupons have absolutely
nothing to do with the value of the remaining four years in this swap.24
The only thing that matters is valuing the remaining cash ﬂows left in this
swap.
For all intents and purposes, this swap is now a 4-year swap. This swap
was put on three years ago, and now has four years remaining. Its value is
going to depend on where the current 4-year swap rate is in relation to the
ﬁxed coupon in this swap: if this swap has a ﬁxed rate higher than current
4-year swap rates, then it will have positive value, or will be in-the-money,
to the ﬁxed rate receiver. If the swap has a ﬁxed rate lower than the current
4-year swap rate, then it will have negative value, or will be out-of-the-money,
to the ﬁxed rate receiver. Since the coupon on this swap is 7.00% and the
4-year swap rate is 5.47%, we know that the swap is going to have positive
value to the client who is receiving ﬁxed and negative value to the dealer
who is paying ﬁxed.
Let’s take a look now at valuing this swap with our model. Let’s model up
this trade from the perspective of the dealer and emphasize who is getting
23As we will discuss in Chapter 8, even if swap rates do not move, the value of a swap
will in general change with the passage of time.
24If we were to value a stock, would we include past dividends when using the dividend
discount model of stock valuation? No, we wouldn’t. The same principle applies here.
104

<!-- Page 128 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.10: Marking to Market a 4-Year Swap with a Coupon of 7.00%
what in this swap by signing the cash ﬂows accordingly.25 As shown in
Figure 3.10, the dealer is receiving the LIBOR cash ﬂows that are worth
$19,260,092 in present value terms and is paying out the ﬁxed coupons of
7.00% that are worth $24,647,284 in present value terms. Thus, the swap is
worth $19,260,092 −$24,647,284 = −$5,387,192 to the dealer and is in favor
of the client by the same amount.26 That’s a lot of dough for the dealer to
25Sometimes, such as in the swap model shown in Figure 3.6, we won’t bother to sign
the cash ﬂows of a swap. In such cases the NPV of the swap is equal to the diﬀerence
between the present values of each leg of the swap. In other cases we will want to emphasize
who is getting what in the swap and we will sign cash ﬂows accordingly, with positive cash
ﬂows representing cash that is received and negative cash ﬂows representing cash that is
paid by a particular counterparty. Of course, when we sign cash ﬂows then the NPV of
the swap is equal to the sum of the present values of each leg, not the diﬀerence between
the present values of each leg.
26Implicit in these calculations is the assumption that these cash ﬂows should be dis-
counted at LIBOR. Despite the fact that the cost of capital of various ﬁnancial institutions
may diﬀer from LIBOR, this has nonetheless been the market standard for calculating
the mark-to-market value of a swap. Since the time of the ﬁnancial crisis, this assumption
has been called into question by many in the industry.
In fact, it would not be an overstatement to say that the industry is going through a (not
so quiet) revolution regarding the issue of what rates to use for discounting purposes when
computing the mark-to-market of a swap. At the root of the issue is the CSAs that many
counterparties have in place with one another. As discussed in Chapter 2, a CSA provides
for the transfer of collateral from one counterparty to another on the basis of the mark-
to-market value of a swap or portfolio of swaps. Furthermore, for USD swaps, the CSA
typically requires that interest on the collateral should be calculated based on the Fed
Funds overnight rate.
When the diﬀerence between LIBOR and the Fed Funds overnight rate was small and
not particularly volatile, most people in the market ignored this issue. But when the
105

<!-- Page 129 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
be down on the trade. Presumably the dealer hedged himself with Treasuries
or Eurodollar futures at the outset of the trade and earned his bid/oﬀer on
the trade and was immunized against changes in the value of the swap.27
Handcheck for Example 1
The value of the swap that we just modeled is not going to be found on
any composite page. A composite page provides current market swap rates
but does not give the value of swaps with oﬀ-market coupons. It would be
comforting if we could come up with a way to make sure the value of the
swap that we just computed makes sense.
To this end, note that in the remaining four-year life of the trade, it is
as if the counterparty is receiving an annuity of 7.00% −5.47% = 1.53%.
This can be seen in Figure 3.11. Let’s work through the “swap math” in this
ﬁgure in which we’ve used a little trick. The top box represents the value of
the swap in which the client is receiving ﬁxed at 7%. Notice that the value
of this trade, whatever it may be, is equal to the value of a swap in which
the client is receiving ﬁxed at 7% (i.e., the very same thing) plus the value
of a swap in which the client is paying ﬁxed at 5.47%. This might seem kind
of weird. In algebraic terms, we just said that A = A+B, which is only true
when B = 0. But this is indeed the case here. The value of a swap in which
the client is paying ﬁxed at 5.47% for four years is zero, since 5.47% is the
LIBOR-OIS spread blew out during the height of the ﬁnancial crisis (as discussed in
Section 1.2.1), people became very aware of this issue. As a result, many in the industry
believe it is more appropriate to discount the cash ﬂows in a swap by zero coupon rates
derived from an OIS curve, rather than from a swap (i.e., LIBOR) curve. Zero coupon
rates formed from an OIS curve will be lower than those formed from the swap curve. The
result of this is that the mark-to-market of swaps under OIS discounting will be higher
(in absolute value terms) than under LIBOR discounting. See Whittall (2010c) for more
details.
This phenomenon has become even more complicated and contentious, as some CSAs
allow for collateral to be posted in multiple currencies, creating a “cheapest-to-deliver
option” in which a counterparty in a swap will be incented to post the cheapest type of
collateral, optimizing among collateral denominated in various currencies. Of course, what
may be cheapest to deliver today may not remain so in the future, and a counterparty
may optimally choose to post collateral denominated in a diﬀerent currency at some point
in time over the life of the swap. See Sawyer (2011) for more details. The pressure in the
industry on this issue is causing certain clearinghouses to change the way they value
swaps (see Whittall [2010a, 2010b] for more details). Despite these issues, we will for our
purposes always compute discounted cash ﬂows using the swap curve.
27As we have discussed in Chapter 1, if the dealer hedged himself with Treasuries, he
would still have spread risk over the life of the trade. Thus, he would not be perfectly
hedged and would face mark-to-market ﬂuctuations over the life of the trade but would face
considerably less risk than if he maintained interest rate sensitivity by not hedging at all.
106

<!-- Page 130 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.11: Looking at the Value of the 7.00% Swap
4-year swap rate. That is, by deﬁnition the 4-year swap rate (which happens
to be 5.47%) is the rate such that a 4-year swap is an NPV = 0 transaction,
and therefore we can conclude that the bottom swap in the ﬁgure is worth
zero.
Now looking at the bottom two boxes in Figure 3.11, we can do a lot of
canceling out. Note that the client is paying out LIBOR in the middle box
and receiving LIBOR in the bottom box. So we can cancel these legs of the
two swaps out with one another. That simpliﬁes things and leaves us with
the client receiving ﬁxed at 7.00% and simultaneously paying ﬁxed at 5.47%.
In other words, the client is receiving an annuity of 7.00% −5.47% = 1.53%
of the notional for the next four years.
What is the present value of this annuity? Although the appropriate
way to compute the present value of this annuity is to discount each cash
ﬂow by its appropriate zero coupon rate, we can come up with a decent
approximation using a ﬁnancial calculator. Let’s set
PMT = 1.53%
i = 5.47%
n = 4
107

<!-- Page 131 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
and compute a present value of approximately 5.367%, of the notional, or
around $5,367,000.28
So how well did our handcheck do? The actual value of the swap (ob-
tained by modeling the cash ﬂows) is $5,387,192, so our handcheck is oﬀby
around $20,000. Is that a lot? Well, yes, $20,000 is a lot of money, but in
the context of this trade, it’s pretty small. Let’s look at it this way: if we
go back to Table 3.9, we see that the PV Coupon 01 in a $100mm 4-year
swap with this swap curve is $35,210. Thus, our handcheck gets us to within
$20,000/$35,210 ≈0.6 of a bp.29 Not bad. Of course it’s not exact, but we
are under a bp away (in terms of bps running) from the actual value. So
since the whole purpose of the handcheck is to just make sure we are in the
right ballpark, we should feel pretty good about this.30
Why did we choose to set i = 5.47% when valuing the annuity? This is a
very reasonable question, and the answer is there is really no good answer.
From the very get-go, it is inappropriate to value the annuity using a single
yield. We should value cash ﬂows using the appropriate zero coupon rates,
but that would take too long: once again, the purpose of the handcheck
is to do a back-of-the-envelope calculation with a few keystrokes.31 So we
are already making a fundamental error by discounting using a yield, so we
shouldn’t get hung up on what is the “right” yield to use to discount. It is
wrong to discount using a yield in the ﬁrst place, so there is no right yield
28Be sure to set your ﬁnancial calculator to assume that these annuity payments are
received at the end of each year, not the beginning of each year. The ﬁrst payment in the
remaining 4-year swap is not today but one year from today, and the last payment is four
years from today.
29Yet another way to handcheck this number is to take the PV Coupon 01 for a 4-
year swap in Table 3.9 and multiply it by 153, the number of bps in the annuity. This
gives us $35,210 × 153 = $5,387,130, which is very close to the model-based number. The
only reason that this number does not tie out exactly with our model is because the PV
Coupon 01 numbers presented in Table 3.9 are rounded to the nearest dollar, introducing
a small rounding error. In general, we will not rely on this type of handcheck because it
requires knowing what the PV Coupon 01 is, and we want a handcheck methodology that
does not rely upon use of a swap model at all.
30What is a reasonable margin of error on a handcheck? That is a matter of personal
opinion, but many in the industry start to get uncomfortable when oﬀby more than a bp
running.
31Suppose we do use the zero coupon rates to exactly value the annuity. In that case we
get the value of the swap is
$1,530,000
1.05
+ $1,530,000
(1.05113)2 + $1,530,000
(1.05312)3 + $1,530,000
(1.05494)4 = $5,387,192,
the exact answer from our model. But that took way too much math to count for a quick
handcheck. By the way, please note that the zero coupon rates shown here are rounded
and thus won’t exactly match the actual unwind value. If the exact zero coupon rates are
used, the values will match exactly.
108

<!-- Page 132 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
to use anyway. We should just use something reasonable. We need to plug
in something for i, which is used to discount four annual cash ﬂows, so it
seems pretty reasonable to use the 4-year swap rate. In fact, you see that the
4-year swap rate of 5.47% looks like it lies somewhere in middle of the four
zero coupon rates (5%, 5.113%, 5.312%, and 5.494%) one would use if being
precise, so that should give us some comfort. But let’s not worry too much
about what yield we throw in there for discounting purposes, as long as we
choose something sensible. If we set i = 20%, that wouldn’t seem too sensible
in the swap curve environment we were given, and we would probably have
trouble convincing people it was a good choice.
2
Example 2
Let’s now look at the market information for swaps on the composite page
shown in Table 1.1, where the standard convention is ﬁxed payments are
made semi bond and the ﬂoating side pays 3-month LIBOR quarterly money.
Suppose that exactly three years ago a client entered into a $100mm 8-year
swap with a dealer in which the client receives 4.647% semi bond and pays
3-month LIBOR quarterly money. The client is interested in knowing the
mark-to-market value of the swap. Let’s assume that mark-to-markets are
done by valuing swaps at mid-market swap rates prevailing in the market.
Handcheck for Example 2
Since our swap model is only set up for annual coupons, we are not going
to be able to model up this swap. But we can use a handcheck approach to
approximate the current mark-to-market. The ﬁrst three years of the swap
have come and gone and have no bearing on the current valuation of the
swap. The client is currently receiving 4.647% when the mid-market 5-year
swap rate 1.627% (the average of the bid and oﬀer side swap rates). The ap-
proach to handchecking this swap is very similar to what we did in Example
1, but we have to be very careful here with the frequency of payments, which
in this example are semiannual on the ﬁxed side. The strategy for valuing
this swap is shown in Figure 3.12.
Once again we can add in the bottom box because it has zero value;
1.627% is the mid-market 5-year swap rate, and thus paying or receiving
this rate in a 5-year swap is worth nothing. Note that the LIBOR legs in
the bottom two boxes, which in this case are paid quarterly, still cancel each
other out exactly. And so we are left with the client receiving an annuity of
4.647%−1.627% = 3.02% that is paid semi bond. The swap is in-the-money
to the client, since the client is receiving a ﬁxed rate above the 5-year swap
109

<!-- Page 133 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.12: Looking at the Value of the 4.647% Swap with Standard Pay-
ment Conventions
rate. To value the swap using a handcheck approach, put
PMT = (4.647% −1.627%)/2 = 1.51%
n = 5 × 2 = 10
i = 1.627%/2 = 0.8135%
in a ﬁnancial calculator, and we get a present value of around 14.446% of
the notional, or around $14,446,000. The important thing to note in this
example is that we had to look at the smallest frequency in which ﬁxed
payments were made. In this case the swap makes payments semiannually
on the ﬁxed side, and it is important to account for the timing of these cash
ﬂows when discounting back to ﬁnd the present value of the annuity.32
2
32If we had forgotten to account for the fact that the ﬁxed side pays semiannu-
ally, our handcheck on this would have (inaccurately) been found by putting PMT =
4.647% −1.627% = 3.02%, n = 5, and i = 1.627% and getting PV = 14.390% of the
notional, or $14,390,000, around $56,000 oﬀfrom the (more appropriate) handcheck
amount of $14,446,000. Although handchecks are approximate, not accounting for the
correct frequency of payments would have introduced an unnecessary rounding error.
110

<!-- Page 134 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
3.3.2
Unwinds
An unwind of an existing swap is an agreement between the two counterpar-
ties to have no further obligations to one another as per the original terms of
the swap. An unwind fee will be made from one party to the other reﬂecting
the current market value of the swap, so as to compensate the party that
is in-the-money to forgo the continuation of the swap. This fee is typically
paid T + 2 days after the trade has been unwound.
Example 3
Let’s continue with Example 2. Suppose that based upon the mark-to-market
we just calculated, the client would like to unwind the trade. Recall that in
the original trade, the client is receiving ﬁxed. Now, in order to unwind this
trade, the dealer is implicitly being asked to receive ﬁxed from the client
to reverse the original trade that was put on. That is, hypothetically, if the
client were at this moment in time to pay ﬁxed in a 5-year swap at a rate
of 4.647% semi bond versus threes, this would exactly negate the position
the client currently has on. And presumably the dealer would be willing to
pay something to the client for this, since that rate is well above the current
oﬀer side 5-year swap rate of 1.647%.
The dealer receives ﬁxed at the oﬀer side swap rate, which in this case is
1.647%, and the amount of the unwind fee is determined relative to this oﬀer
side swap rate. Since the dealer would normally receive 1.647% in a 5-year
swap executed right now but will instead receive a higher rate of 4.647%, this
implies that the dealer will pay an unwind fee to the client for terminating
the swap early.
Handcheck for Example 3
Since this example also has semiannual payments on the ﬁxed side and thus
does not ﬁt into the paradigm of our model, we will have to come up with
a handcheck for the amount of the unwind fee. The logic of computing the
amount of the unwind fee is shown in Figure 3.13. When it comes to un-
winding the swap, the dealer is essentially being asked to receive 4.647%
versus paying 3-month LIBOR. This is not literally the case, but that is
the economic impact of unwinding the swap. As Figure 3.13 demonstrates,
the unwind of the swap (represented in the top transaction) can be broken
up into the dealer receiving the oﬀer side swap rate of 1.647% semi bond
versus 3-month LIBOR (the middle transaction) plus the dealer receiving an
annuity of what is left over: 4.647% −1.647% semi bond (the bottom trans-
action). Since the dealer is receiving the oﬀer side swap rate in the middle
111

<!-- Page 135 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.13: Determining the Unwind Fee in a Swap in Which the Client Is
Currently Receiving 4.647%
box, there will be no upfront fee associated with this piece. To determine
the amount the dealer will pay for the annuity in the third box, we put
PMT = (4.647% −1.647%)/2 = 1.50%
n = 5 × 2 = 10
i = 1.647%/2 = 0.8235%
in a ﬁnancial calculator, and we solve for PV = 14.342% of the notional,
or around $14,342,000. Note that the amount that the dealer is willing to
pay to unwind the trade, $14,342,000, is less than the mark-to-market of the
trade, $14,446,000, determined in Example 2. The diﬀerence represents the
bid/oﬀer that the dealer is making on the unwind.
2
3.3.3
Assignments
In Examples 2 and 3 we considered a client who had originally entered into
an 8-year swap with a particular dealer, which we will denote by Dealer 1.
Under normal market conditions, this does not mean, however, that the client
is beholden to Dealer 1 for purposes of unwinding the trade. If he wishes,
112

<!-- Page 136 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
the client can do an assignment of the trade to another dealer. That is, the
client can always contact another dealer, which we will denote by Dealer
2, to compete on the unwind. Dealer 2 will be asked what upfront fee it is
willing to pay to the client in order to receive 4.647% on a $100mm swap for
ﬁve years. If Dealer 2 is willing to receive ﬁxed in a 5-year swap at a more
aggressive rate than Dealer 1, then Dealer 2 will be able to provide a larger
upfront amount to the client.33 In this case, instead of unwinding the trade,
the trade will be assigned. Dealer 2 will pay the upfront fee to the client and
will now receive 4.647% on a $100mm swap for the next ﬁve years, facing
Dealer 1. In the case of this assignment, the client is known as the assignor,
Dealer 2 is known as the assignee, and Dealer 1 is known as the remaining
party.
There’s one catch here. The client can’t assign the swap to Dealer 2
unless Dealer 1 has given its permission to assign the swap (and presumably
when the client contacted Dealer 2, it checked with Dealer 2 that it would be
willing to face Dealer 1 in the swap). Remember, a swap is a contract between
two counterparties and carries with it counterparty risk. It could be the case
that Dealer 1 and Dealer 2 don’t even have an ISDA Master Agreement in
place with one another to do swaps. This is rather unlikely, however, as most
dealers have documentation in place with all other dealers so that they can
do such trades on assignment, or face other dealers on trades done through
the brokers market. It is conceivable, however, particularly during times
of ﬁnancial stress, that one dealer may refuse to accept another dealer on
assignment. The reputational eﬀects of “turning down a name,” both for the
party who refuses but especially for the party who is refused, are potentially
enormous.
3.3.4
Forward Starting Swaps
A forward starting swap is a swap that has a settlement date beyond the
standard T + 2 New York and London business days. To take a particular
example, consider a 3-year swap, one-year forward (also known as a “1x4”,
which is pronounced “1 by 4”).34 This swap has an eﬀective date that is in
33Suppose, for example, that Dealer 2 was willing to receive in a 5-year swap at a rate
of 1.637%. This is 1 bp more aggressive than a 5-year oﬀer side rate of 1.647% showed by
Dealer 1. In the context of Figure 3.13, this means that Dealer 2 will value the existing
swap as an annuity worth 4.647% −1.637% = 301 bps to the client, as compared to the
300 bp annuity that Dealer 1 is valuing it at.
34Some also refer to this as a “1 into 3,” although this terminology lends itself to
possible confusion since swaptions, which will be discussed in Chapter 5, employ the “x
into y” terminology as well.
113

<!-- Page 137 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.14: Pricing Up a 1x4 Forward Starting Swap
one year.35 Assuming the swap pays ﬁxed semi bond versus 3-month LIBOR
quarterly money, then the ﬁrst ﬂoating payment would be in 1.25 years from
today, the ﬁrst ﬁxed payment would be in 1.5 years from today, and ﬁnal
payments on both sides would be made four years from today.
The ﬁxed coupon in a forward starting swap is set such that the swap is
an NPV = 0 transaction. If we think about pricing swaps using Eurodollar
futures, recall that a spot starting swap is priced oﬀdeposit rates (e.g.,
today’s LIBOR setting) and a sequence of Eurodollar futures including the
one that is next to settle serially. A forward starting swap may very well
be priced oﬀof a deferred sequence of Eurodollar futures, omitting one or
more contracts that are next to settle serially. It is possible, however, to also
consider pricing a forward starting swap as a function of observable spot
starting swap rates, as we will soon see.
Example
Consider again our stylized example, and let’s price up a 1x4 (i.e., a 3-year
swap, one year forward) in which payments on both sides are made annual
bond. Figure 3.14 shows how we price up the 1x4. All of our familiar zero
coupon rates and forwards are there in the model. However, notice that there
are no cash ﬂows that occur at the end of the ﬁrst year. The 1x4 is eﬀective
in one year, and thus its ﬁrst cash ﬂows are at the end of the second year.
The ﬁrst forward (which is actually today’s spot LIBOR) is not employed
35To be completely precise, the exact eﬀective date would be one year after the standard
T + 2 settlement date of spot starting swaps.
114

<!-- Page 138 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
in the model to form the ﬂoating cash ﬂow at the end of the ﬁrst year, since
there is no cash ﬂow in that year. The remaining three forwards are used
to determine the market’s expectation of the ﬂoating ﬂows in the swap over
its three-year life. The ﬁxed rate that we solve for, 5.644%, is such that the
present value of the three ﬁxed ﬂows equals the present value of the three
ﬂoating ﬂows.36
2
Hedging Forward Starting Swaps
We’ve previously talked about how a dealer would hedge a spot starting swap
with either Treasuries or Eurodollar futures. A 1x4 has a ﬁnal maturity that
is four years from today, but does that mean that the dealer should hedge
this forward starting swap in the same way it would hedge a spot starting
swap?
The answer is no. Since there are no cash ﬂows at all in the ﬁrst year, any
hedge that had interest rate exposure in the ﬁrst year would not be ideal.
One good hedge would be to trade in a deferred strip of Eurodollar futures.
Yet another way the dealer could hedge out his position in a forward starting
swap is by trading in two diﬀerent spot starting swaps. Let’s continue with
our example in which the dealer is receiving ﬁxed in a 1x4. As we will see,
the dealer can hedge himself by simultaneously paying ﬁxed in a 4-year spot
starting swap and receiving ﬁxed on the same notional of a 1-year spot
starting swap. Intuitively, by paying ﬁxed for four years, but then receiving
ﬁxed for one year, the dealer will negate the position in the ﬁrst year but
maintain exposure in the back three years. This principle is suggested in
Figure 3.15. In fact, this ﬁgure will be the basis for the handcheck of a forward
starting swap rate that we will now develop.
Handcheck
It used to be that there was very little information about forward starting
swap rates that was made available on any pricing screens. People would rely
on their own swap models to see where a particular forward starting swap
rate should be. Over the years, more information about forward starting
swap rates has become widely available. Many traders will keep an eye on
a forward starting swap matrix, such as the one in Table 3.10, looking for
opportunities to put on interesting trades (some of which we will discuss
later in this chapter and others of which we will discuss in Chapter 8). In this
36Note that in Figure 3.14, we are using the three forward rates f(1, 2), f(2, 3) and
f(3, 4) for purposes of computing the market’s expected nominal cash ﬂows in the ﬂoating
leg of the swap. And we use zc(2), zc(3), and zc(4), respectively, to form discount factors
in order to compute the present value of these nominal cash ﬂows.
115

Table 3.10: Forward Starting Swap Rate Matrix
matrix, the ﬁrst row refers to spot starting swaps of a variety of maturities
(i.e., the swap curve). Subsequent rows contain forward swap rates for a
given delay that are derived from the given swap curve. For example, in
this matrix, the 10-year swap rate is 4.49%, and the 10-year swap rate,
1 year forward (denoted 1x11) is 4.99%.
116

<!-- Page 140 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Despite the proliferation of data on forward starting swaps, an under-
standing of how the rates are derived from spot swap rates is crucial. We
already looked at pricing forward starting swaps in our model, as shown
in Figure 3.14, and we are now going to develop a handcheck for the com-
putation of this rate. This handcheck methodology will be based upon the
logic suggested in Figure 3.15. Let’s look at this ﬁgure again. Suppose for
the moment that the 4-year swap rate happened to equal the 1-year swap
rate, and denote this rate by x. If these two rates are the same, then all cash
ﬂows in the bottom two swaps of this ﬁgure would exactly cancel out over
the ﬁrst year. That is, the two ﬁxed rates would cancel each other out, and
the two ﬂoating rates would cancel each other out for the ﬁrst year. So we
would have no cash ﬂow in the ﬁrst year, and in the back three years the
dealer would be receiving ﬁxed at a rate of x and paying LIBOR. But this
would mean that the 1x4 swap rate is x.
In general, the 4-year swap rate will not equal the 1-year swap rate, and
thus not all of the cash ﬂows in the ﬁrst year will exactly cancel each other
out. But all is not lost; we can still proceed with this methodology as long
as we are careful to “take” any cash ﬂow that would occur in year one and
“move” it to the back three years. When we say “move,” we mean that we
will take any cash ﬂow that occurs in year one and spread it out over the
life of the swap (accounting for its value appropriately, of course) in years
two, three, and four. That is, given any cash ﬂow at the end of year one, we
could ask ourselves what annuity payment we could receive at the end of
years two, three, and four that has the same present value. Based on this
answer, we are then indiﬀerent between receiving the cash ﬂow at the end of
the ﬁrst year or the annuity over the back three years, and we can move the
cash ﬂow from year one into the back three years of the life of the swap.
Example 1
Assume that the swap curve is as given in Table 3.5. Let’s look at what
happens if the dealer receives in four years at 5.47% and pays in one year at
5.00% (let’s refer to this as the “replicating strategy” for the forward starting
swap). The important thing to keep in mind is that this replicating strategy
will imply what the ﬁxed rate in the 1x4 swap must be. To see this, ﬁrst note
that the two LIBOR legs will exactly cancel out over the ﬁrst year, but the
dealer is left paying LIBOR at the end of years two, three, and four. This is
good: in the forward starting swap the dealer also pays LIBOR in years two,
three, and four. So by entering into the replicating strategy, we emulate the
ﬂoating leg of the forward starting swap perfectly.
Let’s now look at the ﬁxed ﬂows that result from the replicating strategy.
Since the two ﬁxed rates diﬀer, this means that the dealer will receive a net
amount of 47 bps (the diﬀerence in the two ﬁxed rates) at the end of year one
117

<!-- Page 141 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.16: The Net Carry from Receiving in Four Years and Paying in One
Year
and will thereafter receive the rate of 5.47% at the end of years two, three,
and four.37 This is demonstrated in Figure 3.16. So this replicating strategy
almost gets us cash ﬂows in all the right places (meaning at the same times)
as the forward starting swap. The only problem is that this strategy earns
carry (cash ﬂow) over the delay (the period of time between the normal T +2
settlement date of a spot starting swap and the eﬀective date of the forward
starting swap). In this case, the replicating strategy earns carry of 47 bps
over the delay of year one. The “•” in Figure 3.16 represents the carry that
is earned in year one before the forward starting swap is eﬀective.
As suggested above, we can solve for an annuity to be paid at the end of
years two, three, and four that is worth the same in present value terms as
37We are talking here about receiving rates and bps, but we shouldn’t forget that
there is some underlying notional upon which these are all being paid. It may be more
comforting to think of this in terms of actual cash ﬂow. In that case, pick a representative
notional, say, $100mm, and translate the 47 bps to $470,000 and the 5.47% to $5,470,000.
118

<!-- Page 142 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.17: Moving the Net Carry from the Eﬀective Date over the Life
of the Swap
the 47 bps received at the end of year one. We need to do this because if this
strategy is to replicate the forward starting swap, then it must not have any
cash ﬂows in the ﬁrst year. This procedure is demonstrated in Figure 3.17.
So instead of regarding this replicating strategy as producing carry of 47 bps
at the end of year one, we can instead regard it as producing an annuity of
17.4 bps over the back three years. But this means that our handcheck for
the 1x4 swap rate is 5.47% + 0.174% = 5.644%.
Why? Let’s review what we just did. First, remember that the 1x4
swap rate is that ﬁxed rate that—when paid over three years, one year
forward—has the same present value as the LIBOR cash ﬂows paid dur-
ing the same time period. Now, if we enter into the replicating strategy,
we will replicate the LIBOR cash ﬂows in the forward starting swap, since
the ﬁrst year LIBOR cash ﬂows cancel out, and we are left with the three
LIBOR cash ﬂows, one year forward. So we’re halfway there. Now, on the
ﬁxed side, we need to come up with a single ﬁxed rate over the forward
three years. By entering into the replicating strategy, we are left with 47 bps
119

<!-- Page 143 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
received at the end of one year, plus we get a rate of 5.47% received over the
back three years. But as we saw, we are indiﬀerent between receiving that 47
bps at the end of year one or receiving an annuity of 17.4 bps over the back
three years. Thus, by receiving in four years and paying in one year, we have
an annuity of 17.4 bps over the back three years plus a rate of 5.47% over
the back three years, for a total of 5.47% + 0.174% = 5.644% over the back
three years. Note that this is the answer we got from our model, 5.644%,
that we priced in Figure 3.14.
There is one thing that we forgot to mention. In Figure 3.17, when we
took the 47 bps and solve for the annuity, we used a rate of 5.47% to discount.
Why did we use 5.47%? We have no good reason, other than it was a readily
available rate to use. We know by deﬁnition that any rate we use is wrong,
since we should be using zero coupon rates to discount individual cash ﬂows.
But this is a handcheck, and we want a quick and dirty approximation, so
we live with it. We need a rate to discount cash ﬂows received over three
years (actually three years, one year forward), so it seemed reasonable to
use the 4-year swap rate. Is that “right”? No, of course it isn’t right. But
this is just a handcheck—we’re not going to trade oﬀof this number; we are
just going to use it to make sure we are in the neighborhood of the right
answer.
2
Example 2
Let’s take a look at another example that is slightly more complicated but
makes use of the same exact methodology that we just employed to calculate
the 1x4 forward starting swap rate. Now let’s consider a 2x5 (i.e., a 3-year
swap, two years forward), and assume that the ﬁxed side pays semi bond
and the ﬂoating side is 3-month LIBOR quarterly money. Suppose that the
swap curve is as given in Table 1.1. Note that the mid-market 2-year swap
rate is 0.685%, and the mid-market 5-year swap rate is 1.627%.
This example won’t ﬁt into our model that has annual cash ﬂows, so
we will resort to a handcheck in order to compute the 2x5 swap rate. A
dealer who receives ﬁxed in a 2x5 swap can hedge the position by paying
ﬁxed in ﬁve years and receiving ﬁxed in two years. A replicating strategy of
receiving the 2x5 swap rate entails receiving in ﬁve years and paying in two
years. Figure 3.18 shows this replicating strategy.
The important thing to keep in mind is that the replicating strategy
will imply what the ﬁxed rate in the 2x5 swap must be. In the replicating
strategy, the two LIBOR legs will exactly cancel each other out over the ﬁrst
two years (i.e., the ﬁrst eight 3-month LIBOR ﬂows that are paid out in the
5-year swap will exactly cancel out the eight 3-month LIBOR ﬂows that are
received in the 2-year swap), and the dealer will be left paying LIBOR only
over the back three years. This is what we want: in the forward starting
120

<!-- Page 144 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.18: Replicating a 2x5 Swap
swap the dealer also pays LIBOR for three years with an eﬀective date two
years from now. So by entering into the replicating strategy, we emulate the
ﬂoating leg of the swap perfectly.
Let’s now look at the ﬁxed ﬂows that result from the replicating strategy.
Since the two ﬁxed rates diﬀer, this means that the dealer earns carry of 94.2
bps (the diﬀerence in the two ﬁxed rates of 1.627% and 0.685%) running
over the ﬁrst two years and will receive the rate of 1.627% for the next
three years. This is demonstrated in Figure 3.19. So this replicating strategy
nearly emulates the cash ﬂows that occur in the forward starting swap. The
only problem is the carry over the delay of the ﬁrst two years, which is
1.627% −0.685% = 94.2 bps. Let’s be very, very careful: this carry is paid
out semiannually at 94.2/2 = 47.1 bps per semiannual period, and it is very
important that we value it as such.
We need to take all this carry over the delay (i.e., the period of time
between when the swap is transacted and when it is eﬀective) and shove it
into the back three years. We will do this in two steps. In step one, we are
going to take the four annuity payments in the ﬁrst two years and compute
their future value (FV) as of the end of year two, which is the eﬀective date
of the forward starting swap. In step two, we are going to take the value of
everything we just pushed forward to the eﬀective date and spread it out
as an annuity over the next three years, which is when the forward starting
swap is in existence. These procedures are shown in Figure 3.20. So instead
121

<!-- Page 145 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.19: The Carry from Receiving in Five Years and Paying in Two
Years
of regarding the replicating strategy as producing carry of 94.2 bps running
over the delay, we can instead regard it as producing an annuity of 32.5 bps
in semiannual installments over the back three years. But this means that
our handcheck for the 2x5 swap rate is 1.627%+(2×0.325%) = 2.277%. This
is shown in Figure 3.21. Let’s be very, very careful to note that when we take
the annuity of 32.5 bps per semiannual period, we must multiply it by two
in order to bring it back to annualized terms before we add it to 1.627%.38 2
38Note that in Figure 3.20, we use the 2-year swap rate of 0.685% as a discount rate for
purposes of computing the future value of the carry over the delay, and we use the 5-year
swap rate of 1.627% as a discount rate for purposes of translating this future value into an
annuity over the life of the forward starting swap. In general, we would use the shorter-
tenor swap rate for the purposes of computing the future value of the carry over the delay
and the longer-tenor swap rate for the purposes of translating this future value into an
annuity over the life of the forward starting swap.
122

<!-- Page 146 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.20: Taking the Future Value of the Carry Over the Delay and
Spreading It Out Over the Life of the Swap
To summarize, here is the suggested procedure to approximate the for-
ward swap rate from the two relevant spot starting swap rates:
1. Calculate the carry from the two spot starting swaps between today
and the eﬀective date of the forward starting swap (a period of time
often referred to as the delay).
2. Compute the forward value of this carry to the eﬀective date of the
swap, using the swap rate on the shorter-tenor swap as discount rate.
3. Take this amount and calculate an annuity over the life of the forward
starting swap, using the swap rate on the longer-tenor swap as discount
rate.
4. Add the annuity (annualized in the case of multiple swap payments
per year) to the longer-tenor swap rate.
123

<!-- Page 147 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.21: Obtaining the 2x5 Swap Rate from the Annuity Computation
Uses of Forward Starting Swaps
There are a number of ways in which investors use forward starting swaps.
We will ﬁrst look at a classic example in which corporations use forward
starting swaps (i.e., by swapping an anticipated asset or liability that is ex-
pected to commence some time in the future). We will also discuss how for-
ward starting swaps have become market benchmarks in more recent times.
Hedging Future Issuance
The most traditional use of a forward starting swap is to hedge an asset
or liability that does not commence today but is anticipated to start at a
known point of time in the future. Consider, for example, a corporation that
knows it is going to need to raise funds in a year to undertake an investment
project. The corporation is going to want to raise $500mm by issuing a 5-
year ﬁxed rate bond. The corporation does not want to issue the bond now,
because it does not need the cash now.
124

<!-- Page 148 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
When the corporation comes to market in a year to issue the bond, the
yield that it will pay to the bondholders will be a function of both 5-year on-
the-run Treasury yields prevailing at the time, T5, as well as the corporation’s
corporate spread, c5, prevailing at the time. That is, the corporation will pay
a yield of
T5 + c5.
(3.9)
If the sum in Equation 3.9 increases over the next year, this will serve to
increase the funding costs of the corporation. Of course, this sum may de-
cline next year as well. But the point is that the corporation is exposed to
movements in this sum; the yield on this bond will depend on the sum in
Equation 3.9 in a year.
The corporation may be concerned that their funding costs are going
to increase when it is time to come to market in a year. It may choose
to execute a swap to hedge against this risk. What’s the best swap hedge
for the corporation? If it enters into a 5-year spot starting swap, that will
produce cash ﬂows over the next year, whereas its anticipated debt does not.
Moreover, this hedge will not provide coverage for the last year that the debt
is outstanding. If it enters into a 6-year spot starting swap, although this
hedge would cover the entire life of the anticipated liability, we would again
have the problem of the hedge existing in the ﬁrst year, whereas the debt
does not.
It turns out that a better hedge would be for the corporation to enter
into a forward starting swap. In particular, the corporation might enter into
a 1x6. This is a 5-year swap, one year forward, which matches the timing of
the anticipated debt. The corporation will want to pay ﬁxed in the 1x6. The
corporation will hold this swap for one year, at which time it will unwind
the swap. Let’s assume that the ﬁxed rate in the 1x6 that the corporation
pays is 5%. There will be no cash ﬂows over the course of the ﬁrst year. Note
that in one year’s time, the swap will then be a 5-year spot starting swap.
If, in one year, 5-year swap rates are above 5%, the swap will be in-the-
money to the corporation. The proﬁts on the swap can be used to oﬀset the
(presumably) higher funding costs associated with the debt. And if 5-year
swap rates are below 5% in one year, the loss on the swap will (presumably)
be met with lower funding costs on the debt. Again, the corporation will
unwind the swap in one year, recognizing the proﬁt or loss on the swap at
the end of the hedging period, and use this gain or loss to oﬀset its funding
costs.
Notice that we used the word “presumably” twice in the preceding para-
graph. This is because the swap being in-the-money does not guarantee that
the funding costs of the corporation will be higher in a year than they would
be today, and the swap being out-of-the-money does not guarantee that the
125

<!-- Page 149 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
funding costs of the corporation will be lower in a year. In one year, the
value of the swap that the corporation entered into will depend entirely on
the then-prevailing 5-year swap rate. We do not know where the 5-year swap
rate will be in one year. Its value is random and can be expressed as
T5 + ss5,
(3.10)
where ss5 represents the unknown 5-year swap spread prevailing at the time.
If we compare the random funding costs of the corporation in Equation 3.9
with the random 5-year swap rate in a year as given in Equation 3.10, the
former depends on the corporation’s random funding spread, the latter on
5-year swap spreads. Presuming the corporation’s 5-year corporate spread
is positively correlated with swap spreads, the hedge will likely be more
eﬀective than if there is negative correlation between the two. This dynamic
is demonstrated in Figure 3.22.
There may be good reason to suggest that the corporation’s credit spread
is positively correlated with swap spreads. As we discussed in Chapter 2,
swap spreads tend to be a barometer of general credit concerns in the mar-
ket. Therefore, an increase (decrease) in the corporation’s credit spread due
to a systemic change in credit spreads may be accompanied by an increase
(decrease) in swap spreads. The corporation may wish to analyze the histor-
ical correlation of its credit spread to swap spreads in determining whether
or not the swap is a good hedge.
Forward Starting Swaps as a Benchmark
Most every market has its benchmarks, or barometers, that people watch to
gauge the market overall. In the Treasury market, for example, the yields of
the on-the-run 2-year, 10-year, and 30-year Treasuries have all been popular
benchmarks. The interest rate swaps market has its benchmarks as well. The
2-year, 10-year, and 30-year swap rates are each watched by many investors
to give a quick snapshot of the market. In recent years, certain forward
starting swaps have become benchmarks as well.
If we think about a 10-year spot starting swap (with standard conventions
of semi bond versus threes) and how it is priced, it is composed of forty
3-month LIBOR forwards, each of which contributes to the value of the 10-
year swap. The nearer-term forwards are very much tied to expectations of
Fed policy, whereas longer-term forwards are less tied to the Fed’s policy
decisions and are more tied to the longer-run prospects for inﬂation, the
economy, and so on.
In addition to looking at 10-year spot starting swaps, some investors in
the market have become just as focused on looking at the 5x10 swap rate
126

<!-- Page 150 -->
Figure 3.22: Corporation Pays Fixed in a 1x6 to Hedge Future Issuance

<!-- Page 151 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
(5-year swap rate, 5 years forward). It is felt by some that the 5x10 rate
strips away that component of a 10-year rate that is very much governed
by Fed policy decisions and exclusively portends longer-term expectations
of inﬂation, the growth of the economy, etcetera.
Former Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, in his semiannual testimony be-
fore the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Aﬀairs, U.S. Senate,
on February 16, 2005, alluded to this concept (i.e., that spot yields can be
thought of as being constructed by a series of forward rates) to try to ex-
plain why the yield curve had ﬂattened so much despite the Fed raising rates
dramatically. Such ﬂattening ran counter to previous tightening cycles.39
In this environment, long-term interest rates have trended
lower in recent months even as the Federal Reserve has raised the
level of the target federal funds rate by 150 basis points. This de-
velopment contrasts with most experience, which suggests that,
other things being equal, increasing short-term interest rates are
normally accompanied by a rise in longer-term yields. The simple
mathematics of the yield curve governs the relationship between
short- and long-term interest rates. Ten-year yields, for example,
can be thought of as an average of ten consecutive one-year for-
ward rates. A rise in the ﬁrst-year forward rate, which correlates
closely with the federal funds rate, would increase the yield on
ten-year U.S. Treasury notes even if the more-distant forward
rates remain unchanged. Historically, though, even these distant
forward rates have tended to rise in association with monetary
policy tightening.40
Example
Figure 3.23 shows the 5x10 swap rate, the 10-year swap rate, and their dif-
ference in 2010. Although the 5x10 and 10-year swap rate are very highly
correlated, their diﬀerence indicates that these two rates can vary signiﬁ-
cantly. As indicated by the data labels, the diﬀerence between the two rates
widened out by 66 bps in a period of just over two months.
39This phenomenon eventually led to conditions that became known as the Greenspan
Conundrum. Despite a prolonged tightening cycle by the Fed, signs of economic growth
and concerns over the risk of inﬂation, long-term rates did not rise, contrary to what
history would suggest should have happened.
40Testimony of Chairman Alan Greenspan at the Federal Reserve Board’s semian-
nual Monetary Policy Report to Congress Before the Committee on Banking, Housing,
and Urban Aﬀairs, U.S. Senate, delivered on February 16, 2005. Transcript found on
http://www.federalreserve.gov/boarddocs/hh/2005/february/testimony.htm
128

<!-- Page 152 -->
T H E P R I C I N G O F S WA P S
Figure 3.23: The 5x10 Swap Rate, the 10-Year Swap Rate, and Their Dif-
ference in 2010
As the graph indicates, the market had rallied signiﬁcantly in the ﬁrst
part of the year, with the 10-year swap rate falling nearly 150 bps between
January and August. The latter part of the year saw a large sell-oﬀ, with
10-year swap rates increasing approximately 100 bps from August to De-
cember. However, during this time, the 5x10 swap rate increased even more
dramatically, moving in excess of 150 bps.
The 10-year swap rate can be bifurcated into the front ﬁve years and
the back ﬁve years, which in our nomenclature is the 0x5 (the spot starting
5-year swap) and the 5x10 (the 5-year swap rate, ﬁve years forward). From
August 26 to November 5, the 5x10 swap rate increased 82 bps, whereas the
10-year swap rate increased only 16 bps over this period. This gap can be
explained by the movement in the 5-year swap rate. In fact, during this time
the 5-year swap rate actually rallied nearly 24 bps.
Of course, the 5x10 swap rate is determined as a function of the 5-year
and 10-year swap rates. But in the example we just considered, some market
participants would interpret this as meaning that the sell-oﬀin the long end
was attributable more to changes in long-term prospects of inﬂation and the
economy, rather than to perceived changes in Fed policy.
2

4. One of your clients has approached you to do an asset swap of a corporate

The classic asset swap is a par transaction (and thus is sometimes referred
