Chapter 4
Caps and Floors
There are two main types of plain vanilla options traded in the swaps market:
caps and ﬂoors, on the one hand, and (single European) swaptions, on the
other. In this chapter we will look at caps and ﬂoors. We will discuss how
they are used by investors and how they are priced using a variant of the
Black-Scholes model known as Black’s formula.
4.1
An Introduction to Caps and Floors
A cap is a call on rates. Caps can be written on a variety of ﬂoating rate
indexes, the most common of which is 3-month LIBOR. For example, the
payoﬀon a cap on 3-month LIBOR depends on the formula
max (3-month LIBOR −Strike, 0).
A ﬂoor is a put on rates. The payoﬀon a ﬂoor on 3-month LIBOR depends
on the formula
max (Strike −3-month LIBOR, 0).
The holder of a cap receives a payment whenever LIBOR exceeds the strike,
whereas the holder of a ﬂoor receives a payment whenever LIBOR is less
than the strike.
The purchaser of a cap or ﬂoor on 3-month LIBOR is actually buying
a series of options. LIBOR resets occur quarterly. A payment based on any
in-the-money LIBOR setting occurs three months after the setting. That

<!-- Page 159 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
Figure 4.1: Reset and Payment Conventions in Caps and Floors
is, the standard reset and payment conventions in a cap or ﬂoor concur
with conventions on the ﬂoating side of a swap, as shown in Figure 1.5 and
repeated here in Figure 4.1.
For example, consider an investor who purchases a 5-year spot starting
cap on 3-month LIBOR on June 23. He is purchasing a sequence of 19 or
20 diﬀerent options, depending on how we look at it. The settlement date
of the cap is T + 2 New York and London Business Days after the trade
date, which will be on June 25, assuming June 24 and 25 are both good
business days. The settlement date is the day on which the premium for the
cap will be paid by the buyer. It is also the day on which the ﬁrst LIBOR
reset will occur. Following the same convention as swaps, the LIBOR that
will be used on any quarterly reset date will be the rate that is set two
London Business Days prior. Thus, the ﬁrst LIBOR reset that occurs on
June 25 will actually use the rate setting two London Business Days prior
to that date, which is June 23, and therefore is known on the trade date.1
The ﬁrst payment (which may very well be zero) based on this setting will
occur three months thereafter on September 25. On September 25 we will
again observe 3-month LIBOR (with a two-day lookback), and based on this
setting, any in-the-money payment will be made three months thereafter.
1Since the ﬁrst reset in a spot starting cap is typically known at the time of trade,
some people view the ﬁrst reset and associated payment as not being an option, in which
case the 5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR is viewed as a series of 19 options. Typically, the
strike on a spot starting cap or ﬂoor is chosen such that the ﬁrst reset is out-of-the-money.
136

<!-- Page 160 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
We continue in a like manner each quarter, until the ﬁnal payment in the
5-year cap is made ﬁve years after the settlement date.
Each individual quarterly option in the cap is referred to as a caplet (sim-
ilarly, the individual options in a ﬂoor are known as ﬂoorlets). The payment
made each quarter by a cap (i.e., the caplet payment) is equal to
Notional × Max (3-month LIBOR −Strike, 0) × (Act/360)
(4.1)
and by a ﬂoor (i.e., the ﬂoorlet payment) is
Notional × Max (Strike −3-month LIBOR, 0) × (Act/360).
(4.2)
4.1.1
Cap-Floor Parity
The analog of put-call parity, cap-ﬂoor parity provides us with a means to
relate cap prices to ﬂoor prices. It is easy to verify that2
Max(LIBOR −Strike, 0) −Max(Strike −LIBOR, 0)
= LIBOR −Strike.
(4.3)
Let τ denote the day count fraction. Multiplying each side of Equation 4.3
by τ × Notional, we have
τ × Notional × max(LIBOR −Strike, 0)
−
τ × Notional × max(Strike −LIBOR, 0)
=
τ × Notional × (LIBOR −Strike).
(4.4)
This expression says the payout from a caplet less the payout from a
ﬂoorlet with the same strike is equal to the cash ﬂow in an individual pe-
riod resulting from paying ﬁxed in a swap (known as a swaplet). Here it is
understood that both the ﬁxed and ﬂoating side of the swap have the same
day count and payment frequencies as the caps and ﬂoors in question. The
relationship in Equation 4.4 is true for each and every caplet, ﬂoorlet, and
swaplet in the trade. Thus the payouts from a cap less the payouts from a
ﬂoor are the same as payouts obtained from paying ﬁxed in a swap. From
this we conclude that cap-ﬂoor parity must hold:
Value of Cap −Value of Floor = Value of Paying Fixed in Swap.
(4.5)
2To show that this is true, suppose ﬁrst that LIBOR ≥Strike. Then the left-hand side
of Equation 4.3 is equal to LIBOR −Strike, which is of course equal to the right-hand
side. Now suppose that LIBOR < Strike. Then the left-hand side is equal to −(Strike −
LIBOR) = LIBOR −Strike, which is again equal to the right-hand side.
137

<!-- Page 161 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
That is, the value of a cap less the value of a ﬂoor with the same strike is
equal to the value of a swap in which we are paying ﬁxed at a rate equal to the
strike. Once again, it is assumed in this case that both the ﬁxed side and the
ﬂoating side of the swap are quoted using the same day count and frequency
as the cap and ﬂoor. For example, if we are talking about caps and ﬂoors on
3-month LIBOR that pay quarterly money, then it is assumed that the swap
pays quarterly money versus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money as well.
Cap-ﬂoor parity tells us that being long a cap and short a ﬂoor with the
same strike is the same thing as paying ﬁxed in a swap at a ﬁxed rate equal
to the strike. We can use the parity relationship in Equation 4.5 to express
the relationship between caps, ﬂoors, and swaps in other equivalent ways.
For example, “multiplying Equation 4.5 by −1” tells us that being short a
cap and long a ﬂoor is the same as receiving in a swap.3
Consider now a cap and a ﬂoor on 3-month LIBOR with the same ma-
turity and strike. Suppose the strike is such that the cap and the ﬂoor have
the same value today. What is the signiﬁcance of this strike? From cap-ﬂoor
parity we know that being long a cap and short a ﬂoor on 3-month LIBOR
is equivalent to paying ﬁxed in a swap, where the ﬁxed rate is quoted quar-
terly money. Since the cap and the ﬂoor have the same value, this means
by cap-ﬂoor parity that the swap also has zero value. Thus, this particular
strike must be the par quarterly money swap rate on a swap of the given
maturity. To put it another way, it is the quarterly money ﬁxed rate in the
swap such that the swap is an NPV = 0 transaction, which is by deﬁnition
the par swap rate.
4.1.2
Uses of Caps and Floors
The buyer of a cap or ﬂoor is protecting against an adverse move in the un-
derlying rate. The buyer of a cap is protecting against rates moving higher,
whereas a ﬂoor buyer is protecting against rates moving lower. It is impor-
tant to remember that caps and ﬂoors are actually series of options, so the
protection extends over multiple periods. Here are some traditional uses of
caps and ﬂoors:
• A bank may have ﬂoating liabilities based on 3-month LIBOR. In order
to protect itself from an increase in rates, the bank may purchase caps.4
The bank may partially oﬀset the cost of the caps by selling lower strike
3Yet another perturbation of this relationship tells us that being long a cap and
receiving ﬁxed in a swap is the same thing as being long a ﬂoor.
4FAS (Financial Accounting Standards) 133, an accounting standard released in 1998,
made it less attractive for banks to purchase caps, as doing so would potentially add to
earnings volatility.
138

<!-- Page 162 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
ﬂoors. Such a position that is long caps and short ﬂoors is known as a
collar.
• A mortgage servicer is responsible for collecting monthly mortgage pay-
ments from borrowers, handling any escrow account associated with
the loan, sending out late notices when necessary, and so on.5 That is,
a mortgage servicer is responsible for administering the loan once it
has been originated. In compensation for performing these duties, the
mortgage servicer is typically given a piece of the monthly mortgage
coupon, say, 25 bps, that is earned on the outstanding mortgage bal-
ance. As the mortgage balance decreases, the payments received by the
servicer proportionately decrease. In the event of prepayment, the fee
arising from that mortgage that is earned by the servicer completely
disappears.
Servicing is a valuable asset. Most large originators of mortgages
retain their mortgage servicing rights but hedge the asset distinctly
from their other assets. Servicers face a tremendous amount of inter-
est rate risk. If the market rallies after the servicing rights have been
purchased, borrowers will have an increased incentive to prepay, and
the outstanding balance on which the servicer earns his 25 bps may
decline dramatically.6 In order to protect against this risk, servicers
have sometimes purchased ﬂoors, which increase in value as the mar-
ket rallies.7 Although servicers sometimes purchase ﬂoors on 3-month
LIBOR, they have more traditionally purchased ﬂoors on either 10-
year CMS or 10-year CMT. The prepayment of mortgages tends to
be driven more by the 10-year part of the yield curve rather than on
the front end, making ﬂoors on these indexes more appropriate hedges.
Swaptions (which we will look at in detail in Chapter 5) are also used,
as their liquidity allows for easier rebalancing and monetization.
• Consider a portfolio manager who purchases $100mm of a 5-year LI-
BOR capped ﬂoater, a bond that pays quarterly coupons equal to
3-month LIBOR + 100 bps, capped at 9%. That is, each quarter LIBOR
is set, and based upon this setting, the bond pays out a coupon equal to
min(3-month LIBOR + 100 bps, 9%).
If the portfolio manager is concerned about an increase in rates, he
may purchase a cap on 3-month LIBOR. Note that the coupon cap on
5For a quick review on mortgages, see Appendix D.
6For those who know a bit about mortgages, servicing rights are an asset that have
similar economic characteristics to mortgage IO (interest only) strips.
7In recent years, many servicers have been less inclined to hedge with ﬂoors and more
inclined to hedge with other instruments, including swaps.
139

<!-- Page 163 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
this bond, the maximum coupon that is obtainable, is 9%. There is a
short option position embedded in this bond that prevents the coupon
from increasing beyond a certain coupon level. This option comes into
the money when 3-month LIBOR is high. That is, the embedded op-
tion is a cap on 3-month LIBOR. The eﬀective strike on the embedded
LIBOR cap is 9% −1% = 8%.8
The portfolio manager may instead decide to buy a cap corridor in
which he buys one cap struck at 8% and simultaneously sells another
struck at a higher strike, say, 10%. In this way, the portfolio manager
would raise the eﬀective coupon cap from 9% to 11% at a lower price
than uncapping the bond completely.
4.1.3
An Embedded Cap Trade
Sometimes options are embedded into swaps (a concept that we explore
in greater detail in Chapter 6), as the following example will demonstrate.
Consider the trade shown in Figure 4.2, in which the dealer receives a ﬁxed
rate of 1.339% semi bond and pays an amount equal to the minimum of
3-month LIBOR and 3% quarterly Act/360 to the client for ﬁve years on a
notional of $100mm.
Let’s take a closer look at what the dealer is paying in this trade. By
paying the minimum of LIBOR and 3% instead of just paying LIBOR, the
dealer is protected whenever LIBOR is above 3% on any reset date. That
is, if LIBOR exceeds 3% on any reset date, the dealer will only have to pay
3%. As we will see, it is as if the dealer is paying LIBOR and has purchased
a cap from the client struck at 3%.
8It is important to recognize that the strike on the LIBOR cap is 8%, and not 9%.
First of all, we need to get one thing out of the way: the underlying on this embedded cap
is 3-month LIBOR, not 3-month LIBOR + 100 bps. While one could certainly make the
argument that the underlying includes the index of 100 bps, industry convention dictates
the underlying is simply 3-month LIBOR. You wouldn’t walk up to an options trader and
ask for the price of a “3-month LIBOR + 100 bps cap.” People would look at you like you
had two heads.
With this established, we can ﬁgure out that the strike on the embedded LIBOR cap is
8% a couple of diﬀerent ways. The ﬁrst way is to just play around with some numbers: if
LIBOR sets at 7.99%, the coupon will be 8.99%; if LIBOR sets at 8%, the coupon will be
9%; but if LIBOR sets at 8.01%, the coupon will still be 9%. Thus, the embedded option
has a strike of 8%. Try the same thing starting with 8.99%, and you’ll see the coupon
stays at 9%, suggestive that the strike on the embedded option is not 9%.
Alternatively (and more formally), notice that
min(LIBOR + 100 bps, 9%) = LIBOR + 100 bps −max(LIBOR −8%, 0%).
Thus, we can equivalently look at the coupon on the bond as paying out 3-month LIBOR +
100 bps, less the payout of an 8% cap on 3-month LIBOR.
140

<!-- Page 164 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
Figure 4.2: Example of a Swap with an Embedded Cap
Notice, for example, that if LIBOR were to set at 3.01%, the dealer
would normally have to pay out 3.01% if he were just paying LIBOR. But
in this trade, the dealer will pay only min(3.01%, 3%) = 3%. We can view
this equivalently as the dealer paying out the LIBOR rate of 3.01% and
simultaneously getting the payoﬀfrom cap struck at 3%, which is max(3.01%
−3%,0) = 1 bp. This means the net payout made by the dealer is 3.01% −
1 bp = 3%. We can formalize this way of thinking by noticing that
min(LIBOR, 3%) = LIBOR −max(LIBOR −3%, 0),
(4.6)
accentuating the fact that the dealer is paying out 3-month LIBOR less
the payout from a cap on 3-month LIBOR struck at 3%.9 This trade can
equivalently be viewed as one in which the dealer is paying 3-month LIBOR
and is implicitly purchasing a 5-year cap struck at 3% from the client.
At the time at which the trade is executed, assume that the 5-year swap
rate is 1.647% and assume further that the cost of a $100mm 5-year cap on
3-month LIBOR struck at 3% is $1,470,000, or equivalently 147 bps of the
9Whenever we see a payout formula involving the minimum of two things, it’s a
pretty good bet that some sort of short option position has been embedded. Most of
our option pricing formulas involve determining the value today of a payoﬀthat involves
the maximum of two things. The trick is to ﬁgure out how to convert a payoﬀthat
depends on the minimum of two things into the maximum of two things, as we did in
Equation 4.6.
141

<!-- Page 165 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
notional.10 Whereas the dealer would be willing to receive 1.647% in a plain
vanilla 5-year swap, the ﬁxed rate in this trade must be changed in order
to reﬂect the fact that the dealer is implicitly buying the cap. Note that
the ﬁxed rate must be lowered: the dealer is buying the cap and, instead of
paying an upfront amount for it, will be “paying” for it by receiving a ﬁxed
rate that is below the 5-year oﬀer side swap rate.
By how much should the ﬁxed rate in this trade be lowered? The ﬁxed
rate should be lowered by an amount such that the present value of the oﬀ-
marketness of the rate is equal to the value of the cap today. To this end, put
PV
=
1.47%
n
=
5 × 2 = 10
i
=
1.647%/2 = 0.8235%
and we get PMT = 0.154%. This payment is a semiannual installment, and
we must multiply by 2 in order to annualize. Thus, the appropriate ﬁxed rate
in this trade is 1.647% −(2 × 0.154%) = 1.339%, as indicated in Figure 4.2.11
4.1.4
Valuing Caps and Floors
In order to value caps and ﬂoors, it is common to use an extension of the
Black-Scholes formula known as Black’s formula (also called Black’s model).
We need to keep a couple of caveats in mind along the way. First, recall that
a cap or a ﬂoor is a series or portfolio of individual options, called caplets or
ﬂoorlets, respectively. In order to value caps and ﬂoors we value each of the
individual options separately, and the value of the cap or ﬂoor is just the sum
of the values of the individual options. Second, the LIBOR reset for a caplet
or ﬂoorlet occurs at the beginning of a period, but the actual payment from
the option does not occur until the end of the period. This time lag between
option expiry and payment is something we may not have seen before. We
typically assume that the payment from an in-the-money option occurs at
expiry. This payment lag is incorporated in the valuation formula.
To price individual caplets or ﬂoorlets, consider a sequence of equally
spaced times t0, t1, t2, . . . , tn and let Δt ≡ti+1 −ti represent the amount of
time between LIBOR resets expressed in days. For example, if we are pricing
10In the market for caps and ﬂoors as well as the market for swaptions, it is more
common to express the upfront premium of an option in basis points of the notional,
rather than a dollar amount. If the dollar premium of the option is $1,470,000, then the
premium as a percentage of the notional is $1,470,000/$100,000,000 = 1.47% = 147 bps.
11It is important to note that the trade depicted in Figure 4.2 is an NPV = 0 transac-
tion. The ﬁxed rate in the trade has been adjusted from the par swap rate so as to exactly
oﬀset the value of the cap that the dealer is implicitly buying.
142

<!-- Page 166 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
caplets on 3-month LIBOR, then Δt is the number of days in a 3-month
period, and if we are pricing caplets on 1-year LIBOR in our stylized example,
then Δt is the number of days in one year, or 360. Deﬁne τ = Δt/360, the
day count fraction. The payoﬀof a single caplet at time ti+1 is equal to
τ × Notional × max [L(ti, ti+1) −X, 0] ,
(4.7)
where L(ti, ti+1) is the LIBOR that sets at time ti. Once again, note that the
LIBOR rate sets at time ti but any in-the-money caplet payment is made a
period later at time ti+1.
Denote by f(ti, ti+1) the current value of the forward LIBOR rate that
sets at time ti. We will obtain f(ti, ti+1) by bootstrapping today’s swap
curve. Under the usual Black-Scholes assumptions, including the assumption
that the forward LIBOR rate, whose initial value is f(ti, ti+1), is lognormally
distributed, we can apply Black’s formula for the value of a caplet that has
a LIBOR reset at time ti and pays oﬀat time ti+1:
τ × DF(0, ti+1) × [f(ti, ti+1)N(d1) −XN(d2)] ,
(4.8)
where
d1 = ln (f(ti, ti+1) /X) + σ2
ti × ti/2
σti × √ti
(4.9)
and
d2 = d1 −σti × √ti.
(4.10)
In Equation 4.8, DF(0, ti+1) is the discount factor for a payment received
at time ti+1—that is, DF(0, ti+1) is the number such that the present value
of $1 received at time ti+1 is equal to $1 ×DF(0, ti+1); σti is the volatility of
the forward LIBOR rate set at time ti; and N(·) is the cumulative normal
distribution function.12
The value of a ﬂoorlet is given by
τ × DF(0, ti+1) × [XN(−d2) −f(ti, ti+1)N(−d1)] ,
(4.11)
where d1 and d2 are as given in Equations 4.9 and 4.10, respectively. To
compute the price of a caplet or ﬂoorlet in dollars, we multiply Equation 4.8
or 4.11, respectively, by the notional of the option.
12What happened here? We started out by talking about how to value an option on
some future unknown LIBOR rate, L(ti, ti+1) in Equation 4.7, and we wind up with an
option pricing formula that involves the forward LIBOR rate. The intuition for this is as
follows: note that at time ti, the forward rate and the spot rate must converge to the same
thing. That is, at time ti, f(ti, ti+1) = L(ti, ti+1). Thus, we can think about transforming
the problem from valuing an option on some future LIBOR rate to instead an option on
the forward. We know the current value of the forward, f(ti, ti+1), from bootstrapping,
assume that the forward itself is lognormally distributed, and apply Black’s formula, which
is a model that was developed to price options on forwards.
143

<!-- Page 167 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
In order to price up the ﬁrst caplet or ﬂoorlet in a spot starting cap
or ﬂoor, respectively, we would not use Black’s formula. In this case, LI-
BOR has already set, and there is no uncertainty about the payoﬀat the
end of the period. In the case of an in-the-money setting, we simply dis-
count the known payment back to today using the appropriate zero coupon
rate.
4.1.5
Vol
The name of the game for dealers that make markets in caps and ﬂoors is
determining what vol assumptions to use when pricing these options.13 One
tends to see more disparate prices from dealers quoting markets on caps and
ﬂoors than in swaps. There are several explanations for this. The ﬁrst is
simply that the vol markets are less liquid: it is harder for dealers to hedge
vol trades, resulting in relatively wider bid/ask markets for options. Second,
the vol markets tend to be a lot less transparent. The information ﬂow for
options pricing tends to be much more limited than for swaps, resulting in
disparate pricing among dealers. Third, diﬀerent dealers may have bona ﬁde
diﬀerences in the way they model and hedge diﬀerent option trades. This
does not tend to be the case for standard caps and ﬂoors but does present
itself for even slightly complicated structures.
As we discussed in Section 4.1.4, a dealer who makes markets in caps and
ﬂoors may choose to make individual vol assumptions for each of the caplets
that he prices. The relationship between the time to expiry of an individual
caplet and the vol used to price that caplet is known as the base vol curve.
Another vol concept pertaining to caps and ﬂoors is average vol. Using
the relevant vols for each caplet or ﬂoorlet according to the base vol curve, we
obtain an upfront cost for the cap or ﬂoor. We could then ask the question:
given this upfront cost, if we constrained each of the individual vols used to
price up each caplet or ﬂoorlet to be the same, what single vol gives us the
same upfront cost? For a cap or ﬂoor of a given maturity, the answer to this
question is average vol. The relationship between the ﬁnal maturity of a cap
or ﬂoor and its average vol is known as the average vol curve.
Figure 4.3 demonstrates what the base vol and average vol curves typ-
ically look like. This base vol curve is formed by looking at the implied
volatilities of various caplets (or ﬂoorlets) of diﬀerent maturities. Given an
option price, the implied vol is that level of vol used in an option model such
as in Equations 4.8 or 4.11 that equates to the given price. Note that the
13In many markets, including the rates markets, the word “volatility” has become
abbreviated to just “vol.”
144

<!-- Page 168 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
Figure 4.3: Base and Average Vol Curves for Caps and Floors
typical shape of the base vol curve is such that it is upward sloping initially
and then tends to decline. That is, caplet volatilities initially tend to increase
with an increase in expiration but then eventually decline. The intuition for
this “humped” vol curve often suggested by those in industry is as follows:
initially, there is some volatility concerning the movement in interest rates.
Over longer periods of time, there is more uncertainty in the economy and
increased risks of possible shocks to the market, and so on, that add further
uncertainty surrounding potential future rates. That is, there is certainly
some volatility in rates that we should expect over the next month or two.
However, over a longer period of time, there may be the additional uncer-
tainty created, for example, by shifts in Fed policy and uncertain economic
conditions that manifests itself in higher implied vols. This would tend to
suggest that for longer periods of time, as more uncertainty exists about the
future, implied vols would tend to go up. But empirically we usually see that
implied vols tend to come down after some point, as depicted in Figure 4.3.
The usual explanation for this phenomenon is that interest rates tend to
be mean reverting, which means that they exhibit the tendency to trend to
some long-run mean. Regardless of these intuitive explanations for the shape
of the base vol curve for caps and ﬂoors, ultimately supply and demand de-

Table 4.1: Composite Page of Implied Cap/Floor Vols. Used by Permission

termines the levels at which caps and ﬂoors trade and thus at which implied
vols trade.14
Table 4.1 shows a sample composite page of indicative implied vols for
caps and ﬂoors based on levels from a number of dealers. There are no
upfront premia displayed on this page; only implied vols are provided. The
ﬁrst set of numbers labeled “Spot Volatility” is the average vol numbers for
caps and ﬂoors of various maturities. For example, the market for 5-year
caps and ﬂoors on 3-month LIBOR is 49.5/50.5. This means that the most
aggressive buyer of 5-year caps and ﬂoors among dealers will use an average
vol of 49.5% to price up the options; all other dealers will use a lower average
vol and thus bid the options at lower prices.15 The most aggressive seller of
5-year caps among dealers will use an average vol of 50.5% to price up caps
and ﬂoors; all other dealers will use a higher average vol and thus will oﬀer
caps and ﬂoors at higher prices. Note that each dealer may well be using
individual base vols to price up each of the 20 caplets in a 5-year cap. But
it would be terribly inconvenient to talk about each of those 20 vols when
comparing prices, so it is all rolled up into one number: average vol.
It is important to note that these indicative markets on this composite
page only represent markets for caps and ﬂoors that are struck at-the-money.
14Although options in the rates market trade on the basis of price, it has become
commonplace to talk about where options trade on the basis of implied vol.
15Both caps and ﬂoors have positive vegas, meaning their cost increases with an increase
in volatility.
146

<!-- Page 170 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
The at-the-money strike for a cap or a ﬂoor is deﬁned to be the mid-market
quarterly money par swap rate for the given maturity, which we know from
cap-ﬂoor parity is the strike for which a cap and ﬂoor have the same value.
The column labeled “ATM” under the “Spot Volatility” heading stands for
at-the-money and provides this par swap rate. Thus, we see that the 5-year
quarterly money swap rate is 1.62%.16 For options that have strikes that are
not at-the-money, the implied vols obtained from the prices of these options
may be diﬀerent from the implied vols obtained from the prices of at-the-
moneys, as we will discuss in Chapter 5.
A forward starting cap or ﬂoor, by analogy to a forward starting swap,
is a cap or ﬂoor that is executed today but has its ﬁrst reset beyond the
standard T + 2 New York and London Business Days. It is common for
dealers to make markets and trade in a variety of forward starting caps and
ﬂoors as well. If dealers were to have large discrepancies among cap and ﬂoor
prices, this most likely means that they are not agreeing on the volatility
assumptions used to price the trades. Since each dealer is using a whole series
of base vols to price the options, these discrepancies may be more prevalent
among certain caplets and ﬂoorlets than others.
For example, suppose two counterparties are pricing a spot starting 5-
year cap. Their respective prices indicate that they are disagreeing on the
average vol input. The two counterparties compare their prices for spot start-
ing 3-year caps and ﬁnd that their prices for these structures are actually
very similar. The pricing discrepancy must therefore be in the eight caplets
constituting the 2-year cap, three years forward, also known as the 3x5 (pro-
nounced “3 by 5”) cap. The second column of numbers on the composite
page labeled “Forward Volatility” gives average vol markets for a variety of
forward starting caps and ﬂoors. For instance, the average vol market for
3x5 caps is 37.4% on the bid side and 38.4% on the oﬀer side. These vols
are applicable only for the at-the-money strike, which is deﬁned to be the
quarterly money par swap rate for the corresponding forward starting swap.
The column labeled “ATM” under the “Forward Volatility” section provides
these at-the-money strikes.
4.1.6
Valuing Caps and Floors in Our Stylized Model
We can apply Black’s formula to price up caps and ﬂoors in the context of our
stylized model in which we have 1-year LIBOR that resets and pays annually

of 1.62% × 365/360 = 1.6425%, which is equivalent to 2 ×

1.646% semi bond. This is higher than the mid-market 5-year semi bond swap rate shown
in Table 1.1. The explanation for this is simple: the data from Table 1.1 is from August
11, 2010, whereas the data from Table 4.1 is from August 27, 2010.
147

<!-- Page 171 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
on a 30/360 basis. Referring to Equations 4.8 and 4.11, the underlying is the
1-year LIBOR forward that sets at time ti, and we have Δt = 1 year =
360 days and τ = 1. The value of a caplet that resets at time ti and pays oﬀ
at time ti+1 is
1
[1 + zc(ti+1)]ti+1 × [f(ti, ti+1)N(d1) −XN(d2)] ,
(4.12)
where
d1 = ln (f(ti, ti+1) /X) + σ2
ti × ti/2
σti × √ti
(4.13)
and
d2 = d1 −σti × √ti.
(4.14)
The value of a ﬂoorlet that resets at time ti and pays oﬀat time ti+1 is
1
[1 + zc(ti+1)]ti+1 × [XN(−d2) −f(ti, ti+1)N(−d1)] ,
(4.15)
with d1 and d2 as given in Equations 4.13 and 4.14, respectively. The quan-
tities
[f(ti, ti+1)N(d1) −XN(d2)]
and
[XN(−d2) −f(ti, ti+1)N(−d1)]
are often referred to as the forward premium for the caplet and the ﬂoorlet,
respectively. To determine the premium in dollars for caplets and ﬂoorlets,
we multiply Equations 4.12 and 4.15, respectively, by the notional.
Example 1
Let’s take a look now at pricing up a cap in our stylized model. Consider
a $100mm 4-year cap on 1-year LIBOR struck at 5.47%. Assume that the
swap curve is as in Table 4.2 and further assume the base vol curve is ﬂat
at 15%.
Figure 4.4 shows how we price up the cap. The ﬁrst thing to note is that
we do not use Black’s formula to value the spot starting caplet. There is no
option associated with this caplet; the LIBOR reset has already occurred.
Since the LIBOR set of 5% is below the strike, the value of the ﬁrst caplet
is zero. For each of the remaining caplets, we ﬁrst compute d1 and d2 as
per Equations 4.13 and 4.14, respectively, and then we compute the forward
premium. We use the appropriate zero coupon rate to compute the value of
the caplet, then multiply by the notional to get the value of the caplet in

Example 2
The value of a $100mm 4-year ﬂoor on 1-year LIBOR struck at 5.47% is
found very similarly as shown in Figure 4.5. The only thing to note here is
that the initial LIBOR setting of 5% is in-the-money. We calculate the ﬁrst
17When referring to the prices of caps and ﬂoors, it is most common to quote prices
in bps of the notional. Prices are often rounded to the tenth of a bp, or to quarter bp
increments. Thus, the price quoted for an option in bps most often does not exactly match
the dollar price of an option from a model. The rounding error is typically small and safely
ignored in industry. In the example we have considered here, the premium would likely
be quoted as 145.4 bps, or $1,454,000 in dollars.
149

<!-- Page 173 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
Figure 4.5: Pricing Up a 4-Year Floor on 1-Year LIBOR with a Strike of 5.47%
ﬂoorlet’s value by hand. Since the strike is 5.47% and the LIBOR setting
for the ﬁrst period is 5%, the ﬁrst ﬂoorlet will pay out 5.47% −5.00% = 47
bps. This amount will be received in one year. To compute the present value
of this amount simply multiply it by the discount factor formed from the
1-year zero coupon rate
[1 + zc(1)]1 × 0.47% =
1
1.05 × 0.47% = 0.448%
1
and then multiply by the notional to get the value of this initial ﬂoorlet in
dollars: $100mm × 0.00448 = $447, 619.
Notice that the value of the 4-year cap with a strike of 5.47% is the same
as the value of 4-year ﬂoor with a strike of 5.47%. This is not a coincidence.
From cap-ﬂoor parity, we know that the value of a cap less the value of a
ﬂoor with the same strike must be equal to the value of a swap whose ﬁxed
rate is equal to the strike. But since 5.47% is the 4-year swap rate, this means
that a swap with a ﬁxed rate of 5.47% is worth zero. Therefore, a 4-year cap
and a 4-year ﬂoor with a strike equal to the 4-year swap rate of 5.47% must
be worth the same.
2
4.1.7
Variations of Standard Caps and Floors
The market has developed numerous variations of standard caps and ﬂoors
that we have focused on thus far. Sometimes these have been created so as
to provide a lower cost alternative to a standard option. Other times these
150

<!-- Page 174 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
have been created in order to better ﬁt the view that an investor has and
wants to take, or to better hedge a risk that he faces.
The ﬁrst two trades that we will look at in this section, step-up strike and
periodic caps, are somewhat common variants. The latter two are extremely
rare. The purpose of discussing them is to demonstrate the ﬂexibility that
the OTC derivative markets have aﬀorded market participants.
Step-Up Strike Caps
As the name suggests, step-up strike caps are caps in which the strike is not
the same for each and every caplet. On the trade date, the counterparties
specify the strike for each of the caplets in the trade. For example, in our
stylized example, one might specify the strikes in a 4-year cap are 5%, 5.25%,
5.50%, and 5.75%. This is priced using a slight modiﬁcation to Black’s for-
mula in which the appropriate strike is used for each caplet. Step-up caps
are often used to decrease the cost of a cap.18 In exchange for the lower cost,
the buyer is giving up some protection the cap may provide if it had the
lower initial strike over its life. Floors with strikes that vary each ﬂoorlet
have also traded.
Periodic (Sticky) Caps and Floors
The easiest way to demonstrate the nature of periodic caps or ﬂoors is
to discuss an asset that has a periodic option embedded in it. Consider a
5-year bond that pays annual 30/360 coupons in accordance with the fol-
lowing formula:19
Year 1:
6%
Years 2–5:
1-year CMT + 100 bps, subject to the condition
that the coupon can be no more than 200 bps
greater than the coupon paid in the prior year.
Assume that 1-year CMT, the 1-year Constant Maturity Treasury, is reset
at the beginning of each of the last four years for purposes of computing the
coupon paid in years two through ﬁve, respectively.
A series of options have been embedded that constrain the coupons that
can be paid by the bond. For example, suppose that in one year 1-year CMT
18Step-up strike caps have typically been popular in a steep swap curve environment
in which forward LIBOR rates are relatively high, making the cost of caps more expensive
than in a ﬂatter curve environment, ceteris paribus.
19Those who are familiar with mortgage securities will note that this ﬁctitious bond is
reminiscent of an ARM, or adjustable rate mortgage.
151

<!-- Page 175 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
sets at 20%. The highest coupon that the bond can pay in year two is 8%,
not the fully indexed amount of 1-year CMT + 100 bps = 20% + 1% =
21%. These embedded options therefore constrain the coupons, which hurts
the bondholder. That is, the bondholder is short these options. Note that
these options constitute some sort of cap: they are in-the-money when 1-year
CMT goes above some level. Thus, the embedded options are a cap on 1-year
CMT.
Now let’s look at the strikes on each of the caplets in this trade. Standing
here today, can we say for certain what the strike on each caplet is going
to be? The answer is: it depends. It depends on what year of the trade we
are talking about. For example, in year two, which has the ﬁrst embedded
caplet, we can deﬁnitively determine what the strike on the caplet is. We
know that in year two the cap will kick in whenever 1-year CMT + 100 bps
> 6% + 2%, that is, whenever 1-year CMT is greater than 7%. But what
about in the subsequent years? Can we deﬁnitively say today what the strike
on the remaining caplets will be? The answer is no, because in each of the
subsequent years the answer depends on where 1-year CMT set previously.
For example, let’s look at the coupon paid in year three, which is of course
equal to 1-year CMT + 100 bps, subject to the condition that the coupon
can be no more than 200 bps greater than the coupon paid in the prior year.
Suppose that at the beginning of year two, 1-year CMT was 2%. Then the
coupon in year two was 3% according to the formula. Now let’s look at the
highest coupon that can be paid in the third year. Since the coupon paid
in year two is 3%, the highest coupon that can be paid in year three is 5%.
Thus, the caplet in year three kicks in whenever 1-year CMT + 100 bps >
3% + 2%, or when 1-year CMT is greater than 4%. Thus the strike on the
caplet in year three is 4% in this case.
But suppose now that at the beginning of year two 1-year CMT was 10%.
Then the coupon paid in year two was 8% (which is equal to 1-year CMT +
100 bps, subject to the condition that the coupon is no greater than 200 bps
more than the coupon paid in the prior year). Now let’s look at the highest
coupon that can be paid in the third year. Since the coupon paid in year two
is 8%, the highest coupon that can be paid in year three is 10%. Thus, the
caplet in year three kicks in whenever 1-year CMT + 100 bps > 8% + 2%,
or when 1-year CMT is greater than 9%. Thus the strike on the caplet in
year three is 9% in this case. These ﬁndings are summarized in Table 4.3.
Thus we cannot say deﬁnitively today what the strikes on the caplets
are. It depends on the path of 1-year CMT over the life of the trade. These
types of caps are known as sticky or periodic caps. The word “sticky” derives
from the nature of the coupons paid by this bond: they can go up, but only
so fast. If 1-year CMT were to go up dramatically each year, the coupons
152

<!-- Page 176 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
1-Year CMT
Coupon in
Highest Possible
Strike on Embedded
in Year 2
Year 2
Coupon in Year 3
Caplet in Year 3
2%
3%
5%
4%
10%
8%
10%
9%
Table 4.3: Strike on Embedded Caplet in Year 3 of Asset as a Function of
1-Year CMT Setting in Year 2
would go up but would be sticky from one year to the next, only able to go
up so much in any one year. Likewise, the embedded option has a strike that
is sticky as well.
Sticky caps and ﬂoors are exotic options whose pricing is beyond the
scope of this discussion. Their usage is somewhat limited these days, but
they have been very popular at times. Given their relatively complex nature,
it was not unusual to observe very disparate prices for these options among
dealers when they ﬁrst became popular in the 1990s.
Chooser Caps
A chooser cap is a cap in which the holder has the right to exercise a given
number of caplets over the life of the cap. On each reset date, the holder
will observe where 3-month LIBOR sets and will decide whether or not to
exercise the caplet, assuming that he has not already exercised the maximum
number of caplets allowable under the terms of the contract.20
As a speciﬁc example, consider a 5-year 50% chooser cap on 3-month LI-
BOR struck at 6%. The holder will have the right to exercise any 10 of the 20
caplets that make up the regular cap. The holder does not choose today but
waits until the reset on each caplet and, based on the reset, decides whether
or not he wants to select a payment from that caplet. Once the holder has
selected payment from any 10 caplets, the trade is over, regardless of the
stated 5-year ﬁnal maturity.
The motivation behind such a trade is as follows: in the course of normal
business cycles, rates will move up and down over time. As such, the pur-
20This is not an industry standard name. I would suggest that you not walk around
dropping the name “chooser cap” and expect that people will know what you are talking
about. In fact, I am only aware of a chooser cap trading once. Despite the infrequency
with which this product trades, it is a good example demonstrating the ﬂexibility that
exists in structuring OTC derivative trades.
153

<!-- Page 177 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
chaser of a cap may expect that some caplets will ﬁnish in-the-money, but
some will ﬁnish out-of-the-money. A chooser cap will be a cheaper alterna-
tive to a standard cap of the same maturity, providing selective protection
for certain periods of time over the life of the standard cap. The holder of
a 5-year chooser cap will still have protection against higher rates until the
same ﬁnal maturity as a regular 5-year cap oﬀers but will choose the exact
periods of time during which the protection will be invoked. If the cap buyer
believes that some of the caplets will ﬁnish out-of-the-money anyway, why
should he bother to pay for them all? The risk associated with buying a
chooser cap instead of a standard cap is that rates move above the strike
and stay above the strike, in which case the limited number of choices asso-
ciated with a chooser cap does not provide as much protection as a regular
cap. In this scenario, the buyer of a chooser cap will have paid a smaller
upfront premium but would not be able to get payments from each of the
in-the-money caplets.
Note that the holder of a chooser cap would never select a payment from
a caplet whose reset is out-of-the-money. He would forgo choosing such a
caplet with zero payoﬀin order to maintain a choice in a subsequent quarter,
leaving the possibility for a positive payout. But even if a particular caplet
is slightly in-the-money, it is not clear that the holder would elect to choose
it. For example, consider again our example of a 5-year 50% chooser struck
at 6%; suppose that on the ﬁrst reset date that a caplet is in-the-money
3-month LIBOR has set at 6.01%. Since the payoﬀfrom this caplet is so
small, perhaps the holder would rationally elect to forgo it, maintaining 10
choices for the future, rather than a small payoﬀin this current period and
only 9 choices remaining.
Pricing up chooser caps is pretty complicated. We won’t be able to give
the pricing full justice here, but let’s make a few observations. First, a chooser
cap must be worth less than a regular cap of the same ﬁnal maturity with
the same strike. The payout each quarter from the regular cap will always
be at least as much as the chooser cap—and may be more.
Now suppose that on the trade date we select any 10 of the 20 caplets in
the regular cap and compare the total price of these 10 caplets to the chooser
cap. The chooser cap must be worth at least as much as these 10 caplets
chosen at random. If the chooser cap were worth less than the 10 caplets
chosen at random, we could buy the chooser today and sell the 10 randomly
selected caplets, taking in money today. Then we could use the chooser to
select the exact 10 caplets that were selected at random, thereby perfectly
covering our short, which locks in zero cash ﬂows in the future. This would
be an arbitrage, and thus the chooser must be worth at least as much.
We can actually come up with a more meaningful lower bound for the
price of the chooser. Suppose now that we compare the value of the chooser
154

<!-- Page 178 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
cap to the 10 most expensive caplets when we price up the cap today.21 The
chooser cap must be worth at least as much as these 10 caplets even though
they are the most expensive. If the chooser cap were less, then we would
again buy the chooser and sell the 10 most expensive (which creates positive
cash ﬂow today), and we would use the chooser to cover our short perfectly,
resulting in zero cash ﬂows in the future, thus giving an arbitrage.22 In
other words, the argument that we just constructed comparing the value of
the chooser to any 10 caplets applies equally as well when those 10 caplets
happen to be the most expensive ones in the cap.
We therefore have the following relationship between the price of a 50%
5-year chooser cap and the regular cap:
10 most expensive caplets ≤50% chooser cap ≤regular cap.
What’s the diﬀerence between the 10 most expensive caplets and the chooser?
We can think of the chooser as enabling the owner to get the payoﬀfrom the
10 most expensive caplets but also giving him the right to switch out of any
or all of those 10 caplets, if over the course of time it becomes advantageous
to do so. Let’s generically refer to that option as a “switch option.” We could
then write the very loose relationship
50% chooser cap = 10 most expensive caplets + “switch option.”
As an example as to why this switch option may prove valuable, suppose
that at the outset of the trade the 10 most expensive caplets are the last 10.
However, assume that shortly after buying the chooser, the curve becomes
extremely inverted. It could be that the back 10 caplets are worth very little,
and the holder of the chooser may ﬁnd it advantageous to take a payoﬀfrom
any in-the-money caplets that come along.23
21By the way, which caplets in a 5-year cap will be the 10 most expensive? Typically,
they will be the last 10: in an upward-sloping curve environment, the ﬁrst ﬁve years of the
forward curve will often be upward sloping, and the combination of higher longer-dated
forward rates and longer times to expiry usually means that the longer-dated caplets are
worth more. It is, however, possible to construct examples in which the 10 most expensive
caplets are not the last 10. If, for instance, the curve is inverted and/or the base vol curve
is signiﬁcantly inverted (meaning vols for longer-dated caplets are signiﬁcantly below vols
for shorter-dated caplets), it may be possible that longer-dated caplets are worth less than
shorter-dated ones.
22In fact, we could do better with the chooser: if any of the 10 most expensive caplets
should expire out-of-the-money, we could forgo this choice and reserve it for one of the
other 10 caplets instead if one should ﬁnish in-the-money, thereby performing better than
the 10 most expensive caplets.
23As indicated, the actual pricing of a chooser cap is quite complicated. Even the
decision to choose a particular caplet for exercise is somewhat of an involved decision.
155

<!-- Page 179 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
Convertible Cap Trade
Let’s take a look now at the following trade, which, for lack of a better name,
we will refer to as a convertible cap trade.24 Consider a trade between a dealer
and a client in which the dealer sells a 5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR struck
at 7% to the client for some upfront premium. This cap is like a regular cap,
except that in one year from today, the dealer has the right to
1. Call the cap from the counterparty and
2. Enter into a 4-year swap with the client in which the dealer receives
6% ﬁxed and pays 3-month LIBOR.
Note that if the dealer calls the cap, then it must also enter into the swap. If
the dealer does not call the cap, then it can’t enter into the swap. The ﬁrst
question that we might ask is how does the premium for the convertible cap
compare to the premium on a regular 5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR struck
at 7%? The price of this convertible cap must be less, since the counterparty
is buying the cap, but is simultaneously selling an option (i.e., to call the
cap and enter into the swap) to the dealer.
The next question we might ask is, In what type of interest rate envi-
ronment will the dealer want to exercise its option and call the cap and
simultaneously enter into the swap? In order to address this question, let’s
ﬁrst suppose that in a year rates are really, really high, say, 20% for all swap
maturities. Then the cap will be worth a lot of money, so the dealer will be
inclined to call the cap away from the counterparty. But not so fast. If rates
are really high, then the dealer will not be so happy entering into a swap in
which it is receiving only 6%. So it is not necessarily so clear whether or not
the dealer will want to exercise its option in a high rate environment.
Now suppose that in a year rates are really, really low, like 2% across the
curve. Well, in this case it seems like a good idea for the dealer to exercise
its option in which case it will be in the swap receiving a high ﬁxed rate of
6%. But, again, not so fast. If the dealer exercises its option, it will also take
Suppose that at a given point in time the holder of a chooser option has n remaining
caplets that he can choose. On the next LIBOR reset date in the chooser cap, the holder
of the chooser cap will need to compare i) the payoﬀfrom the current caplet plus the
value of maintaining another n −1 choices to be deployed among the remaining caplets
and ii) forgoing a payment on the current caplet and maintaining n choices to be deployed
among the remaining caplets. The decision to choose the current caplet will be made if
the ﬁrst of these alternatives is worth more.
24This is also not an industry standard name by any means, nor is this trade at all
standard. In fact, I have only seen it trade once. It was on reverse inquiry, meaning the
terms of the trade were proposed by the client to the dealer, instead of by the dealer to
the client.
156

<!-- Page 180 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
delivery of the cap. But in a low interest rate environment, the cap is not
going to be worth a whole lot. Usually when we think about exercising a call
option and taking delivery of an underlying asset, we usually do so when the
underlying is worth a lot, not a little. So it’s not clear whether or not the
dealer will want to exercise its option in a low rate environment.
So what to do? It’s a little tough to ﬁgure out which way is up and which
way is down in this problem. That is, it’s not so clear in what type of rate
environment the dealer is going to want to exercise its option. Let’s break
this problem down a bit more. From the point of view of the dealer,
Convertible Cap = Short 5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR at 7%
+ Long option that expires in one year to get a
4-year 7% cap and simultaneously enter into a
6% receive ﬁxed 4-year swap.
Let’s focus now on this option that the dealer is long. The dealer has the
right, but not the obligation, to exercise the option and take delivery of a
4-year 7% cap; and if it does, it will have to enter into the 4-year swap in
which it is receiving ﬁxed at 6%. So this looks like a call option: the dealer
can exercise and will take delivery of the underlying cap and will “pay a
strike” that is equal to the swap it must enter into. Regarding this then as
a call option on the cap with a strike equal to the swap, the payoﬀof this
option at maturity (using slightly abusive notation) is equal to
Option Payoﬀat Maturity = max[Cap −Strike, 0]
= max [max(L −7%, 0) −Strike, 0]
= max [max(L −7%, 0) −(L −6%), 0]
= max [max(L −7%, 0) −(L −6%−1% +1%), 0]
= max [max(L −7%, 0) −(L −7%) −1%, 0]
= max [max(7% −L, 0) −1%, 0]
= max [Floor struck at 7% −annuity of 1%, 0] .
That is, the payoﬀof this option at maturity is equal to the maximum
of zero or the value of a ﬂoor struck at 7% less the value of a 1% annuity
running over the four years. Notice that we used a trick of both adding
and subtracting 1% in the fourth line above, then invoked cap-ﬂoor parity
in the sixth line. In this conﬁguration, it is at least easy to determine in
what type of interest rate environment the option will be exercised. The
157

<!-- Page 181 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S
value of the ﬂoor will increase as rates go down, whereas the value of a 1%
annuity won’t change too much when rates move, and thus it is clear that
this option will be exercised in a low interest rate environment. This option
is a compound option, as it gives the holder an option to buy a ﬂoor, which
is an option itself. Valuing this compound option itself is beyond the scope
of this discussion.25
25As mentioned, this is a trade that was proposed to a dealer on reverse inquiry. The
client wanted a price on the option back in 20 minutes. After 5 minutes, the group at
the dealer that was trying to price the option was still not even certain in what type of
interest rate environment the option would be exercised, much less the price of the option.
But one person in the group realized that this question could be answered by invoking
cap-ﬂoor parity, as indicated above.
158

<!-- Page 182 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S

1. A client of yours is interested in purchasing a cap on 1-year LIBOR, struck
at 6.50%, with a notional of $100mm. The maturity of the cap is ﬁve years.
a) What is the value of the cap (expressed in both dollars and bps of the
notional)?
b) Use the value that you computed in part a) to determine the average vol
of the cap. That is, if you constrain each of the base vols to be the same
number, what vol level provides a value for the cap that is equal to the value
in part a)?
c) What is the value of a 5-year ﬂoor on 1-year LIBOR also struck at 6.50%?
In order to price the ﬂoor, use the average vol from part b). Be sure to
include the ﬂoorlet that resets today and pays in one year. There is no op-
tionality to this ﬂoorlet, but there is a known payment that is to be included.

average vol used to price a cap or a ﬂoor should be increased or decreased by
one percentage point, depending on whether or not she is buying or selling
the option. What is the strike on the ﬂoor in a costless collar, taking into

you/your trader (proﬁtability here means the diﬀerence in value between an
option priced using a bid or oﬀered side average vol and using a mid-market

2. A client is interested in paying you ﬁxed in a $100mm 5-year swap and

What is the lowest swap rate you are willing to receive if you pay LIBOR
capped at 8%? That is, what ﬁxed rate makes this an NPV = 0 transaction?

You work for an insurance company, and your boss has asked you to work
up a few quotes for him, based on the prices made available by Swaps ’R Us.
a) What ﬁxed rate will Swaps ’R Us be willing to pay in a 4-year swap if
the insurance company pays 1-year LIBOR −100 bps?26
26The astute reader will note that the quantity 1-year LIBOR −100 bps received by
Swaps ‘R Us would be a negative amount if 1-year LIBOR were to ever set below 1%. We
do not need to worry about embedding some type of option in order to avoid the coupons
from being negative should this ever occur. The Negative Interest Rate Method deﬁned
by ISDA applies to cases in which a ﬂoating payment in a swap is a negative quantity.
Under this method, a counterparty entitled to receive a ﬂoating payment that happens
to be a negative quantity would actually make a payment equal to the absolute value of
the negative amount. That is, in cases in which the quantity 1-year LIBOR −100 bps
is negative, the insurance company would actually receive the absolute value of 1-year
LIBOR −100 bps in addition to the ﬁxed rate it receives. In other words, the insurance
company would actually be receiving twice! Instead of utilizing the Negative Interest Rate
Method, counterparties can alternatively agree to employ the Zero Interest Rate Method.
Under this alternative, a ﬂoating payment in a swap is deemed to be zero when it would
otherwise be negative. Again, we can feel free to ignore this issue for our purposes here.
164

<!-- Page 188 -->
CA P S A N D F L O O R S

and 6%?

165

<!-- Page 189 -->
Chapter 5
Swaptions
The second type of option frequently traded in the swaps market is swap-
tions. Swaptions are options on swaps, giving the holder the right, but not
the obligation, to enter into a swap. We will discuss how swaptions are used
by investors and how they are priced using Black’s formula. We will discuss
some of the shortcomings of Black’s formula for the pricing of swaptions
(and for the pricing of other interest rate options, for that matter) and in-
troduce an alternative known as the normal model, which is now widely used
throughout the industry. We will also discuss Bermudan swaptions.
5.1
An Introduction to Swaptions
A swaption is an option to enter into a swap. There are two categories of
swaptions. A receiver swaption (sometimes called a call swaption) gives the
holder the right to receive ﬁxed (and pay ﬂoating) in a swap. A payer swap-
tion (sometimes called a put swaption) gives the holder the right to pay ﬁxed
(and receive ﬂoating) in a swap. The words “receiver” and “payer” therefore
refer to the position of the option holder as it pertains to the ﬁxed leg of the
underlying swap in the event of exercise.
The exercise of swaptions can either be single European, often simply
called European (exercisable on only one date); Bermudan or multi-European
(exercisable on a sequence of discrete dates); or American (exercisable any-
time during a speciﬁed period of time). In this section, we will consider only
European swaptions. Upon exercise, swaptions can be settled in one of two

<!-- Page 190 -->
S WA P T I O N S
ways. If a swaption is physically settled, then the two counterparties will
actually enter into the swap. If the swaption is cash settled, then instead
of actually entering into the swap, the value of the swap at the time of
exercise will be determined, and the seller will pay that amount to the
buyer.1
As an example, consider a “1 into 4” 7% receiver swaption, semi bond
versus 3-month LIBOR quarterly money. The holder of this option has the
right to enter into a 4-year swap, starting in one year.2 If the holder exercises,
then the counterparty who is long the option will receive ﬁxed at 7% semi
30/360 and will pay 3-month LIBOR quarterly Act/360. Upon exercise, the
ﬁrst ﬁxed payment will occur 1.5 years from today (i.e., six months after
expiration), and the ﬁnal ﬁxed payment will occur ﬁve years from today.
Upon exercise, the ﬁrst ﬂoating payment will occur 1.25 years from today
(i.e., three months after expiration), and the ﬁnal ﬂoating payment will occur
ﬁve years from today. Assuming this swaption is European, the holder has
the right to exercise in one year.3 If the swaption were Bermudan, then the
holder would have the right to exercise in one year and, say, every six months
thereafter.
In order to build some intuition, try answering the following two ques-
tions:
1The process of determining the value of the swap is in accordance with our discussion
of the marking-to-market of swaps in Chapter 3. The market standard is that swaptions
are cash settled. In this way, counterparties avoid credit exposure to one another (and the
need to post collateral to one another under the terms of a CSA) beyond the expiry of
the option. If swaptions are physically settled, then upon exercise counterparties continue
to have credit exposure to one another and may have to post collateral under the terms
of a CSA.
2The “n into m” swaption-speak is easily confused with the “n by m” cap/ﬂoor-speak.
When speaking of swaptions, an n into m swaption refers to an option that expires in n
years and is exercisable into a swap that is m years long. Alternatively, an n by m (also
written n × m) cap refers to a cap whose ﬁrst reset is in n years and whose ﬁnal payment
is in m years from today.
For example, consider both a 5 into 10 swaption and a 5 by 10 cap. The 5 into 10 swap-
tion is an option that expires 5 years from today. Upon exercise, the two counterparties
will enter into a 10-year swap (which therefore has a ﬁnal maturity that is 15 years from
today). On the other hand, the 5x10 cap has its ﬁrst reset in 5 years and its ﬁnal payment
in 10 years.
3The standard in the market is that the buyer will provide the seller two New York
and London Business Days notiﬁcation prior to the eﬀective date of the underlying swap
if he wants to exercise the swaption. For example, suppose that a counterparty purchased
a swaption on Tuesday, August 3, 2004. Recall that a spot starting swap would have an
eﬀective date T + 2 New York and London Business Days after the trade date, which in
this case would be August 5, 2004. If a counterparty were to purchase a 1 into 4 receiver
swaption, the eﬀective date on the underlying swap would be Friday, August 5, 2005, and
the buyer would be required to notify the seller of its intention to exercise the swaption
on Wednesday, August 3, 2005.
167

<!-- Page 191 -->
S WA P T I O N S
• Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% receiver or a 1 into 4 8% receiver?
• Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% payer or a 1 into 4 8% payer?4
Now try these two:
• Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% receiver or a 2 into 3 7% receiver?
• Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% payer or a 2 into 3 7% payer?5
5.1.1
The Value of Swaptions at Expiration
The value at expiration of a European receiver swaption, which gives the
holder the right to receive ﬁxed in the underlying swap, is given by
max(Bﬁxed −Bﬂoating, 0),
(5.1)
where Bﬁxed is the value of the ﬁxed leg of the swap including the ﬁctitious
principal at expiry, and Bﬂoating is the value of the ﬂoating leg of the swap
including ﬁctitious principal at expiry.6 Recall that the value of the ﬂoating
side must be worth par on any reset date. The underlying swap is eﬀective
at the time of expiry and thus has its ﬁrst reset at expiry. Thus, the value
of the ﬂoating bond, Bﬂoating, is par at expiry. The strike of the swaption
determines the coupon of the ﬁctitious ﬁxed rate bond, and thus the value
of the ﬁxed rate bond, Bﬁxed, can be above, below, or equal to par at expiry.
From Equation 5.1, we see that the receiver swaption will be exercised
when Bﬁxed ≥Bﬂoating. This means that the receiver swaption will be exer-
cised whenever Bﬁxed at expiry is worth more than par. This is equivalent
to saying that the holder of a receiver swaption will exercise at expiry if the
strike is greater than the then-prevailing swap rate for the corresponding
maturity swap.7 For example, in the case of a 1 into 4 7% receiver swaption,
the swaption holder will exercise if the 4-year swap rate prevailing one year
from now is less than 7%. The payoﬀfrom the swaption at expiry will depend
4The receiver struck at 8% must be worth more than an otherwise identical receiver
struck at 7% since it is always better to receive more than less. Likewise, the 7% payer
is worth more than the otherwise identical 8% payer since it is always better to pay less
than more.
5The problem set at the end of this chapter will give you an opportunity to consider
these two further.
6Of course, as we discussed in Chapter 3, the two counterparties do not literally
exchange the ﬁctitious principal at the maturity of the swap, but it does not change the
value of the swap if we assume they do, and it is often convenient to assume so.
7At expiry, the then-prevailing swap rate will deﬁne a ﬁctitious bond that is worth
exactly par. Thus, any bond with a coupon greater than the then-prevailing swap rate
will be worth more than par.
168

<!-- Page 192 -->
S WA P T I O N S
on where 4-year swap rates are in one year and how that compares to the
strike of 7%.
Similarly, for a payer swaption, which gives the holder the right to pay
ﬁxed in the underlying swap, the value at expiry is given by
max(Bﬂoating −Bﬁxed, 0).
In the case of a payer swaption, the holder will exercise if the strike is less
than the then-prevailing swap rate for the corresponding maturity swap. For
example, in the case of a 1 into 4 7% payer swaption, the holder will exercise
if the 4-year swap rate prevailing one year from now is greater than 7%. A
receiver swaption is therefore a bullish instrument, increasing in value as the
market rallies, whereas a payer swaption is a bearish instrument, increasing
in value as the market sells oﬀ.
5.1.2
Swaption Parity
Notice that the following equation is true:8
max(Bﬁxed −Bﬂoating, 0) −max(Bﬂoating −Bﬁxed, 0) = Bﬁxed −Bﬂoating.
In words, this means that, at expiration,
Value of Receivers −Value of Payers = Value of Receiving Fixed
in Underlying Swap,
and since the payoﬀs from these positions hold with equality at expiration,
the value of the positions today must be equal as well:
Value of Receiver −Value of Payer
= Value Today of Receiving Fixed in
Underlying Forward Starting Swap
(5.2)
Equation 5.2 is referred to as swaption parity. It applies to a payer and
receiver with the same expiry, the same strike, and the same underlying for-
ward starting swap. It says that the value of the receiver less the value of the
payer must be equal to the value of receiving ﬁxed in the underlying forward
swap that has a ﬁxed coupon equal to the strike. For example, the value
8This equation says that max(A −B, 0) −max(B −A, 0) = A −B. To show that this
is true, consider two cases. First, suppose that A ≥B. Then max(A −B, 0) = A −B,
and max(B −A, 0) = 0, which means max(A −B, 0) −max(B −A, 0) = A −B. On the
other hand, if A < B, then max(A −B, 0) = 0, and max(B −A, 0) = B −A, which means
max(A −B, 0) −max(B −A, 0) = −(B −A) = A −B.
169

<!-- Page 193 -->
S WA P T I O N S
today of a 1 into 4 7% receiver less the value today of a 1 into 4 7% payer
must be equal to the value today of receiving 7% ﬁxed in a 4-year swap,
one year forward. Suppose that we were to solve for the strike such that the
receiver and the payer have the same value today. What is the signiﬁcance
of this strike?9
5.1.3
Uses of Swaptions
There are many ways in which investors use swaptions. Here are just a few:
• A manager of a mortgage portfolio may be concerned about a rally in
10-year swap rates over the next year that would trigger prepayments
and cause the manager to reinvest at lower prevailing rates. The man-
ager may buy 1 into 10 receiver swaptions to hedge against this risk.
• A hedge fund believes that 5 into 10 swaption vol is going to go down
in the near future. The hedge fund may sell some 5 into 10 swaption
straddles. If, after a short time, implied vol on 5 into 10 swaption
straddles declines, the hedge fund will be able to buy the straddles
back at a lower price than which it sold them, recognizing a proﬁt.
• Suppose that a corporation has a $100mm ﬂoating rate liability over
the next ﬁve years equal to 3-month LIBOR + 100 bps. The ﬁrst
quarterly setting on the liability is today, and LIBOR will be reset
each quarter until the liability matures in ﬁve years.
Assume that the current market environment is as in Table 1.1.
Also assume that today’s 3-month LIBOR setting is 0.38428%, and
as such the ﬁrst coupon due to be paid by the corporation in three
months is 0.38428% + 100 bps = 1.38428%. Also note that today’s 5-
year (mid-market) swap rate is 1.627%. It can be shown that the 5-year
swap rate on a quarterly money basis is approximately 1.601%, using
Equations 1.8 and 1.9. Suppose further that the price of a 3-month
into 4.75 year receiver swaption struck at 1.83% quarterly money is
123.1 bps upfront.
The corporation is concerned about an increase in rates, which
would raise its funding costs. Consider the following trade proposed to
the corporation by a dealer:
9This strike must be equal to the par forward starting swap rate. Recall that the par
swap rate in a forward starting swap is that rate that gives the swap zero value. If the
payer and the receiver have the same value, swaption parity tells us that the underlying
forward starting swap must have zero value, which means that the ﬁxed rate in the swap
is equal to the par forward starting swap rate.
170

<!-- Page 194 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.1: Swaption Embedded in Funding Trade
1. The dealer will pay 3-month LIBOR + 100 bps to the corporation
for ﬁve years.
2. The corporation will pay 3-month LIBOR + 75 bps to the dealer
for ﬁve years.
3. The dealer has the one-time right in three months to convert the
cash ﬂows paid by the corporation to 2.58% ﬁxed quarterly money
for the remaining 4.75 years of the trade. If this right is not exercised
by the dealer, then the corporation will continue to pay 3-month
LIBOR + 75 bps for the remaining 4.75 years of the trade.
What’s going on here? Refer to Figure 5.1, which depicts the ﬂoat-
ing liability of the corporation in conjunction with the trade propped
by the dealer. If the swap dealer were to pay 3-month LIBOR + 100 bps
to the corporation in return for 3-month LIBOR + 75 bps, it would lose
25 bps running over the ﬁve years. Using a proprietary swap model,
the present value of 25 bps running over the ﬁve years on $100mm
notional is $1,231,000 bps, or 123.1 bps upfront.
This loss is oﬀset by the third leg of the trade outlined above.
The one-time right that the dealer has to convert the cash ﬂows paid
by the corporation to 2.58% quarterly money is actually a receiver
swaption. Under the terms of the trade, the corporation will be pay-
ing 3-month LIBOR + 75 for ﬁve years. However, in the third leg of
the trade the dealer is essentially buying $100mm of a 3-month into
4.75-year receiver swaption, which gives the dealer the right to re-
ceive 2.58% versus 3-month LIBOR + 75. But receiver swaptions (and
payer swaptions) are typically quoted such that the underlying swap
171

<!-- Page 195 -->
S WA P T I O N S
pays LIBOR ﬂat. That’s not a problem however, because the value of
this receiver is the same as a 3-month into 4.75-year receiver struck
at 2.58% −75 bps = 1.83% versus 3-month LIBOR ﬂat (if these two
receiver swaptions did not have the same value, there would be an ar-
bitrage).10 As mentioned above, the value of this receiver is 123.1 bps
upfront, which oﬀsets the loss the dealer locks in by paying away the
25 bp annuity.
Is this a good trade for the corporation? As stated, the corporation
believes that rates are going to rise. It has sold a receiver swaption,
which is an option that comes into the money as rates fall. If the
corporation is correct and rates rise, the corporation will be paying
3-month LIBOR + 75, saving 25 bps running versus the LIBOR + 100
it would otherwise pay on its liability. However, if rates fall enough,
the dealer will exercise the swaption, and the corporation will wind up
paying 2.58% for the remaining 4.75 years of the trade. In the event
the dealer exercises its receiver swaption, the corporation may well be
paying an above-market ﬁxed coupon, depending upon how much the
market rallies. But even in this case, the corporation is doing better
than if it had initially swapped its ﬂoating rate liability to ﬁxed at a
quarterly money rate of approximately 1.601% + 100 bps = 2.601%.11
5.1.4
Valuing Swaptions Using Black’s Formula
Consider a T into n payer swaption struck at X, the right to pay a ﬁxed rate
X and receive LIBOR in a swap that lasts n years, starting in T years. As-
sume, for simplicity, that each side of the swap pays m times per year. Black’s
formula is often used to value European swaptions. It is assumed that the
underlying forward swap rate (i.e., the n-year swap rate, T years forward) is
10Suppose the receiver swaption with the 1.83% strike versus LIBOR ﬂat underlying
swap were worth more than the receiver with the 2.58% strike versus LIBOR + 75 un-
derlying. Then we would sell the former and buy the latter, pocketing money today. If at
expiry our short were exercised against us, we would exercise our swaption, which would
result in net zero cash ﬂows. If our short were not exercised against us, we are guaranteed
nonnegative cash ﬂows. This would be an arbitrage, and thus the latter must be worth
at least as much as the former. A similar argument shows that the former must be worth
at least as much as the latter. In general, if we have a swaption in which the underlying
swap has a spread to the LIBOR side, it is worth the same as a swaption in which we
adjust the ﬁxed rate accordingly and the ﬂoating side is LIBOR ﬂat.
11The corporation could receive 3-month LIBOR quarterly money for ﬁve years in
exchange for a ﬁxed rate of 1.627%, the 5-year swap rate semi bond. This rate equates
to a quarterly money rate of approximately 1.601%. If the corporation wanted to receive
3-month LIBOR + 100 bps quarterly money, the ﬁxed rate it would have to pay would
be 1.601% + 100 bps = 2.601% quarterly money.
172

<!-- Page 196 -->
S WA P T I O N S
lognormally distributed with an initial rate equal to F and volatility denoted
by σ. Denote by R the (uncertain) n-year swap rate that will be observed
at time T.12
The payer swaption will be exercised at time T if the n-year swap rate
at time T, R, is greater than the strike X. Of course, if exercised the cash
ﬂows in the underlying swap are

associated with the cash ﬂow that may be received at time ti due to exercise
of this option is given by

12Suppose that we are thinking of pricing up a 5-year into 10-year swaption. The
underlying swap rate is the 10-year swap rate, ﬁve years forward. We can bootstrap the
swap curve and compute this rate today and denote its initial value by F. Tomorrow
the underlying will be the 10-year swap rate, four years and 364 days forward (assuming
it’s not a leap year). We won’t know the value of this variable until tomorrow, when we
can bootstrap the curve and ﬁgure out what that forward rate is. The day after that the
underlying will be the 10-year swap rate, four years and 363 days forward, and so on. Each
and every day the forward rate is going to change (actually, the forward rate can change
at any moment in time). It is assumed that the distribution of this forward rate at expiry
in ﬁve years is lognormal with volatility σ. We can play this game each and every day,
but the one thing we do know is that the forward rate will have to converge to the spot
10-year swap rate in ﬁve years, an uncertain rate that we said we would denote by R.

5.4? This comes from our old
trick that we learned in Section 3.3.1: the value of paying X and receiving LIBOR in a
swap is the same as the sum of the value of (1) paying X and receiving LIBOR in a swap
and (2) receiving R and paying LIBOR in a swap, since the latter of these transactions
has zero value. The net position of (1) and (2) is a trade in which we are paying X and
receiving R.

5.1.5
Swaption Vol
The volatility used to price a particular swaption depends on it expiry, the
term of the underlying swap (also referred to as the tail), and the strike. If a
swaption has a strike equal to the forward swap rate of the underlying swap,
then the swaption is referred to as being struck at-the-money forward (often
abbreviated “ATMF”). For example, in Section 3.3.4, we solved for the 1x4
swap rate (i.e., a 3-year swap rate, one year forward) in our stylized example
and found a rate of 5.644%. Thus, a 1 into 3 swaption struck at 5.644% is
referred to as being struck at-the-money forward.
Implied swaption vol refers to the swaption vol that is computed from
a pricing model such as the one in Equation 5.9 or 5.10. That is, given an
option price, implied vol refers to the value of vol input into a pricing model
that results in the given option price. When we generically speak about
implied swaption vol, we refer to volatilities for swaptions that are struck
at-the-money forward. For the at-the-money forward strike, a swaption must

still be identiﬁed by specifying its expiry and its tail. It is typical to show
the implied volatilities of swaptions of various expiries and tails, all struck
at-the-money forward, in a table referred to as a vol grid or swaption matrix.
Table 5.1 shows an example of such a matrix. In this vol grid, the expiry
of the option is found along the ﬁrst column of the matrix, and the tail
(i.e., the maturity of the underlying swap) is found along the top row of the
matrix. For instance, 1 year into 10-year swaptions have an implied volatility
of 22.1%. It is important to note that this implied volatility applies only to
1 year into 10-year swaptions that are struck at-the-money forward. 1 year
into 10-year swaptions that have diﬀerent strikes may (and most often do)
trade at diﬀerent implied volatilities. Short-dated swaptions (considered by
many to be those with time until expiration of a year and under) are referred
to as gamma, whereas longer-dated options are referred to as vega.14
5.1.6
Pricing Swaptions in Our Stylized Example
We can apply Black’s formula to price swaptions in the context of our styl-
ized example in which we have ﬁxed annual bond versus 1-year LIBOR.
This amounts to setting m = 1 in Equation 5.9 and Equation 5.10 and
recognizing that the discount factors we need are formed from the zero
coupon rates obtained from bootstrapping the swap curve. Upon exercise,
the underlying swap has cash ﬂows one time per year for n years at times
T +1, T +2, . . . , T +n. In our stylized example, Black’s formula as per Equa-
tion 5.9 for the value of a T into n payer swaption struck at X reduces to

options tend to have low gamma and high vega, attributing to these choices of names.

the zero coupon rate for time t. The sum in Equation 5.14 represents the
annuity factor: that number which, when multipled by $1, gives the value
of a $1 annuity received at the end of each year in the underlying swap. In
order to determine the premium of a payer and receiver swaption in dollars,
we would multiply the expressions in Equations 5.11 and 5.16, respectively,
by the notional of the trade.
Example
Let’s consider our stylized example and price up a 1 into 3 payer swaption
struck at 5.644%. Assume that the swap curve is as in Table 5.2 and assume
1 into 3 implied vol is 20%. An input in the pricing of the swaption is the
calculation of the underlying forward swap rate. In Section 3.3.4 we showed
that the 1x4 swap rate (i.e., the 3-year swap rate, one year forward) was equal
to 5.644%. Thus, this 1 into 3 payer swaption is struck at-the-money forward,
and we have F = X = 5.644%. Figure 5.2 demonstrates how we price up
the payer swaption. The value of the swaption is around 115.5 bps. For a

Similarly, we can price up a 1 into 3 receiver swaption at the same strike of
15As is the case in the market for caps and ﬂoors, it is common in the swaption market
to round the upfront cost to either the nearest tenth of a basis point or quarter of a basis
point. The premium in dollars, obtained by multiplying the cost of the option in bps by
the notional, has been calculated to the nearest dollar. However, in practice the premium
is often given in some round amount. In this example, the premium might be rounded to
$1,155,000. The beneﬁt of talking about premia in terms of upfront bps rather than dollars
is that the upfront bps is invariant to a change in notional (assuming the aggressiveness
of the price does not vary due to a change in the size of the trade). For example, for a
notional of $200mm, the price of the swaption is still 115.5 bps upfront, or 115.5 bps ×
$200mm = $2,310,000, twice the dollar cost of a $100mm trade.
177
