5.644%, as demonstrated in Figure 5.3. Note that since both swaptions are
struck at-the-money forward, they have the same value as we know must be
the case from swaption parity.
2
5.2
The Link Between Caps/Floors and Swaptions
Suppose that an investor is interested in buying an option to protect against
rates going higher. Both caps and payer swaptions come to mind, but which
one is more appropriate depends on the needs of the investor.
The buyer of a cap is buying a series of options. For example, the buyer
of a 5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR is buying a series of 20 diﬀerent options.
Each option in the series provides protection against a move up in a distinct
setting of 3-month LIBOR. Whereas one LIBOR setting in the series may
be low, resulting in an out-of-the-money option one period, that does not
aﬀect the ability of another setting to be above the strike, resulting in an
in-the-money payout in another period.
The buyer of a payer swaption, on the other hand, is buying a single
option. For example, a 5 into 10 payer swaption will protect the buyer from
an increase in 10-year swap rates, ﬁve years from now. And whereas an in-
the-money setting on a caplet accrues for three months as per Equation 4.1,
178

<!-- Page 202 -->
S WA P T I O N S
an in-the-money setting on a swaption will result in payments over the entire
life of the underlying swap.16
The choice of which option is more appropriate depends on the needs of
the particular investor. If, for example, the investor manages a bank portfolio
that has a liability tied to 3-month LIBOR resetting quarterly over the next
ﬁve years, then it would seem more appropriate for the bank to purchase a
5-year cap on 3-month LIBOR instead of purchasing a swaption. If, however,
the investor manages a mortgage portfolio that has exposure to 10-year
swap rates increasing, say, ﬁve years from now, then it would appear more
appropriate for the investor to buy a 5 into 10 payer swaption rather than
a cap.17 So whereas caps and payer swaptions both oﬀer protection against
higher rates, their use varies in accordance with the needs of investors.
There is an intimate relationship between caps and payer swaptions and
also between ﬂoors and receiver swaptions. Consider now a 2x5 cap on 3-
month LIBOR (a forward starting cap) and a 2 into 3 payer swaption (with
the same strike, quoted quarterly money versus 3-month LIBOR). These two
trades are represented in Figure 5.4.
The 2x5 cap has its ﬁrst reset in two years and its ﬁnal payment in ﬁve
years. The trade is composed of 12 options, covering 12 3-month LIBOR
settings as indicated in the top half of this ﬁgure. By comparison, the 2
into 3 payer swaption is a single option that expires in two years and is
exercisable into a 3-year swap, as indicated in the bottom half of this ﬁgure.
The underlying in this swaption is the 3-year swap rate, two years forward.
If we think about how we price up forward starting swaps as we did in
Section 3.3.4, the underlying 3-year swap, two years forward is determined
by exactly the same 12 3-month LIBOR forwards as comprise the 2x5 cap.
So what’s the diﬀerence between a 2x5 cap and a 2 into 3 payer swaption?
We’ve already talked about the diﬀerent ways in which the two trades are
used by investors. From a pricing perspective, notice that the 2x5 cap must
be worth at least as much as the 2 into 3 payer swaption.18 The cap is a
16If the payer swaption is physically settled, then upon exercise the owner of the option
will pay a ﬁxed rate equal to the strike and receive LIBOR over the life of the swap. If
instead the swaption is cash settled, then instead of actually entering into this swap,
the owner of the option is paid the mark-to-market value of this swap (which we know
how to compute from Section 3.3.1). Although these two methods of exercise result in
dramatically diﬀerent cash ﬂows, their value at the point of exercise is identical.
17Actually, in this scenario the portfolio most likely has exposure to an increase in 10-
year swap rates over the next ﬁve years, not just exactly ﬁve years from now. An increase
in swap rates over the life of the swaption will serve to increase its value, thereby buﬀering
any ill eﬀects the increase has on the value of the mortgage portfolio itself.
18To show this is true, assume that the cap costs less than the payer. Then we could
buy the cap and sell the payer, taking in money today. Let’s consider two cases. First,
if in two years the payer is not exercised against us, then we will have nonnegative cash
179

<!-- Page 203 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.4: Representation of 2x5 Cap (top diagram) and a 2 into 3 Payer
Swaption (bottom diagram)
portfolio of options, and the payer swaption is an option on a portfolio of
forward rates. That is, the 2x5 cap is a portfolio of options on 12 diﬀerent
rates. In comparison, the 2 into 3 payer swaption is an option on a portfolio
of forward rates (i.e., the 12 3-month LIBOR forwards that comprise the
formulation of the underlying 3-year swap, two years forward). Thus caps
and payers are intimately linked. In fact, a popular relative value trade is to
buy one of these options versus selling the other, thereby taking a view on
the value of one of these options versus the other. When we get to Chapter 9,
we will return to this example to discuss another diﬀerence between the two
trades.
5.3
Questioning Black’s Model for Interest
Rate Options
As we have discussed, Black’s model, a simple extension of Black-Scholes, has
traditionally been a workhorse model for the pricing of a variety of interest
rate options. But what if Black-Scholes is wrong?19 Sacrilege? Perhaps. But
ﬂows in the future (and we may make some money as well if any of our caplets ever
set in-the-money). Second, suppose that in two years the payer is exercised against us.
Then for each of the next quarterly periods, we will be receiving ﬁxed at the strike and
paying 3-month LIBOR. That is, our cash ﬂow from the payer swaption being exercised
against us will be Strike −LIBOR. Of course, we are still long our cap, which will pay us
max(LIBOR−Strike, 0) each quarter. Thus, our net cash ﬂow from the cap and the payer is
Strike −LIBOR + max(LIBOR −Strike, 0),
which is nonnegative. Thus, we always have nonnegative cash ﬂows in the future, and
this would be an arbitrage. So it must be that the cap is worth no less than the payer.
19Inasmuch as Black’s model is a relatively straightforward extension of Black-Scholes,
people in industry often use the two names interchangeably. Reference to either Black’s
180

<!-- Page 204 -->
S WA P T I O N S
there is good reason to question whether or not Black-Scholes is a “good”
model to employ for the pricing of interest rate options. Actually, we will dis-
cuss three such reasons: the appropriateness (or rather, inappropriateness)
of the assumption that rates are lognormal, the (sometimes) surprising re-
lationship between swaption prices and implied volatilities when computed
using Black’s formula, and most important, the existence of skew.
With a signiﬁcant amount of doubt cast over the appropriateness of
Black’s model for the pricing of interest rate options, the question is, What
model for the pricing of such options is more appropriate? We will introduce
the concept of the normal model, in which rates are assumed to be normally
distributed as an alternative.
5.3.1
Are Interest Rates Lognormal?
Did you ever notice that stocks that have a relatively high price tend to have
greater daily price ﬂuctuations than stocks that are relatively low in price?
If you’ve ever looked at a stock with a really high price, say, in the $400
range, you will notice that the daily price ﬂuctuations in such a stock can be
much larger than those that have low prices, say, in the $10 range. A stock
that is in the $400 price range may have daily price ﬂuctuations as high as
$10 or $20, or even higher. Low price stocks, say, those in the $10 range,
don’t typically have that magnitude of daily price changes. Most $10 stocks
have daily price ﬂuctuations that are a fraction of a dollar.
The phenomenon that we just referred to is characteristic of asset prices
that follow a lognormal distribution.20 In Appendix A, we discuss that if an
asset price, ST , is lognormally distributed, we can write
2 σ2)T +σ
√
ST = Se(μ−1
T z,
(5.17)
where z is a standard normal random variable and S is today’s asset price.
The standard deviation of the asset price is given by
SeμT 
eσ2T −1

.
(5.18)
model or Black-Scholes implies a model in which the underlying forward rate is assumed
to be lognormally distributed and the volatility of the underlying is constant and does not
vary with strike. Black’s formula is the resultant equation to price options that follows
from these assumptions.
20Although the phenomenon that we just discussed is suggestive of the lognormality of
stock prices, strictly speaking this is a bastardization of the term lognormality as it refers
to the distribution of stock prices. To say that the distribution of a stock price is lognormal
is a statement of the characteristics of an individual stock price in and of itself. It is not a
statement comparing two diﬀerent stocks. Technically, the potential lognormality of stock
prices would be better demonstrated by looking at the distributional characteristics of a
single stock.
181

<!-- Page 205 -->
S WA P T I O N S
From Equation 5.18, we see that the standard deviation of the asset price
increases with the level of the asset price. That is, under the assumption
of lognormality an asset price has a higher standard deviation, the greater
the level of the asset price. When we are working with Black-Scholes, the
volatility parameter σ is a percentage; it is a measure of relative volatility,
meaning that the volatility of the underlying is relative to the level of the
underlying.
The distribution of swap rates, however, does not typically exhibit this
phenomenon. Figure 5.5 shows a graph of daily changes in 10-year swap
rates. The panel on the top samples daily changes during a period of time
when 10-year swap rates were relatively low, and the panel on the bottom
shows a sample when rates were relatively high. The distribution of daily
changes from one panel to the next does not look remarkably diﬀerent from
one another. This would tend to refute the notion that 10-year swap rates are
lognormally distributed. If 10-year swap rates were lognormally distributed,
we would tend to observe larger daily rate ﬂuctuations in a high-rate envi-
ronment than in a low-rate environment.21
An Alternative Distributional Assumption for Interest Rates: Normality
There is a well-known distribution, however, that exhibits the characteristic
that volatility does not depend on the level of rates. Under a normal distri-
bution, the volatility of swap rates has no dependency on the level of rates.
At the risk of getting ahead of ourselves, if a swap rate R to be observed at
time T is normally distributed, we can posit a model in which
R ∼N(μ, σ2
NT),
(5.19)
where μ and σ2
NT are the mean and variance of the distribution, respec-
tively.22 The parameter σN is referred to by various names, including nor-
mal vol or normalized vol or basis point vol or annualized basis point vol.
Note that in this formulation the standard deviation of the swap rate,
21We should caution that this analysis is the most casual of empirical studies. Our pur-
pose here is not to provide rigorous statistical tests crediting or discrediting the assumption
of lognormality for the distribution of swap rates. Rather, what we hope to accomplish
here is to show why those in the industry are often comfortable with the assumption of
lognormality for certain asset classes, such as stocks, but are much less comfortable making
this distributional assumption for interest rates.
22A random variable R is said to be normally distributed with mean μ and variance
σ2, denoted R ∼N(μ, σ2), if it has the probability density function
f(x) =
1
√
2πσ
e−(x−μ)2/2σ2.
See Ross (2009) for more details.
182

<!-- Page 206 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.5: Daily Changes in 10-Year Swap Rates in Both a Low-Rate (top
half ) and a High-Rate (bottom half ) Environment
σN
√
T, does not depend on the level of the swap rate. A model for pricing
interest rate options in which rates are assumed to be normally distributed
is referred to as the normal model. The implied volatility parameter σN ob-
tained from pricing options using the normal model is referred to as normal
implied volatility. We will look at this model extensively in Section 5.4.
Under the normal model, basis point volatility, σN, is assumed to be
a constant. In the normal model, σN is a measure of absolute volatility.
Whereas the parameter σ in Black’s model is usually expressed as a percent-
183

<!-- Page 207 -->
S WA P T I O N S
age (it is a measure of relative volatility), it is often useful to think of σN in
the normal model in terms of basis points. The empirical results depicted in
Figure 5.5 (in which the daily ﬂuctuations in swap rates do not depend on
the level of rates) appear to be more consistent with a normal model than a
lognormal model.
5.3.2
Swaption Prices and Implied Vol
A second unsettling feature of a lognormal model for the pricing of options
on rates can be seen in Figure 5.6. This ﬁgure shows a history of at-the-
money forward 1 into 10 swaption straddle prices and the associated history
of implied vols under two diﬀerent models. The top panel shows the history of
lognormal implied vols. That is, given the swaption straddle prices observed
in the market, and under the assumption that swap rates are lognormal,
implied volatilities are computed using Black’s model as in Equations 5.9
and 5.10. A disturbing feature of this picture is that there are times when
lognormal implied vols and swaption prices move inversely. For example, near
the very end of the time series, we see that although swaption straddle prices
were going up, lognormal implied vols are going down. This runs counter to
our usual intuition that option prices and implied vols move in the same
direction and is suggestive that maybe a lognormal model for interest rate
options does not explain everything.
By comparison, the bottom panel shows the same history of swaption
prices in conjunction with a history of normal implied vols. These normal
implied vols are computed from an option pricing model in which it is as-
sumed that swap rates are normally distributed as given by Equation 5.19.23
As this ﬁgure demonstrates, normal implied vols tend to move in the same
direction as swaption prices, which is intuitively pleasing and suggestive that
perhaps a normal model explains things that the lognormal model does not.
5.3.3
Skew
Suppose that we were to gather market prices on a bunch of diﬀerent swap-
tions on the same underlying swap rate and time to maturity, allowing only
strikes to diﬀer. Suppose further that we compute the implied volatility for
each of these swaptions, and graph the implied volatility as a function of the
strike price.24 Under the assumptions of Black’s model, this graph should be
23We will develop such an option pricing model, known as the normal model, in Sec-
tion 5.4.
24Once again, given the price of an option, the implied volatility is the volatility that
we plug into a pricing model to obtain the price of the option.
184

<!-- Page 208 -->
Figure 5.6: The Relationship Between Swaption Prices and Lognormal Im-
plied Vol (top half ) and Normal Implied Vol (bottom half )

<!-- Page 209 -->
S WA P T I O N S
a ﬂat line. That is, Black’s model says that the lognormal implied volatility
of an option should be a constant that does not vary with the level of the
strike.
But in reality this is often not the case. The pattern that is most often
observed in the swaption market is one in which low-strike options trade at a
lognormal implied vol above that of the at-the-money strike, and high-strike
options trade at a lognormal implied vol below that of the at-the-money
strike. Skew refers to the relationship between the implied vol of an option
and its strike as we allow the strike to vary. The pattern just described is
referred to generically as downward skew. Figure 5.7 demonstrates such a pat-
tern observed from computing lognormal implied vols from swaption prices
trading in the market. This ﬁgure is obtained by observing actual prices
that traded in the market, then using Black’s formula to compute lognor-
mal implied volatilities. A striking (no pun intended) feature of this ﬁgure is
that these lognormal implied vols exhibit downward skew. Yet Black’s model
Figure 5.7: Lognormal Implied Vol as a Function of Strike for 6 Month into
5-Year Swaptions on November 18, 2005
186

<!-- Page 210 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.8: Flat Skew of the Black (Lognormal) Model and Downward Skew
of the Normal Model
makes the assumption of ﬂat skew, meaning it is assumed that there is no
variation in implied vol for diﬀerent strikes.
The normal model, on the other hand, implies a skew pattern in which
low-strike options have lognormal implied vols (also called Black implied
vols) above that of the at-the-money strike, and high-strike options have
lognormal implied vols below that of the at-the-money strike.25 The skew
patterns of these competing theoretical models are demonstrated in Fig-
ure 5.8. If Black’s model is true, then of course lognormal implied vol is a
constant that does not vary with strike. This is depicted by the ﬂat line in
Figure 5.8. If, on the other hand, we assume that the normal model is true
(i.e., we assume that swap rates are normally distributed and that σN is
constant as we allow the strike to vary), the implication is that lognormal
implied vol from Black’s model must exhibit downward skew. This of course
is depicted by the sloped line in Figure 5.8. Notice that the theoretical pat-
tern of lognormal implied vols under the normal model in Figure 5.8 closely
resembles the actual pattern sometimes observed in the market as depicted
in Figure 5.7.
25We will examine the normal model closely and derive this relationship ourselves in
Section 5.4.5.
187

<!-- Page 211 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Unfortunately, neither of these models describes the world perfectly, and
sometimes actual skews observed in the market are more complicated than
either of these two models suggests. To complicate things further, the actual
skew patterns observed in the market have tended to change over the course
of time. The market has come up with colorful names to describe various
skew patterns that have been observed from time to time. For example,
two skew patterns that have existed at various times in the interest rate
options market are often referred to as supernormal and smirk. These two
skew patterns are depicted in Figure 5.9 with the skew pattern implied by
the normal model superimposed. When the volatility skew is supernormal,
lognormal implied volatilities lie everywhere above the levels implied by the
normal model. And when a smirk is observed in the market, volatilities for
very high-strike options are actually above vols of at-the-moneys.
The Importance of Understanding Skew
The understanding of skew dynamics is more than just of theoretical interest,
as the following example will illustrate.
Example
Consider again our stylized example, the swap curve in Table 5.2 and the
lognormal implied matrix in Table 5.1. Suppose that a dealer is asked to
bid (i.e., to buy) $1bn of 2 into 3 payer swaptions struck 50 out (i.e., the
strike is 50 bps above the at-the-money forward strike in the case of a payer
swaption) from a client. Assume that the trader uses Black’s model.
The ﬁrst step to pricing up this swaption is to compute the 2x5 swap
rate. As shown in the top half of Figure 5.10, we ﬁnd that the 2x5 swap
rate is 5.976%. Thus, the strike on the payer swaption is 6.476%. According
to the vol grid in Table 5.1, 2 into 3 vol for swaptions struck at-the-money
forward is 26.5%. Suppose that the trader either forgets or is unaware of the
existence of skew and uses this vol to put a bid on the swaption. As shown
in the bottom half of Figure 5.10, the price at which the dealer will bid the
option is 168.8 bps, or about $16,877,000 on a notional of $1bn. Assume
that soon after buying the swaption from the client, rates have not changed,
and the dealer goes out into the market to try to sell the option in order
to close out his position. Upon looking for bids in the market, he ﬁnds that
other market participants are pricing the option with skew. In particular,
the best bid that he can ﬁnd in the market is 26.25% lognormal implied vol.
In a calculation not shown here, this equates to a bid of 166.7 bps, or about
$16,672,000 on a notional of $1bn. Thus, by not incorporating skew into his
pricing, the trader has lost about $205,000.
2
188

<!-- Page 212 -->
Figure 5.9: Some Volatility Skews Observed in the Swaptions Market: Em-
pirical Lognormal Implied Vols Sometimes Observed in the Market with
Theoretical Lognormal Implied Vols from the Normal Model Superimposed

<!-- Page 213 -->
Figure 5.10: Pricing Up the 2 into 3 Payer Swaption Struck 50 Out but Using
Vol for the At-the-Money Forward Strike

<!-- Page 214 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Why Skew?
The Black-Scholes framework suggests that if we allow the strike of an option
to vary, the lognormal implied vol of the option should be the same. But as
we just discussed, this is far from the case in the interest rate option markets
in which we often observe a downward-sloping skew. Why should such skew
exist?
It is somewhat incontrovertible to say that any skew pattern that is
observed in the market is the result of the supply and demand eﬀects for
options of particular strikes. The traditional structural line-up of buyers and
sellers in the vol markets is complex. Figure 5.11 gives a sense of the various
players that have traditionally either bought or sold options in the interest
rate options market. In this ﬁgure, an arrow at the end of a line pointing to
an entity indicates that the entity represented has traditionally been a net
buyer of options. Thus, for example, mortgage servicers have traditionally
been net buyers of low-strike receivers and CMS ﬂoors. Corporations and
Figure 5.11: Supply and Demand in the Interest Rate Option Markets
191

<!-- Page 215 -->
S WA P T I O N S
the U.S. agencies have been traditional suppliers to the market of high-strike
options in the form of various callable and puttable structures.26 Hedge funds
have traditionally been both buyers and sellers of options, as indicated by
the arrows in both directions.
As this ﬁgure indicates, the supply and demand factors governing the vol
markets is quite complicated, with a variety of players involved. Despite this
complexity, the conventional wisdom in the market is that there tends to be
net demand from customers for low-strike options. This demand is reﬂected
in the higher implied vols at which these options trade. The demand for these
low-strike options comes from a variety of buy-side entities, many of whom
are hedging against the risk of mortgage prepayment.27
It is also conventional wisdom that there has traditionally been a supply
of high-strike options into the market. Entities such as corporations and the
agencies have traditionally issued ﬁxed-rate callable bonds. As we will discuss
in Chapter 6, these issuers often swap these bonds, thereby transforming the
nature of their option-embedded liabilities from ﬁxed to ﬂoating and, in the
process, wind up selling high-strike options to dealers. This is not to say that
there is no buying of high-strike options from other buy-side entities. There
are certainly a number of institutions that have tended to buy high-strike
options.28 But this buying has often been tempered by a decent supply of
high-strike options.
Expectations of what may happen to implied vol if certain rate levels
are obtained may also be a determinant of skew. For instance, consider an
option dealer who sells some low-strike options to one of its clients. In order
to hedge the position, the dealer may wish to buy some options. Oﬀ-the-
money options are not as liquid as at-the-money options, and as such the
dealer may be forced to buy some at-the money options to hedge.29 Suppose
that the market does rally after the fact. In this case the low-strike options
that the dealer sold are nearing the money now (and therefore now have
signiﬁcant vega), and the at-the-money options that were bought as a hedge
are now out-of-the-money (and therefore now have de minimus vega). In this
case, the dealer will ﬁnd himself short vol. In order to rebalance, the dealer
will have to buy some options. Thus, if the market rallies and the strike on
26We will look at some of these structures in Chapter 6.
27See Appendix D for a brief review of mortgages and the risk of prepayment of mort-
gage securities.
28In fact, the buying of high-strike options is sometimes so pronounced in the market
that such options trade at an implied volatility higher than that of at-the-money strikes.
This corresponds to the smirk pattern of implied volatilities shown in Figure 5.9, which
is sometimes observed in the market.
29Such a hedge is known to create strike mismatch, in which the options one is long
and that one is short have diﬀerent strikes. Managing strike mismatch can often be a
challenging feat for option traders.
192

<!-- Page 216 -->
S WA P T I O N S
the dealer’s short option position becomes at-the-money, the dealer knows
that he will have to buy vol, which will on the margin tend to raise the
implied vols at which he can buy options (i.e., the marginal option buying
from the dealer will put upward pressure on option prices in the market).
This may persuade him to initially sell the low-strike options at higher levels
of implied vol.
And of course, another explanation for the existence of skew is that
the distribution of interest rates assumed under Black’s model may just be
wrong. Black’s model assumes that interest rates are lognormally distributed.
As we discussed in Section 5.3.1, there is good reason to doubt the appropri-
ateness of the assumption of lognormality for interest rates. As we discussed,
there is empirical evidence to suggest that a better distributional assumption
for interest rates is normality. And, as we discussed, if the normal model is
assumed to be correct (i.e., if interest rates are normally distributed), then
(as we will demonstrate in Section 5.4.5) the pattern of lognormal implied
vols must exhibit downward skew.
5.4
The Normal Model
With a signiﬁcant amount of doubt cast over the appropriateness of Black’s
model for the pricing of interest rate options, the question is, What option
pricing model is more appropriate? In this section, we explore in detail the
concept of the normal model, in which rates are assumed to be normally
distributed, as an alternative.
5.4.1
Background
Of the three shortcomings of Black’s model for the pricing of interest rate
options discussed in Section 5.3, the existence of skew is perhaps the most
troubling and the one that provided ample motivation to seek an alternative
model that explained skew. But prior to the mid-1990s, the concept of skew
was not well recognized in the industry. Many swaption traders did not focus
on skew to any great extent when pricing options. Many were undoubtedly
unaware of the existence of skew. Others may have just had their hands full
trying to price up at-the-moneys without the distraction of entertaining the
notion of skew. And still there were others who used various “fudge factors”
when pricing up oﬀ-the-money options in an attempt to adjust for skew.
At the same time, transparency in the vol markets was limited and
bid/oﬀers were wide, contributing to further diﬃculties in the monitoring
and analysis of skew. But over time, people who worked in the vol markets
became more and more aware of the existence of skew, and its study and un-
derstanding became of paramount importance. Given the existence of skew,
193

<!-- Page 217 -->
S WA P T I O N S
and the fact that Black’s model did not explain skew at all (Black’s model
assumes, of course, ﬂat skew—that lognormal implied vols should not vary
with strike), the challenge became ﬁnding an option pricing model that did
explain lognormal implied skew that was empirically observed and ideally
one whose own implied volatility parameter did not vary with strike.
5.4.2
The Model
As we discussed brieﬂy in Section 5.3.1, in the normal model we assume that
interest rates are normally distributed. In particular, we assume that a swap
rate R to be observed at time T is normally distributed. That is, we assume
R ∼N(μ, σ2
NT),
(5.20)
where μ and σ2
NT are the mean and variance of the distribution, respectively.
The parameter σN is referred to as normal vol or normalized vol or basis
point vol or annualized basis point vol. The distributional assumption made
in Equation 5.20 is referred to as the normal model. Under the normal model,
σN is assumed to be a constant that does not vary with the level of rates
nor with the strike of the option. In the normal model, σN is a measure of
absolute volatility. Whereas the parameter σ in Black’s model is expressed
as a percentage, σN in the normal model is expressed as an absolute number
of basis points.
The distributional assumption made on swap rates under the normal
model is depicted in Figure 5.12. There are two things worth noting about
the distributional assumption made in the normal model. First, the model
makes a statement only of the terminal distribution of the swap rate. That
is, the assumption being made is that the swap rate R at time T is normally
distributed. The model says nothing about the distribution of the underlying
prior to time T. Second, the normal model does allow for the possibility of
negative interest rates.30
Example
Suppose that the 10-year swap rate in one year is normally distributed with
μ = 5% and σN = 120 bps. Then under the normal model, there is ap-
proximately a 68.2% chance that the 10-year swap rate in a year is between
5% – 120 bps = 3.80% and 5% + 120 bps = 6.20%. There is approximately a
30Some would criticize the normal model for admitting the possibility of negative rates
(a criticism that may be less robust in recent times during which some interest rates have
gone negative). Others would just “live with it,” believing that the probability of rates
going negative under most meaningful parameterizations of the model is small enough to
safely be ignored. Still others would look for more sophisticated models altogether.
194

<!-- Page 218 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.12: Distribution of Swap Rates Under the Normal Model
95.4% chance that the 10-year swap rate in a year is between 5% −(2 × 120
bps) = 2.60% and 5%+(2×120) bps = 7.40%. Under the normal model, there
is a 0.00155% chance that the 10-year swap rate in a year is negative.31
2

To ﬁnd the price of these options in dollars, we would multiple Equations 5.21,
5.25, and 5.26 by the notional of the trade.
The expression for a swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward in
Equation 5.26 is very commonly used throughout the industry. In Equa-
tion 5.26, we see that under the normal model the value of an at-the-money
swaption straddle is linear both in the square root of time and in normalized
implied vol. Note that in the normal model the value of an at-the-money
swaption straddle does not depend on the level of the underlying forward
swap rate.33

33This, while at ﬁrst seemingly surprising, should jibe with our intuition. Under the
normal model, the level of volatility does not depend on the level of rates, and thus the
normal implied volatility σN “tells us everything we need to know” about the possible
dispersion of rates in the model. By comparison, under the lognormal model, the concept
of volatility is a relative one, and thus it is not surprising that the level of the underlying
forward rate, F, enters the equation of a swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward,
as shown in Equation E.14 of Appendix E.

5.4.4
Relationship Between Normal Implied Vol and Lognormal
Implied Vol for At-the-Money Swaptions
There is a relationship between normal implied vol, σN, in the normal model
and lognormal implied vol in Black’s model. In this section, we examine that
relationship for the case of swaptions that are struck at-the-money forward.
For a given price of a swaption computed using the normal model with
normal implied vol σN, let us now denote by σLN the lognormal implied vol
that must be used in Black’s model to result in the same swaption price. As
is also shown in Appendix E, for at-the-money swaptions the relationship
between normal implied vol and lognormal implied vol is
σN ≈F × σLN,
(5.27)
where F denotes the underlying forward swap rate.34 This approximation
is widely used throughout the industry, although it is sometimes mistak-
enly believed to hold with equality. It is very important to note that the
relationship in Equation 5.27 is an approximation. The relationship is very
important actually because under the assumption that the normal model is
correct (i.e., that normal implied vol σN is constant regardless of the level
of rates), it dictates restrictions on the behavior of lognormal implied vol,
σLN. In particular, it indicates that if normal implied vol is constant, then
lognormal implied vol must go down when rates go up, and vice versa.
34Equation 5.27 provides additional insight as to why we have harped on about the fact
that σN is a measure of absolute volatility, whereas σLN is a measure of relative volatility.
In accordance with Equation 5.27, for at-the-money forward swaptions if we take the
relative volatility σLN and multiply by the level of the rate, F, we get (approximately)
the level of absolute volatility, σN.
198

<!-- Page 222 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Example
Suppose that a certain forward swap rate is at 5% and further suppose that
normal implied vol is 100 bps. If this forward rate rises to, say, 8%, then normal
implied vol would remain at 100 bps under a normal model. But what does
this imply about the behavior, then, of lognormal vol? If the forward swap
rate is 5% and we have normalized implied vol of 100 bps, then according to
Equation 5.27 lognormal implied vol is approximately 20%. If rates go up to
8% and normal implied vol remains at 100 bps, Equation 5.27 suggests that
lognormal implied vol will have to decrease to around 12.5%.
2
Empirical Evidence
The approximation in Equation 5.27 and its implications on the behavior of
lognormal implied vol under the assumptions of the normal model (i.e., that
σN remains constant as rates move) is in fact characteristic of what we often
observe in the rates markets.
Take a look now at Figure 5.13. The top half of this ﬁgure shows the
relationship between lognormal implied vols and the level of forward swap
rates. As shown, there is a signiﬁcant relationship, and lognormal implied
vol falls as rates go higher, and vice versa. In contrast, the bottom half of
this ﬁgure shows the relationship between normal implied vol and the level
of forward swap rates. Here we ﬁnd that there is much less of a relationship
(given the lower R2) between the level of normal implied vol and the level
of forward swap rates.
So what does this tell us? First, the low R2 of the bottom panel is consis-
tent with the assumption that normal implied vol is constant and does not
vary with rates, as assumed by the normal model. Second, the top panel,
which shows that there is a more signiﬁcant relationship between lognormal
implied vol and the level of rates, tends to refute the lognormal model. The
lognormal model assumes that lognormal implied vol σLN should remain
constant when rates move. Third, since the top panel is more consistent
with the notion that lognormal implied vol varies inversely with the level of
rates, this lends support for Equation 5.27, which is again consistent with
the normal model. A direct implication of the assumption of constant basis
point volatility in the normal model is that percentage volatility must be
high in a low-rate environment and low in a high-rate environment.
Given such evidence in support of the normal model, it is not surprising,
then, that it has become quite common in the market to speak of vols in
terms of normal implied vol. In Table 5.1, we introduced the concept of a
vol grid or swaption matrix of lognormal implied vols. It is now at least as
common in the market to refer to a matrix of normal implied vols as seen in
Table 5.3.
199

<!-- Page 223 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.13: Regressing Lognormal Implied Vol on the Level of Rates (top
half ) Shows a Signiﬁcant Relationship, Refuting the Black-Scholes Assump-
tions. Regressing Normal Implied Vol on the Level of Rates (bottom half )
Shows a Less Signiﬁcant Relationship, in Support of the Normal Model
Again, Black’s model assumes constant percentage volatility for an under-
lying swap rate, and the numbers in the lognormal swaption matrix are given
as percentages. By contrast, in the normal model, the basis point volatility
of an underlying swap rate is assumed to be a constant, independent of
the level of rates, and the numbers in the normal swaption grid represent
absolute numbers of basis points.
A Better Approximation
Once again, it is important to note that the relationship in Equation 5.27
is an approximation. Moreover, it is a relationship that holds only for at-

Equation 5.28 provides a meaningfully better approximation of normal im-

Example
In Section 5.1.6, we priced up 1 into 3 payer and 1 into 3 receiver swaptions
struck at-the-money forward in the context of our stylized example. Using a
lognormal implied vol σLN = 20%, we found that the value of each option
was around 115.5 bps, and thus the at-the-money forward straddle is around
231 bps. In order to compute these prices, we needed to make use of the 1x4
swap rate, which was found to be 5.644%. We also found that the value of
the annuity factor, A, was 2.5687. What is σN, normal implied vol, in the
normal model?
The price of a 1 into 3 swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward in
the normal model must be the same price as in the lognormal model. Thus,
according to Equation 5.26, we must have

Basis Points per Day Vol
Another concept related to normalized vol that is frequently employed is
known as basis points per day vol. Basis points per day vol is given by
σN

not true; only under the most special of circumstances is this interpretation

valid.35 Nonetheless, basis points per day vol remains a useful concept and
one that is often used to gauge the relative richness or cheapness of a swap-
tion. Figure 5.14 shows an example of a history of basis points per day vol
for a swaption. As shown in this ﬁgure, after a prolonged period of declining
implied volatilities (as a function of reduced structural demand for vol by
certain market participants and a long period of low realized vol in the rates
markets), vol increased dramatically during the credit crunch in the summer
of 2007.
35To see that this interpretation is not valid in general, let’s consider two cases in which
the buyer of the option delta hedges. In the ﬁrst case, the underlying increases each day
by an amount equal to the bps per day vol. In the second case, the underlying oscillates
up and down one day after the next, always hovering around the strike. As indicated in
Appendix E, under the normal model, the gamma of a payer or receiver is given by
A ×
n(d)
σN
√
T
and thus is highest when the underlying is at the strike and diminishes as the underlying
moves away from the strike. In the ﬁrst case the gamma of the option (under a normal
model) decreases steadily, whereas in the second it is relatively high, indicating the proﬁts
from delta hedging would be higher.
204

<!-- Page 228 -->
S WA P T I O N S
5.4.5
Explaining Skew: The Relationship Between
Normal Implied Vol and Lognormal Implied Vol
for Off-the-Money Swaptions
One of the troubling issues of the lognormal model that we identiﬁed earlier
is the existence of skew that this model exhibits. That is, suppose we look at
market prices on diﬀerent swaptions on the same underlying swap rate and
time to maturity, allowing only strikes to diﬀer. What we often ﬁnd is that
the lognormal implied volatility is a decreasing function of strike, referred
to as downward skew. Of course, if Black’s model is correct, there should
be ﬂat skew, meaning that the lognormal implied vol should be the same
regardless of the strike.
As we mentioned previously, one of the pleasing features of the normal
model is that the assumption of constant normal vol actually dictates that
lognormal implied vol must exhibit downward skew. We showed this earlier
in Figure 5.8. In this section, we hope to provide some insight as to why this
is the case.
Let’s start by reviewing a few pricing results that we found in Sec-
tion 5.4.3. In the example of this section, we looked at pricing up some 1
into 3 swaptions using the normal model. We considered our stylized exam-
ple with the swap curve as in Table 5.2. The at-the-money strike is 5.644%.
We assumed that σN was a constant 112.7 bps, regardless of the strike.
Using the normal model, we found that the price of a 100 out 1 into 3
receiver swaption (i.e., a 1 into 3 receiver swaption with a strike 100 bps
below the forward) was 29.8 bps. In addition, we found that the price of
a swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward was 231 bps. Thus, we
can conclude that the price of a 1 in 3 receiver swaption struck at-the-
money forward must be 115.5 bps.36 We’ve collected these prices and used
the normal model to price up a few additional strikes and have presented
these results in Table 5.5.
So let’s consider the following experiment. Suppose now we use Black’s
formula to price up the same swaptions as in Table 5.5. For each of the strikes
listed in this table, we will set the value of the swaption equal to the price
obtained using the normal model, then compute the lognormal implied vol
that equates to that price. For example, if we assume that σN is 112.7 bps,
then the price for a 1 into 3 receiver swaption struck at 4.644% is 29.8 bps. If
we use Black’s formula to price up the same swaption and want to come up
with the same price of 29.8 bps, then we must set σLN to 22.0%. The results
36From swaption parity, we know that the value of a receiver swaption struck at-
the-money forward must be equal to the value of a payer swaption struck at-the-money
forward. Thus, either the payer or receiver must be half of the at-the-money forward
straddle price.
205

of this experiment are shown in Table 5.6. Comparing results for the other
strikes we see that as the strike goes down, the level of lognormal implied
vol must go up, and vice versa. These results are displayed graphically in
Figure 5.15.37 That is, under the assumption of constant normal implied
vol, we must have downward lognormal skew. Thus, the normal model is
consistent with the notion of downward lognormal skew sometimes observed
in the market, as was displayed in Figure 5.7. Of course, the lognormal
model assumes that lognormal implied vol, which in this case is 20% for the
at-the-money forward strike, does not vary with the level of the strike.
5.4.6
The Normal Model: The Industry Standard
The normal model for pricing interest rate options is by no means perfect.
For example, it allows for the possibility of negative interest rates and does
not explain various skew patterns that are sometimes observed in the market.
37The manner in which we have generated the skew pattern in Figure 5.15 is identical
to the procedure that was used to produce the skew pattern in Figure 5.8 earlier in this
chapter.
206

<!-- Page 230 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.15: Flat Skew of the Black (Lognormal) Model and Downward Skew
of the Normal Model
Moreover, there is substantial evidence that at least one of the assumptions of
the normal model, namely, that normal implied vol is a constant, is violated.
Nonetheless, the normal model appears to do a better job of depicting what
is observed in the market and for the pricing of interest rate options than
does Black’s model.38
38Black’s model kind of reminds me of the Jack Nicholson character Colonel Jessup
in the movie A Few Good Men. You could imagine Black’s model sitting up there on
the stand, defending itself from naysayers who say its methods for pricing interest rate
derivatives are inappropriate:
I have a greater responsibility than you could possibly fathom. . . . You
have the luxury of not knowing what I know. . . . And my existence, while
grotesque and incomprehensible to you, saves lives. Because deep down, in
places you don’t talk about at parties, you want me. . . you need me. . . . We
use words like honor, code, loyalty. We use these words as the backbone of a
life spent defending something. You use them as a punch line. I have neither
the time nor the inclination to explain myself to the man who rises and
207

<!-- Page 231 -->
S WA P T I O N S
The normal model for options on swap rates has become the industry
standard, eclipsing Black’s model. That is, it is more popular nowadays to
employ a normal model for the pricing of options on swap rates than it is
to assume lognormality and employ Black’s model. Traders in the market
are more likely to speak of vol in normal terms than in lognormal terms.
That being said, lognormal implied vols remain a benchmark, at least for
at-the-money options.
5.5
Other Models Used to Price Interest
Rate Options
We have barely begun to scratch the surface in terms of the various theoreti-
cal models that are widely used to price interest rate options. The lognormal
and normal models are both powerful paradigms, but neither accurately de-
scribes the dynamics of skew and options prices completely. Depending on
supply and demand eﬀects in the market, there are times when the skew pat-
tern observed from interest rate option prices suggests a lognormal model
is more appropriate, times when a normal model is more appropriate, times
when interest rates seem to be behaving as a blend of a normal and lognormal
process, and times when rates are behaving altogether diﬀerently.
There is a vast literature on alternative models to price interest rate
options, and the industry has adopted a number of other models as favorites
over the course of time. A full description of these models is beyond the
scope of this text, but here are a couple of models that have been popular:39
1. CEV. The Constant Elasticity of Variance (CEV) model is one in
which volatility is assumed to be of the form
σF β,
where F is the underlying forward swap rate, β is a constant known as
the CEV coeﬃcient, and σ is a constant. The CEV model belongs to a
class of models known as local volatility models, meaning it is assumed
that vol depends on the level of rates. This is a more general model
than either the normal model or the lognormal model. The CEV model
collapses to the normal model when β = 0 and to the lognormal model
sleeps under the blanket of freedom I provide, then questions the manner in
which I provide it. I prefer you said thank you, and went on your way.
Excerpts from the movie A Good Few Men, released on December 11, 1992. Used by per-
mission of Sony Pictures Entertainment.
39See Brigo and Mercurio (2006) for more details of alternative interest rate option
models.
208

<!-- Page 232 -->
S WA P T I O N S
when β = 1. A value of β < 0 is consistent with a supernormal skew,
as depicted in Figure 5.9.
2. SABR. The SABR model (standing for “Stochastic Alpha, Beta,
Rho”—the three parameters in the model that need to be estimated)
is a generalization of the CEV model in which the volatility parameter
σ itself is assumed to be stochastic. This model collapses to the CEV
model when one of the parameters (i.e., alpha) is set to zero.
Despite the doubt cast over the appropriateness of the lognormal model,
and the proliferation of alternative models including the normal model, we
will continue to employ Black’s model unless otherwise stated. Moreover, we
will assume ﬂat skew (i.e., varying the strike of an option does not change
its implied vol, all else equal) unless explicitly stated otherwise.
5.6
Bermudan Swaptions
A Bermudan or multi-European swaption is a swaption that can be exercised
on multiple dates.40 Consider, for example, a 6-month into 5-year Bermudan
receiver swaption with semiannual exercise. This swaption gives the holder
the right to receive ﬁxed in a 5-year swap six months from now. If the holder
does not exercise in six months, the holder has the right to exercise every
six months thereafter into the remaining term of the underlying swap. If, for
example, the holder does not exercise at the ﬁrst exercise date in six months,
nor at the next exercise date six months thereafter, but does exercise at the
third exercise date (which is 1.5 years from today), then the holder will re-
ceive ﬁxed at the strike rate for the remaining term of the underlying swap,
which in this case is four years. Once an exercise has been made, there is
no further opportunity to exercise again. Table 5.7 shows the possible ex-
ercise dates and underlying swaps associated with this Bermudan swaption.
The holder of the 6-month into 5-year Bermudan swaption with semiannual
exercise may elect to exercise on at most one of the exercise dates speciﬁed
in Table 5.7 and upon exercise will enter into the corresponding remaining
term of the swap.
One thing to note right oﬀthe bat is that the 6-month into 5-year Bermu-
dan swaption must be worth as much as any of the single European swap-
tions listed in Table 5.7.41 And since the Bermudan must be worth at least
40This diﬀers from an American swaption, which can be exercised continuously through-
out some period of time. American swaptions are pretty unusual to see in the swaptions
market, certainly as compared to Bermudans.
41If the Bermudan were worth less than a particular single European in this table, we
could buy the Bermudan and sell the European, pocketing some cash today. We would
hold the Bermudan until the exercise date of the European. If the European is exercised,

6-Month into 5-Year Bermudan Swaption with Semiannual Exercise
as much as any of the particular European options in this table, it must be
the case that the Bermudan is worth at least as much as whichever of these
Europeans has the greatest value. This therefore places a meaningful lower
bound on the price of Bermudan swaptions.
Example
Consider again our stylized example and suppose the swap curve today is
as in Table 5.2. Suppose further that 1 into 2 European lognormal swaption
vol is 20% and also that 2 into 1 European lognormal swaption vol is 20%.
Consider now $100mm of a 1 into 2 Bermudan payer swaption struck at 5%.
This option gives the holder the right to pay ﬁxed in a 2-year swap at a
rate of 5% in one year and, if not exercised at this point, the right to pay
ﬁxed in a 1-year swap at a rate of 5% in two years. As shown in Figure 5.16,
using Black’s formula the value of $100mm of a 1 into 2 European payer
swaption struck at 5% is $1,211,221, and the value of $100mm of a 2 into
1 European payer swaption struck at 5% is $876,266. Thus, we know that
the 1 into 2 Bermudan payer swaption struck at 5% must be worth at least
$1,211,221.
2
we would exercise the Bermudan as well, resulting in net zero cash ﬂows. If the European
is not exercised, we can possibly exercise the Bermudan at some later time, generating
positive proﬁts. Thus we have nonnegative cash ﬂows in the future, which would be an
arbitrage. So the Bermudan must be worth at least as much as any of the Europeans.

5.6.1
Optimal Exercise of Bermudan Swaptions
At the expiry of a European swaption, optimal exercise depends solely on
how the then-prevailing swap rate in the underlying swap compares with the
strike. For example, the holder of a 1 into 5 European receiver swaption will
exercise in a year if the 5-year swap rate in a year is less than the strike.
For Bermudan swaptions, the optimal exercise decision on any exercise date
may be complicated by the presence of additional options remaining in the
Bermudan.
Example 1
Consider again our stylized example with today’s swap curve as given in
Table 5.2 and consider a $100mm 1 into 2 Bermudan payer swaption struck
at 5% that we are long. In this example, we will consider optimal expiry
of this option in one year. Suppose that in one year, the swap curve is as
in Table 5.8. Suppose further that one year from now 1 into 1 European
swaption vol is 20%. Is it optimal to exercise our Bermudan payer swaption
at this time?
Note that the 2-year swap rate prevailing at this time is 5.01% and is
therefore above the 5% strike of our Bermudan payer swaption. If we exercise
at this point, we will be paying ﬁxed in a 2-year swap at a rate of 5% when
the 2-year swap rate is 5.01%. Thus, the value of the swaption if we exercise
today is equal to the present value of an annuity equal to 5.01%−5.00% = 1
bp of the notional, running for two years.42 On an assumed notional of
$100mm, the value of early exercise is therefore the value of this annuity,
which can be shown to be $18,591.
42Upon exercise of the payer swaption, we will be paying 5% when the 2-year swap rate
is 5.01%. As we discussed in Chapter 3, the value of this swap is the value of an annuity
equal to the diﬀerence between the then-prevailing swap rate and the strike. Of course,
upon exercise, we are not locking in the value of this annuity if the swap is physically
settled. With physical settlement, we will actually enter into the underlying swap upon
exercise and are exposed to changes in value of the swap as the market moves.
211

<!-- Page 235 -->
Figure 5.16: Valuation of 1 into 2 European Payer and 2 into 1 European
Payer

<!-- Page 236 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.17: Valuation of 1 into 1 European Payer Struck at 5% when the
Swap Curve Is as in Table 5.8 and 1 into 1 Vol Is 20%
If, however, we do not exercise our Bermudan swaption, we will be long
the residual option in the Bermudan, which is a 1 into 1 European payer
swaption struck at 5%. As shown in Figure 5.17, the value of this swaption
is $366,095. Since the value of the 1 into 1 payer far exceeds the value of the
annuity obtainable upon immediate exercise, we are much better oﬀforgoing
exercise in a year. Note that even though the 2-year swap rate in one year,
5.01%, is greater than the strike, we optimally choose to not exercise. In this
scenario, exercising the payer swaption that was slightly in-the-money did
not justify giving up the additional option that we were long.
2
Example 2
This example is identical to Example 1 except now we have a diﬀerent swap
curve prevailing in a year. Consider again our $100mm 1 into 2 Bermudan
payer swaption struck at 5% that we are long. Suppose that in one year, the
swap curve is as in Table 5.9. Suppose further that one year from now 1 into
1 European swaption vol is 20%, which was the level of 1 into 1 swaption vol
assumed to be prevailing in one year in Example 1. Is it optimal to exercise
our Bermudan payer swaption at this time?
Note that the 2-year swap rate prevailing at this time is 5.30% and is
therefore signiﬁcantly above the strike of our Bermudan payer swaption. If

we exercise at this point, we will be paying ﬁxed in a 2-year swap at a rate
of 5% when the 2-year swap rate is 5.30%. Thus, the value of the trade if
we exercise at this time is equal to the present value of an annuity equal
to 5.30% −5.00% = 30 bps of the notional, running for two years. On an
assumed notional of $100mm, the present value of this annuity can be shown
to be $555,461.
If however we do not exercise our Bermudan swaption, we will be long
the residual option in the Bermudan, which is a 1 into 1 European payer
swaption struck at 5%. As shown in the top half of Figure 5.18, the value
of this swaption is $520,785. In this case, we are better oﬀwith immediate
exercise since the value from immediate exercise, $555,461, is greater than
the value of the remaining option if we do not exercise.
2
Example 3
This example is identical to Example 2 except now we have a diﬀerent level
of prevailing 1 into 1 European swaption vol in a year. Consider again our
$100mm 1 into 2 Bermudan payer swaption struck at 5% that we are long.
Suppose that in one year the swap curve is as in Table 5.9. Suppose further
that one year from now 1 into 1 European swaption vol is 25%, as compared
to the level of 20%, which was the level of 1 into 1 swaption vol assumed to be
prevailing in one year in Example 2. Is it optimal to exercise our Bermudan
payer swaption at this time?
The 2-year swap rate prevailing at this time is still 5.30%. As before, if
we exercise at this point, we will be paying ﬁxed in a 2-year swap at a rate of
5% when the 2-year swap rate is 5.30%. As before, the value from exercising
is $555,461.
If, however, we do not exercise our Bermudan swaption, we will again
be long the residual option in the Bermudan, which is a 1 into 1 European
payer swaption struck at 5%. The valuation of this option is very similar to
the calculation in Example 2, but now we assume that implied 1 into 1 vol is

25%, instead of 20%. As shown in the bottom half of Figure 5.18, the value

2
Table 5.10 summarizes what we have found in these three examples.
Example 1 demonstrated that if the then-prevailing 2-year swap rate is above
the strike in a year, this does not necessarily mean that we should exercise
the Bermudan at the ﬁrst exercise date. If the then-prevailing 2-year swap
rate is only slightly above the strike, we may be better oﬀforgoing exercise
at the ﬁrst exercise date in order to maintain a long position in the residual
option. Example 2 shows that there are cases in which the then-prevailing
2-year swap rate in a year is high enough above the strike so to make exercise
at the ﬁrst exercise date optimal. Example 3 shows that the decision made at
the ﬁrst exercise date is not purely a function of the level of rates prevailing
in a year; the higher the level of 1 into 1 swaption vol prevailing in a year,
the greater the value that is attributed to holding on to the residual 1 into 1
payer swaption we maintain by forgoing exercise at the ﬁrst exercise date.
Example 4
Consider once again our stylized example and suppose we purchase $100mm
of a 1 into 3 year 5% Bermudan payer swaption. This option gives us the
right to exercise in a year and pay ﬁxed in a 3-year swap or to exercise in
two years and pay ﬁxed in a 2-year swap or to exercise in three years and
pay ﬁxed in a 1-year swap.
Suppose now one year has gone by. If we exercise today, we will pay ﬁxed
in a 3-year swap at a rate of 5%. If we don’t exercise today, our long position
will be a 1 into 2 5% Bermudan payer swaption. As in the previous examples
we have considered in this section, our exercise decision will depend upon
which one of these two is greater in value. It is straightforward to compute
the value of the swaption that results from exercise today. This is simply the
216

<!-- Page 240 -->
S WA P T I O N S
present value of an annuity equal to the diﬀerence between the prevailing
3-year swap rate and the strike of 5% for three years. However, if we do not
exercise today, we remain long a 1 into 2 Bermudan payer struck at 5%.
Thus, our exercise decision is complicated by the fact that it depends on the
valuation of a (residual) Bermudan swaption. The valuation of the 1 into 2
5% Bermudan is beyond the scope of this discussion.
2
The ﬁrst three examples is this section considered the relatively simple
case of a Bermudan swaption in which the remaining option at the ﬁrst ex-
ercise date is a simple European. These cases were relatively easy to analyze.
Example 4 starts to reveal the complexity inherent in Bermudan swaptions;
even the exercise of a Bermudan swaption depends on the valuation of the
residual swaption position, which in general may be a Bermudan option itself.
5.6.2
Valuation of Bermudan Swaptions
The multiple exercise dates of a Bermudan swaption make this an exotic
option. Although exotic options are often regarded as illiquid, the ubiquity
of this type of option in the market has made it a relatively liquid product.43
In fact, it has become very common for many market participants to traﬃc
in Bermudan swaptions, sometimes buying these exotic structures instead of
Europeans if they are trading at relatively cheap levels.44
The analysis and pricing of Bermudan swaptions is very complicated.
The use of Black’s formula is restricted to options that have only a single
exercise date. When a Bermudan swaption is ﬁrst purchased, we are not
certain on which exercise date (if any) the option will be exercised; there is
some probability associated with exercise at each of the exercise dates (and
of course some probability that the option will be exercised at none of the
exercise dates). To further complicate things, as we will see the analysis of
Bermudans is intimately linked to the consideration of skew.
The rigorous valuation of Bermudan swaptions is beyond the scope of
this discussion.45 That being said, we will present a heuristic paradigm for
pricing Bermudans as a portfolio of European swaptions, based on an ele-
gant methodology provided by Zhou (2003). Consider now a Bermudan payer
swaption that has precisely two exercise dates. This Bermudan option gives
the holder the right to pay ﬁxed at a strike rate X. This option may be exer-
43In Chapter 6, we will discuss one of the common uses of Bermudan swaptions.
44Trading Bermudan swaptions versus their close counterparts, European swaptions,
has become a popular relative value trade in which traders express their view on the
relative richness or cheapness of one type of option versus the other.
45For various methodologies used to price Bermudan swaptions, see, for example, Brigo
and Mercurio (2006), Andersen (2000), Pedersen (1999), Andersen and Andreasen (2001),
and Longstaﬀand Schwartz (2001).
217

<!-- Page 241 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Figure 5.19: A Bermudan Payer Swaption Struck at X with Two Exercise
Dates and a Replicating Portfolio of a Short-Dated European Payer Swaption
Struck at X + ϵ and a Long-Dated European Payer Struck at X
Source: Zhou (2003).
cised by the buyer at either Exercise 1 or Exercise 2. This Bermudan payer
is represented in the left-hand panel of Figure 5.19. The underlying swap is
represented by the thick line along the time axis. Due to the two exercise
times, it may be the case that exercise is not elected until the second ex-
ercise date (not to mention the fact that exercise may not occur at all), in
which case the front portion of the underlying swap would not be realized
upon exercise. This is denoted by the dashed portion of the thick line of the
underlying swap.
Each of the two horizontal lines with arrows indicates the range of swap
rates for which the Bermudan option would be exercised on its respective
exercise dates. Although the strike of this Bermudan payer swaption is X, it
is important to note that if the underlying swap rate at the time of the ﬁrst
exercise is just slightly in-the-money, the holder of the option may rationally
choose to forgo exercise at that point in time. If the value of the remaining
option that expires at the second exercise date is greater than the value
associated with exercising the option at the ﬁrst exercise date, then the
option will not be exercised on the ﬁrst exercise date.46
46We saw a situation like this in Example 1 of Section 5.6.1.
218

<!-- Page 242 -->
S WA P T I O N S
However, at the time of the ﬁrst exercise date, there is some swap rate,
denoted X + ϵ, at which it will make sense to exercise the swaption. The
Bermudan will be optimally exercised, provided that the then-prevailing
swap rate on the underlying swap is at or above X + ϵ. This optimal ex-
ercise region is denoted by the horizontal arrow at Exercise 1 that extends
to the right starting at X + ϵ.47 If the second exercise time is reached, the
Bermudan option will be exercised anytime the then-prevailing swap rate on
the remaining underlying swap is above the strike X, since there is no fur-
ther opportunity for exercise and the option is in-the-money. This exercise
range is denoted by the second horizontal line at Exercise 2.
The payoﬀproﬁle of the Bermudan can (more or less) be replicated by
a portfolio of two European payer swaptions, represented in the right panel
of Figure 5.19. The two underlying swaps of the two European payers are
represented, respectively, by the two thick vertical lines. One of these is a long
line, representing the exercise of the short-dated European payer expiring at
the ﬁrst exercise date and struck at X + ϵ into the same underlying swap
corresponding with exercise of the Bermudan at its ﬁrst exercise date. The
shorter of the two vertical lines represents the exercise of the long-dated
European payer expiring at the second exercise date and struck at X into
the shorter swap, corresponding with exercise of the Bermudan at its second
exercise date. The two horizontal lines with arrows represent ranges in which
each of the Europeans would each be exercised.
Consider now replicating $100mm of the Bermudan payer struck at X by
1. Buying $100mm of the long-dated European payer struck at X and
2. Buying $Nmm of the short-dated European payer struck at X + ϵ,
3. Selling back into the market the long-dated payer at its then-prevailing
price at the time of the ﬁrst exercise if the short-dated European payer
happens to be exercised.
Notice that the Bermudan and the portfolio of Europeans will be exer-
cised in the same states of the world and into the same underlying swaps. At
the ﬁrst exercise date the Bermudan will be exercised if the then-prevailing
swap rate on the longer underlying swap is above X + ϵ. The short-dated
European will be exercised in this case as well. At the second exercise date
the Bermudan will be exercised (assuming there was no exercise at the ﬁrst
date) if the then-prevailing swap rate on the shorter underlying swap is above
47This is not to suggest that the threshold swap rate X + ϵ can be determined today
with certainty. As we saw in Examples 2 and 3 of Section 5.6.1, the decision to exercise
depends not only on the level of rates but also on the level of implied vol for the residual
swaption prevailing at the time of ﬁrst exercise.
219

<!-- Page 243 -->
S WA P T I O N S
X. The long-dated European will be exercised in this case as well (assuming
there was no exercise of the short-dated European, which would have meant
that the long-dated European would have been sold back to the market in
accordance with the replicating strategy outlined above).
The notional on the long-dated European must be the same as the Bermu-
dan so that the two have identical cash ﬂows if exercise occurs at the second
exercise date. However, the notional on the short-dated European will in
general be diﬀerent from the $100mm notional on the Bermudan. If the
short-dated European is exercised at the ﬁrst exercise date (which means
that the Bermudan has also been exercised on the ﬁrst exercise date), the
long-dated European will be sold back into the market at its then-prevailing
price. The proceeds from this sale can be used to lower the notional re-
quired on the short-dated European to match the value generated by the
Bermudan.48
48It is important to note that although this heuristic replication shows the relationship
between Bermudans and a portfolio of Europeans of varying expiries, strikes, and under-
lyings, there are a number of nuances that we have (intentionally) glossed over. First, the
threshold swap rate X + ϵ above which the Bermudan should be exercised on the ﬁrst
exercise date is determined on the ﬁrst exercise date itself. How, then, can we consider
buying today the shorter-dated European struck at X + ϵ if that strike rate is not yet
known? Second, we indicated that as part of the replication strategy the longer-dated
European will be sold on the ﬁrst exercise date back into the market at its then-prevailing
price if the shorter-dated European is exercised at the ﬁrst exercise date. The proceeds of
this sale can be used to lower the amount of notional on the short-dated European needed
to replicate the value of the Bermudan in the event of exercise at the ﬁrst exercise date.
Since the proceeds from this sale will not be known until the ﬁrst exercise date, how do
we know by how much we can reduce the notional of the short-dated European that we
are buying today? We don’t know the answer to this question with certainty today. We
will only know the price at which we can sell the long-dated European at the time of the
ﬁrst exercise date once we get to the ﬁrst exercise date, unless of course we could lock
in that price somehow today. The then-prevailing price will depend on the swap curve
prevailing at the time, implied vol prevailing at the time, and skew prevailing at the time.
Nonetheless, we can conceptualize locking in some forward price for this swaption today.
This alludes to a concept known as forward vol, which we will discuss in further depth
in Chapter 9. The link to forward vol is one of the complicating issues in the analysis of
Bermudan swaptions.
Finally, notice that if exercised at the ﬁrst exercise date, the Bermudan will be worth
the value of an annuity equal to R −X paid over the life of the full underlying swap,
where R > X + ϵ is the then-prevailing swap rate for the longer underlying swap, whereas
the short-dated European (which will be exercised as well) will be worth the value of an
annuity equal to R −(X + ϵ) paid over the life of the full underlying swap. The value
of this annuity is less than that paid by the Bermudan. This eﬀect will tend to raise the
notional required on the shorter-expiry European. We could remedy this latter shortfall by
incorporating a European binary option struck at X +ϵ that expires at the ﬁrst expiration
date and pays an annuity of ϵ over the life of the long underlying swap if the underlying
swap rate at expiry is above X + ϵ, but that would seem to unnecessarily complicate our
heuristic analysis.
220

<!-- Page 244 -->
S WA P T I O N S
One ﬁnal important point to consider is that the analysis of Bermudans
is inexorably linked to a consideration of skew. To see this, let us consider
the very simplistic case in which the swap curve is perfectly ﬂat and all swap
rates are equal to a rate we will denote R0. In this case, all forward swap
rates obtained through bootstrapping will also be equal to R0. Now consider
the portfolio of two European payers in the replication of the Bermudan. The
long-dated payer is struck at X and the short-dated payer is struck at X +ϵ,
and thus at least one of these swaptions is not struck at-the-money forward.
Thus, skew must be considered for at least one of these two options. For any
arbitrary swap curve, in general both strikes in the European portfolio will
be away from the money, and skew must be considered when we value both
of the options.
221

<!-- Page 245 -->
S WA P T I O N S
Problem Set 5
1. Consider again our stylized example in which swaps pay annual bond on
the ﬁxed side versus 1-year LIBOR. A 5x10 ﬂoor on 1-year LIBOR struck at
4% has its ﬁrst reset date ﬁve years from now, ﬁrst payment date six years
from now and ﬁnal payment date ten years from now. Consider now a 5 into
5 European style receiver swaption struck at 4% annual bond versus 1-year
LIBOR. The owner of this option has the right to receive 4% ﬁxed in a swap
that is eﬀective ﬁve years from today and has a ﬁnal maturity that is ten
years from today.
Which is worth more, the 5x10 4% ﬂoor or the 5 into 5 4% receiver swaption?
Explain.

the ﬁxed side versus 1-year LIBOR quoted annual bond as well. Suppose the

a) What is the price that the client pays today for the straddle?

3. Can you conclusively answer the following questions:
a) Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% receiver or a 2 into 3 7% receiver?
b) Which is worth more: a 1 into 4 7% payer or a 2 into 3 7% payer?
In each case, explain why one is always worth more than the other or provide
speciﬁc pricing examples to show that the price of one is not always greater
than the price of the other.
4. Does swaption parity hold under the normal model? That is, if swaption
prices are determined by the normal model, does swaption parity hold?
5. Suppose that the swap curve is as follows:

7.00%
Assume that these rates are annual bond coupons versus 1-year LIBOR
annual bond as well. Assume the notional on all trades is $100mm. Un-
less stated otherwise, all pricing is done at mid-market. Use Black’s for-
mula to price swaptions. Use the lognormal swaption vol matrix in Ta-
ble 5.1 to determine the appropriate mid-market volatility and assume ﬂat
skew.
a) What is the value today of a 2 into 3 payer swaption, struck at 7%?
b) What is the value today of a 2 into 3 receiver swaption, struck at 7%?
c) Use swaption parity to determine the value of a 2x5 forward starting swap
with a coupon of 7%. Verify the value of the swap by pricing up this forward
starting swap.
