Chapter 6
Swaps with Embedded Options
In this chapter we will take a look at a variety of swaps that have one or
more embedded options in them. Typically, buy-side investors receive ﬁxed
in such swaps and short the options that are being embedded into the trade.
As we will see, this results in a trade in which the investor receives a higher
ﬁxed coupon than he would otherwise get in a plain vanilla swap.
Oftentimes these trades are geared in such a way that they provide the
highest ﬁxed rate possible to the investor. In the swaps market, such trades
are sometimes referred to as yield enhancing trades, and investors have used
such trades as surrogate investment opportunities. Of course, the high cou-
pons often obtainable through such trades come at a price: the investor has
sold options implicit in these structures in order to achieve a high coupon.
When those options move in-the-money, that has negative consequences for
the investor.
6.1
An Underlying Concept
As with almost every swap that we will examine, any swap with embedded
options that we will look at here is an NPV = 0 transaction when ﬁrst
executed. The ﬁxed rate in the trade will be set in a way such that the trade
is worth zero to both counterparties when transacted. Since the investor
is usually receiving ﬁxed from and selling options to the dealer in most of
these structures, this implies that the ﬁxed rate coupon that the investor
receives will be higher than the par swap rate in a plain vanilla swap, so

<!-- Page 254 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
as to compensate the investor for the short option position in the trade.
Again, the ﬁxed coupon in such a swap, which we will denote by x, is set
such that
NPV of Swap with Embedded Options = 0
(6.1)
when the trade is ﬁrst executed.1 It will often be useful to conceptualize
such trades by bifurcating them into their component parts: a plain vanilla
ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap with a ﬁxed coupon of x, and some options. With this
type of thinking, we can then write that
Value of Plain Vanilla Swap with Coupon of x
+ Value of Embedded Options
= 0,
(6.2)
when the trade is ﬁrst executed. Let’s think about Equation 6.2 from the
point of view of the dealer who is paying ﬁxed in such a trade. The value of
the embedded options that the dealer is buying is a positive number. This
implies that the coupon, x, will be set in the trade such that the value of the
plain vanilla swap is negative to the dealer.2 By extension, the (oﬀ-market)
ﬁxed rate x will be above the par swap rate for the given maturity swap. In
many of the particular trades that we will examine, we will have to iterate
to ﬁnd the ﬁxed rate x that makes the swap with embedded options an
NPV = 0 transaction.
We already looked at one example of a swap with an embedded option
in Section 4.1.3 of Chapter 4 (and you looked at a bunch more in the ﬁfth
question of Problem Set 4). In Section 4.1.3 we looked at a trade in which
a cap was embedded into a swap. In this particular case, the investor ac-
tually paid ﬁxed (whereas in most of the trades considered in this chapter
the investor receives ﬁxed) and sold the embedded cap in order to lower the
ﬁxed rate he had to pay. Despite the fact that the investor is paying ﬁxed in
this swap, Equation 6.2 is still applicable and oﬀers us a way to think about
this trade. The terms of this trade had the client paying a (below-market)
ﬁxed rate of 1.339% in exchange for receiving the minimum of LIBOR and
3%. The dealer was buying a LIBOR cap struck at 3%, which was worth
$1,470,000. From the perspective of the dealer, this option that the dealer is
long represents a positive amount in Equation 6.2. And the value of a plain
vanilla swap with a (below-market) coupon of 1.339% when the par rate in a
1Of course, the value of these trades will in general move away from zero with the
passage of time and the movement of rates, vol, and so on.
2Equivalently, looking at this trade from the point of view of the investor who is
receiving ﬁxed, the options have been shorted and are of negative value to the investor
and the plain vanilla swap with the high coupon x is of positive value to the investor.
231

<!-- Page 255 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
5-year swap was 1.647% was found to be −$1,470,000 from the perspective of
the dealer. Thus, in this trade we solved for a coupon x = 1.339% such that
the negative value of the oﬀ-market swap was exactly oﬀset by the positive
value of the cap that the dealer got long.
6.2
Cancelable Swaps
A (European style) cancelable swap is a swap in which the ﬁxed rate payer
has the right to terminate the trade at some point in time prior to its stated
maturity. For example, consider a “5 noncall 1” (which is also written as
“5nc1”), a 5-year swap in which the ﬁxed rate payer has the right to cancel
the swap after one year. If the ﬁxed rate payer does cancel the swap after
one year, then both counterparties will make ﬁnal coupon payments at the
end of the ﬁrst year, and no further cash ﬂows will be made in the swap.3
If the ﬁxed rate payer does not cancel the trade at the end of the ﬁrst year,
then the swap will remain outstanding for the remaining four years. By the
way, when will the ﬁxed rate payer decide to cancel the swap?4
A cancelable swap, like a regular swap, is an NPV = 0 transaction at in-
ception. The ﬁxed coupon in the swap will be set such that the swap has zero
value when it is transacted. The ﬁxed rate will be adjusted from the plain
vanilla 5-year swap rate in order to reﬂect the value of the option that the
ﬁxed rate payer is long. In fact, it is clear that the ﬁxed rate in the 5nc1 must
be greater than the 5-year swap rate: the ﬁxed rate payer in the 5nc1 will
be paying ﬁxed for ﬁve years and has the option to cancel the trade. This
option has value to the ﬁxed rate payer, and thus in order to compensate the
ﬂoating rate payer for selling this option, the ﬁxed rate must be set higher
than the plain vanilla 5-year swap rate. The augmentation of the ﬁxed rate
serves to exactly oﬀset the value of the option that the ﬁxed rate payer is
long, as we had discussed in Section 6.1.
3Note that this is fundamentally diﬀerent from an unwind of a swap. In an unwind,
one counterparty is paying the other to get out of the remaining obligations of the trade,
and the unwind fee represents the NPV of the remaining cash ﬂows. In a cancelable swap,
the ﬁxed rate payer has the right to cancel at some point and will pay no fee in the event
of cancelation.
4If, in one year, 4-year swap rates are less than the ﬁxed coupon in the swap, the ﬁxed
rate payer will rationally decide to cancel. For instance, suppose that the ﬁxed coupon in
the 5nc1 is 6%, and in one year from now 4-year swap rates are 5.50%. The ﬁxed rate
payer would choose to cancel. If for some reason he still wanted to pay ﬁxed for four
years, he could always do so just by going into the market and paying ﬁxed at 5.50% for
four years rather than by paying ﬁxed at 6% in the existing swap, saving himself 50 bps
running.
232

<!-- Page 256 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.1: Decomposing a Cancelable Swap into a Plain Vanilla Swap and
a Receiver Swaption
We can think of breaking a 5nc1 up into two component parts: a plain
vanilla 5-year swap and an option to cancel the swap. This is demonstrated
in Figure 6.1. The top portion of this ﬁgure represents the cancelable swap.
The ﬁxed coupon in this trade, denoted 5nc1, will be equal to the yield on the
5-year Treasury, T5, plus some spread, y. The middle and bottom portions of
this ﬁgure break the cancelable swap into two components. The middle part
is a plain vanilla swap with the same coupon T5 + y, and the bottom part
represents an option to cancel the swap after one year. Note the following
important point: the option to cancel the swap after one year is equivalent
to the dealer having the right to receive that same ﬁxed rate T5 + y and pay
LIBOR for four years starting in one year. Indeed, if this option is exercised,
then all cash ﬂows resulting from exercise will exactly net out the remaining
cash ﬂows in the plain vanilla swap, and thus the swap will be canceled.
This option, in which the dealer has the right to receive a ﬁxed rate for four
years starting in one year is of course a 1 into 4 receiver swaption struck
at T5 + y.
In a cancelable swap, it is often the dealer who is the ﬁxed rate payer
and who therefore owns the right to cancel. Since the dealer is going to pay
233

<!-- Page 257 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
a ﬁxed rate that is greater than the 5-year swap rate, the plain vanilla swap
represented in the middle portion of Figure 6.1 will have negative value to
the dealer. The receiver swaption will have positive value to the dealer. When
we solve for the cancelable swap rate T5 + y, that rate will be such that the
positive value of the swaption will exactly oﬀset the negative value of the
plain vanilla swap. This results in a cancelable swap that is an NPV = 0
transaction, as per Equation 6.2.
Investors who receive ﬁxed in cancelable swaps enjoy earning a higher ﬁxed
rate (and therefore greater carry) than in a comparable maturity bullet (i.e.,
noncancelable) swap. Of course, this enhanced ﬁxed rate comes at a price:
the swap will be canceled in a low rate environment. A ﬁxed rate receiver in a
cancelable swap whose swap is canceled will face the prospect of lower rates,
should he wish to enter into a new swap.
6.2.1
Some Uses of Cancelable Swaps
Suppose a U.S. agency has decided that it wants to issue debt. The agency
explores the possibility of issuing a ﬁxed rate bond, a ﬂoating rate bond,
and a ﬁxed-rate callable bond and determines that investor appetite is
strongest for the callable bond. Callable bonds will pay the bondholder a
higher ﬁxed coupon than comparable maturity bullet bonds and as such
are often attractive to investors.5 Among all callable bonds, the agency fur-
ther determines that investors will have the greatest interest if the agency
were to issue a 5-year bond, callable after one year. However, the agency
prefers to fund itself in accordance with its ﬂoating rate benchmark, which is
LIBOR. As shown in Figure 6.2, the agency can synthetically create ﬂoat-
ing rate funding by issuing the callable bond and entering into a cancelable
swap.
Upon issuing the bond and entering into the cancelable swap, the agency
has locked in synthetic ﬂoating rate funding of 3-month LIBOR −10 bps
over the ﬁrst year. Issuers of callable bonds who enter into cancelable swaps
to obtain synthetic ﬂoating rate funding will often take their cue to cancel
the bond from the dealer. That is, if the dealer cancels the swap, then the
issuer will likely choose to cancel the bond.6 If after one year the dealer
5Of course, that higher ﬁxed coupon comes at a price: callable bonds will generally
be called in a low rate environment. If the bond is called, the bondholder will get his
principal back and be left with the prospect of having to reinvest his funds in a lower rate
environment.
6It has historically been common for issuers to structure their cancelable swaps such
that the dealer must inform of his intention to cancel the swap some number of days prior
to the issuer needing to inform the bondholders of a decision to call. In this way, the issuer
upon observing the decision of the dealer regarding the swap can decide what it wants to
234

<!-- Page 258 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.2: Agency Swapping Out Callable Debt Back to Floating
cancels the swap and the agency calls the bond, principal is returned to the
bondholders, and there are no further cash ﬂows associated with the swap.
If instead the dealer decides not to cancel after one year and the agency does
not call the bonds, the agency will continue to obtain synthetic ﬂoating rate
funding of 3-month LIBOR −10 for the remaining four years.
6.2.2
Solving for the Fixed Rate in Cancelable Swaps
As mentioned above, the ﬁxed rate in a cancelable swap is set such that
the swap is an NPV = 0 transaction. The process of ﬁnding the ﬁxed rate
such that the value of the trade is zero today is an iterative one. In order
to demonstrate this, let us again consider our stylized example, with the
curve as shown in Table 6.1, and consider solving for the ﬁxed coupon in
a 4nc1.
do about calling the bond. Although issuers often link their decision to call the bond to
the dealer’s decision to cancel the swap, this is not to say that the decision of the issuer
cannot deviate from the decision of the dealer.
235

<!-- Page 259 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Maturity
Treasury Yield
Swap Spread
Swap Rate (Ann 30/360)
1
4.80%
20
5.00%
2
4.88%
23
5.11%
3
5.05%
25
5.30%
4
5.19%
28
5.47%
5
5.25%
35
5.60%
Table 6.1: The Swap Curve
Figure 6.3: Calculating the 4-Year Swap Rate
A 4nc1 is a 4-year swap in which the ﬁxed rate payer has the right to
cancel the trade after one year. We will assume that the dealer is the one
who is paying ﬁxed and has the right to cancel. We are going to follow an
iterative procedure in order to solve for the cancelable swap rate. Let’s start
out by assuming the cancelable swap rate is equal to the 4-year swap rate.
We know this will be wrong since the 4nc1 ﬁxed rate must be higher than the
4-year swap rate, but we have to start somewhere. The curve in Table 6.1
indicates that the 4-year swap rate is 5.47%, and as shown in Figure 6.3
we indeed obtain this rate when we bootstrap the curve. Note that in this
ﬁgure we have accentuated the dealer’s position by signing the cash ﬂows
(negative cash ﬂows indicate what the dealer is paying out, and positive cash
ﬂows indicate what the dealer is receiving).
236

<!-- Page 260 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
So this is our initial guess for the 4nc1 ﬁxed rate: the plain vanilla 4-year
swap rate. Again, we know that this is not going to be the answer because
the 4nc1 rate is going to have to be higher than the 4-year swap rate. Now,
if 5.47% were the cancelable swap rate, that would imply that the dealer
would be long a 1 into 3 receiver swaption struck at 5.47%.
Now let’s use Black’s formula as given in Equation 5.16 to price up this
1 into 3 receiver swaption struck at 5.47%.7 This calculation is shown in
the top half of Figure 6.4, using an assumed implied volatility of 20%. The
value of the 5.47% strike receiver is 92.7 bps of the notional, or $9,271 on the
assumed notional of $1mm. In the bottom half of Figure 6.4, we incorporate
the value of this receiver into our cancelable swap model. Note that from
the dealer’s perspective he is paying out ﬁxed coupons of 5.47%, and he is
long the receiver swaption. The swap is worth nothing (5.47% is the par
swap rate), and the receiver is worth $9,271 in his favor. Thus, as expected,
5.47% is not the cancelable swap rate because it is not that ﬁxed coupon
that makes the cancelable swap an NPV = 0 transaction.
In order to iterate to the solution, we now raise the ﬁxed coupon in our
cancelable swap model until the point where the negative value of the swap
oﬀsets the positive value of this receiver swaption. Figure 6.5 shows that this
occurs when the ﬁxed rate is set equal to 5.733%. So does this mean that
5.733% is the cancelable swap rate? No, it doesn’t. Although we have raised
the ﬁxed rate in our cancelable swap model in Figure 6.5 such that the net
of the value of the swap and the value of the swaption is zero, we need to
be careful here. The value of the swaption is for a receiver with a strike of
5.47%, not 5.733%. So we now need to revalue the swaption with a strike of
5.733%, which is shown in the top half of Figure 6.6. The value of the 5.733%
strike receiver is $12,819, and in the bottom half of Figure 6.6 we incorporate
this value into our cancelable swap model. This ﬁgure shows that the net of
the swap with a 5.733% ﬁxed coupon and the receiver swaption struck at
5.733% does not equal zero. Therefore, we must again raise the ﬁxed coupon
until the net value of the swap and the receiver swaption is equal to zero.
Figure 6.7 shows that this occurs when we set the ﬁxed rate to 5.834%. But
of course the value of the swaption in Figure 6.7 is for a receiver with a
strike of 5.733%, not 5.834%, and thus we need to revalue the swaption with
a strike of 5.834%. And so on, and so on, and so on. We continue to iterate
until we stop needing to move the ﬁxed coupon any higher. This will occur
when the oﬀ-marketness of the ﬁxed coupon exactly oﬀsets the value of the
7Despite the discussion in Chapter 5 indicating that at this point the normal model
has eclipsed Black’s model as the market standard, we will continue to price options using
Black’s model, and we will assume ﬂat skew (i.e., the lognormal implied vol does not vary
with strike).
237

<!-- Page 261 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.4: Computing the Value of the 1 into 3 5.47% Receiver and Incor-
porating That Value into the Cancelable Swap Model
embedded receiver swaption with the same strike. As evident with the few
iterations we have done here, each successive time we have had to raise the
ﬁxed coupon by smaller and smaller amounts. Eventually we will come to a
point where we don’t need to move the coupon any higher, and this rate by
deﬁnition is the cancelable swap rate.
238

<!-- Page 262 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.5: Raising the Fixed Rate Until the Net Present Value of the Swap
and the Embedded Receiver Swaption Equals Zero
Table 6.2 shows this procedure.8 The cancelable swap rate is 5.913%.9
Note that at a cancelable swap rate of 5.913% the negative value of paying
ﬁxed in a 4-year swap with a coupon of 5.913% is exactly oﬀset with the value
of a swaption with a strike of 5.913%. The ﬁxed rate 5.913% in the cancelable
swap is 5.913% −5.47% = 44.3 bps higher than the plain vanilla 4-year swap
rate, representing a nice “pickup” over a regular swap. This pickup has of
course been achieved by the ﬁxed rate receiver by selling the embedded
receiver swaption.
How does the pickup over the plain vanilla swap rate vary as we move
the lockout period, or the period of time during which the swap cannot be
canceled? Table 6.3 shows a variety of cancelable swaps all with the same ﬁnal
8Thankfully, the advent of spreadsheet software makes the tedious process of manu-
ally iterating for the cancelable swap rate as in Table 6.2 unnecessary. One can link the
cancelable swap rate with the strike of the embedded swaption and use a canned iteration
function in the software package to solve for that rate that makes the trade an NPV =
0 transaction. Such a luxury has not always existed, however. There was a time in the
industry when some people’s swap models and swaption models did not always “talk” to
one another (e.g., one was built in a spreadsheet environment and the other was built in a
C++ environment), and using a canned iteration function was not possible. In this case,
one would have to iterate by hand to ﬁnd the cancelable swap rate, much as we have done
here. Disaster would strike when, upon completing the manual iteration process, it was
necessary to change one of the input parameters to the model, requiring yet another bout
of manual iteration.
9Once again, we assumed a ﬂat skew and used an implied lognormal vol of 20% in
Black’s formula to price up each of these swaptions. If we had considered the possibility
of skew, we would have had to use a diﬀerent implied vol in each step of the iteration, as
the strike of the embedded option changed.
239

<!-- Page 263 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.6: Valuing the Receiver with a Strike of 5.733% and Incorporating
This Value in the Cancelable Swap
maturity, allowing the lockout period to vary. As this table demonstrates,
the shorter the lockout period, the greater the pickup is in the cancelable
swap rate over the benchmark Treasury rate.
What’s the intuition behind this result? The augmented ﬁxed rate that
is received by the ﬁxed rate receiver compensates him for selling the option
to cancel to the ﬁxed rate payer. Instead of receiving an upfront premium
for the sold option, the ﬁxed rate receiver is being paid by receiving a higher

ﬁxed coupon than is available in a plain vanilla swap. The only catch for the
ﬁxed rate receiver is that if the swap is canceled, then he stops receiving
this high ﬁxed coupon. With a very short lockout period, the ﬁxed rate
receiver may potentially have a short time to recoup his premium before
the swap is canceled. In order to compensate him for this shorter period of
time to essentially recoup his premium, this will mean that the pickup that
he receives will have to be increased.10
6.2.3
Bermudan Cancelables
A Bermudan cancelable swap is a swap in which the ﬁxed rate payer has
the right to cancel the swap after some lockout period and periodically
thereafter. Note that the exercise style is not American in nature: the ﬁxed
rate payer does not have the right to cancel continuously after some speci-
ﬁed date but rather on multiple discrete dates. The ﬁxed rate on a Bermu-
dan cancelable can be no lower than on an analogous European cancelable.11
10We should be a little careful with this way of thinking since the expiry, tail, strike, and
ultimately the value of the embedded swaption changes as we move from one cancelable
swap structure to another. But nonetheless this dynamic is at play as we move to shorter
lockout periods.
11By analogous we mean that the Bermudan and the European have the same ﬁ-
nal maturity and that the cancelation date of the European is the ﬁrst cancelation date
of the Bermudan. Suppose the ﬁxed rate on a Bermudan cancelable swap were lower than
the ﬁxed rate on an analogous European cancelable swap. Then we could pay ﬁxed on the
Bermudan and receive ﬁxed on the European thereby earning positive cash ﬂow until the can-
cellation date of the European. If the European is cancelled then we should cancel the
Bermudan as well, which means we will earn zero cash ﬂow for the remaining life of the
trade. If the European is not cancelled then we should not cancel the Bermudan either at
this nor any subsequent cancelation date. This would lock in positive cash ﬂow for the re-
mainder of the trade. This then would be an arbitrage and thus the ﬁxed rate on a Bermu-
dan cancelable swap can be no lower than on an analogous European cancelable swap.
242

Table 6.4: Diﬀerential Between Fixed Coupons in Bermudan Cancelable
Swaps and European Cancelable Swaps on a Trading Day in August 2005
Table 6.4 shows the diﬀerential (according to a propriety pricing model) in
ﬁxed rate coupons between Bermudan cancelables and European cancelables
for a number of diﬀerent structures. For example, on the date that this data
was taken in August 2005, the ﬁxed coupon for a 5nc1 Bermudan cancelable
swap was 17.5 bps greater than that obtained in a 5nc1 European cancelable
swap. These diﬀerentials will vary from day to day (or moment to moment),
depending in part on the swap curve and vol grid prevailing, not to mention
on the type of models used to price these trades.
Bermudan cancelables are popular structures, often used to swap out
callable bond issuance as in Figure 6.2 where the bond is callable on more
than one occasion. This type of call feature is common among many callable
bonds, as it provides a higher coupon to the investor than an analogous bond
that is callable on only one occasion.
243

<!-- Page 267 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
The analysis of Bermudan cancelable swaps is complicated, much as is the
analysis of Bermudan swaptions that we looked at in Chapter 5.12 Even the
decision to cancel a Bermudan cancelable on one of its multiple cancelation
dates is nontrivial, much in the same way that the exercise decision of a
Bermudan swaption can be complicated. It has not been unheard of to have
sophisticated counterparties disagree on the decision to cancel a Bermudan
cancelable swap on a particular date.13
Example 1
Consider again our stylized example and suppose that we have a 3nc1 Bermu-
dan cancelable swap on the books in which we are paying ﬁxed at a rate of
5.40%. This is a 3-year swap in which we have the right to cancel in one year
or again one year after that. If we do not cancel the swap in a year, then we
will be left paying ﬁxed for two years with the right to cancel the following
year (which means we will be left paying ﬁxed in a 2nc1 single European in
a year). That is to say, if we do not cancel the swap in a year, then we will
be left paying ﬁxed for two years at a rate of 5.40%, and we will be long a 1
into 1 receiver struck at 5.40%. The fact that we can cancel in a year means
we are also implicitly long a 1 into 2 receiver struck at 5.40%.
Suppose now that one year has gone by. Suppose further that the swap
curve is as given in Table 6.1 and that 1 into 1 swaption vol is 20%. Should
we cancel the swap?
If we do not cancel the swap, the remaining portion of the trade will
have us paying ﬁxed at 5.40% for two years, with the right to cancel in one
year. That is, if we do not cancel, we will be paying ﬁxed in a 2nc1 single
European cancelable swap. There are a couple of ways to approach this. The
ﬁrst way is to simply model up the residual trade: we should cancel if it has
a negative NPV, and we should not cancel if it has a positive NPV. In the
trade at hand, if we do not cancel, we will be paying ﬁxed at 5.40% in a 2nc1.
As shown in Figure 6.8, we would be better oﬀcanceling the trade since it
has a negative NPV equal to −$71,655, whereas if we cancel, we have no
further cash ﬂows.
Although looking at the NPV of the residual trade on a cancelation
date is unequivocably the appropriate way to determine whether or not
to exercise early, we can arrive at the correct exercise decision in another
12A Bermudan cancelable swap can be decomposed into a plain vanilla swap and a
Bermudan receiver swaption, much in the same way a (single European) cancelable swap
can be decomposed into a plain vanilla swap and a single European receiver swaption.
13See Longstaﬀ, Santa-Clara, and Schwartz (2001b) for a discussion of how the deci-
sion to exercise a Bermudan swaption can depend very much on the type of model one
employs.
244

<!-- Page 268 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.8: The Value of a 2nc1 with a Fixed Coupon of 5.40% when
σ = 20%
manner. In Figure 6.9, we solve for the par rate in a 2nc1 single European
cancelable swap. This is a 2-year swap in which we pay ﬁxed and have
the right to cancel in one year. As shown in this ﬁgure, the par rate is
5.346%. If we do not cancel our swap today, we will continue to pay ﬁxed
at 5.40% in what amounts to be a 2nc1 single European. Since the ﬁxed
245

<!-- Page 269 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.9: The Par Rate in a 2nc1 when σ = 20%
rate we are paying is above the par rate in a 2nc1, we should cancel the
swap.14
2
14What if we want to maintain a position in the cancelable swap despite our rational
exercise decision? If we want to maintain a position in a 2nc1, we would be better oﬀ
canceling our trade with the 5.40% coupon and going into the market and entering into a
2nc1 at the par rate of 5.346%.
246

<!-- Page 270 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Example 2
Let’s consider a slightly diﬀerent scenario now. Suppose again a year has gone
by and the swap curve is as in Table 6.1 just like before, but now suppose
that the prevailing 1 into 1 swaption vol is now 25%, instead of 20%. Thus the
only thing diﬀerent between Example 1 and Example 2 is that the prevailing
1 into 1 swaption vol is now higher. Does our answer change?
Yes, it does. In this case, with the higher 1 into 1 swaption vol prevailing,
we are better oﬀnot canceling in a year. We can again come to this realization
in two ways. First, if we compute the NPV of the 2nc1 with the higher vol, we
get a positive NPV equal to $22,623 as shown in Figure 6.10. This indicates
that we should not cancel the swap.
Alternatively, if we solve for the par rate in a 2nc1 with the higher vol
assumption, we get a rate of 5.417%, as shown in Figure 6.11. Thus, since
we can continue to pay at a rate of only 5.40% and the par rate is 5.417%,
it is in our advantage not to cancel.15
2
These two examples collectively show us that our decision to cancel or
not in a year depends not only on the then-prevailing level of rates but also
on the then-prevailing level of 1 into 1 swaption vol. With the lower level
of vol, the remaining embedded option is not worth enough to oﬀset the
negative value of the plain vanilla swap, and we are better oﬀcanceling the
trade. With the higher level of vol, this embedded option is very valuable,
and we are better oﬀnot canceling the trade.
So what is the value of the 3nc1 Bermudan today?16 It’s complicated. As
we have just seen, the decision as to whether or not to cancel in a year de-
pends in part on the then-prevailing level of 1 into 1 swaption vol. The higher
that we think that 1 into 1 swaption vol will be in a year, the higher the value
of the Bermudan 3nc1 today. We will return to this concept in Chapter 9.
15But what should we do if we just no longer want to maintain exposure in this trade,
even though it is rational for us to not cancel the trade? In this case, if we want out of
the trade, we should unwind the position instead of canceling the trade. The trade has a
positive NPV to us, and we would get paid something to unwind it.
16Recall that we said that we have the 3nc1 Bermudan with the 5.40% coupon on our
books today. We did not say that it was an NPV = 0 transaction. It could have been
that we entered into this transaction a year ago when it was originally a 4nc1 Bermudan
cancelable, and now a year has gone by and we have determined not to cancel the trade
today. In fact, if we now assume that the swap curve today is also as given in Table 6.1
and that 1 in 2 swaption vol is now 20%, we can conclude that we would deﬁnitely not
cancel our Bermudan trade today. Under these assumptions it can be shown that the par
rate in a 3nc1 single European cancelable swap is 5.654%. Thus the par rate in a 3ncl
Bermudan cancelable must be at least 5.654%. Since we are currently paying a lower rate
of 5.40% in our 3nc1 Bermudan, we can conclude that we would not cancel today.
247

<!-- Page 271 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.10: The Value of a 2nc1 with a Fixed Coupon of 5.40% when
σ = 25%
6.3
Index Amortizing Swaps
Index amortizing swaps were extremely popular in the 1990s but are prac-
tically defunct now. An elegant trade from a more civilized age, this trade
merits discussion given its historical signiﬁcance. It demonstrated both the
248

<!-- Page 272 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.11: The Par Rate in a 2nc1 when σ = 25%
ﬂexibility of the swaps market and the creativity of those who structured
trades. Index amortizing swaps were heavily used by certain regional banks
as a synthetic investment vehicle, a substitute for a variety of mortgage bonds
typically held by these ﬁnancial institutions.17 It has been suggested by some
17For a further discussion of the use of index amortizing swaps, see, for example, “Banc
One Corporation,” Harvard Business School Case Study, February 24, 1994.
249

<!-- Page 273 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
that the use of index amortizing swaps raised concern about how some ﬁ-
nancial institutions were able to use such option-embedded swaps without
them being marked-to-market under then-current accounting guidelines.18
Broad changes in the accounting of derivatives were subsequently instituted
under FAS 133. Index amortizing swaps were notoriously diﬃcult to value,
and pricing observed among competing dealers was sometimes surprisingly
disparate.19
6.3.1
An Explanation of the Trade
An index amortizing swap (often referred to as an index am for short) is a
swap whose notional can decline over time in accordance with a schedule.
The schedule is driven by the level of a reference rate of interest, such as
3-month LIBOR, as observed on particular reset dates. Although the sched-
ule is agreed to at the time the trade is executed, the actual amortization
that occurs over the life of the trade depends on the path taken by the
reference rate of interest. There may be a lockout period during which no
amortization can occur.20
The easiest way to see how an index am works is to go through a par-
ticular example. Consider $100mm of an index am that has a ﬁnal maturity
of ﬁve years and a lockout period of one year. There can be no amortization
of the notional during the ﬁrst year of the swap. Assume that the reference
rate is 3-month LIBOR and the base rate is 5.25%. After one year and on
each subsequent coupon date, we will look at the level of 3-month LIBOR
to determine the percentage of the remaining notional that amortizes in
accordance with the schedule shown in Table 6.5.21 Linear interpolation of
18See,forexample“UntanglingtheDerivativesMess,” FortuneMagazine,March20,1995.
19Perhaps it would be more appropriate to describe the pricing from diﬀerent dealers as
shockingly diﬀerent. In the swaps market, if a client asks one or more dealers to compete
on a particular trade, it is common etiquette for the client to provide “color” as to where
the trade got done to the losing dealer(s) who competed. At times, the variation in levels
between the winning dealer and other dealers was so large (15, sometimes 20 bps or more in
terms of the ﬁxed coupon quoted) that some losing dealers could not reconcile the pricing
under any parameterizations of their models. In fact, it was believed by some market
participants at the time that the levels printed by at least one winning dealer sometimes
broke arbitrage bounds governing the magnitude of the ﬁxed coupon that could rightly
be paid in such trades. Such a phenomenon suggests that the models employed by such a
dealer to price such trades were ﬂawed.
20An index amortizing swap should not be confused with an amortizing swap, the
latter of which has a declining notional balance set in accordance with a predetermined
schedule. In an amortizing swap, there is no uncertainty regarding the declining notional
of the swap. In an index amortizing swap, the declining notional of the swap is uncertain,
depending on the path of the underlying reference rate over the life of the swap.
21This sample schedule is relatively simplistic. More complicated schedules were often
used in actual trades.
250

<!-- Page 274 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Percentage of
Level of
Remaining Notional
3-month LIBOR
that Amortizes
≤Base rate −100 bps
100%
Base rate
50%
≥Base rate + 100 bps
0%
Table 6.5: Index Amortizing Swap Schedule
this schedule is used to determine amortization for levels of 3-month LIBOR
that do not explicitly appear in this table. For example, if LIBOR were to set
at 4.75% on one of the reset dates after the lockout period, then 75% of the
remaining notional would amortize. In an index am, the investor typically
receives ﬁxed and pays ﬂoating to a dealer.
Now let’s look at what this schedule implies for an investor who is re-
ceiving ﬁxed in an index am. When the market rallies, this typically beneﬁts
an investor who is receiving ﬁxed in a swap. However, as the market rallies,
the notional balance may experience signiﬁcant amortization according to
the schedule, which hurts the investor.22 That is, although the investor may
be receiving a high ﬁxed coupon in a low rate environment, the outstanding
notional upon which he receives this ﬁxed rate is diminishing.
On the other hand, when the market sells oﬀ, this typically hurts an
investor who is receiving ﬁxed in a swap. However, when the market sells
oﬀ, the schedule indicates that the outstanding notional balance is going to
be higher than the forwards originally predicted when the trade was ﬁrst
structured, which hurts the ﬁxed rate receiver.
There are parallels, then, between receiving ﬁxed in an index am and
owning a mortgage security. Both exhibit this type of phenomenon, which
is referred to as negative convexity, in which the trade shortens up in a
rally precisely when we wish it would not, and the trade lengthens in a sell-oﬀ
precisely when we wish it would not.23 The investor who receives ﬁxed in an
22The implicit assumption being made here is that when the market rallies, all rates
(including 3-month LIBOR) decline. However, even if the spot level of 3-month LIBOR
does not decline as the market rallies, the forward LIBOR rates will decline. Models
commonly employed to price index amortizing swaps will in such circumstances predict
that the notional in subsequent periods will decline based on the lower forwards.
23There is an important but subtle diﬀerence between the negative convexity implicit
in this trade and the short option position embedded in other trades such as a cancelable
swap. In a cancelable swap, one counterparty has sold an option to the other, and the
option will be exercised (and the trade thereby canceled) at the discretion of the counter-
251

<!-- Page 275 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
index am is compensated for enduring this negative convexity by receiving
a higher coupon than he would in a plain vanilla swap.
The index amortizing swap was the swap market’s answer to the mort-
gage market in the 1990s. That is, the index am is a trade that was designed
to mimic some of the characteristics found in many mortgage securities (i.e.,
negative convexity) and provide a high yield (i.e., coupon) to the investor.24
Some investors who had typically invested in mortgages sometimes found
more attractive opportunities to receive ﬁxed in index ams, depending on
the relative value of options in the mortgage market and the swaps market.25
6.3.2
Pricing Index Amortizing Swaps
Pricing index amortizing swaps is extremely complicated and beyond the
scope of this discussion.26 The declining notional feature of the trade from one
period to the next based oﬀof the remaining notional implies path depen-
dency. As a testament to this complexity, the pricing shown by various deal-
ers who priced index ams in the mid-1990s often varied signiﬁcantly.
Index ams are typically not modeled analytically. If we were to envision
pricing an index am in a binomial tree framework (in which nodes in the
tree represent possible interest rates observed over the life of the trade), the
setup would be pretty complicated. At each node we would have to keep track
of, among other things, the path of 3-month LIBOR (for purposes of both
computing amortization according to the schedule as well as determining
payment on the ﬂoating side of the swap). We might consider parameterizing
the magnitude of moves in our tree based on the level of implied volatilities
trading elsewhere in the swaps market. This would be an important input
used to determine the possible paths that LIBOR rates and swap rates could
take over the life of the trade. We could use this sort of methodology to
party who owns the option. In an index am, no one really owns an option; the outstanding
notional will decline not based on an option being exercised by a counterparty but, rather,
based on what happens in the market.
24Index ams have some diﬀerent features as compared to mortgages. For one, amorti-
zation in mortgage securities is driven by the level of prepayments, which are relatively
diﬃcult to predict. In an index am, amortization is driven by a single reference rate, typ-
ically 3-month LIBOR. While it may very well not be easy to predict 3-month LIBOR in
the future, some investors liked the fact that the cash ﬂows in an index am could be fully
speciﬁed as a function of LIBOR alone.
25To put it another way, the relative attractiveness of index ams versus mortgages
depended on the level of implied volatility trading in the swaps market and the mortgage
market. In general, as swaption vol increased versus mortgage vol, this would tend to
make index amortizing swaps a relatively more attractive alternative.
26For a more detailed discussion of the pricing of index amortizing swaps, see, for
example, Longstaﬀand Schwartz (2001), Fernald (1993–1994), Galaif (1993–1994), and
Flavell (2010).
252

<!-- Page 276 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
determine the ﬁxed coupon for a given reference schedule that gives the trade
an NPV = 0.
6.3.3
Relationship Between Index Amortizing Swaps
and Cancelable Swaps
It can be shown that for index ams and cancelable swaps with the same ﬁnal
maturity and same lockout period (during which no amortization can occur
in the case of an index am and during which the swap cannot be canceled
in the case of a cancelable swap), the ﬁxed rate in the cancelable swap
must be higher than the ﬁxed rate in the index am. We will suggest some
intuition for the result. A cancelable swap is “more callable” than an index
am. In a cancelable swap, the remaining notional on the trade can go from
100% to 0% at a single moment in time and typically does so when it is
disadvantageous for the ﬁxed rate receiver. In an index am, although the
remaining notional on the swap may disappear as quickly, it is typically
the case that the amortization is less drastic at any one time, since partial
amortization of the swap can occur. Both trades tend to amortize when it is
against the best interests of the ﬁxed rate receiver, but the cancelable swap
experiences a more binary, “all or none” amortization, whereas the index am
can experience partial amortization.
Figure 6.12 shows the relationship between the ﬁxed coupon obtainable
in an index am as a function of a change in the base rate versus the coupon
in a comparable cancelable swap. Both trades have a ﬁnal maturity of three
years and can’t be called in their ﬁrst year. The level of the 1-year and 3-year
swap rates are also depicted. As we look to the right of this ﬁgure, we are
considering cases in which the base rate is set very high. Take a look back at
Table 6.5 and think about what this means for the index am. With the base
rate set very high, it is a virtual certainty that the index am will completely
amortize at the ﬁrst opportunity to do so. That is, the index am would be
expected to amortize completely at the end of one year. Note that in this
range the coupon that would be paid in an index am is very close to that
of the 1-year swap rate. At the other extreme, look to the left of this ﬁgure,
in which we consider cases in which the base rate is set very low. With a
very low base rate, it will be very diﬃcult for the index am to experience
any amortization whatsoever. In this case, we would expect the index am to
remain a bullet swap with a maturity of three years. Note that the coupon
obtainable by the index am in this range is very close to the coupon in a
plain vanilla 3-year swap.
It is in the middle region when the index am becomes more interesting.
When the base rate is set at more moderate levels, the amortization of the
253

<!-- Page 277 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.12: Coupon Comparison Between Index Amortizing Swaps, Cance-
lable Swaps, and Bullet Swaps as a Function of the Base Rate in the Index
Amortizing Swap
notional becomes less certain, and the value of the negative convexity that is
shorted by the ﬁxed rate receiver increases. It is in this region that the ﬁxed
rate in an index am can start exhibiting a signiﬁcant pickup above both the
1-year and 3-year swap rates. Part of the job of someone structuring such a
trade is to ﬁnd a schedule that will pay a high ﬁxed rate so as to be attractive
to investors. Note that in all cases the coupon obtainable in an index am is
always less than the coupon in the cancelable swap.27
Return Comparison
Figure 6.13 shows a return proﬁle comparison between 1-year swaps, 3-year
swaps, a 3nc1, and a comparable index am with a 3-year ﬁnal maturity and a
1-year lockout.28 The 1-year and 3-year bullet swaps are represented by the
27As alluded to earlier, it is believed by some in the industry that there were times
in the 1990s during which at least one dealer would sometimes mistakenly show ﬁxed
coupons in index ams that broke this boundary condition. In particular, they sometimes
showed a ﬁxed rate in the index am that was higher than the ﬁxed coupon in a comparable
Bermudan cancelable. This was suggestive of a major problem with one’s index am model.
28We haven’t discussed the concept of return for swaps, but for our purposes here we
can think of it as any net coupon interest earned till the horizon date plus the mark-to-
market of the swap at that time, divided by the notional of the swap.
254

<!-- Page 278 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.13: Return Comparison Between Bullet Swaps, Cancelable Swap,
and Index Amortizing Swap. Figure Not Drawn to Scale.
straight solid lines. The 3-year has a higher slope than does the 1-year, due to
the fact that the longer-dated swap has a higher PV01 and is therefore more
sensitive to changes in interest rates. The 3nc1 cancelable swap is represented
by the dark kinked dashed line. In a low rate environment, the return proﬁle
of the 3nc1 is parallel to but greater than that of the 1-year swap, and it is
lower than that of the 3-year swap. In a high rate environment, the return
proﬁle of the 3nc1 is parallel to but greater than that of the 3-year swap but
less than that of the 1-year swap. The intuition for this is as follows: as rates
fall, it is likely that the 3nc1 will be called away and therefore will behave like
a 1-year swap. However, the coupon of the 3nc1 is greater than that of the
1-year swap, and therefore the return proﬁle of the 3nc1 is above that of
the 1-year swap. In a rally when we are receiving ﬁxed, we wish that we were
in a swap that will remain outstanding and therefore have a high PV01, and
that is why the return of the 3-year swap is superior in this case.
Conversely, as the market sells oﬀ, it is likely that the 3nc1 will not
be called away and therefore will behave like a 3-year swap. However, the
coupon of the 3nc1 is greater than that of the 3-year swap, and therefore the
return proﬁle of the 3nc1 is above the 3-year swap in this region. In a sell-oﬀ
when we are receiving ﬁxed, we wish that we were in low PV01 swap, and
that is why the return of the 1-year swap is superior in this case.
255

<!-- Page 279 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
In summary, when we are receiving ﬁxed in the 3nc1, as the market rallies
our trade will act like a 1-year swap even though we wish we were in a 3-
year swap, and as the market sells oﬀ, our trade will act like a 3-year swap
even though we wish we were in a 1-year swap. It is only in a rather tight
range in which rates do not move much that the 3nc1 outperforms both the
1-year and 3-year swap consistently. The index am return proﬁle is shown
as the lighter kinked dashed line. Its return proﬁle is less severe than the
cancelable.
6.4
Knockout Swaps
A knockout swap is a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap in which payments on each side can be
“knocked out” (meaning, they will not be made) if a reference rate reaches a
certain level. There are several varieties of knockout swaps, including period-
by-period knockout swaps in which payments may be knocked out one period
but do not aﬀect that status of subsequent payments and full knockout swaps
in which all subsequent payments in a swap are knocked out once a reference
rate has reached a certain level. It’s a trade whose heyday is surely behind
us but that we see occasionally today.
When a client and dealer enter into a knockout swap, it is typically the
client that is receiving ﬁxed. Usually the knockout level of the reference rate
is set “low,” in terms of the spot levels of rates but, more important, in terms
of pricing from the forwards. In this case, the swap will knock out when the
market rallies. In a standard ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap, as the market rallies, the
value of the swap increases to the ﬁxed rate recipient. But it is precisely in
these instances when the payments in a knockout swap disappear. In or-
der to compensate the ﬁxed rate recipient for this knockout feature, the
ﬁxed rate in a knockout swap is greater than that in a comparable maturity
standard ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap. The ﬁxed rate recipient in a knockout swap
is presumably entering into the trade because he does not believe that the
reference rate will ever set low enough such that payments will be knocked
out and is willing to take this risk in exchange for receiving the high ﬁxed
coupon.
Example
Let’s work through a concrete example of a period-by-period knockout swap.
Recall our stylized example, assume that the swap curve is as given in Ta-
ble 6.1, and further assume that the base vol curve is ﬂat at 20%. Consider
a $100mm 4-year swap in which a client receives ﬁxed annual bond and pays
1-year LIBOR to a dealer. This swap has the following knockout feature:
256

<!-- Page 280 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
if, on any LIBOR reset date, 1-year LIBOR is less than or equal to the
knockout trigger of 4.75%, then neither the ﬁxed nor the ﬂoating payment
is made for that period.29 If payments are knocked out in any one period,
they can resume in any subsequent period and will, provided that LIBOR
is above 4.75%. To summarize, the structure of the swap is as shown in
Table 6.6.
Figure 6.14 shows the net coupon each year from the point of view of the
dealer as a function of where LIBOR sets.30 The net cash ﬂow at the end of
the ﬁrst year based oﬀof today’s LIBOR setting, denoted L(0, 1), is known
with certainty and is31
Notional × [L(0, 1) −Fixed].
For the remaining years, the position depicted in Figure 6.14 is equivalent
to a package consisting of 32
1. Paying ﬁxed in a plain vanilla swap and receiving LIBOR
2. A long position in a ﬂoor struck at 4.75%
3. A long position in a binary ﬂoor struck at 4.75% that pays out an
amount equal to the diﬀerence between the ﬁxed coupon and the knock-
out trigger of 4.75%. That is, this option pays out the quantity
Fixed −4.75%
when LIBOR sets below 4.75% and zero otherwise, where Fixed is the
ﬁxed coupon that we are solving for in this trade.
To verify that these three components equate to the payoﬀdiagram in Figure
6.14, consider the payoﬀof these three components at maturity, as shown
29To avoid any confusion, if, for example, LIBOR sets below 4.75% one year from now,
then there will be no cash ﬂows in the swap two years from now.
30When we draw Figure 6.14, how do we know the ﬁxed rate we will solve for is greater
than 4.75%? Assume that the ﬁxed rate is less than or equal to 4.75%. Suppose the dealer
receives on a 1-year swap at the par rate of 5% and pays on the knockout swap. At the end
of the ﬁrst year, he receives 5% in the 1-year swap and pays the ﬁxed rate in the knockout
swap (the ﬁrst LIBOR set is 5% and thus the ﬁrst period does not knock out), which
is net positive cash ﬂow. In subsequent years, if LIBOR sets above 4.75%, the dealer
will be paying the ﬁxed rate and receiving LIBOR, which is positive cash ﬂow. When
LIBOR sets below 4.75%, the swap knocks out, which is zero cash ﬂow. This would be an
arbitrage.
31Today’s LIBOR setting is 5% and is thus above the knockout trigger of 4.75%, and
therefore the cash ﬂow at the end of the ﬁrst year does not knock out.
32See Appendix A for a review of pricing a trade as a package, or combination of options
and cash.

sum of these three component positions to zero. Figure 6.15 shows how this

As shown in this ﬁgure, the ﬁxed coupon is 5.91%, a pickup of 5.91% −
5.47% = 44 bps over the 4-year swap rate. The ﬁxed rate receiver in this
knockout swap is taking the view that LIBOR will not set below 4.75% in any
period. Although low LIBOR settings typically beneﬁt a ﬁxed rate receiver,
in this trade he is willing to forgo a high net payment in the instances in
which LIBOR sets below the knockout trigger. As compensation for accept-
ing this risk, he receives a ﬁxed rate that is 44 bps above the plain vanilla
4-year swap rate.
260

<!-- Page 284 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.16: Fixed Coupon in the Knockout Swap as a Function of the Knock-
out Trigger
Figure 6.16 shows how the ﬁxed coupon in the knockout swap varies
with the level of the knockout trigger. If the knockout trigger is very high,
then there is a very high probability that each of the three LIBOR settings
in the back three years will fall below the knockout trigger, in which case
the swap will knock out in each year starting in one year.33 That is, for very
high levels of the knockout trigger, it is a virtual certainty that the swap will
only pay coupons in one year based oﬀof today’s LIBOR setting. Thus, we
would expect the coupon on the knockout swap to be very close to today’s
1-year swap rate of 5%, which it is.
Conversely, as we move the knockout trigger lower and lower, we decrease
the probability that any of the LIBOR settings over the next three years
will be below the knockout trigger. That is, for extremely low levels of the
knockout trigger, there is a very high probability that the swap will not
knock out in any period, in which case the swap will behave very much like
a plain vanilla 4-year swap. In this case, we would expect the coupon on the
knockout swap to be very close to today’s 4-year swap rate of 5.47%, which
it is.
33In this ﬁgure we assume that coupon payments will be made at the end of the ﬁrst
year regardless of the level of the knockout trigger.
261

<!-- Page 285 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
For intermediate values of the knockout trigger, it is more uncertain
whether or not any of the periods of the swap will knock out. In these cases,
the investor who receives ﬁxed in the knockout swap is compensated for this
risk by receiving a higher coupon. A derivative structurer will try to come
up with a knockout trigger that provides a high coupon and also ﬁts with
the view of the investor.
2
6.5
Swaps with Convexity Adjustments
A convexity adjustment is a somewhat subtle concept to many in the indus-
try. There are a variety of situations in which a convexity adjustment comes
into play in the swaps market.34 We have already seen an example of a sit-
uation that requires a convexity adjustment, namely, the use of Eurodollars
to price a swap as we discussed in Chapter 3. In another example, when a
swap has in its terms a linear payout of a rate that otherwise deﬁnes an
instrument that exhibits convexity, it is necessary to make an adjustment
to the rate that is implied by the forwards. For example, suppose we enter
into a swap in which one counterparty pays a ﬁxed rate and the other pays
10-year CMS. In this case, the swap involves a linear payout of 10-year CMS:
for every bp that 10-year CMS moves, the payment in the swap moves a bp.
But 10-year CMS is by deﬁnition a ﬂoating rate of interest equal to the
10-year swap rate as measured at a certain time each day. As we discussed in
Chapter 3, a swap has convexity. Thus, whereas a 1 bp move in the 10-year
swap rate creates a 1 bp move in 10-year CMS, the impact to the value of
the underlying 10-year swap itself changes as rates move as a function of the
convexity of this instrument. The upshot here is that this creates a situation
in which convexity adjustments (a change or adjustment made to the pricing
of the swap due to the convexity of the underlying instrument) is necessary.
This existence of convexity adjustments is often diﬃcult to see and at this
point is better seen by working through a few examples.
6.5.1
LIBOR in Arrears Swaps
The popularity of LIBOR in arrears swaps has waxed and waned over the
years. LIBOR in arrears swaps were extremely popular in the early 1990s.
But then the trade fell out of favor after Orange County’s investment port-
folio went bankrupt in 1994 due to their use of derivatives.35 Few wanted to
34For further details on the pricing of various swaps with convexity adjustments, see,
for example, Hull (2008), Pugachevsky (2001), and Hagan (2003).
35Orange County invested in bonds whose performance was linked to highly sophis-
ticated and highly leveraged derivatives. These derivative trades performed poorly for
262

<!-- Page 286 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
do a trade that was funky in the years following Orange County. But as time
passed, people were again willing to venture out away from the most plain
vanilla trades. And in 2000, LIBOR in arrears swaps were a popular trade
once again.
Recall that the standard convention in the swaps market is that LIBOR
“sets in advance, pays in arrears,” meaning that LIBOR is set at the begin-
ning of each period and accrues and is paid at the end of the period. In a
LIBOR in arrears swap, the LIBOR leg both sets and pays in arrears. This
means that the LIBOR rate that is paid each quarter sets at the end of the
quarter instead of at the beginning. The accrual period is exactly the same
in each case, but the LIBOR rate that is accrued diﬀers. This diﬀerence is
shown in Figure 6.17.
When we think about pricing up a LIBOR in arrears swaps, the ﬂoating
ﬂows are going to be based oﬀof LIBOR forwards set in arrears. This is
demonstrated in Figure 6.18. This ﬁgure is drawn with our stylized example
and the swap curve in Table 6.1 in mind. This curve is upward sloping, and
the LIBOR forwards are upward sloping as well. Let’s try to shed some light
on this ﬁgure. Suppose that in our stylized example we bootstrap the curve
and obtain forward LIBOR rates. The ﬁrst three LIBOR forwards will be
used to price up a plain vanilla 3-year swap. These are the forwards that
are indicted by an “×” in Figure 6.18. The ﬁxed rate is set such that this is
an NPV = 0 transaction. That is, the ﬁxed rate is set such that the present
value of the ﬁxed ﬂows equals the present value of the ﬂoating ﬂows that are
based oﬀof these LIBOR forwards.
But with LIBOR in arrears we will use a diﬀerent set of LIBOR forwards
to price up the swap. Compared to a plain vanilla swap, an arrears swap will
grab the next successive LIBOR forward in each period in order to price up
the swap. The three LIBOR forwards that will be used to price up a 3-year
swap with LIBOR in arrears are indicated by a “⃝” in Figure 6.18.
Let’s make a few observations in Figure 6.18 about how the pricing of
an arrears swap diﬀers from a regular swap. First, the LIBOR in arrears
trade does not make use of the 0x1 LIBOR forward (which is of course
today’s spot 1-year LIBOR rate) to price the swap. It does, however, make
use of an additional forward rate (represented by the rightmost ⃝) in the
last period that the regular swap does not use. When the forward curve is
upward sloping, this means that the arrears swap will drop a relatively low
forward and replace it with a higher forward. In fact, in each period, the
forward used to price the arrears swap is higher than the forward used to
price a regular swap. The two swaps use some of the same forward rates to
Orange County, and as a result their investment portfolio had to declare bankruptcy in
1994. We’ll talk a lot about the bankruptcy of Orange Country in Chapter 7.
263

<!-- Page 287 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.17: Comparison of LIBOR Settings in Standard Swap Versus LI-
BOR in Arrears Swap
price, but when the curve is upward sloping, the arrears swap will “pay”
higher forward rates a period before the regular swap will.
All of these reasons suggest that when the forward curve is upward
sloping, the ﬁxed rate paid in a LIBOR in arrears swap should be higher
than the ﬁxed rate paid in a regular swap. Let’s take a look now at our
stylized example and the swap curve in Table 6.1 and model up a 3-year
swap with LIBOR in arrears. This is shown in Figure 6.19. The column la-
beled “LIBOR in Arrears” shows the three forwards that are used to price
264

<!-- Page 288 -->
Figure 6.18: Forwards Used in Pricing a 3-Year LIBOR in Arrears Swap
Versus a 3-Year Plain Vanilla Swap
Figure 6.19: Pricing a 3-Year LIBOR in Arrears Swap

<!-- Page 289 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.20: Comparison of Corporation Issuing Fixed and Swapping to
LIBOR Using Standard Swap and LIBOR in Arrears Swap
the swap. Floating cash ﬂows are determined from these three forwards and
are discounted back using the appropriate zero coupon rates.36 The ﬁxed
rate in the swap is that rate that makes the swap an NPV = 0 transaction
and is 5.645%.
The ﬁxed rate of 5.645% in the 3-year LIBOR in arrears swap is 34.5 bps
higher than the 3-year swap rate of 5.30%. For a ﬁxed rate receiver, this is the
attractive feature of LIBOR in arrears swaps. Assuming the curve is upward
sloping, a counterparty who is interested in receiving ﬁxed in a swap can re-
ceive a signiﬁcantly higher ﬁxed rate if he is willing to pay LIBOR in arrears.
The steeper the swap curve, typically the greater the pickup in the ﬁxed rate.
In the early 1990s LIBOR in arrears swaps were extremely popular with cor-
porations who issued ﬁxed rate bonds and wanted to swap these liabilities
to ﬂoating. By paying LIBOR in arrears, these corporations received higher
ﬁxed rates that (seemingly) improved their synthetic ﬂoating rate funding
by the amount of the pickup in the arrears swap. Figure 6.20 demonstrates
this in the context of the arrears swap we priced up in Figure 6.19. As shown
in this ﬁgure, the corporation is assumed to issue a 3-year ﬁxed rate bond at
T3 + 50 bps. If it enters into a plain vanilla swap, it receives ﬁxed at T3 + 25
36Note that although this swap has LIBOR set in arrears, this does not mean that we
use diﬀerent zero coupon rates to compute the present value of cash ﬂows. The present
value of each cash ﬂow is determined using the appropriate zero coupon rate as we always
have, found in the same row as the nominal cash ﬂow in the model.
266

<!-- Page 290 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
(the 3-year swap spread in our stylized example is 25 bps, as shown in Ta-
ble 6.1). This creates synthetic ﬂoating rate funding of LIBOR + 25 bps
for the corporation. Alternatively, if the corporation enters into a LIBOR in
arrears swap, it picks up an additional 34.5 bps on the ﬁxed rate coupon.
That is, the corporation will receive T3 + 59.5 bps versus paying LIBOR in
arrears. This means that against its issuance at T3 + 50, the corporation has
created synthetic ﬂoating rate funding of LIBORarrears −9.5 bps.
The synthetic ﬂoating rate funding obtained by the corporation with a
LIBOR in arrears swaps does appear optically better, but this is at the
expense of taking on diﬀerent risk relative to entering into a plain vanilla
swap. If LIBOR rates increase each period over the life of the swap, the
corporation winds up paying higher ﬂoating rates each period than if they
had entered into a plain vanilla swap.37
Although LIBOR in arrears swaps were very popular in the mid-1990s,
their use nearly vanished by 1995. There were a couple of reasons for this.
First, as mentioned after the bankruptcy of Orange County’s investment
portfolio (which we will discuss in Chapter 7) due in part to their use of
complex derivatives, most investors shied away from anything but the most
plain vanilla of derivative products. Second, we just discussed that although
the higher ﬁxed rate in LIBOR in arrears swaps makes synthetic ﬂoating rate
funding appear more attractive, the ﬂoating rate payments made in LIBOR
in arrears swaps can be dramatically higher in a given quarter if LIBOR
rates do increase over the life of the swap.38 This is precisely what happened
when the Fed started to raise rates in 1994. Corporations that had entered
into arrears swaps, thinking that they had improved their funding costs
dramatically due to the pickup in the ﬁxed rate as compared to a plain vanilla
swap, watched nervously each quarter as they saw LIBOR rates go higher
and higher. Appetite for the product diminished to some extent after this.
Since this time, the use of LIBOR in arrears swaps has been sporadic.
During periods of time when the curve is steep (and, as we will see shortly,
when implied vol is high), the pickup obtainable in a LIBOR in arrears swap
can be signiﬁcant, and interest in the product increases. But when the curve
is relatively ﬂat and implied vol is low, there is little pickup and therefore
little interest in LIBOR in arrears swaps.
37As mentioned, any LIBOR reset in a plain vanilla swap is also a LIBOR reset in the ar-
rears swap, with the exception of the ﬁrst one. It is a question of timing as to when the
resets are paid. And of course the arrears swap also picks up an additional setting at the
end of the swap that the plain vanilla swap would not have.
38Some may consider this risk slightly overstated since each and every LIBOR setting
in an arrears swap corresponds with a setting in a corresponding plain vanilla swap, with
the exception of the last one.
267

<!-- Page 291 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Convexity Adjustment in LIBOR in Arrears Swaps
The ﬁxed rate that we solved for in Figure 6.19 is not exactly equal to the
ﬁxed rate that would actually be paid or received in a LIBOR in arrears swap.
The reason is quite subtle and has to do with the fact that LIBOR rates are
being set in an unconventional manner. Remember that in a plain vanilla
swap LIBOR is set at the beginning of each period, whereas in a LIBOR in
arrears swap, LIBOR is set at the end of each period. This diﬀerence actually
implies an adjustment to the ﬁxed rate above and beyond that which we saw
when we modeled up the LIBOR in arrears swap in Figure 6.19. The nature
of this adjustment is referred to as a convexity adjustment.
The easiest way to show that a convexity adjustment comes into play when
we are pricing a LIBOR in arrears swap is to work through a simple example.
Assume we have annual payments on both sides of the swap and suppose that
when we bootstrap the swap curve, we come up with the following forward
rates:
f(0, 1) = 5%, f(1, 2) = 6%, and f(2, 3) = 7%.
That is, today’s 1-year LIBOR setting is 5%; 1-year LIBOR, one year forward
is 6%; and 1-year LIBOR, two years forward is 7%.
Now let’s look at pricing up a 1-year spot starting LIBOR in arrears swap
(which we will denote as a 0x1 LIBOR in arrears swap). This swap will have
a single LIBOR setting that will occur one year from today. Now, we could
go ahead and model up this arrears swap in a similar manner as we did in
Figure 6.19, but we can probably come up with the ﬁxed rate for this one
in our head. The model would come up with a ﬁxed rate of 6%: since there
is only one setting in this LIBOR in arrears swap that the forwards say will
be 6%, the ﬁxed rate the model would give us would also be equal to 6%.
But it turns out that the actual ﬁxed rate in this swap cannot be 6%, and
the reason for this is due to this so-called convexity adjustment. To show
that the ﬁxed rate in this 0x1 LIBOR in arrears swap cannot be 6%, let’s
assume for the moment that it is, and we will show that leads to an arbitrage.
Suppose that we are paying ﬁxed in the 0x1 LIBOR in arrears swap at 6%
and let’s look at hedging this position using regular swaps. Suppose that we
hedge the LIBOR in arrears swap by receiving in a 1x2 plain vanilla swap.
This is a forward starting swap that has an eﬀective date in one year, and
payments are made two years from today. Now, we can also go ahead and
use our model and solve for the ﬁxed rate in this 1x2 swap, but hopefully
it is pretty clear that it has to be 6%. If we model up this forward starting
swap, there will be one ﬂoating payment based on the forward of 6%, and
thus the ﬁxed rate that we will solve for will also be 6%.
Note that the LIBOR setting in each swap will be identical; both the
0x1 LIBOR in arrears swap and the 1x2 swap will have LIBOR settings
268

<!-- Page 292 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
one year from now. A big diﬀerence between the two swaps, however, is
when cash ﬂows are received: the 0x1 LIBOR in arrears swap pays its cash
ﬂows in one year, whereas the 1x2 swap pays cash ﬂows in two years. If
we enter into $100mm of the 0x1 arrears swap, we will need to enter into
$100mm × 1.06 = $106mm of the 1x2 swap in order for the two swaps to
have the same present value for each dollar of cash ﬂow generated at their
respective maturities.39
So suppose we pay ﬁxed on $100mm of the 0x1 arrears swap at the
presumed rate of 6% and receive ﬁxed on $106mm of the 1x2 forward starting
swap at the known rate of 6%. Let’s assume that we hold each of these trades
in our book for one year. We are going to take a look at the value of each of
these trades in one year as a function of where 1-year LIBOR is in a year.
Let’s consider the following three cases:
Case 1: One year has gone by, and 1-year LIBOR has set at 5%.
Let’s look at the value of each of the two trades now that a year has gone
by. With 1-year LIBOR at 5%, we will be receiving 5% in the 0x1 arrears
swap but paying out the ﬁxed rate of 6%. These coupon payments will be
made now. So the net cash ﬂow from the arrears swap is
$100mm × (5% −6%) = $100mm × (−1%) = −$1mm.
(6.6)
Now let’s take a look at the value of the 1x2 forward starting swap, the
hedge we put on against this trade. With 1-year LIBOR at 5%, we will be
receiving 6% and paying 5% on a notional of $106mm. Thus, the net coupon
payments from this swap will be
$106mm × (6% −5%).
(6.7)
The important thing to realize is that these coupon payments will be re-
ceived one year from now (i.e., two years from when the trade was put on).
Nonetheless, we can value the known cash ﬂows that this swap will pay one
year from now. All we need to do is discount the cash ﬂow that will be
received in a year. Since it is assumed that 1-year LIBOR is 5% now, this
39Remember that if f(t, t + 1) denotes the forward LIBOR rate between times t and
t + 1, then $1 to be received at time t is worth the same as $1 × [1 + f(t, t + 1)] at time
t + 1. In the example at hand, f(1,2) = 6%, and thus $1 received at year 1 has the same
value as $1.06 at year 2.
269

<!-- Page 293 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
means that the 1-year zero coupon rate must be 5% as well, and thus the
present value of this cash ﬂow at the end of the ﬁrst year is40
$106mm × (6% −5%)/1.05 = $100mm × (1%) × 1.06/1.05.
(6.8)
Note that the cash ﬂow in Equation 6.8 is greater than $1mm. The net cash
ﬂow generated from this strategy (i.e., paying ﬁxed in the 0x1 arrears swap
and receiving ﬁxed on the 1x2 forward swap) is the sum of the cash ﬂow in
Equation 6.6 and Equation 6.8, which is a positive quantity.
Case 2: One year has gone by, and 1-year LIBOR has set at 6%.
This case is not too interesting. Since the ﬁxed rate in each swap is 6% and
LIBOR has set at 6%, the value of each trade is zero.
Case 3: One year has gone by, and 1-year LIBOR has set at 7%.
Let’s look at the value of the two trades again. The 1-year LIBOR rate is at
7%, and we will be receiving 7% in the 0x1 arrears swap but paying out the
ﬁxed rate of 6%. So the net value of the arrears swap is
$100mm × (7% −6%) = $100mm × (+1%) = +$1mm.
(6.9)
Let’s look at the value of the 1x2 forward starting swap. With 1-year LIBOR
at 7%, we will be receiving 6% and paying 7% on a notional of $106mm. Thus,
the net coupon payments from this swap will be
$106mm × (6% −7%).
(6.10)
These coupon payments will be made in one year from now (i.e., two years
from when the trade was put on). Since it is assumed that 1-year LIBOR is
7% now, this means that the 1-year zero coupon rate must be 7% as well,
and thus the present value of this cash ﬂow at the end of the ﬁrst year is
$106mm × (6% −7%)/1.07 = $100mm × (−1%) × 1.06/1.07.
(6.11)
Note that the cash ﬂow in Equation 6.11 is negative, but its absolute value is
less than $1mm. The net cash ﬂow generated from this strategy is the sum of
the cash ﬂow in Equation 6.9 and Equation 6.11, which is a positive quantity.
The results of these three cases can be found in Table 6.8. If LIBOR
turns out to be 6% in a year, we break even. But if it is 5% or 7%, we make
40To see why the 1-year zero coupon rate must be 5%, go back and take a look at the
bootstrapping methodology we went over in Section 3.2.
270

<!-- Page 294 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S

$106mm × (6% −5%)/1.05
5%
> 0
= $100mm × (−1%)
= $100mm × (+1%) × 1.06/1.05
6%
0
0
= 0
$100mm × (7% −6%)

7%

= $100mm × (+1%)
= $100mm × (−1%) × 1.06/1.07
Table 6.8: Value of Arrears Swap and Hedge in One Year Given Diﬀerent
Values of 1-Year LIBOR
money. Moreover, it is pretty easy to convince ourselves that if LIBOR takes
on any value other than 6% in a year, then we make money. To this end,
denote by L(1, 2) the level of LIBOR that we observe in a year. In a year,
the net present value of the cash ﬂow from these two swaps is
$100mm ×

[L(1, 2) −6%] +
1.06
1 + L(1, 2) [6% −L(1, 2)]

.
(6.12)
Figure 6.21 shows this net present value as a function of the LIBOR rate
L(1, 2) observed in a year. As this ﬁgure shows, the net present value is
always nonnegative and strictly positive whenever LIBOR does not set at
6% in a year.
But this, then, would be an arbitrage opportunity: if we could pay in the
0x1 arrears swap at 6% and receive in the 1x2 forward starting swap at 6%,
we would have zero cash ﬂows today and would be assured of always having
nonnegative cash ﬂows in a year; and in fact we would earn positive cash
ﬂows anytime 1-year LIBOR was not exactly 6%. Under the assumption of
no arbitrage, this means that the ﬁxed rate in the arrears swap cannot be
6%. The ﬁxed rate must be higher to allow for the possibility of losing money
in some scenarios.
271

<!-- Page 295 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.21: Net Present Value of Cash Flows in Arrears Swap and Hedge
as a Function of LIBOR in a Year
How much higher should the ﬁxed rate be? That is beyond the scope
of this discussion.41 However, we can make one observation. Most models
will place greater value on the payoﬀproﬁle in Figure 6.21, the higher the
implied volatility of the LIBOR forward in one year. This means that the
higher the implied vol, the higher the ﬁxed rate will be. In other words,
the greater the level of base vol for this forward, the greater the convexity
adjustment.
The proﬁle exhibited in Figure 6.21 is somewhat reminiscent of a cap/ﬂoor
straddle, in particular a position consisting of caplet and a ﬂoorlet both
struck at 6%. This suggests that in order to hedge the exposure to vol, we
may want to sell some options (perhaps, heuristically, some caps and ﬂoors
on LIBOR that expire in a year). Selling these options would help ﬂatten
out the P&L proﬁle in Figure 6.21.
To put this another way, the dealer who pays ﬁxed in the LIBOR in
arrears swap gets long the option-like proﬁle in Figure 6.21. This proﬁle is
a beneﬁt to him. Heuristically, we can think of the convexity adjustment in
the trade (i.e., the increase in the ﬁxed rate) as compensation for the options
that the dealer implicitly gets long in the trade.
41See Brigo and Mercurio (2006) for a further discussion of the pricing of the convexity
adjustment in an arrears swap.
272

<!-- Page 296 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
6.5.2
CMS Swaps
CMS swaps are swaps in which at least one leg is ﬂoating tied to a CMS
rate. As we discussed in Chapter 1, a CMS rate is a ﬂoating rate of interest.
For example, 10-year CMS represents a ﬂoating rate of interest reset daily
equal to the 10-year swap rate.
Consider the trade in Table 6.9. This is a 1-year trade. It has a single
payment made on each side at the end of the year. Notice that the ﬂoating
side is set equal to the then-prevailing 3-year swap rate, as determined in
one year. The payment made in one year by the client is equal to
$100mm × 3-year CMS × 360/360.
Every 1 bp change in 3-year CMS one year from now results in a $10,000
(i.e., 1 bp of $100mm) change in the payment made by the client. We will
refer to this trade as the CMS swap.
Let’s consider again our stylized example in which swaps pay annual
30/360 on both sides. But in this section we are going to restrict ourselves
to the case in which the swap curve is ﬂat, and any change in rates occur
only through parallel shifts. In particular, assume that when this trade is
ﬁrst put on the swap curve is as given in Table 6.10.
Dealer Pays:
Fixed
Client Pays:
3-year CMS
Notional:
$100mm
CMS Reset Date:
1 year
Dealer Payment Amount:
Notional × Fixed × 360/360
Client Payment Amount:
Notional × 3-year CMS × 360/360
Payment Date:
1 year
Table 6.9: Terms of a CMS Swap
Maturity
Swap Rate (Ann 30/360)
1
5.00%
2
5.00%
