5.00%
4
5.00%
Table 6.10: Today’s Assumed Swap Curve Used to Examine the CMS Trade
273

<!-- Page 297 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
The nice thing about dealing with a ﬂat swap curve is that all zero
coupon rates, forward LIBOR rates, and forward swap rates are the same.
In particular, in the case of the curve in Table 6.10, it is easy to verify that
the 3-year swap rate, one year forward is 5%.
Since the 3-year swap rate, one year forward is 5%, it would seem very
reasonable to assume that the appropriate ﬁxed rate in the CMS swap is
5%. That is, the forwards allow us to lock in a 3-year swap rate, one year
forward of 5%. Thus, it seems sensible that the ﬁxed rate in this trade would
be 5% since the ﬂoating rate in this trade references the 3-year swap rate in
one year. We will go ahead and make this assumption.
Let’s view this trade from the position of the dealer who is paying ﬁxed
(and receiving 3-year CMS). Suppose the dealer wants to hedge out some of
the risk in this trade by receiving ﬁxed in a plain vanilla swap. In particular,
the dealer chooses to receive ﬁxed in a 3-year swap, one year forward (which
we will refer to as the “hedge”). There is a particular reason that this swap
is being chosen as the hedge. In a year, the value of this swap will depend on
where the 3-year swap rate is trading at the time. And in a year, the ﬂoating
side of the CMS swap will be set equal to the then-prevailing 3-year swap
rate.
One thing we need to consider is how much of the hedge the dealer should
put on. The net cash ﬂow that will result from the CMS trade in a year is
$100mm × (s −5%) ,
(6.13)
where s denotes the 3-year CMS setting in a year. As mentioned previously,
for every bp change in 3-year CMS at maturity, the net cash will change by
$10,000. Thus, the dealer will not want to do $100mm of the 1x4 swap to
hedge. That would be too much. Suppose instead that the dealer receives on
a notional of
$100mm
1
(1.05)1 +
1
(1.05)2 +
1
(1.05)3
= $36,720,856.
(6.14)
Scaling the notional down in this way adjusts for the fact that a 1 bp change
in the 3-year swap rate at maturity changes the value of the hedge by an
amount equal to the present value of a 1 bp annuity over three years. Note
that we are choosing the notional of the forward starting 1x4 swap based on
the PV01 of the spot 3-year swap.
Suppose that the dealer holds the hedge until its eﬀective date in one
year and at that time unwinds the swap. In a year, the hedge will be a spot
starting 3-year swap with a coupon of 5%. From Section 3.3.1 we know that
the value of this swap will be equal to the present value of an annuity of
the diﬀerence between the coupon on the swap (which is 5%) and the then-
274

<!-- Page 298 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
prevailing 3-year swap rate, which we denote by s. That is, in one year the
value of the hedge will be
$36,720,856 ×

5% −s
[1 + zc(1)]1 +
5% −s
[1 + zc(2)]2 +
5% −s
[1 + zc(3)]3

,
(6.15)
where zc(1), zc(2), and, zc(3) represent the then-prevailing zero coupon
rates for one, two, and three years, respectively.
Under the assumption that the curve will move only in parallel shifts
and remain ﬂat, these zero coupon rates that will prevail in a year will all
be equal to one another and furthermore will be equal to the 3-year swap
rate prevailing in a year. Thus, recognizing that zc(1) = zc(2) = zc(3) = s,
we can rewrite Equation 6.15 in terms of s. The value of the hedge in one
year is
$36,720,856 ×
 5% −s

.
(6.16)
(1 + s)1 + 5% −s
(1 + s)2 + 5% −s
(1 + s)3
So the P&L in a year from the dealer’s perspective resulting from the CMS
trade and the hedge collectively are equal to the sum of the quantity in
Equation 6.13 and the quantity in Equation 6.16:
$100mm × (s −5%)
+ $36,720,856 ×

.
(6.17)
(1 + s)1 + 5% −s
(1 + s)2 + 5% −s
(1 + s)3
This future P&L depends on one unknown variable: the prevailing 3-year
swap rate in a year. Although this quantity is uncertain as of today, we can
graph it as a function of the 3-year swap rate prevailing in a year. This is
shown in Figure 6.22. The interesting thing to note here is that the dealer’s
P&L is always nonnegative and is strictly positive except when the 3-year
swap rate in a year happens to be 5%.
But this then would be an arbitrage. Think about it: the dealer entered
into the CMS swap and the hedge with no upfront cash ﬂows, held both
trades for a year, and had nonnegative cash ﬂows in a year (and strictly
positive cash ﬂows, provided the 3-year swap rate in a year is not 5%). Since
we preclude arbitrage opportunities, this must mean that our assumption
that the ﬁxed rate in the CMS swap is 5% must be wrong. In fact, the ﬁxed
rate in the CMS swap must be above 5%; since the dealer is paying the ﬁxed
rate, moving the ﬁxed rate above 5% will mean that there are some scenarios
in which the dealer will have negative P&L on the trade in a year, thereby
precluding arbitrage.
It seemed so reasonable that the ﬁxed rate in the CMS swap should be 5%.
What’s going on here? Look back at Equation 6.13, which shows the net cash
275

<!-- Page 299 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Figure 6.22: Dealer P&L in One Year from CMS Swap and Hedge
ﬂows in the CMS swap in a year from the point of view of the dealer. The
dealer is receiving s, the 3-year swap rate, and the dependence is linear in
nature. But now look back at Equation 6.16. This represented the value of
the 1x4 swap the dealer put on as a hedge as a function of the same s, the
3-year swap rate in a year. But this value is a convex function of s. We chose
the notional on the 1x4 hedge based on the PV01 of a spot 3-year swap.
But as rates go down, the PV01 goes up. And as rates go up, the PV01 goes
down. Much as the buyer of a ﬁxed rate bond beneﬁts from the convexity of
the bond by gaining more than he loses given equal shifts in the yield curve,
the dealer is beneﬁting similarly from moves in the 3-year swap rate when
he receives in the 1x4.
The amount that the ﬁxed rate in the CMS swap must be moved up above
the 1x4 forward swap rate is due to the convexity adjustment. How large is the
adjustment? That is beyond the scope of our discussion.42 However, we can
make a few observations. First, Figure 6.22 suggests that the more volatile
the (forward) 3-year swap rate is, the greater the convexity adjustment. The
dealer is long vol in this trade; an increase in vol increases the value of the
trade to the dealer. Second, the magnitude of this convexity adjustment
increases as the tenor of the underlying swap in the CMS swap increases.
42For a more in-depth development of pricing the convexity adjustment, see, for exam-
ple, Brigo and Mercurio (2006), Pugachevsky (2001), and Hagan (2003).
276

<!-- Page 300 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
That is, the P&L proﬁle would have been greater if we had considered a CMS
swap on, say, 10-year CMS instead of 3-year CMS. Much like the convexity
of a ﬁxed rate bond increases as the maturity of the bond increases, the
value of the convexity increases with an increase in this tenor. Third, as the
maturity of the trade increases (the trade in Figure 6.9 is a 1-year trade),
the convexity adjustment increases as well.
The proﬁle exhibited in Figure 6.22 is somewhat reminiscent of a straddle,
a position consisting of a call option and a put option with the same strike.
This suggests that in order to hedge his exposure to vol, the dealer would
want to sell some options (perhaps, heuristically, some 1 into 3 receiver swap-
tions and 1 into 3 payer swaptions struck at 5%). Selling these options would
help ﬂatten out the P&L proﬁle in Figure 6.22. The 1x4 hedge was put on
by the dealer so as to hedge the delta of the CMS trade. The options would
be sold to hedge the gamma of the CMS trade. The dealer who is essen-
tially getting long these options must compensate the client for them: the
increase in the ﬁxed rate in the CMS swap due to the convexity adjustment
can be interpreted, heuristically, as compensation for these options.
In this section we considered a simpliﬁed case in which the swap curve was
ﬂat and only moved in parallel shifts. Doing so allowed us to isolate the
nature of the convexity adjustment to just one variable and display the
nature of the adjustment easily as we did in Figure 6.22. The same forces
are at work (meaning, convexity adjustments are applicable) if we consider
any arbitrary curve shape and movements in the curve, but the analysis
becomes more complicated.
277

<!-- Page 301 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Problem Set 6
Throughout this problem set, use Black’s formula to price up options and
assume ﬂat skew (i.e., the level of vol used to price a swaption does not vary
with strike) unless explicitly stated otherwise.
For questions 1–4 and 7, suppose that the swap curve is as follows:
Maturity
Swap Rate
1 year
6.00%
2 year
6.25%
3 year
6.50%
4 year
6.75%
5 year
7.00%
Assume that these rates are annual bond versus 1-year LIBOR annual bond.
For questions 1–4 and 7, when pricing swaptions using Black’s formula, use
the lognormal swaption vol matrix in Table 5.1 to determine the appro-
priate mid-market volatility. Unless stated otherwise, all pricing is done at
mid-market.
1. Answer the following questions:
a) What is the 5-year swap rate?
b) Consider a 5nc2 swap, a 5-year swap in which a swap dealer pays ﬁxed
to a counterparty for ﬁve years and owns the right to terminate in two years
(single European). What type of swaption is embedded into this cancelable
swap? Who is long and who is short the swaption? What is the 5nc2 swap
rate?
c) Consider a 5-year cancelable swap in which a swap dealer pays ﬁxed to a
counterparty, and the counterparty pays 1-year LIBOR - 25 bps. The swap
dealer owns the right to terminate in two years (single European). What is
the ﬁxed rate in this cancelable swap?
d) A puttable swap is a swap in which one counterparty receives ﬁxed and
owns the right to terminate prior to the stated maturity. Consider a 5np2
278

<!-- Page 302 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
(5 nonput 2), a 5-year swap in which a swap dealer receives ﬁxed for ﬁve
years and the swap dealer owns the right to terminate the swap after two
years (single European). What type of swaption is embedded into this swap?
Who is long and who is short? What is the 5np2 swap rate?
e) Consider a 5-year swap in which a swap dealer pays ﬁxed versus 1-year
LIBOR and the swap dealer has the right to terminate the swap after two
years. The counterparty has speciﬁed that it would like to be paid a ﬁxed
coupon equal to 7.75% in the ﬁrst two years. What ﬁxed coupon can be paid
in the back three years so that the trade has zero value today?
2. Suppose that one year ago we entered into a 4nc1 Bermudan cancelable
swap, with particular cancelation dates. When we entered into the swap, we
agreed to pay ﬁxed for four years with the right to cancel in one year and in
two years. The ﬁxed rate in this 4nc1 Bermudan is 6.75%.
Suppose now a year has gone by and we are at the ﬁrst cancelation date.
Should we cancel the swap?
3. Suppose that one year ago we entered into a 3nc1 Bermudan cancelable
swap. When we entered into the swap originally, we agreed to pay ﬁxed for
three years with the right to cancel annually. The ﬁxed rate in this trade is
6.77%. Suppose we are now at the ﬁrst cancelation date.
a) As it stands here today, what option position is embedded in the remain-
ing trade? Given this option position, what lognormal implied vol should be
used to value the residual trade assuming ﬂat skew (i.e. assuming vol does
not vary with strike)?
b) Given the lognormal implied vol for swaptions struck at-the-money for-
ward presented in Table 5.1, what is the equivalent normal implied vol for
this option assuming an at-the-money forward strike?
c) Under the assumption of ﬂat lognormal skew (i.e., lognormal implied vol
does not vary with strike), should we cancel the swap if we employ Black’s
model?
d) Under the assumption of ﬂat normal skew (i.e., normal implied vol does
not vary with strike), should we cancel the swap if we employ the normal
model?
e) Are your answers to c) and d) the same? Explain.
279

<!-- Page 303 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
4. Consider a 4nc1, a 4-year swap in which a dealer pays ﬁxed and has the
one-time right to cancel after one year.
a) What type of option (identiﬁed by its expiry and tail) is embedded into
this trade? For this particular option, what is the mid-market level of log-
normal implied vol for the at-the-money forward strike, using the lognormal
swaption vol matrix in Table 5.1?
b) Given your answer to part a), what is the level of normal implied vol for
the at-the-money forward strike of this option?
c) Assume that the dealer prices up the 4nc1 using a lognormal model
(i.e., using Black’s formula) and makes the assumption of ﬂat lognormal
skew. What is the 4nc1 swap rate?
d) Assume that the dealer prices up the 4nc1 using a normal model and
makes the assumption of ﬂat normal skew. What is the 4nc1 swap rate?
e) How does the rate you came up with in part c) compare to the rate you
came up with in part d)? Explain.
5. Consider again our stylized example in which swaps pay annual bond on
the ﬁxed side versus 1-year LIBOR. Suppose that a dealer and a client enter
into a 5ncl Bermudan cancelable swap. In this trade, the dealer pays a ﬁxed
rate of 6% for ﬁve years and has the right to cancel annually starting in one
year.
Suppose now that one year has gone by. Suppose that the par rate on a 4ncl
Bermudan cancelable swap (with the right to cancel annually) is currently
5.50%.
The dealer currently has the right to cancel the swap that it entered into a
year ago. The dealer is concerned that if it does cancel the swap today, the
client may enter into another swap and possibly choose a diﬀerent dealer with
which to execute this new trade. Suppose the dealer approaches the client
and says that instead of canceling the trade, the dealer will keep the trade
outstanding if the client is willing to move the coupon down from 6% to
5.75%.
Is this a good proposal for the dealer to be making?
280

<!-- Page 304 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S

(LM + LH)/2, then 25% of the remaining notional would amortize at that

a) Assume that the index amortizing swap is “single European” in nature,
meaning that amortization of the notional can occur only at one time. As-
sume that this occurs at the end of year one. That is, we will look at the
schedule one time only at the end of the ﬁrst year. Whatever the notional is

Denote the ﬁxed coupon in this trade by fi and assume without loss of gen-

Now consider a single European 5nc1, a 5-year swap in which one counter-
party pays ﬁxed and has the one-time right to cancel in one year. Assume

S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
b) Assume that the index amortizing swap is “multi-European” or “Bermu-
dan” in nature. In particular, amortization of the notional can occur in one
year and in two years. That is, we will look at the schedule at the end of the
ﬁrst year to determine the notional for the second year. And we will look at
the schedule at the end of the second year to determine the notional for the
remaining three years of the trade. Denote the ﬁxed coupon in this trade by
Fi and assume without loss of generality that this coupon is paid quarterly
30/360 versus 3-month LIBOR.
Now consider a multi-European or Bermudan 5nc1, a 5-year swap in which
one counterparty pays ﬁxed and has the right to cancel in one year and,
if not canceled at the end of the ﬁrst year, the counterparty has the right
to cancel the trade at the end of the second year. Assume that the ﬁxed
coupon in this trade is also paid quarterly 30/360 versus 3-month LIBOR
and denote the ﬁxed coupon by Fc.
Assuming that all transactions are done at mid-market, show that Fi ≤Fc.
(Hint: You will also need to set up an arbitrage argument here as well. As-
sume that Fi > Fc and show that this assumption will lead to arbitrage.
In doing so, you will have to consider all cases of what may happen in the
future. You can save yourself quite a bit of time on part b) if you generalize
your result from part a); that is, if you recognize that it applies to any single
European index am and cancelable swap with the same ﬁnal maturity and
lockout/cancelation date, then apply it appropriately to particular parts of
part b).)
7. Consider an extendible swap in which a dealer pays ﬁxed for one year and
receives LIBOR and has the right to extend the trade for an additional three
years at the same ﬁxed rate. That is, at the end of the ﬁrst year, the dealer
may elect to continue paying the same ﬁxed rate and receive LIBOR for an
additional three years. If the dealer does not elect to extend the trade, then
coupon payments will be made at the end of the ﬁrst year, and the trade
will end.
a) What is the ﬁxed rate in this trade?
b) How does the ﬁxed rate in this extendible swap compare to the ﬁxed rate
in a 4nc1 that you solved for in problem 4 c)? Explain.

A (period-by-period) knockout swap is a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap in which pay-
ments on each side can be “knocked out” if LIBOR rates reach a certain level.
To take a concrete example, consider a $100mm 5-year swap in which a client
receives ﬁxed annual bond and pays 1-year LIBOR to the swap dealer Swaps
’R Us.
This swap has the following knockout feature: if, on any LIBOR reset date,
1-year LIBOR is less than or equal to 5.00%, then neither the ﬁxed nor the
ﬂoating payment is made for that period. If payments are knocked out in any
one period, they can certainly resume the next period, and will, provided
that LIBOR is above 5.00%.
To summarize, the structure of the swap is as follows:
Swaps ’R Us Pays:
Fixed
Client Pays:
1-year LIBOR
Notional:
$100mm
Maturity:
5 years
Payment Frequency:
Annual, on both sides
Day Count:
30/360, on both sides
Knockout Feature:
If, on any reset date, 1-year LIBOR is less
than or equal to 5.00%, then there will be
no payments on either side for that period
Furthermore, assume that the base vol curve is ﬂat at 20%.
a) What is the ﬁxed coupon in the swap? That is, what ﬁxed coupon gives
this swap an NPV = 0?
b) What is the value of a vega in this trade, deﬁned to be the change in the
value of the trade, given a 1% parallel shift upwards in the base vol curve?
That is, once the ﬁxed coupon has been set, if each of the base vols is in-
creased 1%, how does this aﬀect the value of the trade? Which counterparty
is long vol in this trade?

11. A ﬂip-ﬂop swap is a swap in which one counterparty has the right to
switch the coupon it pays from ﬁxed to ﬂoating after a certain period of
time. As an example, consider a $100mm 4-year swap in which Swaps ’R Us,
a major swap dealer, faces one of its clients. The client pays 1-year LIBOR.
Swaps ’R Us pays a coupon that may be ﬂip-ﬂopped. To be more speciﬁc,
Swaps ’R Us will pay a ﬁxed coupon of x%. After two years, Swaps ’R Us
has the right to switch the coupon for the remaining two years in the swap
to 1-year LIBOR −25 bps.
To summarize, the structure of the swap is as follows:
Swaps ’R Us Pays:
Flip-Flop Coupon
Client Pays:
1-year LIBOR
Flip-Flop Coupon:
x%, subject to Flip-Flop Option
Notional:
$100mm
Maturity:
4 years
Payment Frequency:
Annual, on both sides
Day Count:
30/360, on both sides
Flip-Flop Option:
After two years, Swaps ’R Us has the right
to pay 1-year LIBOR −25 bps for the
remaining two years of the swap
Please note that the option to ﬂip-ﬂop the coupon after two years is “single
European” in nature; if Swaps ’R Us decides to switch the coupon from ﬁxed
to ﬂoating in two years, then the ﬁnal two payments made by Swaps ’R Us
will be ﬂoating equal to 1-year LIBOR −25 bps. If Swaps ’R Us decides to
keep the coupons ﬁxed after two years, then the ﬁnal two coupons will be
ﬁxed coupons equal to x%.
a) Based on the market today, what is the ﬁxed rate x in the swap?
b) Suppose now that one year has gone by. What is the mark-to-market
value of the swap that you structured in part a)?
c) Suppose now that two years have gone by. Today is the day that Swaps
’R Us must decide if it wants to continue to pay ﬁxed coupons of x% for the
remaining two years of the swap or if it instead wants to pay ﬂoating coupons

<!-- Page 311 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
equal to 1-year LIBOR −25 bps for the remaining two years of the swap.
Should Swaps ’R Us exercise its option and pay 1-year LIBOR −25 bps for
the remaining two years, or should it continue to pay x% for the remaining
two years? Explain.
d) This question is a continuation of part c) above. Suppose again that two
years have gone by. Swaps ’R Us has just decided whether or not to exercise
the Flip-Flop Option and has informed the client of its decision. Based on
the rational exercise decision of Swaps ’R Us, what is the mark-to-market
value of the swap?
e) This question is a continuation of part c) above. Suppose again that

Us incorrectly determines whether or not to exercise the Flip-Flop Option
(i.e., if Swaps ’R Us should exercise, it doesn’t, and if it shouldn’t exercise,

sion. Based on this incorrect decision, what is the mark-to-market of the

tion given above in the original terms of the trade is modiﬁed as follows:
Flip-Flop Option:
After two years, and each year thereafter,
Swaps ’R Us has the right to pay
1-year LIBOR −25 bps for that year
Thus, in this trade Swaps ’R Us can decide after two years if it wants to
pay a ﬁxed coupon or 1-year LIBOR −25 bps in the third year. And irre-
spective of what Swaps’ R Us decides to do at the end of year two, it can

−25 bps in the ﬁnal year. Denote the ﬁxed coupon in this trade by y. What

to pay a ﬁxed coupons of y% at the end of this year or if it instead wants
to pay ﬂoating coupons equal to 1-year LIBOR −25 bps at the end of this
year. (Again, irrespective of the decision made by Swaps ’R Us today, it will

again decide in one year whether or not to pay a ﬁxed coupon of y% in the

A (full) knockout swap is a ﬁxed-ﬂoating swap in which payments on each
side can be “knocked out” entirely (meaning all subsequent cash ﬂows will
no longer be made) if some reference rate reaches a certain level. To take
a concrete example, consider a $100mm 5-year swap in which a client re-
ceives ﬁxed annual bond and pays 1-year LIBOR to the swap dealer Swaps
’R Us.
This swap has the following knockout feature: if, at the end of one year, the
then-prevailing 4-year swap rate is less than or equal to the knockout trigger
of 5.75%, then none of the ﬁxed or the ﬂoating payments for the remainder
of the swap will be made. That is, if in one year’s time 4-year swap rates are
less than or equal to 5.75%, then payments will be made in the swap at the
end of year one, and none of the remaining cash ﬂows in the remaining four
years will occur.
To summarize, the structure of the swap is as follows:
289

<!-- Page 313 -->
S WA P S W I T H EM B ED D ED O P T I O N S
Swaps ’R Us Pays:
Fixed
Client Pays:
1-year LIBOR
Notional:
$100mm
Maturity:
5 years
Payment Frequency:
Annual, on both sides
Day Count:
30/360, on both sides
Knockout Feature:
If, in one year, the 4-year swap rate is less
than or equal to the Knockout Trigger,
then there will be no further payments in
the swap on either side
Knockout Trigger:
5.75%

the swaption matrix is ﬂat at 20%.

Chapter 7
Structured Notes
It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the
age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch
of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of
Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it
was the winter of despair, we had everything before us, we had
nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were
all going direct the other way.1
In the early 1990s, the U.S. economy was in a deep recession, and the Fed
had cut short-term interest rates signiﬁcantly in an eﬀort to stimulate the
economy. Investors who had previously enjoyed a period of high interest
rates and high returns from the ﬁxed income markets were now faced with
the prospect of historic low yields. At the same time, the sophistication of
the derivative markets was on the rise, allowing dealers to price and hedge
more complex, riskier trades. Such trades would often oﬀer potentially high
returns to investors but came at the expense of tremendous risks—risks that
were not necessarily completely understood.
1From A Tale of Two Cities by Charles Dickens, originally published in weekly in-
stallments in his own periodical All the Year Round from April 30 to November 29, 1859.
Although Dickens was writing about a time during the French Revolution, these words
are not a bad summary of the early 1990s that saw tremendous growth and innovation
(and some abuses) in the structured note market, leading up to the ultimate bankruptcy
of the Orange County investment portfolio as a result of its investment in highly complex
structured notes.

<!-- Page 316 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Structured notes (SNs) represented the marriage of traditional ﬁxed in-
come debentures and derivatives. Prior to the advent of structured notes,
the coupons paid by bonds were typically either ﬁxed rate or ﬂoating rate in
nature, and there was not too much possible variation otherwise.2 In struc-
tured notes, an underlying derivatives transaction is embedded in the bond,
allowing for a coupon type that is as nontraditional as the dealer commu-
nity can create or investors can demand.3 A myriad of products were created
rapidly in the mid-1990s, some of which had disastrous consequences for the
investors who bought them.4
After Orange County declared bankruptcy in late 1994 as a result of its
investment in highly leveraged structured notes, the structured note market
came to a nearly complete halt.5 Few would buy structured notes afterward.
2While it is true that the advent of the collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO)
market in the early 1980s allowed for a variety of coupon types not otherwise seen (e.g.,
inverse ﬂoaters), the range of coupon types available in the CMO market was relatively
small as compared to the structured note market. Traditional debentures issued by banks,
corporations, or the U.S. agencies did not oﬀer such variety.
3Some structured notes also contain underlying principal-linked derivatives, allowing
investors the opportunity to obtain principal in excess of the face amount of the bond in
certain states of the world.
4This description of structured notes (correctly) suggests that a structured note is
often just a swap with embedded options that is then embedded in a bond. Historically
certain swaps with embedded options have more commonly appealed to investors in funded
form (meaning through structured notes) and are included in this chapter. Other such
swaps have typically appealed to investors in unfunded form (meaning through the swap
itself) and were discussed in Chapter 6. And some trades have been popular in both
funded and unfunded form.
Given the work we did in Chapter 6, why then would investors not just always enter
into the underlying swap with embedded options? Why would they want to instead buy a
structured note that has the same swap embedded that they could otherwise trade directly
with a dealer? There are at least two answers to these questions. First, some investors
who manage portfolios simply need to invest cash. Although entering into a swap with
embedded options may provide them with the exposure that they are looking for, doing
so does not require an investment of cash. Buying a structured note requires an outlay of
cash. Second, in addition to the myriad of products that were created rapidly during this
time, a slew of investors new to the derivatives markets rapidly entered the scene during
this time. Some investors, such as money market funds, who could never traditionally
“do derivatives” due to restrictions in their bylaws, could get around these restrictions
by buying structured notes. Their bylaws typically had little in the way of provisions
preventing them from buying many structured notes but would merely provide guidelines
on maturity, credit rating, and so on. Ironically, this often meant that the notes that these
new entrants to the derivatives market bought were giving them exposure to some of the
most complex and risky derivatives trades created.
5This is not to suggest that Orange County only invested in structured notes, which
certainly was not the case. More important, as we will discuss later in this chapter, the real
culprit behind the bankruptcy of Orange County was not so much its investment in struc-
tured notes but rather its use of repurchase agreements that allowed it to lever up and buy
even more notes. In the end it was Orange County’s inability to fulﬁll its obligations under
its repos that caused its bankruptcy and ultimate liquidation of its investment portfolio.
293

<!-- Page 317 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
But time heals all wounds. After months, and in some cases, years, investors
returned from the sidelines and bought structured notes again. Many new
investors have entered the structured note market over the years.
It is important to bear in mind that we will be looking only at one seg-
ment of the structured note market: interest rate–linked structured notes.
There are structured notes created that give investors exposure to a variety of
other asset classes, including credit, commodities, and equities. In this chap-
ter we will examine a variety of structured notes that are common in the
market. We’ll take a look at how some of these notes are priced. And we
will examine the beneﬁts and risks inherent in these bonds. For a more thor-
ough examination of the structured note market, see, for example, Peng and
Dattatreya (1995) or Knop (2002).
7.1
The Rise of the Structured Note Market
The structured note market ﬁrst came into the spotlight in the early 1990s.
Peng and Dattatreya (1995) report that volume of newly issued structured
notes increased more than fourfold from 1990 to 1994, with structured note
new issue volume at under $20bn in 1990 and in excess of $80bn in 1994.
There were two factors in the market in the early 1990s that contributed to
such signiﬁcant growth. First, the U.S. economy had plunged into recession
in the early 1990s, and in an eﬀort to stimulate the economy the Fed cut the
Federal Funds rate signiﬁcantly during this time. The funds rate declined
from a high of 8.25% in 1990 down to 3% in 1992. Investors who had enjoyed
high yields (and high returns) in the ﬁxed income markets were now faced
with the prospect of a low rate environment. Of course for many investors,
particularly money market investors, LIBOR is more of a benchmark than
is the Fed Funds rate. Figure 7.1 shows a history of 3-month LIBOR during
this time. In the early 1990s, many investors were starving for the high yield
levels that they had enjoyed only a few years before and were looking for
alternative investments that would bring them high returns.
At the same time, the innovation found in the derivatives markets was
rising dramatically. New types of structured notes were being created that
provided investors with high yields. In order to create such high (current)
yields for investors, such notes often required their holders to assume tremen-
dous risks. Leveraged inverse ﬂoaters and range accrual notes (both of which
we shall look at) are examples of such notes. These notes in general performed
very well in the early 1990s. But by the end of 1994, a year during which the
Fed had raised the Fed Funds rate six times, the results for investors who
held such bonds were often disastrous. As shown in Figure 7.1, during this
period 3-month LIBOR increased dramatically, hurting investors who had
expressed the view that LIBOR would remain low.
294

<!-- Page 318 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.1: Historical 3-Month LIBOR
7.2
A Glossary of Structured Notes
It’s impractical to list every type of structured note out there, because these
notes are typically customized for the investors who buy them. Nevertheless,
there are a number of basic types of structured notes that have been common
over the years, some of which remain popular with investors today. Before
beginning to analyze structured notes in detail, in this section we will present
a menu of various types of structured notes that have been common in the
market. As previously mentioned, although the structured note market rose
from the interest rate derivatives market, over the years notes have been
created that give investors exposure to a variety of assets classes, including
equities and commodities. We will focus exclusively on structured notes tied
to interest rate derivatives.
Structured notes are characterized ﬁrst and foremost by the type of
coupons they bear. The following list, while not complete, contains a number
of varieties:
• Floater: A bond whose coupon depends on a ﬂoating rate index, such
as LIBOR, Prime, T-Bills, or Fed Funds. As an example, such a bond
may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
Prime −2.90%
295

<!-- Page 319 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
• Deleveraged Floater: A bond whose coupon depends on a percent-
age of the movement in some underlying ﬂoating rate index. Often
these bonds initially pay an (above market) ﬁxed rate coupon for some
period of time, followed by ﬂoating coupons. As an example, such a
bond may pay the following coupons:
Year 1:
5%
Years 2–5:
0.5 × 3-month LIBOR + 1.50%
• Capped/Floored Floater: A bond that pays a ﬂoating coupon,
which has a stated maximum or minimum coupon level, regardless
of where the underlying ﬂoating rate sets. These bonds may also be
callable. As an example, such a bond may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
6-month LIBOR + 30 bps, subject to a maximum
coupon of 8%
Other variations of this bond allow for the maximum or minimum
coupon to vary over the life of the bond. These bonds are referred to
as stepped capped ﬂoaters or stepped ﬂoored ﬂoaters.
• Callable Multi-Step Bonds: A bond that pays a “teaser” rate of
interest, higher than a comparable ﬁxed rate, bullet maturity bond,
and then is either called or “steps up” to a higher coupon rate. Such a
bond may pay successively higher ﬁxed rate coupons in later periods
of its life, assuming the bond has not yet been called (and is referred
to as a variable step-up bond).
Year 1:
6%
Year 2:
7%
Year 3:
8%
Year 4:
10%
Year 5:
12%
Call Feature:
This bond is callable in one year
and every quarter thereafter
• Extendible: A bond that often pays a “teaser” rate of interest, higher
than a comparable ﬁxed rate, bullet maturity bond, and the issuer has
the right to extend the bond for an additional period of time.
Year 1:
6%
Extend Feature:
This issuer has the right in one year to extend the
maturity of the bond for an additional four years
296

<!-- Page 320 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
• Flipper: A bond whose coupon can “ﬂip” from ﬁxed to ﬂoating after
a period of time, at the option of the issuer.6 As an example, such a
bond may pay the following coupons:
Year 1:
6%
Years 2–5:
6% or 3-month LIBOR −25 bps, at the option
of the issuer
• Index Amortization Notes (IANs): A bond that may repay princi-
pal prior to the stated maturity based on a predetermined amortization
schedule and change in a reference rate. As an example, such a bond
may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
6%, but if 5-year CMT < 5% on any reset date,
then the entire bond is retired early
• Inverse Floater: A bond whose coupon varies inversely with some
ﬂoating rate of interest. As an example, such a bond may pay the
following coupons:
Years 1–5:
6% −3-month LIBOR,
subject to a minimum coupon of 0%
• Leveraged Inverse Floater: A bond whose coupon varies inversely
with some ﬂoating rate of interest. However, a multiplier that is greater
than one is used in the coupon formula to amplify movements in the
coupons given movements in the underlying ﬂoating rate of interest.
As an example, such a bond may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
15% −4 × 3-month LIBOR,
subject to a minimum coupon of 0%
• Dual-Index Floater: A bond whose coupon depends on two diﬀerent
ﬂoating rate indexes. Often these bonds initially pay an (above mar-
ket) ﬁxed rate coupon for some period of time, followed by dual-index
ﬂoating coupons. As an example, such a bond may pay the following
coupons:
Year 1:
5%
Years 2–5:
10-year CMS −3-month LIBOR + 0.50%,
subject to a minimum coupon of 0%
6Question 11 of Problem Set 6 gave you an opportunity to look at the underlying
swap used to create such a structured note.
297

<!-- Page 321 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
• Rachet Floater: A bond that pays a coupon that is a ﬂoating rate
of interest, subject to coupon caps and ﬂoors that reset every period
based on the current level of interest rates. As an example, such a bond
may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
3-month LIBOR + 15 bps, subject to the following:
current coupon is at least as large as prior coupon;
current coupon is no more than 25 bps above the
prior coupon
• Range Bond: A bond that accrues a (high) coupon for each day some
underlying index stays within a prespeciﬁed range and accrues a (low)
coupon for each day the underlying index is outside of the prespeciﬁed
range. These bonds are often callable. As an example, such a bond
may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–5:
8%, accruing each day 3-month LIBOR
is between 0% and 4%;
0%, accruing each day 3-month LIBOR is above 4%
• Leveraged Capped Floater: A bond that behaves like a ﬂoating rate
note when the underlying index is below a certain level and behaves
like a leveraged inverse ﬂoater when the underlying index is above the
predetermined level. As an example, such a bond may pay the following
coupons:
Years 1–5:
6-month LIBOR + 50 bps, if 6-month LIBOR < 8%;
24.50% −2 × 6-month LIBOR, if 6-month LIBOR
≥8%
The minimum coupon that this bond will pay is 0%, and the maximum
coupon is 8.50%.
• Snowball: A bond that will appeal to investors who believe that inter-
est rates will remain steady or decrease. The bond is structured such
that the coupon goes up each period as a function of the prior period’s
coupon, provided that the interest rates remain steady or decline.7
Such bonds are often callable.
7The word “snowball” is based on a snowball rolling down a hill and increasing in size
as it goes. Similarly, this bond is structured such that the coupons will increase in size as
it goes, provided that rates remain the same or decline.
298

<!-- Page 322 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Year 1:
4%
Years 2–5:
Previous Coupon + 2.50% −6-month LIBOR
The minimum coupon that this bond will pay is 0%.
• Non-Inversion Notes: This is also a bond whose coupons depend
on the diﬀerence between two ﬂoating rate indexes. Often these bonds
initially pay an (above-market) ﬁxed rate coupon for some period of
time. Thereafter, these bonds will pay either the above-market ﬁxed
coupon or zero, depending on whether or not the curve (as measured
by the diﬀerence between the two ﬂoating rate indexes) remains above
a predetermined level. Oftentimes, these bonds are also callable. As an
example, such a bond may pay the following coupons:
Years 1–3:
6.00%
Years 3–10:
6.00%, accruing each day Curve ≥0;
0.00%, otherwise
Curve:
10-year CMS −2-year CMS
Call Feature:
This bond is callable in three years and
every quarter thereafter
One thing to note is that a structured note (or any bond, for that matter)
can never, ever pay a negative coupon. In many of the formulas above, there
is speciﬁc mention of a minimum coupon that safeguards against a coupon
formula that could take on negative values in certain instances. When we
read the terms of a structured note, it is always implicitly understood that
the coupons can never be negative even if this is not explicitly stated. As
we will discuss in detail later on in this chapter, this sometimes necessitates
that an option will need to be embedded into the note in order to guarantee
that the coupon is never negative.
Another thing to note is that many of the bonds listed above are struc-
tured in a way such that they are callable by the issuer. Such a call feature
is a valuable option and serves to increase the magnitude of the coupons
that are paid in the note. Of course, the issuer will call the bond when it
is advantageous for it to do so, which is usually at a time when it is to the
disadvantage of the bondholder.
7.3
Size of the Market
Since the 1990s, the volume of newly issued structured notes has waxed
and waned. Some types of structured notes have largely fallen out of favor
with investors (e.g., leveraged inverse ﬂoaters), whereas others have remained
popular with investors (e.g., range accrual notes). And in more recent years,

other types of notes such as non-inversion notes have become popular. Ta-

over the past number of years, as reported by mtn-i.
7.4
What Are Structured Notes?
Structured notes are bonds that are linked to some underlying derivatives
transaction. These bonds can be issued by a variety of institutions, including
banks, corporations, and certain government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs).
The GSEs played a key role in the formation of the structured note market
in the 1990s, but in more recent times other institutions, particularly banks,
have emerged as the primary issuers of structured notes. In general, the is-
suers of structured notes have been relatively highly rated institutions, as
investors who buy structured notes prefer to express the views that they are
taking through the underlying derivatives transaction separately from their
views on credit.
The key feature of a structured note is that it is created through an
underlying swap transaction. In fact, the issuer of a structured note rarely
retains any of the risks embedded in the bond. The issuer typically hedges out
the risk inherent in paying out the coupons of the bond (whatever the nature
300

<!-- Page 324 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.2: Issuer of a Structured Note Swapping the Coupons Back to
LIBOR
of those coupons may be) by entering into an underlying swap transaction
with a dealer.
This dynamic is shown in Figure 7.2. In this ﬁgure, an investor purchases
a structured note issued by an issuer. The investor pays for the bond (as-
sumed to be a par amount of $100mm in this case) and will receive the struc-
tured note coupons over the life of the bond and principal at maturity. The
issuer receives the purchase price for the bond when it is issued and is obliged
to pay out the coupons in the structured note as well as principal at maturity.
Bear in mind that the coupons in the structured note may be very complex;
they may be leveraged inverse ﬂoating rate coupons, capped ﬂoating rate
coupons, binary accrual ﬁxed rate coupons, and so on.
As mentioned, the issuer usually does not maintain exposure to the struc-
tured note coupons and will enter into a swap transaction with a dealer in
which the dealer pays the identical coupons to the issuer and the issuer usu-
ally pays a ﬂoating rate of interest, typically 3-month LIBOR plus or minus
a spread. In this way, the issuer creates synthetic ﬂoating rate funding for
itself and has completely hedged the risks inherent in paying the structured
note coupons. Since the issuer is paying out the exact coupons that it is
receiving from the dealer in the swap, it has no exposure to these cash ﬂows.
The issuer is left paying only the ﬂoating amount 3-month LIBOR + x bps
over the life of the transaction. Often the synthetic ﬂoating rate funding ob-
tainable by the issuer in a structured note transaction is signiﬁcantly more
301

<!-- Page 325 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
attractive (i.e., oﬀers lower funding costs) than alternative funding opportu-
nities. The dealer will earn bid/oﬀer in the swaps transaction between itself
and the issuer. This bid/oﬀer compensates the dealer for the risks inherent
in the swap transaction.
Dealers usually create separate structured note desks responsible for the
marketing, structuring, underwriting, and the hedging and risk management
of structured notes. This means that structured note desks need to enter into
swaps and buy and sell options (often exotic in nature) from market makers
of swaps and options, respectively. Although structured note desks may be
captive customers of these other market making desks in their ﬁrm, others
have found it advantageous to become customers of market making desks in
swaps and options at other dealers. In our discussion, we will not draw such
a distinction and just refer to the entity that enters into the underlying swap
transaction in the creation of a structured note generically as a dealer.
How Do Structured Notes Come About?
In other words, why do the three entities (issuers, dealers, and investors)
come together to create the transaction shown in Figure 7.2? There may be
diﬀerent potential drivers in this process, but most often it is a salesperson
(or salespeople) at the dealer (or potentially at another ﬁrm altogether) who
is the driver in the process.8 Salespeople may contact various investors to so-
licit interest in a particular structured note. If one or more investors is found
who is interested in buying the proposed structured note, the dealer would
arrange an acceptable issuer to issue the bond, typically oﬀof some issuance
vehicle such as a medium-term note shelf.9 Structured notes are relatively
proﬁtable transactions for the ﬁrms who arrange them, and salespeople in-
volved in identifying buyers have been compensated accordingly.10
8Structured notes are sometimes sold by salespeople at so-called regional dealers who
often do not have their own structured note desks and therefore work in conjunction with
the desk at another dealer to create the note.
9It is important to note that an investor who purchases a structured note has exposure
not only to the structured note coupons but also to the issuer as well. If the issuer were
to go bankrupt and were unable to pay either coupons or principal in a structured note,
the investor has no further recourse to the dealer who arranged the structured note trans-
action. Investors tend to prefer issuers of high creditworthiness when buying structured
notes, thereby minimizing credit risk while often taking substantial interest rate risk in
their purchases.
10At one point in time, it was common to have underwriting fees paid by issuers of
structured notes to the ﬁrms who arranged those notes, and salespeople were presumably
compensated based on these fees. In recent years, the payment of an upfront fee in a swap
to recoup underwriting fees has been challenging for some issuers from an accounting
perspective. Once the fee has come and gone at the inception of the swap, the remaining
swap is no longer an NPV = 0 transaction and as such fails to qualify for certain preferred
302

<!-- Page 326 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
It should be noted that there is a certain amount of “give and take”
implicit in the situation depicted in Figure 7.2. The issuer will always want
to pay the lowest funding costs possible, everything else the same. That is,
for example, the issuer in Figure 7.2 would always prefer to pay funding costs
of 3-month LIBOR −25 bps instead of 3-month LIBOR −10 bps. However,
lower funding costs that are paid by the issuer to the dealer imply that
the dealer will be willing to pay “lower” structured note coupons. That is,
as the value of the cash ﬂows that the dealer receives decreases, that will
mean that the value of the cash ﬂows that the dealer is willing to pay will
decrease as well.11 The “lower” structured note coupons will be passed on
to the investor. There may come a point at which the investor is no longer
interested in buying the bond if the value of the structured note coupons is
decreased beyond a certain level. If this should be the case, either the issuer
will have to be willing to live with higher funding costs or the dealer will
have to be willing to make less bid/oﬀer in the trade in order to create a
note that will be attractive to the investor.
In summary, there are three degrees of freedom implicit in the transaction
shown in Figure 7.2: the issuer’s funding costs, the investor’s coupons, and
the dealer’s bid/oﬀer. Any of these may be altered so as to provide greater
or lesser value to any of the three constituents in the trade. Table 7.2 sum-
marizes the net cash ﬂows to and from each of these participants over the
life of the trade.
Although the issuer is economically unaﬀected by the structured note
coupons that are paid to the investor, this does not imply that the issuer
is necessarily completely passive regarding their nature. The complex na-
ture of structured notes means that investors who buy them, particularly
retail investors, sometimes have limited ability to value these notes. Due
to reputational eﬀects associated with its name, an issuer typically wants
to make sure that the bond it is issuing is being sold at a price that is
reasonably in line with its value. That is, if it is subsequently determined
by investors that the diﬀerence between the price the bond was sold for and
its true value was egregious, this could signiﬁcantly hamper the ability of
the issuer to sell further such notes to investors. This dynamic helps keep
the magnitude of the bid/oﬀer in the underlying swap transaction and the
accounting treatment under FAS 133. It has therefore become more commonplace for
salespeople to be compensated by receiving credit for a portion of the bid/oﬀer earned in
the underlying swap transaction, rather than through an underwriting fee.
11The dealer is earning a certain amount of bid/oﬀer in the swap transaction. If the
funding costs of the issuer decrease, the dealer could possibly maintain the same structured
note coupons if it were willing to forgo some of its bid/oﬀer in the trade.
303

<!-- Page 327 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Investor
Issuer
Dealer
Net Cash Flow
−Principal
+ Principal
At Inception
Net Cash Flows
+ SN Coupons
−LIBOR Coupons
+ LIBOR Coupons
Over Life
−SN Coupons
Net Cash Flow
+ Principal
−Principal
At Maturity
Beneﬁts
Customized
Attractive
Bid/Oﬀer
Note
Funding
in Swap
Table 7.2: Cash Flows and Beneﬁts of Structured Note Transaction by Par-
ticipant
synthetic ﬂoating rate funding obtainable by the issuer within reasonable
limits.
How could an investor become aware that egregious fees were taken out
of the bond at inception? After the bond is sold, the dealer who arranged
for the oﬀering will often have to send periodic mark-to-market levels on
the bond, providing an indication of the bond’s value in the current mar-
ket environment. If the mark on the bond is too far below its issue price,
particularly soon after it has been issued, this could call into question the
initial pricing of the bond. Moreover, on occasion investors will want to sell
structured notes out of their portfolios. The secondary market for structured
notes is not very liquid to begin with, and excessive fees taken out of these
trades at their inception could impact the bid that an investor sees if he tries
to sell the note in the secondary market.
One ﬁnal point worth emphasizing is that investors who buy structured
notes are invariably taking a view against the realization of the forward curve.
A structured note buyer is buying the bond because he believes it to be an
investment vehicle through which he can express his view on the market,
whether that view has to do with the direction of rates, the shape of the
curve, or the level of implied volatility. Structured notes are created in ac-
cordance with the forward curve; that is, the coupons paid by the issuer
are valued based on the forward curve obtained through bootstrapping since
these are rates that can be hedged. Inasmuch as the forwards are wrong, and
the actual realization of rates is diﬀerent from that implied by the forward
curve, the buyer of a structured note may end up with an enhanced return.
304

<!-- Page 328 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
7.5
In the Beginning . . . Floating Rate Notes
In the early days of the structured note market, the most popular bonds
created were ﬂoating rate notes. These are bonds whose coupons are not
ﬁxed but reset periodically oﬀof some ﬂoating rate index such as LIBOR,
Prime, T-Bills, Fed Funds, and CMT. These bonds are not terribly exciting
in terms of their derivatives complexity. But, nonetheless, this category of
structured notes is where it all began.
Floating rate notes became very popular among investors who were con-
cerned that rates would rise. In a rising rate environment, ﬁxed rate bonds
lose their value. But ﬂoating rate notes tend to retain their value in a rising
interest rate environment. They have very little interest rate exposure, and
the reset mechanism tends to bring their value back near par on any reset
date.
7.5.1
A Prime Floating Rate Note
A Prime ﬂoating rate note is a ﬂoating rate note whose coupons are based on
Prime. As an example, let’s consider the Prime ﬂoating rate note whose terms
are shown in Table 7.3.12 Prime is a ﬂoating rate of interest that banks charge
to their middle-market clients. Figure 7.3 shows the relationship between
Prime and LIBOR in the late 1980s and early 1990s. There are two features
about Prime that are worth noting. First, some people in the market have
suggested that Prime is a “sticky” index. Referring to Figure 7.3, what this
means is that during periods of time in which LIBOR is falling, Prime will
fall as well, but the rate of descent of Prime may be more gradual. From the
point of view of a ﬂoating rate note buyer, any stickiness in Prime would give
it a relative advantage over LIBOR-based notes in a falling rate environment.
Whether or not the stickiness of Prime is an accurate statement, it was a
selling point that structurers would use when trying to discuss the relative
advantages of a Prime ﬂoater over a LIBOR ﬂoater.
The second feature worth noting is that the standard convention for
Prime ﬂoating rate notes is that the Prime rate has a daily (weighted) reset
mechanism. The daily reset mechanism indicates that we will look at each
of the Prime settings daily throughout a period, average those settings, and
pay the average at the end of the period.13 This daily reset mechanism is a
12For a more detailed analysis of Prime ﬂoating rate notes, see Peng and Dattatreya
(1995).
13The daily weighted reset mechanism found in Prime ﬂoating rate notes concurs with
the standard convention on the Prime leg of a Prime-LIBOR basis swap, which also has a
daily weighted reset mechanism. Whereas the standard convention for LIBOR in a swap
is that we set the rate once at the beginning of the period, accrue that single rate, and
305

<!-- Page 329 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Issuer:
U.S. Agency
Principal:
$100mm
Maturity:
2 years
Coupon:
Prime −2.80%
Day Count:
Act/360
Reset and Payment Dates:
Prime resets daily and is paid quarterly
Issue Price:
100%
Table 7.3: Terms of a Prime Floating Rate Note
Figure 7.3: The Relationship Between Prime and LIBOR
Source: Figure adapted from Peng and Dattatreya (1995).
good feature in a rising rate environment. If rates are rising, the daily reset
mechanism allows the holder of a Prime note to participate in the higher
rate settings over the period. In a LIBOR ﬂoater, since we set the rate once
at the beginning of each period, if rates were to rise in a period after the
pay it at the end of the period, the Prime leg uses an average of rates over the period.
The word weighted refers to the fact that the reset for Friday counts for both Saturday
and Sunday as well.
306

<!-- Page 330 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
initial rate was set, the holder of the note would not get to participate in
the higher rates for the remainder of that period.
How Did We Get the Coupon of Prime – 2.80%?
Let’s refer back to Figure 7.2 and keep the dynamics of structured note
issuance in mind in order to ﬁgure out how the coupon in this, and any,
structured note was determined. The coupon in the note was determined by
the economics implicit in the underlying swap transaction between the dealer
and the issuer. In this case, the dealer is going to be paying Prime-based
coupons to the issuer, and the issuer will be paying LIBOR-based coupons
to the dealer in order to create synthetic LIBOR-based funding for itself.
Thus, it is a Prime-LIBOR basis swap that the dealer and the issuer
have transacted in order to create this note. The economics of this basis
swap will determine the ultimate coupon that can be paid out in the note.
Suppose that the issuer indicates that it is willing to fund itself at 3-month
LIBOR −25 bps for two years. Suppose further that mid-market in a 2-
year Prime-LIBOR basis swap is Prime −250 bps versus 3-month LIBOR
ﬂat. Suppose further that the dealer is willing to trade the basis swap 5 bps
from mid-market on either side. Thus, the dealer is willing to pay Prime −
255 bps versus receiving 3-month LIBOR for two years. As a reminder, the
standard convention in the Prime-LIBOR basis swap is that Prime is reset
daily (weighted) and paid quarterly Act/360. Since the issuer will actually
be paying 3-month LIBOR −25 bps instead of 3-month LIBOR ﬂat, this
means that the dealer would be willing to pay Prime −255 −25, or Prime −
280 bps.14
14Think about it: if the dealer is willing to pay Prime −255 versus 3-month LIBOR,
then by “subtracting” 25 bps from each side that must mean the dealer is willing to pay
Prime −280 versus 3-month LIBOR −25.
By the way, note that with a coupon of the form Prime −280 bps, it is theoretically
possible for this quantity to be negative. This would occur if Prime were to be lower than
2.80%. As mentioned previously, when we see a coupon like Prime −280 bps, it is implicitly
understood that there is a coupon restriction implicit, and the minimum coupon is zero. As
will become clearer later in this chapter, in order to maintain a minimum coupon of zero,
it is (technically) necessary to embed a ﬂoor on Prime struck at 2.80%. However, when
the structured note market ﬁrst took oﬀ, there was not a vibrant market for such ﬂoors.
With an eye to Figure 7.3, most dealers viewed the chances of Prime going below 2.80%
as essentially zero. Dealers often wrote these options for free (or a negligible premium),
willing to live with the consequences if they were wrong (meaning, they would have to
provide the payments on this in-the-money ﬂoor, without having collected an upfront
premium for its sale). During the 1990s when these notes were popular, Prime never set
low enough for this issue to be of consequence.
307

<!-- Page 331 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Prime ﬂoaters were often marketed to investors who were used to buying
more traditional ﬂoating rate notes based on LIBOR. There were periods
of time when the initial coupons paid by Prime ﬂoating rate notes were in
excess of what could be obtained in comparable LIBOR-based ﬂoating rate
notes. This made Prime ﬂoaters relatively attractive to certain investors.
7.6
Capped Floaters
Capped ﬂoating rate notes are ﬂoating rate notes in which the coupon no
longer ﬂoats when the ﬂoating rate index rises above a certain prespeciﬁed
level. In this case, the coupon is ﬁxed at the coupon cap for that period. The
buyer of a capped ﬂoater is giving up some of his potential upside; if rates
rise, the holder of the bond will never be able to earn a coupon above the
coupon cap. In return for giving up this upside, the bondholder receives an
enhanced spread to the ﬂoating rate index.
As an example, suppose that 3-year ﬂoating rate notes issued by a AA
bank pay a coupon of 3-month LIBOR −15 bps. Capped ﬂoaters issued by
the same bank may pay coupons of, say, 3-month LIBOR + 15 bps, capped at
7%. The terms of this note are shown in Table 7.4. The buyer of this capped
ﬂoater receives an additional 30 bps margin in his coupon (i.e., the diﬀerence
between coupons of 3-month LIBOR + 15 bps and 3-month LIBOR −15
bps) but will never be able to earn a coupon higher than 7%. By the way,
what prespeciﬁed level does LIBOR have to reach in order for the coupon
to no longer ﬂoat?15
In general, the coupons of a capped ﬂoater are of the form LIBOR + y
bps, capped at z%. These coupons can be written as min(LIBOR + y, z%).
The term y is referred to as the margin, and the term z is referred to as the
coupon cap. Notice that
min(LIBOR + y, z%) = LIBOR + y −max [0, LIBOR −(z% −y), 0] . (7.1)
This shows that the buyer of a capped ﬂoater is actually long a ﬂoater
paying a coupon of LIBOR + y and short a LIBOR cap struck at z% −y.
The investor receives an enhanced margin to LIBOR to compensate him for
selling the LIBOR cap. This enhanced margin implies a higher initial coupon
for the note holder. His risk, however, is that his coupons will be capped if
rates rise high enough. In that case, he may have been better oﬀbuying a
plain vanilla ﬂoater instead of a capped ﬂoater.
15The coupon will no longer ﬂoat when 3-month LIBOR + 15 bps is greater than 7%
or when 3-month LIBOR is greater than 6.85%. If on a reset date 3-month LIBOR is
greater than 6.85%, the holder of the bond will only receive a coupon of 7%.
308

<!-- Page 332 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Issuer:
AA Bank
Principal:
$100mm
Maturity:
3 years
Coupon:
3-month LIBOR + 15 bps, capped at 7%
Resets and Payments:
Quarterly
Day Count:
Act/360
Issue Price:
100%
Table 7.4: Terms of a Capped Floating Rate Note
Capped ﬂoaters are appealing to investors who think that short-term
rates will not rise as quickly as the forwards predict.16 They therefore do
not mind giving away some of their upside in exchange for an enhanced
margin to their coupon. Implicitly, the holder of a capped ﬂoater is also
taking a view on volatility. Since the buyer of the note is selling a cap (he is
giving away his upside in case LIBOR rises up too high, and he will only get
coupons equal to the coupon cap), he is short volatility. That is, the value
of the note will increase (decrease) when option implied volatility decreases
(increases).
In the early 1990s when the structured note market was just taking
oﬀ, capped ﬂoaters were very popular with investors who thought that the
U.S. economy would remain in a recession. During this time the swap curve
was steep, implying that the forwards were predicting that short-term rates
would soon rise. Some investors thought the Fed would cut short-term rates
to stimulate the economy. Some people were willing to give up some of their
upside by selling caps that were high in value due to the steepness of the
curve. In addition, implied cap volatility during this time was relatively high,
which also made the prospect of selling caps attractive.
7.6.1
An Example: Pricing Up a Capped Floater
Recall our stylized example in which the swap curve is quoted annual bond
versus 1-year LIBOR and consider the swap curve shown in Table 7.5. Sup-
pose there is an investor who wants to purchase a capped ﬂoating rate note
16Actually, in the early days of the structured note market, it might have been more
accurate to say that capped ﬂoaters were popular with investors who thought that
short-term rates will not rise quickly. Some investors at this time were analyzing the pric-
ing inherent in these notes not based on using forward curves but, rather, based on using
their views on the market and the optics of the note. The fact that such notes oﬀered a sub-
stantial pickup in the margin (e.g., 30 bps in the note in Table 7.4) was enough to induce
some investors to buy the note without careful consideration of other aspects of the trade.
309

<!-- Page 333 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
that matures in ﬁve years and has a coupon of LIBOR + 10 bps. Assume
an issuer has been found who will pay LIBOR −15 bps. What coupon cap
must be imposed on the note so that the dealer breaks even?
In the top half of Figure 7.4, we bootstrap the curve and compute the
value (from the point of view of the dealer) of paying out LIBOR + 10
coupons and receiving LIBOR −15 coupons. Note that this is equivalent to
the dealer paying out an annuity of 25 bps running. As this ﬁgure shows,
these cash ﬂows cost the dealer $1,070,322 in net present value terms. The
dealer will make up this value by buying the cap that is embedded in the
structure (i.e., that the investor is shorting when he buys the capped ﬂoater).
That is, the cap that the dealer purchases must be worth precisely $1,070,322
in order for the dealer to break even. We must solve for that strike on a 5-
year cap such that the cap is worth this amount.17 As shown in the bottom
half of Figure 7.4, assuming the base vol curve is ﬂat at 15%, the appropriate
strike on the cap is 6.46%. Since the margin on the bond is y = 10 bps and
the strike on the embedded cap is z% −y = 6.46%, Equation 7.1 tells us
that the coupon cap is z = 6.56%.
Let’s now consider a second example. Suppose all the assumptions made
above still hold, except that the issuer is willing to fund itself at LIBOR −
20 bps rather than at LIBOR −15 bps. Thus, the issuer is demanding more
advantageous funding for itself. In the underlying swap transaction between
the dealer and the issuer, the dealer will now be receiving LIBOR −20 bps
instead of LIBOR −15 bps. If we solve for the strike on the cap in this
example, we ﬁnd (in a calculation not shown here), that the strike on the
embedded cap is z% −y = 6.23%. Note that the margin on the bond is still
assumed to be y = 10 bps. Thus, from Equation 7.1 we have that the coupon
cap is z = 6.33%. Note that as a consequence of the issuer demanding an
improved funding level for itself, the economics oﬀered to the investor who
buys the note are diminished. Instead of buying a bond that pays coupons
equal to min(LIBOR + 10, 6.56%), the investor now gets a bond that pays
min(LIBOR + 10, 6.33%). The result of improving the funding level to the
issuer by 5 bps running was to lower the coupon cap by 6.56% −6.33% =
23 bps.
7.7
Inverse Floaters
Inverse ﬂoating rate notes are ﬂoating rate notes whose coupons increase (de-
crease) when the underlying ﬂoating index decreases (increases). Assuming
17This can be easily accomplished using a canned iteration function in a spreadsheet
package.

Swap Rate (Ann 30/360)

20
5.00%

4.88%

5.11%
3
5.05%
25
5.30%

5.60%
Table 7.5: The Swap Curve
Figure 7.4: Valuing the Cash Flows in the Capped Floater and Solving for
the Strike on the Cap

<!-- Page 335 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
the inverse ﬂoater is tied to movements in a LIBOR index, the coupon is of
the generic form18
C% −N × LIBOR, subject to a minimum coupon of 0%.
(7.2)
In Equation 7.2, C is a ﬁxed constant, and N is a positive integer. If N = 1,
then the bond is referred to as an inverse ﬂoater. If N is greater than
one, then the bond is referred to as a leveraged inverse ﬂoater. People some-
times refer to leverage inverse ﬂoaters generically as just inverse ﬂoaters.
As we have discussed previously, in an ordinary ﬂoating rate note, the re-
set mechanism tends to bring the value of the note back to or near par on any
reset date. This is not the case with an inverse ﬂoater. From Equation 7.2, we
see that as LIBOR goes up, the coupons go down, and vice versa. If LIBOR
goes up high enough, the coupons will be zero. In an extremely high in-
terest rate environment, in which all of the coupons in an inverse ﬂoater
are predicted (as suggested by the forwards) to be zero, the note will trade
much like a zero coupon bond, which certainly is not worth par.
As an example, consider the terms of the leveraged inverse ﬂoater shown
in Table 7.6. This bond pays a coupon of 20%−4×1-year LIBOR, subject to
a minimum coupon of zero. The ﬁrst thing to note is that an option must be
embedded into the structure in order to guarantee that the minimum coupon
is zero regardless of what happens to 1-year LIBOR. This option (the one
that guarantees that the coupon will never be negative) will be in-the-money
when
20% −4 × 1-year LIBOR < 0%,
or when 1-year LIBOR > 5%. Thus, it is a cap that is embedded into the
structure.
Note that an investor who buys the bond is actually long the cap; the
cap works to the advantage of the investor to make sure that the minimum
coupon level is always observed, regardless of where 1-year LIBOR sets. In
addition, note that for a principal amount of $100mm of the bond the struc-
ture requires $400mm, not $100mm, of a cap struck at 5%. The reason that
a notional of $400mm and not $100mm is needed on the embedded cap is a
direct consequence of the leverage factor of 4.
In order to see why $400mm of the cap is necessary, suppose that 1-year
LIBOR resets to 5.01% on a reset date. In this case, if there were no minimum
coupon restriction, the coupon would reset to 20% −4 × 5.01% = −0.04%.
18There are inverse ﬂoaters that provide a minimum coupon above zero, but we will
keep things simple here by assuming the minimum coupon is zero.
312

<!-- Page 336 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Issuer:
U.S. Agency
Principal:
$100mm
Maturity:
5 years
Coupon:
20% −4 × 1-year LIBOR
Minimum Coupon:
0.00%
Day Count:
30/360
Resets and Payments:
Annually
Issue Price:
100%
Table 7.6: Terms of an Inverse Floating Rate Note
On a principal amount of $100mm, this would mean that the coupon pay-
ment corresponding to a reset of 5.01% would be −$40,000 without the
minimum coupon restriction. Of course, the coupon can’t actually be a neg-
ative amount, and that is precisely why there is a minimum coupon restric-
tion in the bond. A cap with a notional of $100mm struck at 5% would pay
out max(5.01% −5%, 0) × Notional = $10,000 when LIBOR sets at 5.01%.
Since we need the option to pay out $40,000 in this case, we need four
times as much cap, or a notional of $400mm in order to meet the minimum
coupon guarantee. For every bp that LIBOR sets above 5%, the quantity
20% −4 × 1-year LIBOR will decrease by 4 bps, or $40,000 for a principal
amount of $100mm. Thus, a notional of $400mm on the cap is required so
that for every bp above the strike of 5% the option pays out the necessary
amount of $40,000.
To see this a bit more formally, note that we can write the coupon of the
bond as
max(20% −4 × LIBOR, 0).
That is, the bond pays 20% −4 × LIBOR, except when this quantity is
negative, in which case it pays zero. Notice that19
max(20% −4 × LIBOR, 0)
=
20% −4 × LIBOR + max(4 × LIBOR −20%, 0)
=
20% −4 × LIBOR + 4 × max(LIBOR −5%, 0).
(7.3)
19This ﬁrst equality follows from the identity max(A, B) = A + max(0, B −A), where
we have set A = 20% −4 × LIBOR and B = 0.

Table 7.7: Assumed Forward Rates and Projected Coupons of Leveraged In-
verse Floater in Table 7.6 Both With and Without Minimum Coupon Re-
striction
The last term in Equation 7.3 is a cap struck at 5% multiplied by a leverage
factor of four. This cap ensures that the coupon will be zero in the event
that the quantity 20% −4 × LIBOR is negative.
Suppose that we buy the note whose terms are outlined in Table 7.6.
Further suppose that today’s 1-year LIBOR set is 3%, and suppose that
we bootstrap a swap curve (which is not speciﬁed here) and obtain forward
LIBOR rates as shown in Table 7.7. Based on these assumed forwards, the
projected coupons without the minimum coupon restriction of zero and with
the minimum coupon restriction are also presented.
As this table demonstrates, when the forwards are steep (which occurs
when the spot swap curve is steep, as it was in the early 1990s when inverse
ﬂoaters were extremely popular), the projected coupons paid by the bond
decline quickly. The near-term coupons must compensate for the predicted
below market coupons that the investor is expected to obtain in the future.
When we have coupons of the form
max(C% −4 × LIBOR, 0),
the level of C is such that it balances out the cash ﬂows that the bondholder
is projected to receive over the life of the bond. When the curve is upward
sloping, the current coupon income received from an inverse ﬂoater will be
above market in order to compensate for the below market coupons that the
forwards are predicting that the investor will receive later on. In the case
of the hypothetical forwards in Table 7.7, the investor is receiving a current
coupon of 8% in an environment where short-term rates are only 3%! This
is a huge pickup in yield. Inasmuch as the investor believes that the for-
wards are wrong, and that future LIBOR rates will be lower than implied
314

<!-- Page 338 -->
