Table 7.8: Hypothetical Forward Rates a Year After the Leveraged Inverse
Floater in Table 7.6 Was Purchased, and Projected Coupons Both With and
Without Minimum Coupon Restriction
by the forwards, the bond will provide higher coupons than those projected
in Table 7.7.
An investment in an inverse ﬂoater in a steep yield curve environment
is therefore a play against the realization of forward rates. The investor will
receive above-market current coupon income from the bond based on the
(relatively) low level of the underlying reference rate and is taking the view
that future spot rates of the underlying reference rate are not as high as
predicted by the forwards. This is precisely the view that Orange County
was expressing, as we will discuss in Section 7.7.2.
If, however, the investor is wrong, and rates rise dramatically, the eﬀect
on the value of a leverage inverse ﬂoater can be devastating. For exam-
ple, suppose that a year after buying the leverage inverse ﬂoater that pays
coupons of max(20%−4×LIBOR, 0), rates spike up dramatically as shown in
Table 7.8. In this example, the then-current setting of 1-year LIBOR (a year
after the bond has been purchased) is 5%, and the forward curve is very
steep, reﬂecting the market’s expectation that rates will rise even further.
In this case, the projected coupons on the bond are all zero according to
the forwards. The price of the bond will be very close to the price of a zero
coupon bond of the same maturity.
7.7.1
An Example: Pricing Up a Leveraged Inverse Floater
Recall now our stylized example in which the swap curve is quoted an-
nual bond versus 1-year LIBOR. Suppose the swap curve is as shown in
Table 7.5 and the base vol curve is ﬂat at 15%. Suppose an investor wants
to purchase a 4-year leveraged inverse ﬂoater with a coupon of the form
315

<!-- Page 339 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Y % −2 × 1-year LIBOR, subject to a minimum coupon of 0.00%. Assume
the issuer pays LIBOR ﬂat. What level of Y can be provided to the investor
in the note?
In the underlying swap transaction, the swap dealer will be receiving
LIBOR ﬂat and will be paying coupons of the form Y %−2×1-year LIBOR,
subject to the minimum coupon of 0.00%. That is, the dealer will be paying
coupons equal to
max(Y % −2 × 1-year LIBOR, 0).
Notice that
max(Y % −2 × LIBOR, 0)
=
Y % −2 × LIBOR + max(2 × LIBOR −Y %, 0)
=
Y % −2 × LIBOR + 2 × max(LIBOR −Y %/2, 0).
(7.4)
Thus, we can equivalently view this as the dealer paying coupons of the
form Y % −2 × 1-year LIBOR (without the minimum coupon restriction)
and selling $200mm of a LIBOR cap struck at Y %/2. By selling the cap, the
dealer will ensure that the coupons the investor receives will never go below
0%. We iterate to ﬁnd the value of Y such that the value of the cash ﬂows
Y % −2 × 1-year LIBOR
plus the value of the cap are equal to the value of the LIBOR ﬂat ﬂows that
the dealer receives. As shown in Figure 7.5, we ﬁnd that Y = 16.346%.
Interest Rate Sensitivity of Leveraged Inverse Floaters
Now that we have structured the bond, let’s take a look at how the value
of the bond will change given changes in interest rates. In Figure 7.6, we
show how the price of the bond varies given an instantaneous parallel shift
of the swap curve.20 As expected, as interest rates go down, the value of the
bond goes up, and vice versa. It is important to note that the interest rate
sensitivity of the bond changes with the level of interest rates.
The metric that is most often employed to examine the price sensitivity
of bonds to a change in interest rates is duration. In particular, for bonds
20Once the terms of the bond have been established, we can determine the value of the
bond given a parallel shift in the swap curve simply by shifting the swap curve and looking
at the value of the cash ﬂows of the bond in the new rate environment. This valuation
also considers the value of the embedded cap in the bond. We have made the assumption
that implied vol does not change as we shift the curve. We have also made the assumption
that the ﬁrst coupon of the bond varies as we shift the curve.
316

<!-- Page 340 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.5: Computing the Coupon of the Leveraged Inverse Floater
with embedded options (such as a leveraged inverse ﬂoater), we typically use
eﬀective duration.21 The eﬀective duration of the bond is given by
P−Δy −P+Δy
2 × Δy × P0
,
(7.5)
where P−Δy and P+Δy are the value of the bond when the swap curve is
shifted down or up by an amount Δy, respectively, and P0 represents the
21For a review of eﬀective duration, see Appendix B.
317

<!-- Page 341 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.6: Price Sensitivity of the Leveraged Inverse Floater Given Instan-
taneous Parallel Shift in the Swap Curve
value of the bond when the swap curve is not shifted. The duration of the
leveraged inverse ﬂoater based on Equation 7.5 is shown in Figure 7.7.
A striking result from this ﬁgure is that this leveraged inverse ﬂoater,
which has a ﬁnal maturity of 4 years, has a duration of nearly 10 in the cur-
rent swap curve environment! Most bonds have a duration less than their
maturity, but this is not the case for the leveraged inverse ﬂoater. The mag-
nitude of the duration indicates that the value of this bond is extremely
sensitive to movements in interest rates. The fact that the duration is pos-
itive indicates that as the market rallies, the value of the leverage inverse
ﬂoater goes up, and as the market sells oﬀ, the value of the leverage in-
verse ﬂoater goes down. But it is the magnitude of the duration that illus-
trates just how incredibly sensitive this bond is to movements in interest
rates.
Decomposing the Leveraged Inverse Floater
Why does the leverage inverse ﬂoater have such a high duration? In order to
understand this, let’s take a look at the two portfolios in Table 7.9. Portfolio
A contains a long position of $100mm of the leveraged inverse ﬂoater that
we just structured. Portfolio B contains a long position of $300mm of ﬁxed
rate bonds with a coupon of 16.34%/3, a short position of $200mm LIBOR
ﬂoaters, and a long position of $200mm caps struck at 16.34%/2. These two
318

<!-- Page 342 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.7: Eﬀective Duration of the Leveraged Inverse Floater
Portfolio A:
Long $100mm of 16.34% −2 × 1-year LIBOR Leveraged Inverse Floater
Portfolio B:
Long $300mm Fixed Rate Bonds that Have a Coupon of 16.34%/3
Short $200mm LIBOR Floaters
Long $200mm Caps Struck at 16.34%/2
Table 7.9: Decomposition of Leveraged Inverse Floater
portfolios are in fact identical from risk and return perspectives. To show
that this is true, ﬁrst note that each portfolio has a long position in $100mm
of principal. This is obviously the case for Portfolio A. Portfolio B is long
$300mm ﬁxed rate bonds and short $200mm LIBOR ﬂoaters, which is a
net long of $100mm of principal as well. Now let’s show that each portfolio
generates the same coupon ﬂows. Portfolio A, the leveraged inverse ﬂoater,
pays coupon interest equal to
$100mm × max(16.34% −2 × LIBOR, 0).
(7.6)
Portfolio B pays coupon interest equal to
319

<!-- Page 343 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
$300mm × 16.34%/3 −$200mm × LIBOR +
$200mm × max(LIBOR −16.34%/2, 0)
= $100mm × 16.34% −$100mm × 2 × LIBOR +
$100mm × max(2 × LIBOR −16.34%, 0)
= $100mm × (16.34% −2 × LIBOR) +
$100mm × max(2 × LIBOR −16.34%, 0)
= $100mm × [(16.34% −2 × LIBOR) + max(2 × LIBOR −16.34%, 0)]
= $100mm × max(16.34% −2 × LIBOR, 0),
which is the same as Equation 7.6. Thus, the two portfolios are the same.
But this gives us some insight as to why the leveraged inverse ﬂoater has
such a high duration. Let’s focus on Portfolio B now, which is long $300mm
ﬁxed rate bonds and short $200mm LIBOR ﬂoaters (and also long some
caps, which we will ignore for now). So it’s sort of like Portfolio B consists
of a long position of three ﬁxed rate bonds of $100mm principal each and
short two ﬂoating rate bonds of $100mm each. What is being accomplished
in this portfolio is that a leveraged long position is being established. That
is, $200mm LIBOR ﬂoaters (which have near zero duration) are being sold
to help ﬁnance the purchase of $300mm of the ﬁxed rate bonds (each of
which has positive duration). The net result is a long leveraged position
that has a very high duration. The buyer of this inverse ﬂoater is essentially
buying three ﬁxed rate bonds (and selling two ﬂoaters to partially ﬁnance the
position), and thus the duration of $100mm of the inverse ﬂoater is similar
to the duration of $300mm of ﬁxed rate bonds.22
By the way, notice in Figure 7.7 that when the market sells oﬀdramat-
ically, the duration of the inverse ﬂoater begins to decline. When rates are
very high, the market’s expectation of all future coupons paid by the inverse
ﬂoater is zero. Thus, the risk of the inverse ﬂoater resembles that of a zero
coupon bond in high rate scenarios. And zero coupon bonds have modiﬁed
durations that are slightly less than their maturities.23
22The caps in Portfolio B should also be accounted for when we are considering the
duration of the portfolio. As interest rates fall, the caps will lose value, and thus their
existence will reduce the duration of Portfolio B. But these high-strike caps will have a
relatively small eﬀect in terms of the overall price sensitivity of the portfolio in a low inter-
est rate environment. However, in a high interest rate environment, their price sensitivity
is signiﬁcant.
23Recall in our decomposition of the inverse ﬂoater we said that an inverse ﬂoater is
equivalent to a long position in ﬁxed rate bonds, a short position in ﬂoaters, and a long
position in caps. In examining the duration of the inverse ﬂoater in the current yield curve
environment, we ﬁrst ignored the caps. That is a reasonable thing to do when the inverse
ﬂoater is ﬁrst priced, and the cap strike is relatively high. In this case the sensitivity of
320

<!-- Page 344 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
7.7.2
Orange County
Orange County, a municipality located in Southern California, came into
the spotlight in 1994 when it announced bankruptcy in December of that
year. The bankruptcy was the result of the mismanagement of its investment
portfolio that invested in a variety of ﬁxed income securities, most notably
structured notes. The county’s $7.5bn investment portfolio had staggering
losses totaling approximately $1.7bn.
In particular, Orange County was known to have invested heavily in
inverse ﬂoaters. There is nothing inherently wrong with inverse ﬂoaters,
or their high-octane cousins, leveraged inverse ﬂoaters. But in the case of
Orange County, the combination of inverse ﬂoating rate notes and lever-
age and a Fed tightening cycle combined to bring about the bankruptcy
of the county’s investment portfolio.24 How much the county’s treasurer,
Bob Citron, understood about what he was buying is a matter of some
debate.25
Technically, it wasn’t derivatives that brought about the bankruptcy of
Orange County. It was repo. But perhaps the diﬀerence is semantics. The
essential strategy of Orange County, as we will discuss in some detail, was
to borrow and to make leveraged bets that were long the market. The tech-
nology to implement leverage within a portfolio has been around a long time
and certainly doesn’t require derivatives. But the structured note market
did make it convenient to make very highly leveraged bets on interest rates
(i.e., that rates would remain low). This strategy worked out remarkably
well when rates were low. But when the Fed went on a protracted mission
of raising interest rates in 1994 (the Fed raised rates six times in that year),
the results were disastrous.
Orange County added to its risk from inverse ﬂoaters by adding leverage
via repo. It entered into repo transactions with the very same dealers who
sold the county these bonds so that it would have more money to buy even
more bonds. For example, when Orange County bought a leveraged inverse
the cap to changes in interest rates is small. But as the market sells oﬀ, the interest rate
sensitivity of the caps increases signiﬁcantly.
24This is not to suggest that Orange County only purchased inverse ﬂoaters. In his
book Big Bets Gone Bad, Jorion (1995) states that around the time of the bankruptcy
the Orange County investment portfolio was composed of around 60% of structured notes,
although there were positions in other securities such as mortgages and corporates.
25Jorion (1995) reports that when Citron was asked in 1993 why he believed that
interest rates would remain low, he responded, “I am one of the largest investors in
America. I know these things.” But in 1994, the Fed started raising rates. Jorion quotes
then–Assistant Treasurer of Orange County Matthew Raabe as saying that Citron was in
“a state of denial over this,” referring to the troubles of the county’s investment portfolio.
Jorion reports that Raabe claims that Citron began consulting a psychic and a mail-order
astrologer at work.
321

<!-- Page 345 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.8: Orange County Financed Its Purchase of Levered Inverse Floaters
ﬂoater (which is a leveraged long position in and of itself), it would often
ﬁnance the bond (creating even more leverage) so that it could buy even
more inverse ﬂoating rate notes.
This arrangement is demonstrated in Figure 7.8. In this ﬁgure, Orange
County purchases $100mm of an inverse ﬂoater. In order to ﬁnance the
purchase, Orange County enters into a repo agreement with the repo desk
of the dealer who sold Orange County the bond. In the repo, which might
last typically for some number of days or weeks or months, Orange County
pledges the bond as collateral on a loan and pays some rate of interest,
known as the repo rate. Typically, there is a haircut that is applicable, which
stipulates that Orange County will not receive the full value of the pledged
asset but only a portion of it. In this case, we are assuming a 5% haircut,
meaning that Orange County was able to ﬁnance $95mm of the $100mm
purchase price. Moreover, and very important, the terms of the repo would
stipulate that as the value of the bond fell, Orange County would be required
to post more collateral to the repo desk. Thus, if the bond was purchased
today at par, and tomorrow it was worth only $98mm, Orange County was
responsible for posting an additional $2mm of collateral to the repo desk.
In the meantime, Orange County could go ahead and purchase more bonds
with the $95mm cash it received via the repo transaction. In other words,
322

<!-- Page 346 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
$100mm of leveraged inverse ﬂoaters could be purchased by actually putting
up only $5mm.
Between 1992 and 1993 when the Fed was on hold and rates were stable,
the coupon interest that Orange County earned on its bonds more than oﬀset
the repo interest it paid to ﬁnance some of its purchases. But when rates
shot up in 1994, it was a very diﬀerent story.
In 1994, the Fed became very concerned about the risk of inﬂation and
raised the Fed Funds rate from 3% to 5.5%. The bond market sold oﬀtremen-
dously, and of course LIBOR rates also rose. This served to diminish the
value of ﬁxed rate bonds. Leveraged inverse ﬂoaters were particularly hard-
hit due to their extreme sensitivity to changes in interest rates (refer again
to Figure 7.7). As rates rose, this high duration eroded the value of the in-
verse ﬂoaters that Orange County had repoed. Under the terms of the repo,
Orange County was required to deliver cash to the dealer when the value of
the bond went down. At the same time, with LIBOR rates going up over
the course of the year, the coupon interest earned on the inverse ﬂoaters was
going down. And to make matters still worse, the repo rate Orange County
would face on any additional repos would increase with the general rise in
rates. It was the perfect storm.
As rates ﬁrst started to rise, Orange County initially had enough cash on
hand to satisfy its obligations. But as time went on, and as the Fed continued
to raise rates and the market sold oﬀfurther, Orange County had to post
more and more collateral under its repo agreements. According to Jorion
(1995), Orange County expended around $515mm between February 1994
and May 1994 to satisfy the collateral calls from its repo obligations. Between
August and November of that same year, Jorion’s analysis shows that the
cash balance of the fund fell around $1bn. Shortly thereafter, Orange County
declared bankruptcy as banks started liquidating the collateral they held
when Orange County was unable to meet its margin requirements.
This is a dramatic simpliﬁcation of the dynamics at work that brought
on the ultimate bankruptcy of Orange County. But it contains the salient
features of Orange County’s bankruptcy and the ultimate liquidation of its
investment portfolio.26 Recall that in Figure 2.2 of Chapter 2, we pointed out
that there was a period of time at the end of 1994 when 2-year swap spreads
gapped up dramatically when it was announced that Orange County had
gone bankrupt, and liquidation of its investment portfolio seemed imminent.
The market was aware that Orange County had purchased a great many
inverse ﬂoaters and that when it came time to liquidate, it would have to
26Orange County hired the investment bank Salomon Brothers to help advise on the
portfolio, which eventually conducted a liquidation of its portfolio of structured notes.
Dealers bid on many of the bonds in an auction.
323

<!-- Page 347 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
sell these bonds. Any dealer who bought inverse ﬂoaters from Orange County
during the liquidation would be getting long the market. In order to hedge
this position, swap dealers would need to short the market, which they often
do by paying ﬁxed in swaps. It was also well known in the market that most
of these bonds in Orange County’s portfolio had a few years left to maturity.
Thus, dealers who bought the bonds would have to pay ﬁxed in swaps whose
maturity matched the maturity of these bonds. This explains why 2-year
swap spreads moved so dramatically during this time. Orange County was
not known to have purchased any leveraged inverse ﬂoaters maturing in, say,
10 years. Thus, we did not see a similar move in 10-year spreads.
7.8
Range Notes
Range notes were fairly exotic structures when they came on the scene in
1993. There are several diﬀerent versions of range notes that have been pop-
ular over the years (such as binary accrual notes and LIBOR Enhancement
Accrual Notes [LEANs]). The basic structure of these bonds is that they pay
coupons of the following form:
Daily Coupon Accrual =
 C%
when Index is within Range
Y %
when Index is outside Range.
The coupon C is an above market coupon, and Y is a below market coupon,
often zero. The underlying Index is often 3-month LIBOR. The Range is set
at the beginning of the trade. For example, consider a range note written
on 3-month LIBOR and a Range of 3% to 4.25%. Provided that 3-month
LIBOR remains in the Range of 3% to 4.25%, an investor who owns the
bond earns the high coupon C and otherwise earns the low coupon Y .
7.8.1
LEANs
The ﬁrst type of range note that we will look at is the LEAN. This is a
ﬂoating rate note that pays a very high coupon when the underlying index
LIBOR is in a range and a low coupon (often zero) when LIBOR sets out of
the range. The terms of such a bond are shown in Table 7.10. The margin x
was typically much higher than what one could obtain on comparable ﬂoating
rate notes. One thing to note in the terms of the LEAN in Table 7.10 is how
LIBOR is used to determine coupon resets versus how LIBOR is used to
determine how many days each quarter the nonzero coupon accrues. To take
a particular example, suppose that on a coupon reset date, 3-month LIBOR
is equal to 4.00% and suppose that the margin x is equal to 0.50% for the
trade. Then for that quarter the maximum possible coupon that will accrue
324

<!-- Page 348 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Issuer:
U.S. Agency
Principal:
$100mm
Maturity:
3 years
Coupon:
3-month LIBOR + x each day LIBOR is between 3% and 4.25%
0% otherwise
Day Count:
Act/360
Resets:
Quarterly for the determination of the Coupon that is to be accrued
Daily for purposes of calculating the number of days in a quarter
the coupon is accrued
Payments:
Quarterly
Issue Price:
100%
Table 7.10: Terms of a LIBOR Enhancement Accrual Note
each day is 4.50%. During the quarter, 3-month LIBOR is observed daily
for the purposes of determining accrual. Denote by N the number of days
during the quarter that 3-month LIBOR is in the range of 3% to 4.25% and
denote by M the actual number of days in the quarter. Then the payment
that the bondholder will receive for the quarter is
4.50% × $100mm × N/M × Act/360.
LEANs ﬁrst came into existence in January 1994. According to Peng
and Dattatreya (1995), within a two-week period, over $1bn of new issue
LEANs had been created. Investors rushed in to buy LEANs because of the
low level of LIBOR volatility that had recently been observed and the belief
that there would not be any rate hikes to push LIBOR out of the range.
As shown in Figure 7.9, LIBOR had been setting in a very tight range for
the prior two years. Some people who looked at a picture like the one in
Figure 7.9 thought LEANs were a pretty good bet. But in early 1994, the
Fed became concerned about inﬂationary pressures and raised short-term
rates a total of six times that year (the Fed Funds rate went from 3% to
5.5% over the course of that year). This pushed LIBOR up signiﬁcantly,
as seen in Figure 7.1, which pushed many LEANs outside of their accrual
ranges. Thus, instead of getting above market coupons from these bonds,
many of these bonds started paying zero coupons. Moreover, based on high
forward rates, most pricing models placed very little value on future coupons
on these range notes. These bonds, which had been bought at par, were now
trading more like zero coupon bonds.
325

<!-- Page 349 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.9: Historical LIBOR in the Few Years Preceding the Existence of
LEANs
Source: Figure adapted from Peng and Dattatreya (1995).
7.8.2
Binary Accrual Notes
Another popular type of range bond (which has been more popular in recent
years) is the binary accrual note. These are similar to LEANs except the
coupons paid, provided the index remains in the predetermined range, are
ﬁxed. As a particular example, consider the terms of a binary accrual note
as shown in Table 7.11.
Example: Pricing a Binary Accrual Note
We will price up a 4-year binary accrual note in the context of our stylized
example in which swaps pay annual bond versus 1-year LIBOR. Assume the
swap curve is as in Table 7.5. Suppose that an investor wants to purchase a
binary accrual note that pays a coupon of C% if 1-year LIBOR is between
4% and 6.5% and zero otherwise. We will make the simplifying assumption
that 1-year LIBOR is observed only once at the beginning of each year to
determine whether the investor will receive a coupon of C% or 0%. Assume
326

<!-- Page 350 -->

0% otherwise

Table 7.11: Terms of a Binary Accrual Note
the issuer pays LIBOR ﬂat. What level of C can we provide to the investor?
The coupon paid to the investor each year as a function of LIBOR is
shown in Figure 7.10. This payout diagram is referred to as a binary corri-
dor.27 For the payment in the binary accrual note that occurs in one year
based oﬀof today’s LIBOR set, there is no uncertainty. The coupon payment
in one year will be C% since today’s LIBOR setting of 5% falls inside the
range of 4% to 6.5%. The remaining coupon payments at time ti+1, where
i = 1, 2, 3, are uncertain.
Under the usual Black assumptions, the value of a binary option that pays
out C% at time ti+1 if LIBOR at time ti is greater than X and zero otherwise is
1
[1 + zc(ti+1)]ti+1 × C% × N(d2[X]),
(7.7)
where
d2[X] = ln [f(ti, ti+1)/X] −σ2
ti × ti/2
σti × √ti
.
(7.8)
In Equations 7.7 and 7.8, we have accentuated that fact that d2 depends on
the strike X by employing the notation d2[X]. Using Equation 7.7 and 7.8,
the value of the binary corridor shown in Figure 7.10 is
1
[1 + zc(ti+1)]ti+1 × C% × [N(d2[X1]) −N(d2[X2])] ,
(7.9)
where X1 denotes the lower boundary of 4%, and X2 denotes the upper
boundary of 6.5%.
27For a review of binary options, see Appendix A.
327

<!-- Page 351 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.10: Coupon Paid Each Year in the Binary Accrual Note
We need to iterate for that value of C such that the value of the binary
accrual coupons is equal to the value of the LIBOR ﬂows that the issuer
pays. This is shown in Figure 7.11, with an assumed constant base vol of
15%. As shown, we solve for a binary accrual coupon C equal to 6.786%, a
pickup of 131.6 bps over the 4-year swap rate.
Price Sensitivity of the Binary Accrual Note
Figure 7.12 shows the price sensitivity of the binary accrual note to instan-
taneous parallel shifts in the swap curve.28 This bond, which is purchased
for par, will only trade above par for a relatively narrow band of shifts in the
curve.29 In fact, if the market sells oﬀat all, this bond starts trading below
par. However, the bond can sustain a substantial rally and will still trade
above par. What is the reason for this? This bond is essentially a ﬁxed rate
bond but is one in which the investor has sold options that will wipe out the
28We have made the assumption that the ﬁrst coupon of the bond remains set at
C = 6.786% as we shift the swap curve.
29It makes intuitive sense that this bond will only trade above par provided that rates
don’t move that much. The owner of this bond has sold options (i.e., a binary put and a
binary call) in order to enhance his yield. The bondholder will beneﬁt provided that the
underlying does not move much, as is often the case when one sells options.
328

<!-- Page 352 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.11: Solving for the Fixed Coupon in the Binary Accrual Note
coupons if the market moves too much in either direction. If the market sells
oﬀ, this impacts the value of the bond in two ways. First, as with most any
ﬁxed rate bond, a sell-oﬀwill diminish the value of the bond.30 Second, given
where the forwards are, as the market sells oﬀ, this will increase the chance
that the upper boundary is broken, which will send the coupons to zero. Both
of these eﬀects serve to decrease the value of the bond if the market sells oﬀ.
Let’s examine now the eﬀect of a rally on the value of this binary accrual
note. A slight rally in rates has two eﬀects on this bond. First, the value of the
ﬁxed rate coupons will tend to go up, as is the case with most any ﬁxed rate
30One notable exception is an IO bond, a mortgage security that often increases in
value as the market sells oﬀ.
329

<!-- Page 353 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.12: Price Sensitivity of the Binary Accrual Note for Instantaneous
Shifts in the Swap Curve
bond. Second, a slight market rally serves to pull the forwards back toward
the middle of the range of 4% to 6.50%, decreasing the probability that the
coupons will be knocked out. Both of these eﬀects serve to increase the value
of the bond for a slight market rally.
Next, suppose that the market rallies a lot. While the initial rally raises
the value of a ﬁxed rate bond, a substantial decline in rates increases the
probability that LIBOR will set below the lower boundary of 4%, resulting
in a decline in the value of the bond. If rates fall enough, this latter eﬀect
outweighs the former, and the bond will trade below par and decline in
value as the market rallies further. For an extreme rally (corresponding to
the left-most region of Figure 7.12), we see that the price of the bond once
again starts to increase as rates fall. When rates fall enough, the probability
that LIBOR sets below the lower boundary becomes quite high, and the
bond begins to behave like a zero coupon bond. Any further rally increases
the value of the bond, just as a zero coupon bond increases in value as the
market rallies.
We can use the information implicit in Figure 7.12 and Equation 7.5 to
compute the eﬀective duration of the bond. Starting at the extreme left of
Figure 7.13, we see that when rates are extremely low, the binary accrual
note has positive duration. In this region, the market has rallied so far that
there is virtually no probability that the bondholder will ever receive the high
coupon C, and the bond trades like a zero coupon bond, which has positive
330

<!-- Page 354 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.13: Eﬀective Duration of the Binary Accrual Note
duration. As rates begin to rise, the probability that some of the LIBORs
may set in the range rises and boosts the value of the bond. Thus, in this
region, as the market sells oﬀ, the bond actually increases in value, which
is to say the bond has negative duration. However, as we continue to move
to the right, we see that the duration once again ﬂips sign and becomes
positive. Beyond a certain rate level the probabilities that the LIBORs ﬁnish
in the range decline, and the bond decreases in value. In addition, in this
range we also suﬀer from the eﬀect that an increase in rates diminishes the
value of the ﬁxed coupons. Any further move to the right both decreases
the probability that the LIBORs ﬁnish in the range and the value of any
ﬁxed coupons received, which decreases the value of the bond. Thus, once
again the duration of the bond turns positive, and it remains positive for
any higher rate levels.
2
7.9
Regulatory Response
Even prior to the bankruptcy of Orange County in December 1994, regulators
became concerned about the negative impact that structured notes were
having on a variety of ﬁnancial institutions. In the spring and summer of
1994, a number of money market funds had substantial losses due to their
investment in structured notes. There was concern that some of these funds
would “break the buck,” meaning that the funds’ shares might fall below a $1
net asset value.
331

<!-- Page 355 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
The SEC wrote a letter on June 30 of that year, warning money market
funds about structured notes in general and speciﬁcally about investment in
ﬁve types of notes including inverse ﬂoaters, dual-index ﬂoaters, and range
bonds. In July of that year, the Oﬃce of the Comptroller of the Currency
(OCC) wrote an Advisory Letter known as AL 94-2. This letter was intended
for regional and community banks and warned against a variety of struc-
tured notes that were believed to be “inappropriate investments for most
national banks.” The Federal Reserve followed with its own Supervision and
Regulation (SR) Letter in August, SR 94-45, in which it indicated that cer-
tain structured notes could be acceptable investments for banks but warned
banks to understand the risks inherent in structured notes. See Peng and
Dattatreya (1995) for more information.
The years that followed saw lighter volumes of structured note issuance.
Many investors shied away from the product in no small part due to the
regulatory environment. Although some investors eventually returned, over
the years new investors came into the market as well. Over the past 10 years,
increased buying of dollar-denominated structured notes has been seen from
both overseas investors as well as retail investors.
7.10
Non-Inversion Notes
We will end this chapter by looking at a structured note that has been very
popular in recent years. Non-inversion notes, also called CMS spread range
accrual notes, are structured notes in which the investor is paid an above
market coupon, provided that the diﬀerence between two speciﬁed CMS rates
remains greater than some prespeciﬁed amount. Consider, for example, the
terms of the bond in Table 7.12.
This bond has a maturity of 15 years. It pays the investor a coupon
of 8.50% in the ﬁrst year. In subsequent years, the bond accrues a coupon of
8.50%, provided that the diﬀerence between 30-year CMS and 10-year CMS
is not inverted. On days where 30-year CMS −10-year CMS < 0, the bond
accrues a coupon of 0%. This bond is callable by the issuer starting in one
year and semiannually thereafter.
Starting in around 2005, non-inversion notes became a very popular
choice with investors who bought structured notes. Popular structures have
had coupons that depended on the diﬀerence between 30-year CMS and
10-year CMS (as does the one in Table 7.12), as well as on the diﬀerence
between 10-year CMS and 2-year CMS. Investors were attracted to the high
coupons that these bonds paid. Moreover, investors were comfortable with
the risks that they were taking by buying non-inversion notes. Despite the
tremendous ﬂattening and eventual inversion seen in the Treasury curve in
332

<!-- Page 356 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S

U.S. Corporate
Principal:
$100mm
Maturity:
15 years
Coupon:
Year 1:
8.50%
Year 2–15:
8.50% each day 30-year CMS −10-year CMS ≥0
0.00% otherwise

Semiannually
Day Count:
30/360

Daily for 30-year CMS and 10-year CMS for purposes of
calculating the number of days the coupon is accrued
Issue Price:
100%
Call Feature:
This bond is callable in one year and semiannually thereafter
Table 7.12: Terms of a Non-Inversion Note
the mid-2000s, the swap curve had never inverted since detailed records of
swap rates had been kept in the early 1990s. As the Treasury curve inverted,
swap spreads adjusted (with long-end spreads widening relative to front-ends
spreads) so that the swap curve did not invert.
The swap curve did experience signiﬁcant ﬂattening during this time, and
this made the pricing of new non-inversion note even more attractive. With
a ﬂatter swap curve, the diﬀerence between CMS rates on a forward basis
declined as well. Thus, pricing models attributed a greater chance that the
curve would invert in the future, which meant that the coupons that could
be oﬀered in such bonds increased.
The swap curve did eventually invert in 2006. For example, the 2s10s
swap curve (the diﬀerence between the 10-year swap rate and the 2-year swap
rate) inverted for the ﬁrst time in February of that year and did again in
November. But, as shown in Figure 7.14, the inversion was short-lived.31
7.10.1
The Pricing of Non-Inversion Notes
As with most all structured note issuance, the issuer enters into a swap
when issuing a non-inversion note so as to create synthetic ﬂoating rate
funding. Referring to Figure 7.2, the issuer and a swap dealer enter into a
swap transaction in which the dealer pays the coupons on the bond to the
31As we will discuss in Chapter 9, other parts of the swap curve inverted as well. For
example, 10s30s also inverted brieﬂy in February 2006 and subsequently experienced more
substantial inversion during the ﬁnancial crisis.
333

<!-- Page 357 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.14: The 2s10s Swap Curve in 2005–2007
issuer, and the issuer pays 3-month LIBOR plus or minus a spread. Using
the terms of the non-inversion note in Table 7.12 as an example, the two
counterparties enter into a 15-year swap. The dealer pays a coupon of 8.50%
in the ﬁrst year and in the remaining years accrues 8.50% on the days 30-
year CMS −10-year CMS ≥0 and 0% otherwise. The issuer pays 3-month
LIBOR–based coupons. The call feature of the swap gives the swap dealer
the right to cancel the swap in one year and semiannually thereafter. A
representation of the underlying swap transaction to a non-inversion note
can be found in Figure 7.15. Although a full pricing methodology of this
swap is beyond the scope of this discussion, we can envision setting up
a pricing model in which we iterate for the ﬁxed coupon (which happens
to be 8.5% in this bond) such that the underlying swap is an NPV = 0
transaction.
The Creation of Curve Caps
For the moment, let’s ignore the cancelation option in the underlying swap
depicted in Figure 7.15. For the back 14 years of the swap, the swap dealer
is paying a coupon that has a daily accrual equal to
334

<!-- Page 358 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
Figure 7.15: Underlying Swap Transaction Accompanying the Issuance of a
Non-Inversion Note
Coupon Payoﬀ=
 8.50%
if 30-year CMS −10-year CMS ≥0
0.00%
otherwise.
(7.10)
Equivalently, we can regard the swap dealer paying a coupon equal to 8.50%
less the payoﬀof a particular binary option. To this end, consider a binary
option that resets daily and has a payoﬀas follows:
Binary Payoﬀ=
 8.50%
if 30-year CMS −10-year CMS < 0
0.00%
otherwise.
(7.11)
Then the payoﬀin Equation 7.10 is equivalent to receiving 8.50% less the
payoﬀof the binary option in Equation 7.11. To see this, consider the payoﬀ
comparison in Table 7.13.
The fact that the dealer is paying 8.50% less the payoﬀof this bi-
nary coupon means that the dealer is actually getting long these binary
options (the ones whose payoﬀis given in Equation 7.11) through the is-
suance of non-inversion notes. These options are often referred to as bi-
nary curve options. Their valuation is not quite as straightforward as some
335

<!-- Page 359 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
SN Coupon

other binary options.32 These binaries are a function of the random variable
S = 30-year CMS −10-year CMS. It would be a poor choice to assume that
S is lognormally distributed, since S can assume signiﬁcant negative values.
In addition, the underlying random variable S is the diﬀerence between two
random variables, and thus the variance of S depends on their correlation.
This takes us outside the realm of anything we have looked at previously.
We will discuss further the valuation of such options in Chapter 9.
The tremendous surge in the issuance of non-inversion notes over the
years had meant that dealers ended up owning many of these binary curve
options. There was no natural hedge for these options, and there was limited
demand for these binary options from the buy-side. As dealers became long
massive amounts of these options as the issuance of these notes continued,
the bid for these options declined. Despite the initial lack of demand for bi-
nary options on the curve, the growth of the market of caps and ﬂoors on the
diﬀerence between two CMS rates increased over time. This market even-
tually saw tremendous demand from investors such as hedge funds, which
served as a way for dealers to lay oﬀsome of their risk. We will return to
this topic in Chapter 9.
The Call Feature
The non-inversion note in Table 7.12 contains a call feature that allows
the issuer to call the bond starting in one year and semiannually thereafter.
This call feature is fairly common in most non-inversion notes. Of course, the
issuer would normally pass on the call feature to the dealer in the underlying
swap transaction, by way of giving the dealer the right to cancel the swap.
32For a review of binary options, see Appendix A.
336

<!-- Page 360 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S

Principal:

Maturity:

X × N/M
where:
X = 8% for years 1–5
X = 12% for years 6–10
X = 25% for years 11–15
and
N = number of days in accrual period
that Curve ≥0

calculating the number of days the coupon is accrued

This bond is callable quarterly starting in three months
Table 7.14: Terms of a Step-Up Non-Inversion Note
And the issuer will normally take its cue to call the bond based on the
dealer’s decision to cancel the swap.
The dealer will cancel the swap depicted in Figure 7.15 when it is ad-
vantageous for him to do so. Assuming the curve is steep (in which case the
forward diﬀerential between 30-year CMS and 10-year CMS is usually steep
as well), the dealer will typically cancel the swap in a low rate environment
and not cancel in a high rate environment. If the curve is steep, then there is
a high probability that the dealer is paying out a coupon of 8.50% versus re-
ceiving 3-month LIBOR. The dealer may have economic incentive to cancel
this trade when rates are low. If instead the curve is ﬂat, then the forward
diﬀerential between 30-year CMS and 10-year CMS will often be ﬂat, and
most pricing models will put a lot of weight on the possibility of the dealer
paying out a coupon of 0%. In this case a pricing model will likely indicate
that the dealer should not cancel the swap.
The callability of non-inversion notes is a powerful feature that allows for
bonds with high (potential) coupons to be structured. For example, consider
a bond whose terms are in Table 7.14. Lest we think this bond is ﬁctitious,
a bond similar to this one was issued in 2004. At the time, the 10-year swap
rate was around 4.50%, and the 30-year swap rate was around 5.25% (and
337

<!-- Page 361 -->
S T R U C T U R ED N O T E S
thus 30-year CMS −10-year CMS was around 5.25% −4.50% = 75 bps). So
how on earth is this bond able to pay such high coupons in the back years?
The answer is that it probably won’t. The fact that this bond is callable in
three months and every three months thereafter means that it is extremely
unlikely that the 25% coupons will ever be paid. Based on the forwards, it
is very likely that this bond is called well prior to that time.
338

In the ﬁfth quarter it is assumed that 3-month LIBOR sets at 2.40%. Given
this rate setting, the coupon sets at 10%−2×3-month LIBOR = 5.20%. This
coupon accrues for the quarter on a 30/360 basis, meaning the bondholder
receives 5.20% × 90/360 = 1.30% for the quarter. Thus the sum of accrued
coupons at the end of the ﬁfth quarter is the 10% coupon earned in the
ﬁrst year plus the 1.30% accrual in the ﬁfth quarter for a total of 11.30%.
Given the assumed path of 3-month LIBOR, the Lifetime Target of 13.05%
is reached in the 7th quarter, at which point in time the bond redeems early
and the bondholder receives back his principal.33

The Underlying Swap Transaction
This bond is created through an underlying swap transaction between the
issuer and a swap dealer. This swap transaction is shown in Figure 7.18.
In the swap, the dealer pays the structured note coupons equal to 10%
in the ﬁrst year and 10% −2 × 3-month LIBOR, ﬂoored at 0% in years
2 through 10. The issuer is assumed to pay a ﬂoating rate of 3-month
LIBOR + 55 bps. The swap has a knockout feature: if the sum of ac-
crued coupons over the life of the swap ever exceeds the Lifetime Target
of 13.05%, then the swap knocks out. We looked at an example of a knock-
out swap in Chapter 6. This is another example of a type of knockout swap,
albeit a much more complicated one. The knockout feature here is path
dependent. This swap will not knock out based on a single observation of
LIBOR but rather on the path of LIBOR that is observed over the life of the
swap. One of the things that we will be interested in doing is deducing the
price that the swap dealer is implicitly attributing to this exotic knockout
feature, without having to resort to computing the value of the knockout
directly.34
In order to ﬁgure out the price of this embedded knockout feature, it will
be useful to decompose the transaction in Figure 7.18 in a manner anal-
ogous to how we decomposed cancelable swaps. In Figure 6.1, we showed
that a cancelable swap can be decomposed into a plain vanilla swap and
a receiver swaption. Moreover, since a cancelable swap is an NPV = 0

<!-- Page 376 -->
Chapter 8
Relative Value and Macro Trades
It’s impractical to completely categorize all the types of speculative trades
that people put on in the swaps market—there are just too many of them.
But what we will try to do in this chapter is give a sense of some of the major
categories of trades that people often look at. Please bear in mind from the
outset that this will be far from complete.
Broadly speaking, we can classify speculative trades as being either rela-
tive value or macro in nature. By relative value, we refer generically to trades
that involve “buying” one or more components (which are thought to be
relatively cheap) and simultaneously “selling” other components (which are
thought to be relatively rich) in hopes that over time the long position will
richen relative to the short position, and hence the trade will make money.1
Macro trades refer to trades that involve an outright long or short position
in one component, thereby expressing a view that the price of something will
go up or down over time.
Many investors believe that over the years “good” relative value trades have
been harder and harder to ﬁnd.2 That is, trades in which there is a blatant
mispricing between the components are a lot less evident. The reason for this
is that over time the markets have become more eﬃcient as more and more
1The words “buying” and “selling” are in quotes because when these components are,
say, swaps, there is no bona ﬁde buying or selling involved but rather the paying of or
receiving of ﬁxed.
2This was not the case after Lehman fell during the ﬁnancial crisis. Many investors
believed there were very attractive relative value trading opportunities from the fall of
2008 into 2010 due to dislocations across markets.

<!-- Page 377 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
people have been looking for relative value opportunities to trade, dissipating
pricing discrepancies otherwise found in the market. In fact, these days many
trades that people refer to as (pure) relative value trades are hardly that.
They may contain some other sources of risk, such as having some macro
bent to them or having basis risk.3
In this chapter we will take a look at a variety of trades that people put
on in order to take a view on, for example, movements in swap spreads, the
shape of the swap curve, or asset swap levels. We will also introduce the no-
tion that sometimes people use options to exploit opportunities found in the
outright market. That is, by buying certain (cheap) options and selling other
(rich) options, one might be able to put on a trade that is (perhaps) more at-
tractive than what could otherwise be achieved by trading in the underlyings
of those options. An important point to keep in mind, however, is that when
one uses options to enhance the terms of a trade, this can sometimes lead
to the original trade view becoming obfuscated. In addition, it introduces
new risks (e.g., exposure to movements in implied volatility) that must be
considered.
8.1
Carry and Roll-Down Analysis
Oftentimes when investors put on speculative trades, they expect to hold
the position for some period of time until their objective in the market is
achieved. They may ﬁrmly believe that their view will come to fruition but
are uncertain as to the exact timing. Investors often opt to put on a trade
immediately, rather than risk missing out on the opportunity if they wait on
the sidelines. During the time period that a trade is held, it may produce
cash ﬂows (or accrue interest) that will contribute to the overall P&L of the
trade. In addition, even if the market is unchanged (e.g., if swap rates do not
change), the value of the (residual) position may change purely as a function
of the passage of time. A common metric employed in the ﬁxed income
markets to capture both of these phenomena is referred to as carry and
roll-down analysis.
We introduced the notion of carry in Chapter 3. For a given horizon, carry
is deﬁned as the net interest that is earned from holding a trade. Components
of carry may include, for example, cash ﬂows from a swap, coupon interest
3Basis risk refers to the residual risk left in a trade that consists of a long position
in one asset that is hedged by a short position in another asset. For example, if we buy
mortgages and hedge the duration of this position by paying ﬁxed in swaps, we will
not have eliminated all of the risk in our long position (e.g., we will still be exposed to
movements in volatility, prepayments, etc.). This residual risk is referred to as basis risk.
In fact, putting on this type of trade, either buying or selling mortgages and hedging the
duration by paying or receiving in swaps, is referred to as trading the mortgage basis.
354

<!-- Page 378 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
on a Treasury, and the cost of ﬁnancing a Treasury position. Carry includes
not only coupon cash ﬂow that is paid out but also any accrued interest
earned since the trade’s inception.
Let’s turn our attention now to a discussion of carry and roll-down as it
pertains to swaps. For any given swap and any stated investment horizon,
we can partition the cash ﬂows of the swap into the carry earned over the
investment horizon and the residual cash ﬂows remaining in the swap. Let
(today’s) forward price denote the present value of the residual cash ﬂows
remaining in the swap as of the horizon. Then it must be that
Spot Price of Swap
= Present Value of Carry + Forward Price of Residual Swap, (8.1)
where the spot price denotes the present value of the whole swap today.
This equation holds true for any swap, not just a par swap whose coupon
makes the trade an NPV = 0 transaction. Sometimes the three prices in
Equation 8.1 may be expressed in dollars, and at other times they may be
expressed equivalently in bps of the notional. The logic behind Equation 8.1
is depicted in Figure 8.1.
Any par swap that we enter into today is by deﬁnition an NPV = 0
transaction, which is to say that its spot price is zero. Thus by Equation 8.1
it follows that for an on-market swap that we enter into today, and for any
given investment horizon, we have
Present Value of Carry = −Forward Price of Residual Swap.
(8.2)
Equation 8.2 is very powerful. It lets us calculate the forward price for any
par swap given the present value of the carry, or vice versa. In addition, as a
consequence of this equation, the carry and the forward price of the residual
swap must be of opposite sign. As we will see shortly, this is a useful thing
to remember when thinking about trades.
Consider once again our stylized example in which the swap curve is as
in Table 8.1 and assume we enter into a 4-year swap in which we receive
ﬁxed. Given a horizon of one year, the carry on the trade is 5.47% −5.00% =
47 bps, the diﬀerence between the ﬁxed rate of 5.47% and spot 1-year LI-
BOR, which is 5.00%. This cash ﬂow is received at the end of the ﬁrst year.
Recall that the 1-year zero coupon rate for this curve is 5%, and thus the
present value of the carry is 47 bps/1.05 ≈44.76 bps, or $447,619 on an
assumed notional of $100mm.
Of course, a 4-year swap with a par coupon of 5.47% is an NPV = 0
transaction, which is to say that its spot price is zero. Then according to
Equation 8.2, the forward price of the residual swap should be −44.76 bps,
355

<!-- Page 379 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.1: An n-Year Swap Partitioned into Carry Over an Investment
Horizon t and Residual Cash Flows
or −$447,619 to the ﬁxed rate receiver. The residual swap in this case is
a 3-year swap, one year forward (i.e., a 1x4) with a coupon of 5.47%. As
Figure 8.2 shows, the value of this swap to the ﬁxed rate receiver is indeed
−$447,619. So I got that goin’ for me, which is nice.4
To summarize our ﬁndings here with our 4-year swap, the present value
of the carry over the horizon was positive, and we found that the present
value of the residual swap, or its forward price, was negative. And the value
of the carry is exactly oﬀset by the value of the residual swap, since the swap
itself is an NPV = 0 trade.
4A quote from the character Carl Spackler, played by Bill Murray, in the movie Cad-
dyshack, the entirety of which merits repeating:
A looper, you know, a caddy, a looper, a jock. So, I tell them I’m a pro
jock, and who do you think they give me? The Dalai Lama, himself. Twelfth
son of the Lama. The ﬂowing robes, the grace, bald . . . striking. So, I’m on
the ﬁrst tee with him. I give him the driver. He hauls oﬀand whacks one—
big hitter, the Lama—long, into a ten-thousand foot crevasse, right at the
base of this glacier. Do you know what the Lama says? Gunga galunga . . .
gunga, gunga-lagunga. So we ﬁnish the eighteenth and he’s gonna stiﬀme.
And I say, “Hey, Lama, hey, how about a little something, you know, for
the eﬀort, you know.” And he says, “Oh, uh, there won’t be any money, but
when you die, on your deathbed, you will receive total consciousness.” So I
got that goin’ for me, which is nice.
Excerpt from the movie Caddyshack, released on July 25, 1980. Used by permission of
Warner Bros.
356

Table 8.1: The Swap Curve Today
Figure 8.2: Value of a $100mm 1x4 Swap with a Coupon of 5.47%
Let’s look at this another way. Suppose we enter into a 4-year swap in
which we receive ﬁxed, hold the trade for a year, and unwind it thereafter.
How much money should we expect to make with this strategy? We should
expect to make zero proﬁts from this strategy. When we enter into the
4-year swap and hold it for a year, we will lock in carry of 47 bps, which
has a present value of $447,619. How much should we expect to make when
we unwind the swap at that point in time? In one year, the swap will be a
3-year swap with a coupon of 5.47%. The market’s expectation of the 3-year
swap rate in one year is the par rate on a 1x4 rate, or the 3-year swap rate,
one year forward. We have shown previously in Chapter 3 that for the swap
curve in Table 8.1 the 1x4 rate is 5.644%. Receiving on the 1x4 at a rate
of 5.47% is therefore receiving a below market coupon, the present value of
which is −$447,619 to us as we saw in Figure 8.2.
For a given investment horizon, the roll-down of a swap is deﬁned as the
value of the residual swap assuming that the swap curve prevailing at the
357

<!-- Page 381 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.3: Computing the Roll-Down on the Swap
horizon date is exactly as it is today. For example, consider our stylized ex-
ample and suppose we enter into some swap and look at a horizon date of one
year. In order to examine the roll-down of this trade, the assumption we will
make is that after entering into this trade, when we come back in one year,
the swap curve is exactly as it was before (i.e., the 1-year swap rate is 5%, the
2-year swap rate is 5.11%, etc.). We do not literally expect this to happen,
of course. Roll-down is an artiﬁcial construct that we use to analyze trades.
Once again, the roll-down of a swap is therefore the value of the swap
as of the stated investment horizon assuming that the swap curve has not
changed. It is the value of the swap after some period of time, recognizing
that is has now aged and “rolled down” the curve.5 Consider again our
4-year swap in which we receive ﬁxed. As of the investment horizon of one
year, the original 4-year swap that we entered is now a 3-year swap with a
coupon of 5.47%. This swap has aged one year and has now “rolled down”
the curve; it was a 4-year swap with a coupon of 5.47%, and it is now a
3-year swap with a coupon of 5.47%. When we ﬁrst entered into the swap, it
had no value. Under the assumption that the curve has not changed, we are
now in a 3-year swap with a coupon of 5.47% when the 3-year swap rate is
5.30%. The value of this swap, representing the roll-down on the trade, is
calculated in Figure 8.3. As shown in this ﬁgure, the roll-down of the trade
is $461,319 on a notional of $100mm, or approximately 46.13 bps. Thus, the
total carry and roll-down for this trade is 47 bps + 46.13 bps ≈93.1 bps for
a one year horizon.
5The swap curve is usually upward sloping. Thus as a swap ages, we can think of it
like rolling down a hill, from which the phrase “roll-down” comes.
358

<!-- Page 382 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.4: Converting the Carry and Roll-Down to Basis Points Running
Over the Lives of the Respective Swaps
This quantity 93.1 bps represents the carry and roll-down as of the one
year investment horizon. Many in the industry ﬁnd it more intuitive to think
of carry and roll-down instead in terms of bps running over the life of the
underlying swaps.6 As shown in Figure 8.4, 44.76 upfront bps of carry is
6We need to be a little careful here because the two upfront numbers for the carry
and roll-down are actually converted into bps running over the life of the same swap. By
virtue of Equation 8.2, the carry is expressed as an annuity over the life of the residual
swap. The roll-down, a measure of value of the aged swap, is expressed as an annuity over
the life of the aged swap as of the horizon date. In our example of a 4-year swap and a
one year horizon, this means that the upfront carry will be translated into bps running
over the residual swap, which is a 1x4. And the upfront roll-down is translated into bps
running over a 3-year spot starting swap as of the horizon date.
359

<!-- Page 383 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.5: Mark-to-Market on Remaining $100mm 3-Year Swap with a
Coupon of 5.47% Assuming All Swap Rates Are 34.4 bps Higher in a Year
Than They Are in Today’s Swap Curve Given in Table 8.1
equivalent to 17.4 bps running over the 1x4 swap, and 46.13 upfront bps of
roll-down is equivalent to 17.0 bps running over the 0x3 swap in a year.7
So what does this tell us? Carry and roll-down tells us how much we
will make on the trade if rates are unchanged as of the horizon. Carry
and roll-down expressed as bps running also tells us (approximately) how
much we can be wrong and still break even on the trade. When we started
this example and received ﬁxed on a 4-year swap as an outright, macro
trade, we were presumably expressing the view that the market would rally.
But suppose we are wrong. Suppose that we receive ﬁxed in a 4-year swap,
and a year from now instead of the same swap curve prevailing, rates are
all 34.4 bps higher than they are now. Then we should just about break
even on our trade. If we put on this trade and in one year swap rates
are 34.4 bps higher than they are now, the mark-to-market loss on the
residual trade will be approximately oﬀset by the carry earned in the ﬁrst
7Actually, it is not necessary to go through the calculations in Figure 8.4 in order
to translate the carry and roll-down into bps running. Let s0,T represent the par swap
rate in a T-year swap. Consider a horizon t, where t < T. Denote by st,T the par swap
rate in a T −t-year swap, t years forward, and denote by s0,T −t the par swap rate in a
T −t-year swap. Then the bps running for the carry is st,T −s0,T , and the bps running
for the roll-down is s0,T −s0,T −t. Let’s consider now our stylized example with a 4-year
swap and a one year investment horizon. Recall ﬁrst that we showed in Chapter 3 that in
this numerical example the 1x4 swap rate is 5.644%. Thus, the bps running for the carry
is 5.644% −5.47% = 17.4 bps, and the bps running for the roll-down is 5.47% −5.30% =
17 bps, just as we found in Figure 8.4.
360

<!-- Page 384 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
year.8 This phenomenon is demonstrated in Figure 8.5: assuming swap rates
are 34.4 bps higher in a year than they are now, the mark-to-market on
the remaining 3-year swap with a coupon of 5.47% is −$469,152, which just
about oﬀsets the positive carry of $470,000.
High carry and roll-down is a desirable attribute of a trade because it
provides a buﬀer if the trade happens to go against us, not to mention a
pure beneﬁt if the market does nothing. If, for example, we receive ﬁxed
in a 4-year swap in hopes that the market will rally, and the market does
nothing at all over the course of a year, we will be up by an amount equal
to the nominal carry and roll-down. As was shown in Figure 8.3, the mark-
to-market on the remaining trade is $461,319, assuming rates are unchanged
in this year. Adding this to the nominal carry earned in the ﬁrst year gives
a total of $461,319 + $470,000 = $931,319.
This is not to suggest that investors necessarily put on trades solely for
their carry and roll-down characteristics. But if an investor has a view on the
market and ﬁnds a trade to express that view, high carry and roll-down will
only serve to make the trade that much more attractive to him. Moreover,
if an investor has a trade idea (e.g., receive ﬁxed in swaps) in mind and is
determining the best area to deploy it (e.g., determining what maturity swap
to receive on), a comparison of carry and roll-down may be a deciding factor.
Figure 8.6 shows the carry and roll-down associated with the swap curve
in Table 8.1 for a one year investment horizon. If an investor were thinking
of receiving ﬁxed somewhere in the 2- to 5-year sector but was indiﬀerent
as to maturity, he might use the results of Figure 8.6 and decide to receive
ﬁxed in 3-year swaps, since this maturity has the highest carry and roll-
down. Alternatively, an investor who wanted to pay ﬁxed (and will therefore
be experiencing negative carry and roll-down) but was indiﬀerent as to the
exact maturity might decide to pay ﬁxed in 2-year swaps, since this maturity
has the lowest carry and roll-down.
8.2
Curve Trades
Sometimes people are interested in putting on trades that express a view on
the diﬀerence between two diﬀerent points on the yield curve. Such trades
are generically referred to as curve trades. For example, a 2s10s swap curve
8As mentioned previously, the bps running computations for carry and roll-down are
computed over 3-year swaps, as indicated in Figure 8.4 by the two diﬀerent boxed areas.
The carry number is expressed as an annuity over a 3-year swap, one year forward. The
roll-down number is expressed as an annuity over a spot 3-year swap, assuming rates are
unchanged in a year. Thus, adding these two annuities is a bit of an apples and oranges
exercise. Therefore the total annuity value will only serve as an approximate measure of
how much of a sell-oﬀwe can endure and still break even.
361

<!-- Page 385 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.6: Comparison of Carry and Roll-Down (in bps Running) on Swaps
of Diﬀerent Maturities for a 1-Year Horizon, from the Perspective of the
Fixed Rate Receiver, Based on the Swap Curve Given in Table 8.1
steepener (pronounced “twos tens”) is a trade in which someone is taking a
view that the diﬀerence between the 10-year swap rate and the 2-year swap
rate will increase. A 2s10s swap curve ﬂattener is a trade in which someone
is taking the view that the diﬀerence between the 10-year swap rate and the
2-year swap rate will decrease.9
An investor will put on a curve trade by entering into two diﬀerent swaps.
If the investor receives ﬁxed on the shorter-tenor swap and pays ﬁxed on
the longer-tenor swap, such a trade is a curve steepener, as the trade will
increase in value if the diﬀerence between the longer-tenor swap rate and
the shorter-tenor swap rate increases. Conversely, if the investor pays on the
shorter-tenor swap and receives on the longer-tenor swap, such a trade is a
curve ﬂattener, as the trade will increase in value if the diﬀerence between
the longer-tenor swap rate and the shorter-tenor swap rate decreases.
We will restrict ourselves to consider curve trades that are duration-
neutral. This means that the PV01s on both swaps are chosen to be the same
9It is somewhat customary in the swaps market to identify a curve trade by specifying
the shorter-tenor swap ﬁrst, followed by the longer-tenor swap.
362

<!-- Page 386 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
when the trade is initiated. This will require that diﬀerent notional amounts
are used on the two swaps. By choosing notionals such that the two swaps
have the same PV01s, a change in either swap rate by a bp has the same
dollar value for the investor who has put on the trade.10 In particular, this
means that the investor is completely indiﬀerent as to whether or not swap
rates in general go up or go down; all that matters from the point of view
of the investor putting on a duration-neutral curve trade is the diﬀerence in
the two swap rates.11
Why enter into curve trades? When an investor enters into a curve trade,
he is expressing a view on the diﬀerence between two diﬀerent points of the
curve. Such a view may be driven by a combination of fundamental factors,
macroeconomic factors, or technical factors. For example, suppose an inves-
tor believes that the Fed is going to have to initiate an unexpected and
prolonged bout of lowering the Fed Funds rate in order to stimulate the
economy before it enters into recession. At the same time, such easing from
the Fed may raise concerns about the risk of inﬂation in the minds of in-
vestors. The investor may believe that the front end of the curve will likely
rally as unanticipated rate cuts become known to the market and that the
back end of the curve will likely sell oﬀas the market comes to grips with
the longer-term risk of inﬂation. The investor may want to express this view
by entering into a 2s10s steepener.
Example
In order to delve into curve trades a bit more, let’s turn our attention now
to our stylized example and consider again the swap curve in Table 8.1. The
current diﬀerential between the 5-year swap rate and the 2-year swap rate
is 5.60% −5.11% = 49 bps. That is, 2s5s (in swaps) is 49 bps.12 Suppose
10With the passage of time and changes in swap rates, a duration-neutral curve trade
often does not remain duration-neutral. Many investors will readjust the notional on one
or both legs of the trade periodically in order to maintain a duration-neutral trade.
11Let’s consider a particular example to elucidate this point. Suppose an investor enters
into a 2s10s steepener and at the time of entry the 2-year swap rate is 5% and the 10-year
swap rate is 6%. Consider the following three rate scenarios that may prevail shortly after
the trade is put on: 1) the 2-year swap rate is 4.85%, and the 10-year swap rate is 5.95%;
2) the 2-year swap rate is 5.05%, and the 10-year swap rate is 6.15%; and 3) the 2-year
swap rate is 4.95%, and the 10-year swap rate is 6.05%. In a duration-neutral curve trade,
the investor is indiﬀerent to any of the three scenarios. In each case the 2s10s curve has
steepened 10 bps and is now at 110 bps. The ﬁrst scenario happened to occur as the market
rallied; the second, as the market sold oﬀ; and the third, where the front end rallied and
the back end sold oﬀ.
12Some investors prefer to look at curve trades involving other yield curves, such as
the Treasury curve. If it is clear from context which curve we are talking about, then
we might just identify which points on the curve we are discussing by saying something
363

<!-- Page 387 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
we think that 2s5s will steepen from its current level and want to put on a
trade that risks $100,000 per bp.
We will want to enter into a 2-year spot starting swap and a 5-year spot
starting swap. We will need to duration weight the trade, such that a bp
movement in either the 2-year or the 5-year swap rate is worth $100,000.
Recall from Section 2.1.1 that there are a number of ways to consider the
PV01 of a swap. We will choose to compute the PV01s using the “PV Coupon
01” approach.13 As shown in Figure 8.7, the notional weightings that give a
PV01 of $100,000 on each leg are (approximately) $233.57mm of the 5-year
swap and $538.37mm of the 2-year swap.14 If we want to express the view
that the curve is going to steepen, we should pay in 5s at a rate of 5.60%
and receive in 2s at a rate of 5.11%.15
Now suppose that at the end of the same day the swap curve is as in
Table 8.2. The 2s5s curve is now 5.65% −5.18% = 47 bps and has thus
ﬂattened 2 bps on the day. Since we put this trade on for $100,000 per bp,
we would expect to be down $200,000. In order to value the steepener at
the end of the day based on these levels, we must value each leg separately,
as shown in Figure 8.8. In the steepener, we paid in 5s at 5.60%, and 5-
year swap rates ended the day at 5.65%. So we have made 5 bps (running)
on this leg of the trade, and as shown in Figure 8.8 the value of the leg is
$499,389. We also received in 2s at 5.11%, and 2-year swap rates ended the
day at 5.18%, so we lost 7 bps (running) on this leg. As shown in Figure 8.8,
like “2s5s.” If there is any ambiguity regarding which curve we are discussing, we might
say something like “2s5s in swaps” or “2s5s in Treasuries.” There are even times when
investors enter into curve trades in which one leg is put on in Treasuries and the other
leg is in swaps. For example, suppose an investor wants to enter into a curve steepener,
believing that there will be a ﬂight-to-quality bid in the front end of the Treasury curve
(meaning buying of short-dated Treasuries will cause short-end yields to come down).
At the same time the investor believes that the ﬂight-to-quality will cause swap spreads
across the curve to increase. If the investor is thinking of putting on a 2s5s steepener,
he may want to consider buying 2-year Treasury notes and paying ﬁxed in a 5-year
swap.
13This is a little sloppy. It would be more correct to base notionals on PV01s based on
a change in the swap curve; however, the two numbers are very close and as such many
investors choose to use the “PV Coupon 01” numbers for ease.
14In Figure 8.7 we have solved for the notional on the 2-year swap such that when we
move the ﬁxed coupon oﬀmarket by 1 bp (from 5.11% to 5.12%), the value of the swap
is the desired $100,000. We do a similar thing for the 5-year leg.
15An easy way to ﬁgure out which way to go around in order to put on a steepener is
to hold one leg constant, say, the 2-year, and then ask which way we have to go on the
5-year in order to make money if the curve steepens. If we are holding the 2-year constant,
then for the curve to steepen means that the 5-year swap rate goes up. In order to beneﬁt
if this happens, we will want to pay in 5s.
364

<!-- Page 388 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.7: Computing the Notionals on Each Leg of the 2s5s Steepener for
a Duration-Neutral Trade with a PV01 of $100,000
the value of this leg is against us $699,307. Thus, our P&L on the trade is
$499,389 −$699,307 = −$199,918.16
2
16Note that our loss is not exactly $200,000, which is the number we expected, but
very close to that amount. The reason has to do with the fact that we chose the “PV
Coupon 01” methodology for choosing notionals, not to mention the fact that the (albeit
quite small) shift in rates has caused the PV01 of each leg of the trade to change slightly.
As indicated by our P&L number, the error is quite small.

8.2.1
Yield Curve Trades for Longer Holding Periods
In the previous section, we calculated the P&L of a curve trade on the same
day that we had put it on. Oftentimes an investor who puts on a curve trade
will anticipate keeping the trade on for some holding period as he waits for his
view to come to fruition. For example, in the 2s5s steepener that we just ex-
amined, the P&L was negative at the end of the ﬁrst day. An investor would
likely continue to hold the trade, provided that he still has conviction in his
view of the curve steepening and provided that he did not hit his stop-loss on
the trade.17 In this case, the carry and roll-down characteristics of the trade
may be something that the investor wants to consider prior to putting it on.
Let’s go back to our original swap curve as shown in Table 8.1 and as-
sume that we put on the 2s5s steepener for $100,000 per bp by paying on
$233.57mm of the 5-year swap and receiving on $538.37mm of the 2-year
swap. We are in the trade at 5.60% −5.11% = 49 bps and are hoping the
2s5s curve steepens. Suppose now that instead of taking the position oﬀat
the end of the ﬁrst day, we hold the position for one year. Suppose further
that after one year the swap curve is as shown in Table 8.3. Notice that
after one year the 2s5s curve is 5.75% −5.23% = 52 bps. But we should
be a little careful here. Since one year has gone by, the 2-year swap that we
traded is now a 1-year swap, and the 5-year swap that we traded is now a
4-year swap. Thus, the 2s5s steepener that we put on at 49 bps is now a
1s4s steepener at 49 bps. And, from Table 8.3, the 1s4s curve is currently
5.62% −5.10% = 52 bps. The 1s4s curve is indeed the relevant benchmark
to consider after one year, because as of this time each of the two swaps has
bled its ﬁrst-year coupons, and our trade is now a 1s4s steepener.
Suppose that we want to unwind our trade now based on these levels. In
order to compute our P&L on the trade, we need to compute both the carry
17A stop-loss is a predetermined mark-to-market loss that a trader establishes on a
trade that, if reached, will prompt the trader to get out of the trade so as to limit any
further downside.
367

<!-- Page 391 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
over the ﬁrst year and the unwind values of the remaining trades that we
have on. Recall that when we ﬁrst entered into the trade, we received in 2s
at 5.11% and we paid in 5s at 5.60%. Also note that 1-year LIBOR at the
time was 5.00%, since the 1-year swap rate was given as 5.00%. The carry
in the ﬁrst year from receiving on $538.37mm 2s is18
$538,368,293 × (5.11% −5.00%) = $592,205.
The carry in the ﬁrst year from paying on $233.57mm 5s is
$233,574,545 × (5.00% −5.60%) = −$1,401,447.
Thus, the net carry in the ﬁrst year is $592,205−$1,401,447 = −$809,242. Now
we need to compute our mark-to-market on the remaining trade. Remember,
although we originally paid in 5s and received in 2s, a year has gone by and now
our current position has us paying in 4s at 5.60% and receiving in 1s at 5.11%.
As shown in Figure 8.9, the value of the 4-year leg is $164,013 and the value
of the 1-year leg is $51,224, both in our favor, giving a total value of $215,237.
The total mark-to-market on the trade is the value of the carry plus the value
of the remaining position, or −$809,242 + $215,237 = −$594,005. So we put
on a 2s5s steepener at 49 bps. After a year, 1s4s, the relevant benchmark, is 52
bps. The curve steepened, but we still lost money. How can that be?
In order to get a handle on why we lost money even though the curve
steepened, let’s go back to our original curve in Table 8.1 and see what the
forwards have to say. We can compute forward swap rates eﬀective in one
year from this curve, and these are displayed in Table 8.4. Remember, we can
loosely regard forward rates as the market’s expectation of where rates will
be in the future. At a minimum, they are rates that we can deﬁnitively lock
in by trading forward starting swaps. So, for example, the market expects
that the 1-year swap rate in one year will be 5.226% and expects that the
4-year swap rate in one year will be 5.772%. Note that the diﬀerence between
these two rates, 5.772% −5.226% = 54.6 bps, is what the market expects
1s4s will be in a year.
So we put on a 2s5s steepener at 49 bps. After a year, 1s4s (the benchmark
we should look at) was 52 bps. The curve steepened, but we lost money because
it didn’t steepen enough. The forwards indicate that the market was expecting
the 1s4s curve to steepen more than the 52 bps that we wound up with. The
market was telling us at the time of trade inception that 1s4s was expected
to be 54.6 bps in a year, but we only got 52 bps. That’s why we lost money.19
18For ease of exposition, we are displaying rounded notional amounts throughout the
text. However, all calculations are based on the exact notional amounts.
19As mentioned previously, another issue to contend with in this trade is that it does
not remain duration-neutral over time. Whereas the notionals of the 2-year and 5-year
legs of the trade where chosen so as to be duration-neutral, the residual 1s4s trade we are
368

<!-- Page 392 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.9: Computing the Value of the 2s5s Steepener After One Year Based
on the Curve Shown in Table 8.3
Let’s take a step back and think about the following strategy: suppose
we put on a curve trade and
1. Earn carry (either positive or negative) in the ﬁrst year
2. Unwind the remaining position at the end of the ﬁrst year.
left with after a year is not. As a practical matter, investors will often adjust the notional
on one or both of the legs (by paying or receiving in additional swaps) so as to maintain
a duration-neutral position.
369

Our expected proﬁts (as determined by the forwards) from this strategy
should be zero. That is to say, if we put on a curve trade and earn carry in
the ﬁrst year, and the forwards are realized come the end of the ﬁrst year,
then our proﬁt will be zero.20 To see that this is the case, suppose we put on
our 2s5s steepener today. As we computed previously, our carry over the ﬁrst
year is −$809,242. Now assume that the forwards are realized in one year,
so that the swap curve in one year is as shown in Table 8.5. As shown in
Figure 8.10, the mark-to-market of the remaining 1s4s trade assuming the
forwards are realized is exactly $1,401,447 −$592,205 = $809,242. Thus,
assuming the forwards are realized, our proﬁt is zero. We put on a steepener
and the market expected the curve to steepen. We earned negative carry in
the ﬁrst year (intuitively, we “had to pay” carry in the ﬁrst year since we were
putting on a steepener when the market expected the curve to steepen; if we
had instead put on a ﬂattener, we would have been going against the forwards
and would have “gotten paid,” that is, earned positive carry, in the ﬁrst year
20To say that the forwards are realized in one year means that in one year from now the
prevailing swap curve is the one suggested by the forwards. That is, given our example, it
means that the prevailing swap curve in one year is the one given in Table 8.4, with the
1-year swap rate at 5.226%, the 2-year swap rate at 5.462%, and so on. The fact that this
strategy must result in zero proﬁts if the forwards are realized is a direct consequence of
Equation 8.1.
370

<!-- Page 394 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.10: The Mark-to-Market of the Steepener in One Year Assuming
the Forwards Are Realized
for doing so). When the curve did indeed steepen at the end of the ﬁrst year
in accordance with the forwards, the mark-to-market gain on our remaining
position was just enough to oﬀset our negative carry, giving us zero proﬁt.
Carry and roll-down analysis is a common tool for looking at curve trades
as well. Table 8.6 shows carry and roll-down numbers for a variety of curve
trades in basis points running. It also shows a tabular representation of the
carry and roll-down numbers for outright swaps from the perspective of the
ﬁxed rate receiver taken from Figure 8.6.
In computing the carry and roll-down numbers for curve trades, we as-
sume that we receive on the shorter-tenor swap and pay on the longer-tenor
swap on PV01 weighted notionals (i.e., that we enter into a swap curve
steepener).

Table 8.6: Carry and Roll-Down on Swaps of Diﬀerent Maturities (Reﬂective
