So what do these numbers mean? Suppose, for example, we enter into a
2s5s curve steepener and choose notionals such that we are duration neutral.
This trade will experience −7.6 bps of carry and roll-down over a year. In
other words, if we enter into the steepener and if after a year the residual
swap curve has not steepened at least 7.6 bps, our mark-to market on the
trade will be negative.21
Sometimes people will talk about a trade they really love (from a fun-
damental or technical perspective) but may make mention that it carries
really badly. For example, suppose an investor really likes the 2s5s steepen-
ing trade, believing that sector of the curve is likely to steepen out. When he
puts the trade on, he doesn’t know if the curve will steepen today, tomorrow,
in a month from now, or longer. But in general he has conviction that the
curve will steepen and wants to put the trade on now so that he doesn’t
miss out on the opportunity. But every day he is in the trade and the curve
doesn’t steepen costs him money.
21Of course, if we had entered into a ﬂattener, these numbers would be reversed, and
under the assumption that the curve had not moved after a year, our mark-to-market
would be in our favor.
372

<!-- Page 396 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
If he is somewhat ﬂexible as to what points on the curve he wants to ini-
tiate a steepener, these carry and roll-down numbers suggest that he should
look for a diﬀerent trade. From Table 8.6 we see that all of the steepeners
involving a 2-year swap have poor carry and roll-down characteristics.22 He
might be tempted to put on a 3s5s steepener instead, which has a relatively
attractive carry and roll-down proﬁle, owing to the high carry and roll-down
proﬁle of 3-year swaps.
8.2.2
Forward Yield Curve Trades
Sometimes people look to exploit opportunities for curve trades in forward
space.23 Let’s say, for instance, that we think that the 1s3s curve (referring
here to 1-year swaps versus 3-year swaps, not a 1s3s basis swap) is going to
steepen. Moreover, we may think that either fundamental or technical forces
will cause 1s3s to remain steep into the foreseeable future. This means that
we think that for some considerable period of time each day when we come
in and look at the screens we will see 1s3s steeper than it is today. Given
our view, we may be interested in looking at forward curves to see if we can
ﬁnd an attractive opportunity for putting on a steepener.
Let’s go back to our original swap curve, as shown in Table 8.1. Note
that 1s3s is currently at 5.30% −5.00% = 30 bps. So we can get into a
spot starting 1s3s steepener at 30 bps, but there may be opportunities to
put on the trade at more attractive levels if we enter into a forward curve
trade, a curve trade using forward starting swaps. Let’s look two years out.
We can compute forward swap rates eﬀective in two years from our swap
curve in Table 8.1, as shown in Table 8.7. Notice that if we put on a 1s3s
steepener two years forward, we can get in at the more attractive level of
5.976% −5.712% = 26.4 bps.24 That is, we can improve our entry level
from the spot level of 30 bps by 3.6 bps. Let’s assume that we want to put
on this 1s3s steepener two years forward for $100,000 per bp. As shown in
Figure 8.11, we need to receive on $1,167.98mm of the 2x3, and we need to
pay on $412.57mm of the 2x5 swap.
Suppose that we hold this trade for two years, and after two years we
were proved wrong and the curve did not steepen at all. Assume in fact that
22This is because 2-year swaps, as shown in this ﬁgure, have relatively poor carry and
roll-down characteristics as compared to other points on the curve.
23Space has become an industry buzzword of the new millennium. We have Treasury
space, mortgage space, agency space, LIBOR space (a fancy way of saying swap space),
and so on. A good deﬁnition for space in this context is the span of securities or tradeable
instruments within a particular asset class or subset of an asset class.
24If we are putting on a steepener, we beneﬁt when the curve diﬀerential is greater than
our entry point. Thus a low entry point is advantageous.
373

for a Duration-Neutral Trade with a PV01 of $100,000

R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.12: Computing the Value of the Forward Steepener After Two Years
Based on the Curve Shown in Table 8.1
the curve two years later is exactly the same as it was when we got into the
trade, as depicted in Table 8.1. Let’s look at the P&L on this trade. The
ﬁrst thing to note is that there was no carry on this trade during the ﬁrst
two years since both of these trades were forward starting in two years. In
fact, the trade that we put on two years ago is now simply a position in
spot starting 1-year and 3-year swaps. As shown in Figure 8.12, the P&L of
the trade after two years is $7,922,288 −$7,562,752 = $359,536. Note that
despite the fact that the curve did not steepen at all, we still made money
375

<!-- Page 399 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
by entering into the forward steepener and taking advantage of the better
entry level.25
8.2.3
Conditional Yield Curve Trades
We saw in Section 8.2.2 that people sometimes enter into forward curve
trades in order to improve their entry point from levels obtainable from the
spot curve. Sometimes people will look to use options to further enhance the
entry level that they can obtain in a yield curve trade. The primary motiva-
tion behind entering such trades, referred to generically as conditional yield
curve trades, is a view on the curve. However, using options introduces other
risks as well.
Let’s go back to our original swap curve as shown in Table 8.1, and let’s
suppose that we are still interested in putting on the 1s3s steepener, two
years forward. Recall from Table 8.7 that the 2x3 swap rate is 5.712% and
the 2x5 swap rate is 5.976%, meaning we can get in the forward steepener at
5.976% −5.712% = 26.4 bps. Suppose further that swaption vols for at-the-
money swaptions are as shown in Table 8.8. Now consider the following trade:
1. Buy $412.57mm of a 2 into 3 payer swaption struck at 5.976%
2. Sell $1,167.98mm of a 2 into 1 payer swaption struck at 5.712%.
If both of these trades ﬁnish in-the-money and are exercised in two years,
then we will be paying in 3s and receiving in 1s from the counterparty who
exercised against us. That is, conditional on both options being exercised,
we will be in the 1s3s forward steepener that we discussed before. This
25Recall from Chapter 3 that a forward starting swap can be decomposed into two spot
starting swaps. For instance, receiving on a 2x3 is equivalent to receiving on a 3-year spot
starting swap and paying on a 2-year spot starting swap of the same notional. Similarly,
paying on a 2x5 is equivalent to paying on a spot starting 5-year swap and receiving on
a spot starting 2-year swap, both with the same notional. If we decompose our forward
curve trade into these four components, we will ﬁnd that we have a bunch of carry in the
ﬁrst two years (and all LIBOR legs cancel out). And after two years we will be receiving
for a year at a rate of 5.30% on $1,167.98mm and paying for three years at a rate of 5.60%
on $412.57mm, which is a diﬀerence of 30 bps. Recall that in the forward curve trade we
were able to get into the trade at 26.4 bps. Referring again to the decomposition of our
forward curve trade into four components, can you guess the sign of the carry in aggregate
over the ﬁrst two years?
The carry must be positive over the ﬁrst two years. Think about it this way: we can
earn some carry over the ﬁrst two years and get into a forward 1s3s steepener in two years
at a (relatively poor) level of 30 bps, or we can just get into a forward 1s3s steepener in
two years at a (relatively good) level of 26.4 bps. We should be indiﬀerent between these
two trades. Thus, the carry in the ﬁrst two years aﬀorded by the decomposition into four
spot starting swaps must be positive.
376

<!-- Page 400 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Swaption
Lognormal Vol
Normal Vol
2 into 1
18%
102.5 bps
2 into 3
16%
95.4 bps
Table 8.8: Swaption Vols for At-the-Money Swaptions
type of conditional curve trade is referred to as a bearish steepener.26 Note
that, conditional on exercise, our entry point is still 5.976% −5.712% =
26.4 bps. However, as shown in Figure 8.13, we will get paid to enter into
this trade. The $412.57mm 2 into 3 payer costs us $5,382,623, and we sell
the $1,167.98mm 2 into 1 payer for $5,785,353, so we wind up pocketing
$402,730. So by using options we were able to get into a steepener trade,
conditional on both options ﬁnishing in-the-money, and we were able to
take out an upfront as well. We were able to do this because we exploited
the diﬀerence between implied volatility on 2 into 1 swaptions and 2 into 3
swaptions, buying the (relatively) cheaper 2 into 3 swaption vol and selling
the (relatively) more expensive 2 into 1 swaption vol.27
It is important to note, however, that this conditional curve trade does
not exhibit the same view as the forward curve trade we analyzed in Sec-
tion 8.2.2. The view being taken in this trade is that the 1s3s curve will
steepen in two years conditional on a market sell-oﬀ. Now that may jibe
very well with our view. Or we may not be interested in this trade at all.
We may have put on the outright forward steepener and really have in the
back of our mind that the market was more likely to steepen in a rally and
more likely to ﬂatten in a sell-oﬀ.
26The word bearish denotes that this steepener uses payer swaptions that will ﬁnish
in-the-money if the market sells oﬀ. Note that these options are both struck at-the-money
forward, although swap rates will have to sell oﬀfrom their current (i.e., spot) levels in
order for the payer swaptions to ﬁnish in-the-money.
27Table 8.8 presents implied vols in both lognormal and normal terms. Given our
discussion in Chapter 5, it is probably more informative in general to look at the normal
vols in order to gauge the relative richness or cheapness of swaption vol. In this particular
case, we get the same answer either way.
What if instead we were to consider taking advantage of the vol diﬀerential by struc-
turing a trade using receiver swaptions? In this case we would want to sell 2 into 1 year
receiver swaptions and buy 2 into 3 receiver swaptions. These options would ﬁnish in-the-
money, conditional on the market rallying. Assuming both options ﬁnish in-the-money, we
would be receiving in a 3-year swap and paying in a 1-year swap. Thus, conditional on a
rally, we would be in a ﬂattener. This type of trade is known as a bullish ﬂattener. If some-
one had a ﬂattening view, this type of trade might be an attractive alternative that would
provide a better entry point than would be obtained by putting on a forward ﬂattener.
377

<!-- Page 401 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.13: Pricing Up the Swaptions
Figure 8.14 shows the payout proﬁle of the conditional curve trade as a
function of where 1-year and 3-year swap rates are at the expiry of the
options in two years. Note that both options ﬁnishing in-the-money does
not necessarily mean that we will have a net positive payout from the options;
378

<!-- Page 402 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.14: Net Payout at Expiry of Option Position (Exclusive of Upfront
Fee) as a Function of Then-Prevailing 1-Year and 3-Year Swap Rates
if the curve is steeper than 26.4 bps in this scenario then we will have a
positive payout at expiration, and if it is ﬂatter than 26.4 bps, then we will
have a negative payout at expiration. Furthermore, it is possible for the
curve to steepen dramatically at expiry but for there to be no payout from
either of the options. For example, if in two years the 1-year swap rate is 4%
and the 3-year swap rate is 5%, we will ﬁnd ourselves in the lower left-hand
quadrant with no payout. Note that it is also possible for exactly one of these
options to ﬁnish in-the-money. This corresponds to us ﬁnishing in either the
upper left-hand quadrant or the lower right-hand quadrant.
There are a few other important things to recognize about this trade.
First, the upfront amount of $402,730 that we collect should not be regarded
as free money. There is a reason we are getting it. We are selling one option
that is more valuable than the option we are buying. In fact, once the upfront
fee has cleared and is in our pocket, the mark-to-market on the trade will
be −$402,730, all else equal.
379

<!-- Page 403 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Second, this conditional swap curve trade introduces volatility risk. If
after entering the trade, 2 into 3 swaption vol cheapens relative to 2 into
1 swaption vol, that will hurt us on a mark-to-market basis. In addition,
as rates move around, the strikes on the two options may be signiﬁcantly
away-from-the-money, which means we must take skew into account when
pricing the options. These are sources of risk that are very diﬀerent from the
essential view on the curve that we may have started with.28
Another Variation of the Trade
In another variation of this trade, we can get rid of the upfront fee and
instead adjust one of the strikes of the swaptions so as to improve our entry
level, should the options expire in-the-money. As shown in Figure 8.15, we
have solved for the strike on the 2 into 3 payer swaption that we are buying
such that it has the same value as the 2 into 1 payer swaption that we are
selling.29 In this version of the trade, there is no upfront fee, the strike on the
2 into 1 payer we are selling remains 5.712%, but the strike on the 2 into 3
payer we are buying is now 5.889%. Thus, the strike diﬀerential of 5.889% −
5.712% = 17.7 bps aﬀords us a better entry point for the steepener than
does the other version of the trade that has an upfront payment but a strike
diﬀerential of 26.4 bps. Once again, both trades are NPV = 0 transactions.
The choice of which one is preferable is up to the individual putting on the
trade.
28There are cases in fact in which someone who puts on this trade is correct on the
curve component of the trade (i.e., the curve steepens in forward space), but their P&L
is overwhelmed (to the downside) by an adverse move in vols. There are some traders
who prefer to avoid trades like this, believing if they have a view on the shape of the
curve they should not obfuscate that view by comingling it with a trade that has a vol
position. Other traders don’t mind taking on the vol position for enhanced entry levels,
especially when the net result is to provide them with a trade that more closely matches
their view. Still other traders might put this trade on with little or no belief that either
option will ﬁnish in-the-money; they believe that the market will rally and are happy to
take the upfront payment from the trade with no expectation of any further payout. In this
conditional curve steepener, there is a payoﬀwhen the curve steepens conditional on the
market selling oﬀ. If the market does not sell oﬀ, the options will ﬁnish out-of-the-money,
and it doesn’t matter where the curve ends up.
It is also important to note that after we put this trade on, as rates move around we
may not remain delta-neutral. The notionals on this trade were chosen so as to provide a
delta-neutral trade, and yet with the passage of time and movement of rates we may end
up long or short the market. In practice, investors may tweak the notional on either or
both legs of the trade over time in order to remain delta-neutral.
29We have assumed for simplicity that changing the strike on the 2 into 3 payer swaption
does not change the implied vol used to price the option. That is, we have assumed
ﬂat skew. Given the skew prevalent in the swaption market, this assumption may be
inaccurate.
380

<!-- Page 404 -->
Figure 8.15: Solving for the Strike on the 2 into 3 Payer Such That It Is
Worth the Same as the 2 into 1 Payer

<!-- Page 405 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
8.3
Trading Swap Spreads
Recall from Section 2.2.1 that the summer of 2003 was a very volatile period
of time in the ﬁxed income markets, and this was particularly so for swap
spreads. Figure 2.4 displayed the movements in swap spreads and Treasury
yields during this time. This was a period of time that was ripe for specu-
lation on the direction of swap spreads. Those who had insight into the dy-
namics that caused swap spreads to increase dramatically during this time,
then only to collapse again toward the end of the summer, could make huge
proﬁts if they put on the right trades at the right time.30
As we discussed in Chapter 2, if we are long swap spreads or buy swap
spreads, we are expressing the view that swap spreads increase. If we are
short spreads or we have sold spreads, we are expressing the view that swap
spreads decrease.31 Figure 8.16 shows an example of a client entering into
a long spread position on 5-year swap spreads. As shown in this ﬁgure, the
client pays ﬁxed in ﬁve years and simultaneously buys (a duration-weighted
amount of) 5-year Treasuries.32 Oftentimes, the client who puts on a spread
position trades the Treasuries with the swap dealer himself. Thus, the swap
dealer has on the reverse spread position that the client has put on. From the
point of view of the client, the position shown in Figure 8.16 will beneﬁt if
5-year swap spreads widen. Intuitively, if the client has paid ﬁxed at a spread
of 21 and a moment later spreads are at 22, that is good for him. By paying
ﬁxed and buying Treasuries, he has hedged against the duration position in-
herent in the swap but remains exposed to changes in swap spreads.
To take a particular example, suppose an investor wants to go long
5-year swap spreads. Refer now to the composite page depicted in Table 1.1.
An investor who wants to buy spreads will be paying ﬁxed on the oﬀer side,
30Those who entered into trades too soon that expressed the view that the move in swap
spreads was overdone after swaps spreads increased, say, the ﬁrst 10 bps that summer,
were quickly underwater and were often stopped out.
31Some would classify a swap spread trade as a macro trade, since it is a trade in which
one takes an outright view on a ﬁnancial variable either going up or going down. However,
as will become clear in Section 8.4, it is also correct to view a swap spread as the relative
credit spread or liquidity premium between Treasuries and the swap curve, which suggests
more of a relative value bent.
32A Treasury security that matures in exactly ﬁve years will typically have a duration
that is greater than a spot starting 5-year swap. This would suggest that the duration-
weighted number of 5-year Treasuries used to hedge a swap is always less than the notional
of the swap itself. However, whereas the maturity of a spot starting 5-year swap is exactly
ﬁve years, the maturity of the on-the-run 5-year Treasury may be considerably less than
ﬁve years which, everything else the same, will increase the number of Treasuries needed
to make the trade duration-neutral. From a practical point of view, for a 5-year swap with
a notional of $100mm, the duration-weighted number of 5-year Treasuries typically varies
between $97mm and $103mm.
382

<!-- Page 406 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.16: Entering in a Long Swap Spread Trade
and thus it is the oﬀer side spread in 5-years of 21 bps that is relevant
here. Conversely, if the investor wanted to sell spreads, he would receive
ﬁxed on the bid side, and thus the bid side spread of 17 would be where
he would get done. Suppose that the investor wants to pay on $100mm
notional of the swap, and further suppose that the duration-weighted num-
ber of 5-year Treasuries he should buy is $98mm. Assume that the investor
and swaps dealer agree to exchange the Treasuries at the price of 101-15 3
4,
which equates to a yield of 1.438%.33 Thus, the ﬁxed rate in the swap is
1.438% + 0.21% = 1.648%.
Suppose that as we approach the end of the trading day, the market for
5-year swap spreads is 22 on the bid side and 26 on the oﬀer side, and the
investor decides to unwind his position. In order to unwind, the investor is
going to have to receive ﬁxed, and thus it is the bid side of the spread market
33The exact price at which the two counterparties agree to trade Treasuries is not very
crucial in determining the economics of a spread trade. Any price that is agreed on is
converted to a yield, which is used to determine the ﬁxed rate in the swap. So if the
investor suggests a lower price to sell the Treasuries to the dealer (which equates to a
higher yield), a higher yield will be used to determine the swap rate that the investor
will be paying. So the lower price at which the Treasuries are sold, although seemingly
advantageous, is exactly oﬀset by the higher swap rate that will be paid. Thus, assuming
the hedge is delta-neutral, there is no real advantage to be had by trying to alter the
Treasury price from one level to another. The market standard is to set the Treasury
price on-market, meaning at the price at which the Treasuries are trading at that time.
383

<!-- Page 407 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
that is relevant. Suppose that, at the time, 5-year Treasuries are trading at
101-18+ (which is a yield of 1.420%), and the two counterparties agree to
exchange Treasuries at that price.
Intuitively, the investor bought spreads at 21, and he is getting out at
22, so we will expect that his P&L will be the present value of 1 bp running
on the 5-year swap.34 As a check for the P&L on this trade, we can go ahead
and compute the present value of 1 bp running by setting
PMT
=
1 bp/2 = 0.5 bp

1.64%/2,
which suggests that the P&L on the trade should be around $48,000. In
order to determine his P&L exactly, we need to consider both the gain or
loss implicit in the Treasury position as well as the unwind of the swap.
Again, this is a spread trade, and thus its value does not depend on move-
ments in Treasuries. However, it is most appropriate to consider the change
in value of each component of the position. Although the 5-year Treasury
price has moved, we expect any gain in the Treasury to be oﬀset by a loss in
the swap due to the movement in the Treasury component of the rate. The
trader bought Treasuries at 101-15 3
4 and sold them back later that day at
101-18+. Thus, he has a gain of 2 3
4 ticks on his Treasury position, which is
worth
$98mm ×

= $84,218.75.
(8.3)
4
In order to ﬁgure out the gain or loss in the swap, recall that the trader had
originally paid ﬁxed at 1.648%, and in order to unwind that position, the
trader will essentially be receiving ﬁxed at the now-prevailing swap rate of
1.42% + 0.22% = 1.64%. Thus, the swap is out-of-the-money to the trader,
and it is as if the trader is paying an annuity of 1.648% −1.64% = 0.8 bps.
In order to ﬁnd the approximate value of the swap, set
PMT
=
0.8 bp/2 = 0.4 bp
n
=
10

=
1.64%/2,
and we solve for PV = 3.8 bps of the notional, or around $38,000. Thus,
the total proﬁt on the trade is the proﬁt from the Treasuries as computed
34In this example, mid-market swap spreads have actually moved 5 bps on the day, but
the trade has made only a 1 bp proﬁt. This is due to the 4 bp bid/oﬀer in swap spreads
that the investor had to overcome. Oftentimes the bid/oﬀer spread in swap spreads is
much tighter than this.
384

<!-- Page 408 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
in Equation 8.3, less the loss on the swap, which is $84,218.75 −$38,000 ≈
$46,000. This is very close to the number $48,000 that we suggested would
be in the ballpark.
8.3.1
Spread Trades for Longer Holding Periods
Thus far we have looked at a spread trade that was unwound the same day
that it was put on. Oftentimes people hold spread trades for days, weeks,
or even months. The valuation of such trades becomes a little bit more
complicated. If the trade is not being unwound the same day that it was
put on, then the Treasury position in Figure 8.16 must be ﬁnanced. The
investor will need to enter into a repurchase agreement for these purposes.
The valuation of the trade to the investor will incorporate the ﬁnancing costs
that he must bear in order to hold the Treasuries, as well as interest earned
on the long Treasury position itself. In addition, once we get beyond the
ﬁrst day of the trade, the investor starts accruing the ﬁxed rate and the
ﬂoating rate on the swap. Collectively, all of these daily (accrued) cash ﬂows
constitute the carry of the position.
Depending on the relative level of swap spreads, LIBOR, and the repo
rate, the carry may be either positive or negative on the trade. For relatively
short holding periods, the carry of the trade is typically of lesser signiﬁcance
than the reason the trade was put on in the ﬁrst place, namely, to express
a view on a move in the underlying swap spread. However, for trades that
are held for long periods of time, the carry can deﬁnitely add up, having a
signiﬁcant impact on the P&L of the trade.
Of course, in addition to the carry on the trade, we must also consider
the mark-to-market of the remaining position. Suppose, for example, that
we hold a 5-year spread trade for six months and then decide to unwind it.
We will have to consider the carry over the six-month holding period. When
it comes time to unwind the trade, it will no longer be appropriate to look
at where 5-year swap spreads are trading; six months have gone by and the
5-year spread trade is now a 4.5-year spread trade. That is, our trade has
rolled down the curve, and its current valuation is now a function of 4.5-year
swaps rates and 4.5-year Treasury yields, not 5-year swap rates and 5-year
Treasuries.35
35Unlike carry and roll-down analysis, we must value the rolled-down swap as a func-
tion of where the market currently is, as compared to the ex ante analysis in which we
assume the swap curve is unchanged between the initiation of the trade and the investment
horizon.
385

<!-- Page 409 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Example
Consider once again our stylized example and suppose an investor buys
4-year swap spreads. The investor has the view that swap spreads will in-
crease. Assume the market is as indicated in Table 8.1. In order to proceed
with this example, we will make the following simplifying assumptions in our
stylized example:
• Treasuries of each yearly maturity are issued annually on the same day
at a price of par.
• Today is the day upon which Treasuries have been issued.
When the investor buys $100mm 4-year spreads at 28, he pays ﬁxed in a
$100mm 4-year swap and buys $100mm 4-year Treasuries at a price of par.36
Note that since the 4-year Treasury has been issued today at a price of par,
its coupon is 5.19%. Assume the investor plans to hold this position for a
year and ﬁnances the Treasuries by entering into a term repo agreement at
a rate of 4.80%. Furthermore, assume there is no haircut. Suppose that a
year later the market is as presented in Table 8.9. Let’s look at the P&L on
the trade. The carry on the trade over the year is composed of the coupon
earned on the Treasury, the cost of ﬁnancing the Treasury, and the net cash
ﬂow on the swap. The coupon interest earned on the Treasury is
$100mm × 5.19% = $5,190,000.
The cost of ﬁnancing the Treasuries is
$100mm × 4.80% = $4,800,000.
And the net cash ﬂow on the swap is
$100mm × (5.00% −5.47%) = −$470,000.
Thus, the net carry on the trade for the year is $5,190,000 −$4,800,000 −
$470,000 = −$80,000.
We now need to consider the mark-to-market on the outstanding posi-
tion in a year. In fact, let’s suppose that the investor decides to unwind
the spread trade at this time. Thus, he will sell the Treasuries and unwind
the swap. The Treasuries that were bought one year ago at par now have
three years remaining and are yielding 5.19%. Since this is the coupon on
36Based on the diﬀerent PV01s of the swap and the Treasury, a duration-weighted
Treasury hedge would consist of $99.73mm Treasuries. We will just round this to $100mm
Treasuries.
386

Table 8.9: The Market in One Year
the bond, this means that the Treasuries are trading at par.37 Thus, there
will be no P&L associated with the sale since they were purchased at this
price (i.e., the sale of the Treasuries at par will provide the proceeds needed
to oﬀset the funds needed to close out the repo transaction).38
Now let’s consider an unwind of the swap. The investor is currently paying
5.47% in a swap with three years remaining. The 3-year swap rate is 5.46%.
It can be shown that the mark-to-market on this trade is −$26,999. Thus,
the total P&L on the trade is
−$80,000 −$26,999 = −$106,999.
In this case, the negative carry on the trade (which was −$80, 000) was a
major contributing factor to the P&L loss on the trade.39
2
8.3.2
Spread of Spread Trades
Sometimes people put on spread trades that do not involve the outright
movement in a single swap spread but rather the relative movement of one
swap spread to another. Such a trade is referred to as a spread of spreads
trade. Figure 8.17 shows a graph of both 10-year and 2-year swap spreads
37We are making the implicit assumption here that there is no premium for on-the-run
Treasury securities. In a year, the 4-year Treasuries that we had purchased at the inception
of the trade are now 3-year oﬀ-the-run Treasuries with a coupon of 5.19%. And a newly
issued 3-year Treasury issued at par also has a yield of 5.19%.
38The total P&L on the Treasury should incorporate the ﬁnancing charge incurred on
the Treasuries.
39In this trade, the investor bought 4-year spreads at 28, held the position for a year,
and took a loss when spreads ﬁnished at 27. How much would spreads have had to widen
in order for the investor to break even?
When the investor pays ﬁxed in a 4-year swap today and holds the position for a year, he
will experience negative carry of 5.00% −5.47% = 47 bps. We have previously shown that
the 1x4 swap rate given the curve in Table 8.1 is 5.644%. That is, the market expects that
the 3-year swap rate in one year will be 5.644%. Although we are experiencing negative
carry in the ﬁrst year, the expected mark-to-market on the residual 3-year swap with a
387

<!-- Page 411 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.17: The Spread of Spreads Between 10-Year Swap Spreads and
2-Year Swap Spreads
as well as the diﬀerence between them. People often look at these spread of
spread relationships and look for trading opportunities. Sometimes people
put these trades on to take a view on either the credit curve (using swap
coupon of 5.47% in a year exactly oﬀsets the negative carry in the ﬁrst year. We know
this from Equation 8.1.
When we buy $100mm 4-year Treasuries with a coupon of 5.19% at par and ﬁnance the
position for a year at 4.80%, we earn carry of $390,000 for the year (the diﬀerence between
the coupon interest of $5,190,000 and the ﬁnancing costs of $4,800,000). By adapting Equa-
tion 8.1 for Treasuries, we can lock in a forward price of $100mm −$390,000 = $99.61mm
at which we can sell these Treasuries in a year. The forward yield of the Treasury, y, cor-
responding to this price solves
$5,190,000
1 + y
+ $5,190,000
(1 + y)2
+ $105,190,000
(1 + y)3
= $99.61mm,
or y = 5.334%. Note that in a year from now the 4-year Treasuries that we purchased
will have three years until maturity, and a new 3-year Treasury will be auctioned. Swap
spreads are typically quoted over the on-the-run Treasury of the appropriate maturity. We
will assume (somewhat artiﬁcially) that there is no premium for on-the-run Treasuries,
and thus the newly issued 3-year will also have a yield of 5.334%. Thus, the forward swap
spread must be equal to 5.644% −5.334% = 31.0 bps. Thus, we needed spreads to widen
out 31.0 −28.0 = 3.0 bps from the spot level of spreads in order to break even.
388

<!-- Page 412 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
spreads as a proxy for credit spreads), forward GC-LIBOR expectations, or a
view on future Treasury supply. Other times people are interested in putting
on these trades when the spread of spreads gets out of whack, meaning it
deviates from its long-run mean signiﬁcantly. If an investor believed that the
spread of spreads between 10s and 2s was too narrow, he might look to put
on a trade that will proﬁt if the spread of spreads widens back out. This
would amount to simultaneously buying 10-year spreads and selling 2-year
spreads, as discussed earlier. Typically, one would duration-weight each leg
of the trade such that the value of a bp move in each spread would have the
same dollar value.
8.3.3
Conditional Spread Trades
As mentioned in the introduction to this chapter, sometimes people use
options to exploit opportunities they ﬁnd in the outright market. This typ-
ically involves buying one or more options that are considered “cheap” and
simultaneously selling one or more options that are considered “rich.” Buy-
ing and selling a combination of options may put an investor in a trade that,
at option expiry, has “better levels” than could otherwise be obtained in the
outright market. But this is not going to leave the investor in a trade that
exhibits the same exact view as the original trade that didn’t employ options,
and it will also introduce a new set of risks that should be considered.
Example
Suppose that 10-year Treasury yields are at 5.66%, 10-year swap rates are at
6.05%, and thus 10-year swap spreads are at 39 bps. Consider the following
trade:
• Buy $100mm of a 6 month into 10 year payer swaption struck at 6.15%
for 62 bps.
• Sell $100mm of a 6-month put option on the then-on-the-run 10-year
Treasury struck at 5.80% for 66 bps.40
There are a few things worth noting about this trade. First, right oﬀthe
bat an investor who puts this trade on puts $40,000 in his pocket. He sells
one option to a dealer for 66 bps and buys the other from the dealer for
62 bps, netting himself 4 bps, which, on a notional of $100mm, is $40,000.
We should not refer to this as proﬁt; this is simply the net premium received
for entering the trade. In fact, the moment that the investor takes in this
40A put option on a particular Treasury is an option that gives the holder the right
to sell the Treasury at a prespeciﬁed price. We could of course regard this equivalently
389

<!-- Page 413 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
$40,000, the net mark-to-market on the two options is −$40,000, assuming
the overall trade is an NPV = 0 transaction.
In general, people tend to favor such option trades that pay them an
upfront and shy away from ones that require they shell out cash to enter.
A trade that pays out an upfront is oﬀering no free lunch. A trade that is
structured to pay an upfront payment today will have a negative mark-to-
market of equal magnitude once the payment has been made, such that the
trade overall is an NPV = 0 transaction.
In order for both options to ﬁnish in-the-money, the market needs to
sell oﬀfrom its current levels. If both options ﬁnish in-the-money, then (1)
the investor will exercise his payer swaption in six months and pay ﬁxed at
a rate of 6.15% for 10 years to the dealer, and (2) the put option will be
exercised against him, and the dealer on exercise will sell on-the-run 10-year
Treasuries to the investor at a yield of 5.80%. That is, the investor will buy
10-year on-the-run Treasuries at a yield of 5.80% in the event the dealer
exercises.
So if both options are exercised, the investor will be paying ﬁxed and will
be buying Treasuries—just like the spread widening trade that we talked
about earlier. Remember, this will only happen conditional on both options
ﬁnishing in-the-money, which happens when rates are high enough. Thus,
conditional on rates being high enough at maturity, the investor will be in
a 10-year swap spread widener at a level of 6.15% −5.80% = 35 bps. Note
that the entry level of 35 bps for the widener compares favorably to the spot
level of 10-year swap spreads that was stated to be 39 bps.41 A lower entry
point is beneﬁcial on a swap spread widener trade, since the trade proﬁts by
the amount that spreads ﬁnish above the entry point.
as an option to sell the Treasury at a particular yield, since for every Treasury price
there corresponds a unique yield. At expiry, the put option will be exercised if the price
of the Treasury is below the strike, or equivalently if the yield of the Treasury is above
the yield strike. Let’s consider now the concept of a put option on a then-on-the-run
Treasury. Recall that the U.S. Treasury issues new Treasury securities on a regular cycle.
The 10-year Treasury that is the on-the-run today will not be the on-the-run in, say, six
months. A put option on the then-on-the-run 10-year Treasury that expires in six months
is an option on a security that does not yet exist. This put option is written on whatever
security happens to be the on-the-run 10-year at the time of expiration six months from
now. Although the underlying itself does not exist at this point in time, dealers who make
markets in these types of options are willing to make certain assumptions as to how this
security will trade in relation to existing securities so as to be comfortable putting a price
on such options. Since the underlying security does not yet exist, it does not yet have a
coupon, and hence we cannot speak about a dollar price of the security. Therefore, options
on then-on-the-run Treasuries have a strike that is quoted in yield terms.
41Comparing the conditional entry point on this trade, which is 35 bps, to the spot
level of 10-year spreads is an apples to oranges comparison. It would be more meaningful
to compare this level to where the market implies 10-year swap spreads are six months
forward. Such a trade exists in the swap market and is referred to as a spread lock, which
is a forward on a swap spread.
390

<!-- Page 414 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
It is important to recognize that an investor who puts on this conditional
spread widener is taking a fundamentally diﬀerent view (whether he realizes
it or not) than an investor who puts on the plain vanilla (unconditional)
spread widener we examined earlier. A regular spread widener beneﬁts if
spreads widen, no matter what happens to rates. This conditional spread
widener beneﬁts at expiry only if spreads widen in conjunction with a market
sell-oﬀ.
Note that if both options ﬁnish in-the-money, this does not guarantee the
investor a positive payout at maturity (exclusive of the $40,000 upfront). If,
for example, in six months 10-year swap rates are at 6.20% and 10-year Trea-
sury yields are at 5.90%, the investor will lose money. The payer swaption
that he is long is in-the-money 5 bps (running), but the put option he is
short is in-the-money 10 bps (running).
Figure 8.18 shows the net payoﬀat expiration of the options as a function
of where rates are. Swap rates and Treasury yields (which diﬀer only by the
swap spread) tend to move in the same direction. Thus, an investor who puts
Figure 8.18: Net Payout at Maturity of Option Position (Exclusive of Upfront
Fee) as a Function of Treasury Yields and Swap Rates at Expiration
391

<!-- Page 415 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.19: The Relationship Between 10-Year Treasury Yields and 10-Year
Swap Spreads in the Second Half of 2005
this trade on will tend to ﬁnd himself in either the lower left-hand quadrant
or the upper right-hand quadrant at expiry. The lower left-hand quadrant
corresponds to the case in which the market has rallied, and both options
ﬁnish out-of-the-money. In this case, the investor earned the $40,000 upfront
in the trade and had no further payout. The upper right-hand quadrant cor-
responds to the case in which the market has sold oﬀ, and both options are
in-the-money. In this case, the payout at expiry can be either positive or
negative. If 10-year spreads are tighter than 35 in this scenario, the payout
will be negative, and otherwise it will be positive. Of course, the investor may
ﬁnd himself in the upper left-hand or lower right-hand quadrants, obtaining
a negative payout or positive payout, respectively.
The dynamic of spreads widening in a sell-oﬀis one that many investors
have grown accustomed to over the years. For example, as we discussed in
Section 2.2.1, during the summer of 2003 the violent sell-oﬀin the market was
accompanied by a widening of spreads. Figure 8.19 shows the relationship
between Treasury yields and swap spreads during the second half of 2005.
As this ﬁgure shows, the general trend during this period of time is one of
higher Treasury yields corresponding with higher swap spreads.42 Scenarios
such as these have made many people accustomed to the belief that as the
market sells oﬀ, swap spreads should widen. This trade positions one for
42This ﬁgure also shows, however, that there are times when high Treasury yields are
associated with tighter swap spreads.
392

<!-- Page 416 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
exactly that; provided that the market sells oﬀenough from current levels
and spreads remain wide enough, the trade provides a positive payout at
expiry. Of course in the case in which the market sells oﬀand spreads tighten
(a dynamic that has been observed at certain times in the past), this trade
will have a negative payout at expiry. And if at expiry spreads tighten when
the market rallies (another commonly held belief), then one will not be hurt
at expiry since both options will ﬁnish out-of-the-money. In fact, in this case
the investor will keep the $40,000 that he originally pocketed.43
This trade is referred to in the industry as puts payers. One of the drivers
of being able to get in at the seemingly great strike diﬀerential of 35 bps (as
compared to the spot level of 10-year spreads of 39 bps) is the diﬀerential
in the Treasury volatility one is selling versus the swaption volatility one is
buying. Treasury vol and swaption vol do not necessarily move together, and
as Treasury vol tends to richen versus swaption vol, such puts payers trades
become attractive. Of course, one of the risks an investor bears on entering
this trade is the exposure to both volatilities. If after this trade is put on,
implied volatility on the Treasury option that has been sold increases and
implied volatility on the swaption decreases, the investor will face a mark-
to-market loss on the trade.
The risks associated with implied volatility in this trade can be a sig-
niﬁcant factor in its success or failure. Suppose, for instance, that one month
after entering into the trade, the payer swaption is in-the-money and the
Treasury option is at-the-money. In this case the trade appears to be going
well for the investor. Suppose, however, that there is large overseas buying
of Treasury options. Implied Treasury vol may spike dramatically. This will
hurt the mark-to-market on the Treasury option. Although swaption vol
may rise somewhat out of sympathy for the move in Treasury vol, the payer
swaption is in-the-money and thus has limited vega exposure.44 This adverse
move in vol, despite the desired move in spreads in conjunction with a sell-oﬀ,
may move the mark-to-market on the trade signiﬁcantly against the investor,
causing him to unwind the trade if he hits his predetermined stop-loss.
One ﬁnal thing to note about this trade: sometimes investors would rather
forgo an upfront fee (in this case, the $40,000) and improve their entry level.
Thus, as an alternative to the original terms of the trade, one may be able
to put on the following trade:
43Although the investor will have zero payout at expiry in the case in which spreads
tighten and the market rallies, the investor may have a substantially negative mark-to-
market during the life of the trade.
44In markets that are somewhat related, such as the swaption vol and Treasury vol
markets, a move in one market due to a particular catalyst may spur movement in the
other market. Such a move is referred to as occurring out of sympathy.
393

<!-- Page 417 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
• Buy $100mm of a 6 month into 10 year payer swaption struck at 6.14%
for 66 bps.
• Sell $100mm of a 6-month put option on the then-on-the-run 10-year
Treasury struck at 5.80% for 66 bps.
In this case, the strike on the payer swaption has been reduced to 6.14%,
implying that the conditional entry point on the spread widener is now
6.14% −5.80% = 34 bps, instead of the 35 bps that was available in the
version of the trade with an upfront fee of $40,000.45 Many investors prefer
the version with the upfront, because if the market rallies and the options
ﬁnish up out-of-the-money, they at least get to keep the upfront. But the
choice is a matter of investor preference.
2
8.4
Asset Swaps Revisited
Recall that in Section 2.5.2 we said that an asset swap referred to the simul-
taneous purchase of a bond and use of a swap to transform the nature of the
asset from either ﬁxed to ﬂoating, or vice versa. Most of the time the bond in
question is a ﬁxed rate bond, and the buyer of the asset swap buys the bond
and pays ﬁxed against it. Asset swaps represent a large segment of the types
of trades that relative value investors put on in the swap market. The most
commonly asset swapped securities are Treasuries and agency debentures.
Investors both buy and sell asset swaps, looking to buy asset swaps when
they think they are cheap and sell them when they are rich.
There are a number of diﬀerent methodologies that are employed to put
on asset swaps. The classical transaction (which is seldom used anymore for
reasons we shall discuss) is referred to as a par asset swap. In order to buy
a par asset swap, an investor
1. Buys a ﬁxed rate bond;
45As a handcheck on the strike diﬀerential obtainable with no upfront fee, consider the
following. By setting
PMT
=
1.0 bp/2 = 0.5 bp
n
=
20
i
=
6.14%/2,
we ﬁnd that a 1 bp change in coupon on this 10-year swap is worth approximately $74,000.
An at-the-money option has a delta around 0.5, and thus a 1 bp change in the strike is
worth around $37,000. Thus, forgoing the fee of $40,000 implies that we should be able
to improve the strike diﬀerential by approximately 1 bp.
394

<!-- Page 418 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
2. Pays ﬁxed in a swap (in which the notional matches the face amount
of the bond) where the ﬁxed payments exactly match the coupon pay-
ments of the bond in both timing and magnitude;
3. Makes or receives an upfront payment equal to the dirty price minus
par (if the dirty price minus par is positive, then the investor receives
this amount; otherwise, he pays it as an upfront);46
4. Receives a ﬂoating rate (typically 3-month LIBOR) plus an agreed-
upon spread to the ﬂoating rate on a notional that is again equal to
the face amount of the bond.
The agreed-upon spread is referred to as the asset swap spread. The asset
swap spread is set such that the swap is an NPV = 0 transaction. It is
important to note than an investor who buys a bond and asset swaps it still
has credit exposure to the issuer. If the credit should deteriorate, this will
hurt the investor.47
Example
Let’s look at an asset swap in the context of our stylized example, and
suppose that the swap curve is as given in Table 8.1. Consider a 4-year agency
bond with a 6% coupon (annual bond) that is trading at a yield of T4 + 16.
The bond has no accrued interest, and it can be shown that its (dirty) price is
102.286252.48 If an investor buys $100mm face of the bond and asset swaps it
with a swap dealer, the dealer will be receiving the ﬁxed 6% coupons on the
bond, paying an upfront fee of $2,286,252 (the diﬀerence between the dirty
price and par) and will be receiving LIBOR plus a spread. This transaction
is depicted in Figure 8.20. In the top half of Figure 8.21, it is shown that
the spread to 1-year LIBOR that makes this an NPV = 0 transaction is
46The dirty price of a bond refers to the full-invoice price. It is the quoted price plus
accrued interest.
47In the event of default by the issuer of the bond, the investor who entered into
the asset swap will still have to make ﬁxed payments versus LIBOR in the swap he has
entered into, unless a speciﬁc clause in the terms of the trade was negotiated in which
case payments in the swap would cease upon default by the issuer of the reference credit.
Such clauses are rare and nontrivial to negotiate and price and would potentially impact
the asset swap spread in the trade.
48From Table 8.1, we have that T4 is 5.19%, and thus the yield on the bond is 5.19% +
0.16% = 5.35%. The price of the bond is
102.286252 =
6
1.0535 +
6
(1.0535)2 +
6
(1.0535)3 +
106
(1.0535)4 .
395

<!-- Page 419 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.20: Asset Swapping a 4-Year Agency Security
−11.9 bps. We say that the asset swap spread for this agency bond is −11.9
bps, or that the bond asset swaps to −11.9 bps.49
2
The million-dollar question with asset swaps is what spread to LIBOR
the investor is able to get. If the investor is buying the asset swap, then he
is looking for a large spread; the larger the spread, the “cheaper” the asset
swap, and the smaller the spread, the “richer” the asset. If he is selling the
asset swap, then he wants the spread to be as small as possible.50
49Recall that the agency bond yields T4 + 16, and the swap rate is equal to T4 + 28.
Notice that the asset swap spread is approximately equal to the diﬀerence between the
agency spread and the swap spread. That is,
−11.9bps ≈16bps −28bps.
As we will see, this is not a coincidence.
50It should not be surprising that agency bonds sometimes have asset swap spreads
that are negative. Many market participants think of LIBOR as representing the credit
quality of a AA bank. The agencies have (at times) been regarded as having better than
AA credit quality, which would suggest that they might asset swap through LIBOR, or
“sub-LIBOR” as people sometimes say.
396

<!-- Page 420 -->
Figure 8.21: Determining the Asset Swap Spread of the 4-Year Agency (top
half ) and Showing That the Present Value of the Asset Swap Spread Is Equal
to the Diﬀerence Between the LIBOR Price of the Bond and Its Dirty Price
(bottom half )

<!-- Page 421 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
What determines the level of the asset swap spread? It is a function of the
relative pricing of the agency security versus the swap curve. It is a function
of both the credit spread of the bond (deﬁned as the diﬀerence between the
yield of the bond and the yield of the relevant Treasury benchmark) and
swap spreads. Two important facts to keep in mind are as follows:
• As the credit spread of the asset increases (i.e., the asset becomes
cheaper), the asset swap spread increases as well.
• As swap spreads increase, the asset swap spread decreases.
To gain intuition for these two facts, refer again to Figure 8.20. If the credit
spread of the asset increases (everything else the same), then this will
decrease the dirty price of the bond. A decrease in the dirty price decreases
the upfront fee that the swap dealer has to make. As the upfront fee de-
creases, the swap dealer can aﬀord to pay a larger spread to LIBOR, and
hence the asset swap spread increases. If, on the other hand, swap spreads
increase (everything else the same), then swap rates increase, and the 6%
ﬁxed coupon that the investor is paying to the dealer becomes “worth less.”
That is, the value of receiving a 6% ﬁxed coupon declines as swap rates in-
crease. If the dealer is receiving ﬁxed ﬂows that are worth less, it will pay
out a lower spread to LIBOR to compensate. So as swap spreads increase,
asset swap spreads decrease. Thus, the asset swap spread depends on the
relative pricing of the credit spread of the underlying security and the swap
spread that matches the maturity of the bond.51
8.4.1
Asset Swap Math
The asset swap spread is the spread to LIBOR that makes the swap outlined
in Figure 8.20 an NPV = 0 transaction. In this section, we will give some
further insight into how exactly that spread is determined. Let us assume,
without any loss of generality, that the investor and the swap dealer exchange
principal at maturity in the terms of the swap. As we know, this will not
change the value of the swap at all.
Let’s take a look at the swap now. The investor receives ﬂoating payments
(LIBOR plus a spread) plus an upfront payment equal to the dirty price
minus par. The present value of these ﬂows can be written as
PV(Floater) + PV(Spread) + Dirty Price −Par,
(8.4)
51Recall that in Section 8.3 we said that some would regard a seemingly macro swap
spread trade as a relative value trade. Hopefully this discussion sheds some light on why
that interpretation is reasonable. If we put on a swap spread widener, then we are buying
Treasuries and paying ﬁxed. Recall that an asset swap is a trade in which we buy a security
and pay ﬁxed, and thus a swap spread trade is really an asset swap.
398

<!-- Page 422 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
where Floater represents all the ﬂoating coupons and principal at maturity.
The investor pays ﬁxed coupons and principal at maturity, the present value
of which we can write as
PV(Fixed Bond),
(8.5)
where Fixed Bond represents all of the ﬁxed coupon payments in the swap
plus principal at maturity. Since the swap is an NPV = 0 transaction, the
present value of the cash ﬂows coming in must equal the present value of the
cash ﬂows going out. Equating Equations 8.4 and 8.5 gives
PV(Floater) + PV(Spread) + Dirty Price −Par = PV(Fixed Bond). (8.6)
Recognizing that PV(Floater) = Par, we can simplify Equation 8.6 and write
PV(Spread) = PV(Fixed Bond) −Dirty Price.
(8.7)
The dirty price of the bond is of course that price at which the bond is trading
in the market. It is a function of discounting the cash ﬂows of the bond
implicitly using its own credit curve. The PV(Fixed Bond) represents taking
those same exact cash ﬂows but instead discounting them using discount
factors generated by the swap curve. Some people refer to the PV(Fixed
Bond) as the “LIBOR price of the bond.”
To say all of this another way, the dirty price of the bond is the actual
price of the bond; it is the price of the bond as determined by the market.
The PV(Fixed Bond), or LIBOR price of the bond, answers the following
question: if we take all the cash ﬂow from the bond (including principal)
and discount those cash ﬂows in accordance with zero coupon rates from the
swap curve, what price do we get? It is the price of the bond according to the
swaps market. As shown in Equation 8.7, the “purpose” of the spread is to
reconcile these two diﬀerent prices. That is, the bond market says the bond
is worth one amount; the swaps market says if we take those same cash ﬂows
from the bond and discount them using the swap curve, we get a diﬀerent
amount; and the spread makes up the diﬀerence between those two amounts.
Let’s go back now to the asset swap shown in Figure 8.20. Again, this
was a 4-year agency security paying coupons of 6%, and we said that it
asset swaps to LIBOR −11.9. In the top half of Figure 8.21 we showed
the calculation of the asset swap spread. In the bottom half of ﬁgure 8.21
we show that the diﬀerence between the LIBOR price of the bond and the
dirty price of the bond is $101,866,152 −$102,286,252 = −$420,100, or −42
bps of the notional. And we also show that −42 bps is indeed the present
value of the asset swap spread of −11.9 bps running.
399

<!-- Page 423 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
8.4.2
Asset Swaps Today
We just spent a considerable amount of time going over par asset swaps,
but, as mentioned, this methodology of executing asset swaps is no longer in
favor. There are several reasons why this style of asset swap has gone out of
vogue. The main reason is the upfront fee associated with a par asset swap.
Let’s say an investor does a par asset swap of a high coupon, long maturity
Treasury. The dollar price on this bond could very well be around $150. So
if the investor does a par asset swap on, say, $100mm of bonds, that means
that the swap will have an upfront fee of $50mm. It also means that once
this fee is paid, the remaining swap will be around $50mm oﬀ-market. This
creates signiﬁcant credit exposure for the party who has paid the upfront
fee. Although many counterparties have a Credit Support Annex in place
with one another that speciﬁes the transfer of collateral against the mark-
to-market of swaps so as to mitigate credit risk, the transfer of collateral can
be costly and time-consuming.
It is nonetheless worthwhile to go through the mechanics of par asset
swaps if for no other reason for its historical context. Moreover, the analysis
of the par asset swap lends itself well to understanding the inherent position
being put on in any asset swap, namely, the relative movement of the credit
spread of the bond versus swap spreads.
Today’s favored asset swap transaction is actually much simpler. Referred
to as a yield-yield asset swap (and sometimes as a matched-maturity asset
swap), it is accomplished by buying a bond and paying ﬁxed on a spot start-
ing swap whose maturity matches the maturity of the bond.52 There is no
upfront fee in the swap, and there is no spread to the LIBOR side. The
coupon on the swap is the par swap rate for that maturity; that is, the
coupon is the rate that gives the swap an NPV = 0. This method of asset
swap is typically much faster to execute in the market.
Example
Consider our stylized example and suppose that the swap curve is as given in
Table 8.1. Consider an investor who buys the 4-year agency bond at a price
52Thus, it is often the case that the swap in a yield-yield asset swap does not have
a maturity date that falls an exact number of years (or an exact number of semiannual
periods) after the eﬀective date. In this case, the swap will typically be structured with
a short stub upfront on both sides, meaning the ﬁrst ﬁxed ﬂow will occur in less than six
months and the ﬁrst ﬂoating ﬂow will occur in less than three months. The ﬁrst ﬁxed
coupon will occur at the same time as the next coupon payment of the bond. The ﬁrst
ﬂoating ﬂow will occur at this time as well, unless that happens to be more than three
months from the eﬀective date. In this case, the ﬁrst ﬂoating ﬂow will occur three months
prior to the ﬁrst ﬁxed payment.
400

<!-- Page 424 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
of $102.286252 that corresponds to a yield of 5.35%. To execute a yield-yield
asset swap of this bond, the investor would pay ﬁxed in four years at the par
swap rate, which is 5.47%. The yield-yield asset swap spread is simply the
diﬀerence between the bond yield and the swap rate, or 5.35% −5.47% =
−12 bps.53
2
More generally, in a yield-yield asset swap, the asset swap spread is de-
ﬁned to be
Yield-Yield Asset Swap Spread = Bond Yield −Swap Rate,
(8.8)
where Bond Yield is the yield of the bond, and Swap Rate is the par swap
rate on a spot starting swap that has the same maturity as the bond.54 In
the event that the Bond Yield and the Swap Rate are quoted as spreads to
the same Treasury benchmark, then Equation 8.8 reduces to
Yield-Yield Asset Swap Spread = Credit Spread −Swap Spread,
(8.9)
where Credit Spread is the spread, in bps, that is added to the yield of the
relevant Treasury benchmark to come up with the Bond Yield, and Swap
Spread is the number of bps that is added to the yield of the relevant Trea-
sury benchmark to come up with the swap rate.55 From Equation 8.9, we see,
then, that a yield-yield asset swap is also a measure of the relative value of
the credit spread of the bond to the swap spread of the same maturity. As the
Credit Spread increases (i.e., the bond cheapens), the Yield-Yield Asset Swap
Spread increases (i.e., the asset swap cheapens), and as the Swap Spread
increases, the Yield-Yield Asset Swap Spread decreases (i.e., the asset swap
richens). These are the same dynamics we observed with the par asset swap
transaction.
53Notice that the level of the yield-yield asset swap spread, −12 bps, is very close to
the par asset swap level of −11.9 bps that we found earlier. Oftentimes, these levels are
very close to one another. In fact, a good handcheck (in many situations) when calculating
the par asset swap spread is the level obtained from the yield-yield asset swap.
54Most often both the Bond Yield and the Swap Rate are quoted on a semiannual
30/360 basis.
55In the case where both the bond and the swap are quoted over the same Treasury
benchmark,
Bond Yield −Swap Rate
= (Treasury Yield + Credit Spread) −(Treasury Yield + Swap Spread)
= Credit Spread −Swap Spread.
In the case in which the bond and the swap are quoted over diﬀerent Treasury benchmarks,
the Treasury yields do not cancel out. In this case, the yield-yield asset swap spread is a
function not only of the diﬀerence between the Credit Spread and the Swap Spread but
of a (typically de minimis) Treasury curve component.
401

<!-- Page 425 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.22: The Asset Swap Curve for FNMA Debt, October 25, 2002
FNMA Asset Swaps
As mentioned previously, one very popular type of bond to asset swap is
agency ﬁxed rate bullet debentures.56 If an investor buys an agency bond on
asset swap, he is not taking an outright position in the credit of the underly-
ing agency but, rather, he is taking a position on agency spreads relative to
swap spreads. Pretty much the entire universe of agency non-callable debt
is looked at routinely on an asset swap basis by a myriad of investors and
dealers. Figure 8.22 shows the yield-yield asset swap spread curve for a sam-
pling of Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA) bullet debentures
using data from October 25, 2002. That is, this graph shows the relationship
between the asset swap spread of FNMA debt (as deﬁned in Equation 8.8)
and maturity. In 2002, FNMA actively issued securities with a variety of
maturities of ten years and less but was no longer issuing benchmark deben-
tures with a maturity of greater than ten years (and the last time it had
done so as of this time was when it had issued a 30-year bond, the 6.625%
56Some have suggested that liquidity in certain agency debentures, both on an outright
basis as well as on asset swap, has diminished since the ﬁnancial crisis.
402

<!-- Page 426 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
of November 15, 2030, in 2000.) As of this date, FNMA had no benchmark
issues with a maturity between ten and 26 years.
As this ﬁgure demonstrates, the FNMA asset swap spread curve has
traditionally been upward sloping (albeit with a kink near the front end
of the curve), indicating that longer-dated maturity securities are cheaper
on a LIBOR basis than are shorter-dated securities. At the time at which
this data was compiled, FNMA securities traded “LIBOR minus” (meaning
their asset swap spreads are less than zero) for securities that had a maturity
of about ﬁve years or less and traded “LIBOR plus” for longer maturities.
Investors take views on the relative richness or cheapness of particular bonds
on asset swap and trade them accordingly.57
FNMA Versus FHLMC Asset Swaps
Historically, the debt issued by FNMA has asset swapped at nearly the
same levels as the debt issued by the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corpo-
ration (FHLMC). Figure 8.23 provides a comparison of FNMA asset swap
spreads on its benchmark on-the-run 10-year debt versus asset swap spreads
on FHLMC 10-year on-the-run benchmark debt during 2002 when concerns
mounted about FNMA’s risk management policies.58 Traditionally, FNMA
had been considered by many in the market a slightly stronger credit than
FHLMC, and this manifested itself in terms of the slightly richer asset swap
levels for FNMA often observed (i.e., the diﬀerence between FNMA’s asset
swap spread and FHLMC’s asset swap spread was negative). However, in
2002 FNMA reported extreme levels for its duration gap, calling into ques-
tion its risk management processes in the minds of some investors.59 During
this period of time, FNMA debt cheapened to FHLMC on an asset swap
basis. As shown in this ﬁgure, the diﬀerence between FNMA’s asset swap
57An investor who believes that a bond is cheap on asset swap (i.e., believes that the
asset swap spread will decline) will want to buy the bond and pay ﬁxed on the swap.
An investor who owns a bond on asset swap and believes the asset swap is rich (i.e.,
believes that the asset swap spread will go up) will want to sell the bond and unwind the
swap.
58During this time, both FNMA and FHLMC routinely issued bullet debentures of a
variety of maturities on a regular schedule. For a given maturity series, the most recently
issued benchmark bond maintained an “on-the-run” status, much as on-the-run Treasur-
ies do.
59FNMA’s duration gap, the diﬀerence between the duration of its asset and the dura-
tion of its liabilities, was viewed by many market participants as a measure of its interest
rate sensitivity. The duration gap was measured in months, and in 2001 FNMA’s stated
objective was to keep the gap between −6 months and +6 months. In three consecutive
months starting in July 2002, the duration gap was −9 months, −14 months, and −10
months, raising concern among many investors. See Frame and White (2004) for more
details.
403

<!-- Page 427 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.23: FNMA Versus FHLMC Rolling 10-Year Benchmark Asset Swap
Spreads
spread and FHLMC’s asset swap spread turned positive, indicative of the
relative cheapening of FNMA to FHLMC.
FNMA Asset Swap Spreads Before and After Lehman Fell
Let’s refer back now to the asset swap spread curve for FNMA, such as
the one depicted in Figure 8.22. Although there is deﬁnite variation in the
asset swap spreads of particular bonds from one day to the next, the general
shape and level of the asset swap spread curve for FNMA remained more
or less stable for many years. For example, Figure 8.24 shows the FNMA
asset swap spread curve for data taken in March 2008. Both Figure 8.22
and 8.24 look rather similar, with the most expensive bonds asset swapping
around −20, and the cheapest bonds asset swapping around 30 to 35 bps.60
60This is not to suggest that asset swap levels on agency bonds never change. On
the contrary, it was not uncommon during this time for asset swap levels to have daily
ﬂuctuations of several bps. Nevertheless, the general shape and levels depicted in these
two ﬁgures were commonplace. Market participants take views on the levels of asset swap
404

<!-- Page 428 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.24: The Asset Swap Curve for FNMA Debt, March 31, 2008, with
Data Label Indicating Asset Swap Spread of FNMA 6.625% of November
15, 2030
In Figure 8.24 we have placed a data label next to FNMA’s longest-dated
bullet debenture at this time, the 6.625% of November 15, 2030. This bond,
which had approximately 22.5 years until maturity at the time, had a yield-
yield asset swap level of 28.4 bps on this date.
The agency asset swap curve changed dramatically after Lehman fell in
the fall of 2008. Figure 8.25 shows the asset swap curve for FNMA using
data from April 2009. This ﬁgure shows that the asset swap curve during
this time had the same basic shape as it did in the other two periods we
discussed. The startling thing to notice, however, is that the asset swap
spread for long-dated securities was nearly 100 bps higher than it had been
historically. There is a data label next to the 6.625% of November 15, 2030
which, at the time, was a bond with approximately 21.5 years until maturity.
It indicates an asset swap level of 112 bps for this security, as compared to
28.4 bps in March of the previous year. This was an unprecedented move in
asset swap spreads. It’s important to remember that an asset swap spread
spreads, especially when they diverged from their long-term means, usually looking to
make at least a few bps on the trade. For trades held for longer holding periods, the
implications of carry and roll-down are also a consideration, just as with many of the
other trades we have looked at in this chapter.
405

<!-- Page 429 -->
R EL AT I V E VA L U E A N D M AC R O T R A D E S
Figure 8.25: The Asset Swap Curve for FNMA Debt, April 13, 2009, with
Data Label Indicating Asset Swap Spread of FNMA 6.625% of November 15,
2030
depends on the diﬀerence of two diﬀerent spreads (i.e., the credit spread of
the underlying bond versus the swap spread of the relevant maturity swap).
The dramatic move in the asset swap spread of this longer-dated paper was
a result of two phenomena that were going on during this period of time:
1. During the crisis, credit spreads in general widened out signiﬁcantly.
In particular, long-dated agency spreads (the diﬀerence between the
yield on these securities and the Treasury benchmark to which they
are compared) widened out signiﬁcantly.
2. At the same time, long-end swap spreads tightened dramatically. For
example, 30-year swap spreads tightened from 34.5 bps on March 31,
2008, to −27.75 bps on April 13, 2009.61
Keeping Equation 8.8 in mind, Table 8.10 provides an explanation as to why
61As mentioned in Chapter 1 and as will be discussed further in Chapter 9, 30-year
swap spreads traded in negative territory following the collapse of Lehman, the ﬁrst time
in history USD swaps spreads ever traded below zero.
406

<!-- Page 430 -->

Agency
Matched Maturity
Yield-Yield
Date
Yields
Swap Rates
Asset Swap Levels
March 31, 2008
T30 + 58
T30 + 29.6
T30 + 58 −(T30 + 29.6) = 28.4

T30 + 83

Table 8.10: Comparison of Yield-Yield Asset Swap Spreads of FNMA 6.625%
of November 15, 2030, Before and After Lehman Fell
these two phenomena (i.e., the widening of agency spreads and the narrowing
of swap spreads) contributed to a move of nearly 100 bps in the asset swap
spreads of long-dated agencies, by looking at the FNMA 6.625% of November
15, 2030, in particular.62
62The yield of the FNMA 6.625% of November 15, 2030, was, during this period of time,
quoted as a spread to the then-current on-the-run 30-year Treasury yield. On March 31,
2008, these agency bonds yielded 4.892%, and the then-current 30-year Treasury yielded
4.312%, implying a spread to Treasuries of 4.892% −4.312% = 58 bps. But on April
13, 2009, the agency bonds were yielding 4.515%, and the then-current 30-year Treasury
yielded 3.685%, implying a spread of 83 bps. Thus, we saw a substantial widening of
agency spreads over this period.
The matched maturity swap for this bond is a spot starting swap that matures on
November 15, 2030. On March 31, 2008, this means the swap was approximately 22.5 years
long. The par swap rate for this swap was 4.608%. Although not conventional (we would
normally think about spreading this rate to an interpolated Treasury yield), we can think
of this rate as a spread to the then-current 30-year Treasury yield of 4.312%, or 4.608% −
4.312% = 29.6 bps. On April 13, 2009, the matched maturity swap was about 21.5 years
long. The par swap rate for this swap was 3.395%, which we can also think about spreading
to the then-current 30-year Treasury yield of 3.685%, or 3.395% −3.685% = −29 bps.
Thus, whereas there was a substantial widening of agency spreads over the period, there
was an even greater decline in swap spreads. These two eﬀects resulted in the large move
in the yield-yield asset swap spread.
407

swap, for a horizon of one year? Specify your answer in terms of both bps

is the P&L on the trade in dollars?
2. A swap is referred to as being positive carry for a counterparty if the net
cash ﬂow to be received by the counterparty in the current accrual period in
the swap is positive. Obviously, if a swap is positive carry to one counterparty
in the swap, then it must be negative carry to the other counterparty.

ter into the positive carry swap, earning positive net cash ﬂow in the ﬁrst

positive carry. How much money should you expect to make employing this

a) What notional of 1-year swaps should you do if you want to have the
value of a bp running in both trades be the same? That is, what notional of
1-year swaps should you do if you want this trade to be duration neutral? If
you do this trade, what view are you taking?
b) Suppose now that you pay ﬁxed on $100mm of a 4-year swap, one year
forward and simultaneously receive ﬁxed in a 1-year swap, one year forward.
What notional of the 1x2 should you do to make this trade duration neutral?
What are the two rates in the swap? If you do this trade, what view are you
taking? How does this trade compare to the trade in part a), and is the entry
level better or worse for you?
c) Suppose now that 1 into 4 year payer swaptions trade at implied vols of
20% and that 1 into 1 payer swaptions trade at implied vols of 25%. Suppose
that you buy $100mm of a 1 into 4 year payer swaption struck at-the-money
forward and that you simultaneously sell 1 into 1 payer swaptions on the
notional amount you solved for in part b). What strike on the 1 into 1 payer
swaption do you solve for that gives this trade zero upfront cost? What view
are you taking in this trade? How does your entry level compare to the entry
level you found in part b)? Can you explain why the entry level is better or
worse?
5. Suppose you purchase a 1 into 4 receiver swaption struck at-the-money
forward.
a) How much does this cost, assuming 1 into 4 swaption vol is 20%?
b) Suppose that the swap curve moves only in parallel shifts. Assuming that
you hold this position until expiry, where must 4-year swap rates be in order
to break even on this trade? How does this break-even compare with the
at-the-money forward strike? How does this break-even level compare with
4-year spot swap rates?
c) Suppose you purchase a 1 into 4 payer swaption struck at-the-money for-
ward. How much does this cost, assuming 1 into 4 swaption vol is 20%?
d) Assuming that you hold this position until expiry, where do you need
4-year swap rates to be in order to break even on this trade? How does this

break-even level compare with the at-the-money forward strike? How does
