Chapter 9
More Recent Product Innovations
The few years preceding the ﬁnancial crisis were marked by a number of
new, exotic trades in the swaps market following what had been considered
by many to be a long period of relatively little innovation. Some of these
trades were somewhat pathbreaking in nature, whereas others simply bor-
rowed well-known concepts from other markets and recast them in the swaps
market. Still others were a by-product of other products whose popularity
was on the rise.
The trades that we will look at in this chapter exhibit correlation risk—
the feature that more than one underlying variable determines the value
of the trade and the correlation between the variables must be taken into
account to properly value the trade. A correlation trade is a trade that has
correlation risk. Correlation trades have been around in certain markets for
a long time, but the rates markets have not traditionally been thought of as
a hotbed for them.1
1The credit markets come to many people’s minds as a place where correlation trades
abound. In a synthetic collateralized debt obligation (CDO), for example, a number of
credit default swaps are placed in a “basket.” Suppose that 100 credit default swaps are
placed in a basket, and two tranches are formed: an equity tranche and a senior tranche.
The senior tranche is protected against the ﬁrst ten defaults in the portfolio, with the
equity tranche absorbing those losses. What does an increase in pairwise correlation among
defaults in the 100 names in the basket do to the value of each of the tranches? An increase
in correlation decreases the value of the senior tranche and increases the value of the equity
tranche. With 100 names in the basket, the senior tranche can withstand a few random
blowups in the portfolio. However, as correlation increases, the occurrence of a single
blowup implies that many other defaults are more likely to occur, which will erode the

<!-- Page 438 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
That is not to say that the rates markets have been completely devoid
of correlation trades. There was a trade that we looked at in Chapter 5
that is actually a correlation trade, and we’ll take a look at it again shortly.
But examples such as these correlation trades were traditionally few and far
between in the rates markets. Then in 2004 to 2005, we started seeing a surge
of correlation trades. One reason for the increase in these types of trades was
the popularity of a certain type of structured note known as a non-inversion
note, which has embedded correlation risk. As we will discuss, as more and
more of these structured notes were printed, dealers started getting long a
lot of options with correlation risk. In order to oﬄoad or oﬀset some of this
risk in their books, it became desirable for dealers to come up with trades
that had embedded correlation risk and could be marketed to investors. This
led to the birth of curve options.
In this chapter we will focus on two products: forward vol and curve
options. Although these two trades are quite distinct, they have some simi-
larities: their popularity grew as an oﬀshoot of other products, and, as men-
tioned, they are both examples of correlation trades. It should be noted that
the pricing of these products is complex and is outside the scope of this text.
Nonetheless, we will be able to make some progress in describing the issues
and challenges involved in accurately pricing these complicated products.
9.1
An Introduction to Correlation Trades:
Caps Versus Payer Redux
In Section 5.2, we talked about how caps and payer swaptions are closely
related. Look again for a moment at Figure 5.4, which is a representation
of a 2x5 cap on 3-month LIBOR and a 2 into 3 payer swaption (with a
quarterly money strike versus 3-month LIBOR). We discussed some simi-
larities and diﬀerences between these trades and, in particular, how the 2x5
cap is a portfolio of options on forward rates, and the 2 into 3 payer swap-
tion is an option on a portfolio of forward rates. What we did not discuss at
the time is that another diﬀerence between the two trades can be explained
by correlation.
If the strikes on the two options are the same, then we said that the
cap must be worth more than the payer swaption. But how much more?
The answer depends on correlation. The two trades are written on the exact
same set of 12 3-month LIBOR forwards. The degree of pairwise correlation
value of the senior tranche. Thus, as correlation goes up, the senior tranche is hurt. Inas-
much as the risk of the entire basket is unaﬀected by an increase in correlation, this must
mean that as correlation goes up, the value of the equity tranche must also go up.
415

<!-- Page 439 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
between forward rates determines the relative pricing of the cap and the
payer swaption.
To see that correlation plays a role in the relative value of the cap versus
the payer swaption, let’s consider a couple of cases. First, suppose that each
of the forward rates is pairwise uncorrelated. In this case, if the forward
rates are volatile, then there will be a lot of value to the cap, which is a
portfolio of options on the individual forward rates. However, even though
there may be a lot of movement in the individual forward rates, the 3-year
swap rate, two years forward will not be too volatile in this case; as some
of the individual LIBOR rates go up, others may go down, canceling one
another out and leaving the forward swap rate relatively stable. In this case,
the cap will be much more valuable than the payer swaption.
But suppose instead that the 12 forward rates are all pairwise highly
correlated. Then as one forward rate goes up, they will all tend to go up,
and that implies that the 3-year swap rate, two years forward will tend to go
up as well. Similarly, if a 3-month LIBOR forward goes down, then they will
all tend to go down, in which case the forward swap rate will tend to go down
as well. In this case, a lot of volatility in the LIBOR forwards tends to mean
a lot of volatility in the 3-year swap rate, two years forward. Thus, in the
case of high pairwise correlation between each of the forward LIBOR rates,
the cap will still be worth more than the payer swaption, but the diﬀerential
in price will not be as extreme as the case in which all of the forward LIBOR
rates are pairwise uncorrelated.2
Caps versus payers has been a popular relative value correlation trade for
years. It refers to a trade in which an investor buys a cap and sells a payer
swaption (or vice versa), with a view that a move in correlation will make the
long increase in value relative to the short. For example, if an investor expects
that the pairwise correlation between forward LIBOR rates will increase in
the near future, he may want to put on a trade in which he sells caps and
buys a payer swaption today. If the pairwise correlation between forward
LIBOR rates does indeed increase, the trade may subsequently be unwound
at a proﬁt.
9.2
Forward Vol Trades
A forward vol trade is a rather elegant product that became mainstream
around 2004. It is, as the name suggests, a forward contract on (implied)
2This is an application of results presented in Merton (1973). He shows that the value
of an option on a portfolio must be less than or equal to the value of a corresponding
portfolio of options.
416

<!-- Page 440 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
volatility.3 A forward vol trade therefore allows an investor to express a view
on the direction of implied volatility. Prior to the creation of this product, an
investor who was interested in expressing a view on implied volatility would
most likely trade some options. For example, an investor who thought that
implied 5 into 10 swaption vol would go up might buy some 5 into 10 swaption
straddles struck at-the-money forward.4 However, with the passage of time
and the movement of rates, the buyer of a swaption straddle has a position
that is aﬀected by much more than just movements of 5 into 10 swaption vol.
For instance, the straddle position may acquire signiﬁcant delta exposure as
rates move and the strike is no longer at-the-money forward.5 Moreover, with
the passage of time, the buyer of a 5 into 10 swaption straddle will no longer
have exposure to 5 into 10 implied vol. For example, after a year, the buyer
will have exposure to 4 into 10 vol. Someone who enters a forward vol trade,
by comparison, avoids many of the issues faced when trading a swaption
straddle in order to take a view on implied vol.
9.2.1
Preliminary
Before diving in and tackling forward vol, let’s take a look at the following
(seemingly unrelated) trade. Consider the swaption on a forward starting
swap depicted in Figure 9.1. This is a 2 into 5x15 receiver swaption (which
is pronounced “two into ﬁve by ﬁfteen”).6
3Prior to 2004, forward vol was a concept that was implicit in the valuation of Bermu-
dan swaptions; as we alluded to in Chapters 5 and 6 and will discuss further later in this
chapter, the decision to exercise a Bermudan swaption on an expiration date depends in
part on expectations of what implied volatility will be in the future. But it was not until
around 2004 that the concept of trading forward vol as a stand-alone product emerged.
4An investor who wanted to express the view that 5 into 10 implied vol would go up
could alternatively buy either a payer swaption or a receiver swaption. However, buying
one of these options in isolation may also give the investor substantial delta risk. By
contrast, a swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward will be delta-neutral or nearly
delta-neutral at trade inception, depending on what model we use (question 12 of Problem
Set 5 gave you an opportunity to ponder this).
5Very often, investors buy swaption straddles struck at-the-money forward to take a
view on vol. Such straddles have limited delta initially but may acquire signiﬁcant delta
as rates move around. Oftentimes, investors will use swaps to hedge any acquired delta
position in order to maintain a position that has exposure to vol, not an exposure to rates.
6There is a lot of jargon being employed in the description of this trade, so let’s review
a few things. The terminology “x into y” for swaptions refers to an option that expires in
x years and gives one the right to enter into a y-year swap. Regarding forward starting
swaps, the terminology “x x y” refers to a swap that starts x years from today and ends
y years from today (and thus the swap lasts y −x years). Thus, a 2 into 5x15 receiver
swaption is a receiver swaption that gives the owner the right in two years to receive in a
10-year swap, ﬁve years forward.
417

<!-- Page 441 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.1: A Swaption on a Forward Starting Swap
This swaption expires in two years, and the underlying swap is a 5x15, a
10-year swap, ﬁve years forward. That is, the eﬀective date on the underlying
swap is ﬁve years after the expiry of the option, and the underlying swap has
a 10-year maturity. Notice that this is quite a bit diﬀerent from any other
swaption that we have looked at before. In standard swaptions, the eﬀective
date of the underlying swap immediately follows the expiry of the option.7
In this somewhat exotic swaption, there is a 5-year delay from the expiry of
the option until the eﬀective date of the swap. Referring now to Table 5.3,
which implied swaption vol should be used to price up this swaption?
The answer is that it is not appropriate to use any of these implied vols.
The nature of this swaption is such that it does not lend itself well to simply
using a single implied vol from Table 5.3. To see this, consider again that
the underlying is a 5x15 swap. As we discussed in Section 3.3.4, any forward
starting swap can be decomposed into two spot starting swaps. In particular,
if we receive on a 5x15 swap, it is equivalent to receiving on a spot starting
15-year swap and paying on a spot starting 5-year swap. So we can start to
think of this 2-year option on a 5x15 swap as an option on a portfolio of a
15-year swap and a 5-year swap. Thus, the implied volatility of this swaption
depends not only on the implied volatilities of the underlying 15-year and
the underlying 5-year swap rates but also on the correlation between them.8
We will see shortly what this has to do with forward vol, but for now let’s
just keep it in our back pocket that a swaption on a forward starting swap
is a trade that has a correlation component to it.9
7Recall that in a standard USD swaption the eﬀective date of the underlying swaption
is typically two New York and London Business Days after expiry, which is commonly
referred to as “T + 2.”
8Movements in the forward starting swap rate depend greatly on movements in each
of the two spot starting swaps. Consider again the example of the 5x15 swap rate that can
be decomposed into a position in a spot starting 15-year swap and a spot starting 5-year
swap. Movements in the 5x15 swap rate are driven by movements in each of the 15-year
and 5-year spot starting swap rates. The volatility of the 5x15 swap rate will depend not
only on the volatility of the 15-year swap rate and the volatility of the 5-year swap rate,
but also on their correlation.
9So how does one value this exotic swaption? One way is to ignore the fact that the
5x15 swap rate can be decomposed into two spot starting swaps and just regard the 5x15
418

<!-- Page 442 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
9.2.2
Description of Forward Vol
A forward vol trade is an agreement to buy or sell some underlying swaption
straddle at a given time in the future. The forward price is the price at which
the underlying swaption straddle will ultimately be bought or sold. It is de-
termined at the time the trade is transacted. The forward price can be either
above or below the current spot price of the swaption straddle, depending
on the shape of the vol grid. A forward vol trade is a forward contract, and
as such no cash ﬂow is exchanged over the life of the contract. At settlement,
the value of the contract is the diﬀerence between the forward price and the
then-prevailing spot price for the swaption straddle in the market.
One important feature of a forward vol trade is that the strike on the
underlying swaption straddle is not set until the settlement of the contract.
At the settlement of the contract, the strike will be set to the then-prevailing
at-the-money forward strike on the underlying swaption straddle. It is often
thought then that a forward vol contract trade does not have any exposure to
movements in rates over the life of the contract, and there are no associated
delta nor gamma positions to potentially hedge over the life of the contract.
In addition, until the strike is set, there is no concept of theta decay over
the life of the contract. Therefore, trading in a forward vol contract is often
regarded as taking a pure view on the level of implied vol, as compared to
someone who purchases swaption straddles and has associated delta, gamma,
and theta positions.10
Example
Consider a forward vol trade in which an investor agrees to buy $100mm 5
into 10 swaption straddles, two years forward. Suppose that the forward price
on the straddle is 725 bps, as compared to the current spot price of 5 into 10
straddles, which is 748 bps. Under the terms of the contract, in two years,
the investor is committing to buy $100mm of a then-at-the-money forward
5 into 10 swaption straddle for 725 bps. The strike will be determined in two
years by looking at the strike on then-prevailing 5 into 10 swaption straddles
struck at-the-money forward that are trading in the market. In other words,
as the underlying itself. This ignores the correlation component of the trade, of course. One
would then make a distributional assumption about the underlying and use some model
to derive a price. For example, one could assume lognormality, and use a Black’s formula–
type approach. This approach would not be terribly robust. Moreover, it would be incon-
sistent with the assumption that each of the underlying 15-year and 5-year spot starting
swaps are lognormal.
10The statement that a forward vol contract is a pure view on vol (and therefore, in
particular, has no exposure to the movement of rates) is not entirely accurate, as we shall
discuss later in this chapter.
419

<!-- Page 443 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.2: Historical 5 into 10 Swaption Straddle Prices
the strike will be set to the then-prevailing 10-year swap rate, ﬁve years
forward. There will be no cash ﬂow over the ﬁrst two years of the contract.
In two years’ time, the payoﬀto the investor who has bought the forward
vol contract is the then-prevailing price on an at-the-money forward 5 into
10 swaption straddle, less the forward price of 725 bps. If, in two years, 5
into 10 swaption straddles struck at-the-money forward are trading above
725 bps, the investor makes money. Otherwise, the investor loses money.11
Figure 9.2 shows a history of 5 into 10 swaption straddle prices from
October 2006 through April 2007. The graph shows the price, in basis points,
of a 5 into 10 swaption straddle that is struck at-the-money forward each day
during this period. As shown in this ﬁgure, 5 into 10 at-the-money forward
swaption straddles did not trade much below 750 bps during this six-month
period of time. An investor who could buy 5 into 10 at-the-money forward
swaption straddles, two years forward at a price of 725 bps (below anything
seen in recent history) may have found this an attractive opportunity.12
2
11Forward vol trades can be either cash settled or physically settled. Depending on
opportunities in the market, investors may wish to either buy or sell forward vol.
12Actually, we did not see prices for 5 into 10 swaption straddles struck at-the-money
forward trade anywhere near the 750 bp area again for several years thereafter. In fact, in
the fall of 2011 the price of these 5 into 10 swaption straddles sometimes exceeded 1500
bps, more than double the price observed at times in early 2007. The substantial price
420

<!-- Page 444 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.3: A Forward Vol Trade: 5 into 10 Swaption Straddles, Two Years
Forward
9.2.3
Heuristic Pricing of Forward Vol Trades
Above all, it should be noted that the pricing and behavior of forward vol
trades are very model dependent.13 Developing a fully rigorous methodology
to price forward vol trades is beyond the scope of this discussion. However,
in this section we will present a heuristic guide to the pricing of forward vol
trades. Let’s go back to the example that we considered earlier: a 5 into 10
swaption straddle, two years forward. This forward vol trade is depicted in
Figure 9.3.
In this ﬁgure, the white box indicates the period of time that the investor
is long vol, and the gray box represents the underlying swap. Note that the
investor who buys 5 into 10 straddles, two years forward does not have
any signiﬁcant exposure to realized volatility (i.e., movements in rates) over
the ﬁrst two years during the life of the contract since the strike on the
underlying straddle has not yet been set.14 However, during the two-year
life of the contract, the trade has exposure to changes in implied vol. For
example, suppose an investor enters into a forward vol contract in which he
increase of these swaption straddles was due both to a pronounced increase in (normal)
implied vol during this time and to a signiﬁcant drop in swap rates. Under the normal
model, the price of a swaption straddle struck at-the-money forward is

√
2
π × σN × A
T ×
where T is the time to expiry of the swaption, σN is normal implied vol, and A is the
present value of a 1 bp annuity over the life of the underlying swap. As swap rates fall
the value of the annuity A increases. As is evident from this equation, both an increase in
normal implied vol and a decrease in rates serve to increase the value of an at-the-money
forward swaption straddle under the normal model.
13The characterizations of how forward vol trades behave that we note throughout this
chapter are in general agreement with most pricing models used throughout the industry.
That being said, certain models may provide results that do not concur with all of the
observations made in this chapter.
14As we will discuss, forward vol trades based on swaption straddle premia have a rate
bias, and thus one could argue that such forward vol trades do have exposure to realized
vol.
421

<!-- Page 445 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
agrees to buy vol forward. Suppose shortly thereafter all normal implied vols
in the vol grid rise by an equal amount. In general then the contract will be
in-the-money to the investor on a mark-to-market basis.15
This ﬁgure is somewhat reminiscent of the work we did in Chapter 3 on
forward starting swaps by virtue of the fact that there is a period of exposure
in the future following a period of delay during which there is no exposure.
Recall our basic methodology for computing a forward starting swap rate.
For example, in order to compute the 3-year swap rate, two years forward
(a 2x5), we discussed that we could receive in a spot starting swap for ﬁve
years and simultaneously pay for two years. The net eﬀect of this was (with
some manipulation) to create no cash ﬂows over the ﬁrst two years and equal
cash ﬂows over the subsequent three years on the ﬁxed rate side, which led
us to the 2x5 swap rate we desired.
The same logic can be applied to replicating a forward vol trade. In order
to recreate the 5 into 10 swaption straddle, two years forward, consider buy-
ing 7 into 10 at-the-money forward swaption straddles today and simulta-
neously selling 2 into 10 at-the-money forward swaption straddles, as shown
in Figure 9.4. In this ﬁgure, the white boxes represent periods of time in
which we are long vol, and the black box represents periods of time during
which we are short vol. By buying 7 into 10 swaptions and selling 2 into 10
swaptions (in the right proportions), our vol exposure over the ﬁrst two years
“cancels” out, and we are left with forward vol exposure. The gray box in
the top line of the ﬁgure represents the underlying forward rate on which the
forward vol trade is written. This is the same forward rate on which 7 into
10 swaption straddles are written, corresponding with the gray box in the
middle part of the ﬁgure. In the bottom part of this ﬁgure, corresponding to
the sale of 2 into 10 swaption straddles, the gray box represents the forward
rate on which a 2 into 10 swaption straddle is written. But this is not the
forward rate that we wish to recreate. Therefore, this strategy of buying 7
into 10 straddles and selling 2 into 10 straddles does not perfectly replicate
the forward vol trade, as there is a mismatch in the underlying forward rate
that is in question.
We can, however, consider modifying this replication somewhat to ad-
dress this mismatch. Instead of selling 2 into 10 swaption straddles as in
Figure 9.4, consider instead the replication shown in Figure 9.5. In this
replication, the short 2 into 10 swaption straddles have been replaced by
2 into 5x15 swaption straddles. A 2 into 5x15 swaption straddle is a com-
15It may be useful to think of the following analogy. Consider our stylized example
and the swap curve in Table 3.5. We have previously shown that the 1x4 swap rate is
5.644%. If after paying ﬁxed in a 1x4 at this rate the entire swap curve should increase
by a parallel shift, the swap will be in-the-money to the investor.
422

<!-- Page 446 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.4: Replicating the Forward Vol Trade—Part I
bination of a payer and receiver swaption on a forward starting swap, as
we discussed in Section 9.2.1. By making this modiﬁcation in the replica-
tion, both the periods of vol exposure and the underlying forward rate on
which the contract is written match up. In Section 9.2.1, we discussed that
a swaption on a forward starting swap had a correlation component to it.
Thus, as an immediate consequence we see that a forward vol trade must
also have a correlation component in the pricing of the contract (provided
that we do not regard the underlying forward starting swap in the bottom
part of Figure 9.5 as a primitive, of course).
Does the replicating strategy in Figure 9.5 perfectly recreate the forward
vol trade? Not exactly. The 7 into 10 straddles and the 2 into 5x15 straddles
will have strikes that are set today, whereas the forward vol trade does not
have strikes that are set until settlement in two years. This implies that the
replicating strategy will have associated delta, gamma, theta, and so on,
over the ﬁrst two years, whereas the forward vol trade does not. Thus, this
heuristic pricing model does not capture all aspects of the forward vol trade.
To put it another way, the forward vol trade will have its strike (which is
determined on the settlement date) set at-the-money forward, whereas the
423

<!-- Page 447 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.5: Replicating the Forward Vol Trade—Part II
strikes in the replicating strategy are set today and thus will potentially be
away-from-the-money at settlement. Thus, the replicating strategy may have
skew considerations, whereas the forward vol trade will not.16
9.2.4
Will the Forward Price Be Higher or Lower Than the
Spot Price?
The answer to this question depends on the shape of the vol grid, not to
mention the pricing model employed. Investors who are interested in buying
forward vol will often look for trades in which the forward price is lower than
today’s spot price. Conversely, investors interested in selling forward vol are
16In two years at settlement, the strike will be set on the underlying swaption in the
forward vol trade to the then-prevailing at-the-money forward strike. Thus, there will be
no skew associated with the forward vol trade at settlement. However, in the replicating
strategy, the strike on the 7 into 10 swaption straddle will be set today. Thus, in two
years when valuing what is then a 5 into 10 swaption straddle, the strike may be signif-
icantly away-from-the-money, which implies that the value of the option will depend on
skew.

often interested in forward vol trades in which the forward price is above
today’s spot price. Let’s look at a normal implied vol swaption matrix, such
as in Table 9.1. Refer now to Figure 9.4 in which we suggested as our ﬁrst
(and far from exact) stab at recreating a 5 into 10 swaption straddle, two
years forward that we might buy some 7 into 10 straddles and sell some 2 into
10 straddles. Despite the inaccuracies in this approach, it turns out from a
practical point of view that if we compare 7 into 10 normal implied vol with
2 into 10 normal implied vol, this will usually give us a good indication as
to whether most pricing models used in industry will indicate that forward
vol is trading at a discount or premium to spot. For example, as shown
in Table 9.1, 7 into 10 is cheaper (in normalized vol terms) than 2 into
10, thereby suggesting that the forward price will be less than today’s spot
price. As we discussed in Section 9.2.2, 5 into 10 swaption straddles had a
spot price of 748 bps, whereas the forward price was only 725 bps.
9.2.5
Are Forward Vol Trades Truly a Pure View on Vol?
Not exactly. Recall that under the normal model, the value of an at-the-
money forward swaption straddle is

√
2
π × σN × A,
(9.1)
T ×
where T is the time to expiry of the swaption, σN is normal implied vol,
and A is the present value of a 1 bp annuity over the life of the underly-
ing swap. Under the normal model, as the market sells oﬀor rallies, even
425

<!-- Page 449 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
if σN remains constant (as the normal model assumes to be the case), the
value of the swaption will still change since the value of the annuity, A, de-
pends on the level of rates. Therefore, as the market rallies and the value
of the annuity A goes up, so will the value of the swaption, and vice versa.
Thus, swaption straddle prices have an inherent rate bias. The implication
of this is that forward vol trades do not represent a pure view on vol. For
example, the buyer of forward vol will beneﬁt, all else equal, as the mar-
ket rallies as that eﬀect in and of itself will raise the price of swaption
straddles.
In order to avoid this rate bias, some investors prefer to quote their
forward vol trades in terms of normal implied vol, σN, instead of swaption
straddle prices. From Equation 9.1, we have
T × A
×
π
σN = Straddle Price
√
2 .
(9.2)
In this parameterization, a forward vol trade is a forward contract on the
normal volatility of some underlying swaption straddle. Instead of quoting
a forward price, we quote a forward normal vol on an underlying swap-
tion straddle and deﬁne a set dollar amount for every basis point that
normal implied vol is above or below the forward vol. For example, sup-
pose an investor buys forward vol on 5 into 10 swaption straddles, two
years forward. The investor buys vol forward on a normal vol basis, in-
stead of on a price basis, and locks in a level of 70.7 bps. Assume that
the investor risks $100,000 per bp of normal implied vol. The payoﬀto
the investor in two years is $100,000 × (NIV −70.7), where NIV is the
normal implied vol of the underlying 5 into 10 swaption straddle in two
years.17
9.2.6
Bermudan Cancelable Swaps Revisited
In Section 6.2.3 we considered a 3nc1 Bermudan cancelable swap in which
we pay ﬁxed for three years and have the right to cancel in one year and
again in two years. We showed that our decision to cancel the trade in a year
depended (in part) on where 1 into 1 swaption vol was prevailing in a year.
We considered a case in which we would cancel the Bermudan in a year if
17The normal implied vol, NIV, of the swaption straddle is determined at the time of
settlement using Equation 9.2 and the then-prevailing spot price of the swaption straddle.
426

<!-- Page 450 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
then-prevailing 1 into 1 swaption vol were low and another case in which we
would not cancel the Bermudan in a year if then-prevailing 1 into 1 swaption
vol were high. When 1 into 1 swaption vol prevailing in a year was low, we
weren’t leaving much on the table by canceling early and forgoing the second
option. With a higher level of then-prevailing 1 into 1 swaption vol, it was
advantageous not to cancel so as to recognize the value of that option.
We concluded that the value of the 3nc1 Bermudan cancelable depends
(in part) on where we think 1 into 1 swaption vol would be in a year. The
higher that we think that 1 into 1 swaption vol will be in a year, the more
we think that we will likely keep the trade on for an additional year and not
cancel it at the ﬁrst cancelation date. By not canceling in a year, we will
be able to recognize the value of the remaining embedded option. Therefore,
the higher we think that 1 into 1 swaption vol will be in a year, the greater
value there is to the 3nc1 Bermudan cancelable.
This concept of where we “think” that 1 into 1 swaption vol will be pre-
vailing in a year is an example of forward vol. There is no reason to think
that 1 into 1 swaption vol prevailing in a year will be the same as today’s 1
into 1 swaption vol. The level of 1 into 1 swaption vol prevailing in a year
can be either less than, greater than, or equal to today’s 1 into 1 swaption
vol. Just as forward swap rates are sometimes interpreted as the market’s ex-
pectation of where swap rates will be in the future, forward vol is sometimes
interpreted as the market’s expectation of where swaption vol will be in the
future. Thus, the valuation of Bermudan cancelables is inexorably linked to
a consideration of forward vol. The higher 1 into 1 swaption vol, one year
forward is, the greater the value of the 3nc1 Bermudan cancelable.
The relatively recent advent of forward vol as a stand-alone trade has its
roots in the longer-established market for Bermudans. Traders who dealt in
Bermudans for years before the rise in popularity of forward vol were well
aware of the links between the two. And the issue of correlation risks that
are implicit in forward vol has been well understood by many to have a role
in the valuation of Bermudans.
9.3
Curve Options
A curve option or CMS spread option is an option whose payoﬀdepends on
the diﬀerence between two diﬀerent CMS rates.18 For example, a (linear)
curve cap on 2s10s has a payoﬀthat depends on
18As a reminder, CMS stands for Constant Maturity Swap. A CMS rate of a given
maturity is a ﬂoating rate of interest. For example, 10-year CMS is the ﬂoating rate of
interest equal to the 10-year swap rate.
427

<!-- Page 451 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
max[(10-year CMS −2-year CMS) −Strike, 0].
(9.3)
The buyer of a CMS curve cap is taking the view that the 2s10s curve is too
ﬂat (on a forward basis) and will steepen over time. Conversely, CMS curve
ﬂoors are used to take the view that the curve is too steep (on a forward
basis) and will ﬂatten over time. A linear curve ﬂoor on 2s10s has a payoﬀ
that depends on
max[Strike −(10-year CMS −2-year CMS), 0].
(9.4)
Example
Consider the curve option trade whose terms are shown in Table 9.2. In
this trade, the client pays an upfront premium of 80 bps of the notional,
or $800,000, to the dealer. Starting in three months and every three months
thereafter over the life of the trade, both 30-year CMS and 2-year CMS are re-
set. For any settings that are in-the-money (meaning, the diﬀerence between
30-year CMS and 2-year CMS is above the strike), the associated payment
is made three months later. Final payments (if any) occur ﬁve years from
the start of the trade.
2
When trades like these were ﬁrst being actively marketed to investors, the
terms of trades such as the one shown in Table 9.2 were often accompanied by
graphs like the one in Figure 9.6. This ﬁgure shows a long-term history of the
2s30s swap curve with the strike and breakeven on the trade superimposed.
The 2s30s curve needs to be in excess of 47 bps on a reset date in order for
the option to set in-the-money. Incorporating the upfront premium into the
trade, an average setting of 65 bps on each reset date is the level necessary
to break even on the trade.19 As shown in this ﬁgure, there were periods of
time in which 2s30s was far steeper than that, making this an attractive
trade to many.
Note that with this particular curve option, the buyer is actually pur-
chasing a series of quarterly options, much like the buyer of a cap or ﬂoor as
we discussed in Chapter 4. Sometimes linear curve option trades were struc-
tured such that the buyer would receive a payout depending only on a single
reset. As we will see in Section 9.3.1, other curve option trades are structured
19If the 2s30s curve were to set at the strike of 47 bps every period, then there would
never be any payoﬀfrom the option, and the net loss on the trade would be the 80 bps
upfront premium paid for the option. If the curve 2s30s sets at 65 bps every quarter, then
the buyer of the option would be receiving an annuity payout of 65 bps −47 bps = 18 bps,
paid quarterly for ﬁve years. The present value of this annuity is 80 bps, suﬃcient to oﬀset
the upfront premium for the option.
428

Table 9.2: Terms of Curve Cap on 2s30s
Figure 9.6: History of 2s30s with Trade Analysis
with binary payouts, in which case the buyer of the option would receive ei-
ther a ﬁxed payment or zero, depending on what happens to the underlying
curve.
Trades like these became very popular with investors in 2005 and 2006.
In 2004, products like these were nearly nonexistent. According to Sawyer
(2005), by the end of 2005 nearly $30 billion of these types of trades had
been printed, and some market participants have suggested that by 2007
hundreds of billions were in existence.
429

<!-- Page 453 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
9.3.1
Why Did Curve Options Come About?
In Chapter 7 we took a look at a very popular structured note called a non-
inversion note. As a reminder, let’s take a look at a non-inversion note whose
terms are found in Table 9.3.20
What’s going on here? The investor who is buying this note is receiving
an (above-market) ﬁxed coupon of 7% for the ﬁrst three years. Thereafter,
the investor will accrue this high ﬁxed coupon each day that the 2s10s swap
curve does not invert. If, however, the 2s10s curve inverts on any given day,
the investor accrues a 0% coupon for that day instead of a 7% coupon. To
put this diﬀerently, for the last seven years of this bond, the investor is
receiving a 7% coupon and is short some options. The options the investor
is short are binary in nature: they pay out a 7% coupon any day the 2s10s
curve inverts, and zero otherwise. As we discussed in Chapter 7, the dealer
that does the underlying swap transaction in conjunction with this note is
getting long these options when the note is created.
The nature of the options that the dealer is getting long is depicted in
Figure 9.7. In this ﬁgure, S denotes 10-year CMS −2-year CMS. On an
expiry date, when S ≥0, the option has zero payout. However, when S < 0,
the option pays out a ﬁxed amount equal to the 7% ﬁxed coupon of the bond.
Given the terms of the note in Table 9.3, the dealer is actually purchasing a
series of binary options of this form that expire daily, the ﬁrst of which expires
in three years and the last of which expires ten years after trade inception.
An Attractive Curve for Buying Non-Inversion Notes
The years 2005 and 2006 were marked by two very notable characteristics
of the swap curve. As shown in Figure 9.8, the curve (as measured here by
2s10s) was getting very ﬂat, and the curve was becoming less volatile. The
ﬂatness of the curve was thought to be driven primarily by the activities
of pension funds, which were known to be actively buying long-dated as-
sets and receiving in long-dated swaps. As the curve ﬂattened dramatically
(and the curve on a forward basis also ﬂattened), non-inversion notes be-
came more and more attractive. With the ﬂattening of both spot and for-
ward curves (as shown in Figure 9.8), pricing models placed a greater chance
on the curve inverting, an event whose occurrence would mean that the
buyer of a non-inversion note would get a zero coupon. As compensation for
bearing this risk, the ﬁxed coupons that could be oﬀered in newly issued
20Many non-inversion notes that have been printed are callable, meaning the issuer has
the right to call the bond from investors after some period of time. The existence of this
call feature enhances the coupon that can be paid to the bondholder. For simplicity, the
ﬁctitious example that we consider here is assumed to be non-callable.
430

<!-- Page 454 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Principal:
$100mm
Issuer:
U.S. Corporate
Maturity:
10 years
Coupons:
Years 1–3: 7% paid quarterly 30/360
Years 4–10: 7% × N/M paid quarterly 30/360
where N = number of days in quarter that Curve ≥0
M = number of days in quarter
Curve:
10-year CMS −2-year CMS
Table 9.3: Terms of a Non-Inversion Note
Figure 9.7: Payoﬀof a Binary Curve Option at Expiry
non-inversion notes were higher than what could be obtained in a steeper
curve environment, making these bonds even more attractive to investors
who believed that the 2s10s curve in swaps would never invert. Despite the
tremendous ﬂattening observed, the swap curve had historically been very
resistant to any inversion. This made some investors comfortable taking the
risks inherent in non-inversion notes.21
21The 2s10s swap curve did invert in 2006, ﬁrst in February and again in November,
but the inversion in each case was short-lived.
431

<!-- Page 455 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.8: 2s10s Swap Curve, Both Spot and One Year Forward, in 2005–
2006
Historically, a ﬂat curve has also been associated with low levels of re-
alized curve volatility. This was certainly true in 2006. Typically when the
curve is ﬂat, it is a period of time in which many market participants believe
the Fed to be on hold, as was the case during this period of time. In addi-
tion, dealers were getting long curve options from all of the non-inversion
notes that were being printed and, in many cases, had to maintain their
long position in these options as a liquid secondary market for such options
was then unavailable. Dealers who owned these options delta hedged them,
which meant that they had to put on outright ﬂatteners or steepeners as the
curve moved. As these binary options were out-of-the-money (forward), the
delta hedging activities by dealers who were long these options meant that
they would put on steepeners as the curve ﬂattened and put on ﬂatteners as
the curve steepened. This meant that, on the margin, the delta hedging of
dealers actually served to reduce volatility in the curve during this time. This
factor is thought by many market participants to have greatly contributed
to the low level of realized volatility in the curve during this time.22
22Most of these binary curve options that dealers implicitly purchased through the is-
suance of non-inversion notes were “low-strike” options. For example, in the non-inversion
note in Table 9.3, the embedded binary options are struck at zero. As suggested by Fig-
ure 9.8, these binary options were thus struck out-of-the-money (forward).
Consider now an option trader who is long a put option on a stock and is delta hedging
the option. As the stock price goes down, the option trader will buy the stock to delta
hedge, which puts (marginal) upward pressure on the stock price. As the stock price goes
up, the option trader will sell the stock to delta hedge, which puts (marginal) downward
432

<!-- Page 456 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
9.3.2
Implied Correlation
Let’s consider again the nature of the options that the dealer is buying when
the non-inversion note in Table 9.3 is issued. Deﬁne S = 10-year CMS −
2-year CMS. Consider an option that has a daily look each day for a period
of seven years, starting in three years. The daily accrual from the option is
as follows:
Payoﬀ=
 7%
if S < 0
0
otherwise.
(9.5)
Based on these daily accruals, payments from the option are made at the end
of each quarter over this seven-year period. The payment made at the end of
each quarter is a function of the quantity
Notional × 7% × N/M,
where N is the number of days in the quarter that S < 0, and M is the
number of days in the quarter.
This curve option is binary in nature (as compared to the curve options
with linear payouts that we looked at in Equations 9.3 and 9.4). Furthermore,
it is referred to as a curve ﬂoor because it pays out when the curve, as
measured by S, ﬁnishes below some strike (in this case, 0).
What’s the volatility of the underlying, S? Its variance is given by
σ2
S = σ2
10Y + σ2
2Y −2ρ2Y,10Y σ10Y σ2Y ,
(9.6)
where ρ2Y,10Y is the implied correlation between the 2-year swap rate and
the 10-year swap rate. Thus, the implied volatility of the curve, S, depends
pressure on the stock price. Thus, the delta hedger of this put option actually serves to
stabilize the price of the underlying on the margin. If the option position is large enough,
the delta hedging of the option position could actually exert signiﬁcant pressure that would
cause the underlying stock price to remain remarkably nonvolatile. See Appendix A for a
review of these dynamics.
This is precisely what happened as the supply of curve options mounted at dealers,
and they delta hedged these positions. In the case of curve options, to delta hedge means
to either put on a steeper or ﬂattener as the curve changes shape. As dealers were long out-
of-the money binary curve options, as the curve steepened, they put on ﬂatteners. And as
the curve ﬂattened, they put on steepeners. Thus, the delta hedging of curve options by
dealers tended to lower the realized vol of the curve. The large position of curve options
dealers owned and the related delta hedging of these options were thought to be a major
force behind the limited curve movements observed in 2006.
The nature of the delta hedging activities of dealers who are long binary curve options
depends crucially on whether or not the options are in-the-money or out-of-the-money.
As indicated, when these options are out-of-the-money, the delta hedging activities of
dealers serves to limit curve movements. As we shall discuss, the delta hedging activities
by dealers who are long these options dramatically changes and serves to exacerbate curve
moves when these options are in-the-money.
433

<!-- Page 457 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
not only on the implied volatility of 10-year CMS and the implied volatility
of 2-year CMS but also on the correlation between them. As suggested by
Equation 9.6, implied volatility of the curve and implied correlation of the
curve have an inverse relationship; as the implied volatility of the curve goes
up, this must mean that the implied correlation of the curve must go down
(all else equal), and vice versa.
Thus, the proliferation of this type of option introduced a new type of
risk; not only were dealers and investors exposed to the volatility of the
2-year swap rate and the 10-year swap rate, but they were also exposed to
the correlation between them. We refer to this type of exposure as correlation
risk. There was not a well-formed market for correlation risk in the rates
markets at this time, and often dealers had to live with this correlation risk
in their books, as there were limited avenues to get out of it.
9.3.3
Implied Volatility Versus Realized Volatility
We have thrown around two concepts of volatility, implied and realized, and
should be careful to distinguish between the two. Implied volatility refers to
the volatility computed from option prices. In the case of curve options, as
the supply of curve options increased as more and more non-inversion notes
were printed, the price dealers were willing to pay for these options went
down. And as the price of curve options went down, this meant that implied
volatilities went down as well.23 Recall again Equation 9.6, which shows the
relationship between the variance of the diﬀerence of two random variables
and their correlation. From this equation, we see that as implied volatility
goes down, this must mean that implied correlation must go up.24
The implied volatility of curve options depends on the expiry of the op-
tion. A typical relationship often found in 2005–2006 between the implied
vol of a curve option and its expiry is shown in Figure 9.9. As shown in this
ﬁgure, short-dated curve options usually traded at implied volatilities that
were higher than those found with longer-dated curve options.25 There are
23As discussed in Appendix A, the vega of binary options can be either positive or
negative, depending on the moneyness of the option. In the case of binary curve options
written in newly issued non-inversion notes, the options were out-of-the-money, and as
such they had positive vega and a decrease in the price of the option was associated with
a decrease in implied vol.
24This is not as mysterious as it may seem. As implied volatility of the curve goes
down, this means that the curve, say, 2s10s, is not expected to move a lot. If the curve is
not moving around a lot, this means that the 2-year swap rate and the 10-year swap rate
must be moving together. That is to say, there is high correlation between the two rates.
Low implied volatility of the curve implies high implied correlation, and vice versa.
25The pattern observed here, with short-dated curve options trading at a higher implied
volatility than longer-dated options, tended to be much more pronounced than what was
typically observed in most other rate option markets.
434

<!-- Page 458 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.9: Implied Volatility of Curve Options on 2s10s Typically Observed
in 2005–2006
two reasons often cited for this pattern. First and foremost, there tended
to be relatively little supply of short-dated curve options from non-inversion
notes. Most of the these notes had ﬁxed coupons for at least a year, and thus
the options embedded in these notes tended to be longer dated. For example,
the note in Table 9.3 has ﬁxed coupons for three years, and the curve op-
tions have expiries of three years and longer. The supply of long-dated curve
options depressed the vol of these options as we have discussed, certainly rel-
ative to scarcer short-dated curve options. Second, some have suggested that
the downward-sloping shape of the vol curve for curve options is consistent
with the notion of mean reversion of interest rates.
Realized volatility (sometimes called actual volatility) refers to the empir-
ically observed volatility of a traded variable. As mentioned, due to dealer
delta hedging of curve options in 2005–2006, realized vol of the curve was
decreasing over time. This can be seen by again looking at Figure 9.8; not
only was the curve ﬂattening signiﬁcantly over this time, but the realized
volatility of the curve was decreasing as well. For example, in 2006 the 2s10s
curve stayed between 0 bps and 20 bps most of the year, whereas in the prior
year it had traded in a range in excess of 100 bps.
In Section 4.1.5 we said that the name of the game for dealers that make
markets in options is determining what vol assumptions to use when pricing
these options. That is only half of the game. The other half of the game
has to do with implied volatility versus realized volatility. When dealers buy
or sell options, they often delta hedge them. A dealer who sells an option
435

<!-- Page 459 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
takes in premium today and will delta hedge the option based on the realized
moves in the underlying over the life of the option. Assuming the option has
positive gamma, delta hedging will be costly. The premium can be thought of
as compensation for any costs of delta hedging over the life over the option.
A dealer who sells an option and delta hedges is therefore hoping for muted
moves in the underlying.
On the other hand, a dealer who buys an option pays a premium and will
also delta hedge based on realized moves in the underlying over the life of
the option. In this case delta hedging the option based on realized moves will
provide proﬁts to the dealer, again assuming the option has positive gamma.
A dealer who buys an option and delta hedges is therefore hoping for large
realized moves in the underlying.
Thus as dealers buy and sell options, determining the implied volatilities
at which they are willing to trade, they are also implicitly expressing a view
on the level of implied volatility versus realized volatility. Those who think
that there will be relatively low realized volatility in the underlying will want
to sell options to take in premium and hope to experience low delta hedging
costs over the life of the option. Those who think that there will be high
realized volatility in the underlying will prefer to buy options and delta
hedge, expressing the view that their proﬁts from delta hedging will exceed
the cost of the option.
9.3.4
Supply and Demand of Curve Options
As more and more non-inversion notes were printed in 2005–2006, dealers
were getting longer and longer binary curve ﬂoors. There were a number of
underlying curve points that were popular: 10s30s was a very popular choice,
representing by some estimates approximately 40% of all non-inversion is-
suance during this period, with 2s10s and 2s30s representing a bulk of other
types (see Patel [2006]). In addition, notes were written with a variety of dif-
ferent strikes. Although a strike of zero was a popular choice, other strikes
existed as well. For example, some investors looking to buy non-inversion
notes wanted a bit more protection than aﬀorded by having a strike of 0 bps
and were interested in notes with lower strikes such as −20 bps.
In general, though, the theme was that through the surge of issuance of
non-inversion notes, dealers were getting long low-strike binary curve ﬂoors.
The best way to hedge this position would just be to sell it, but oftentimes
it was hard to ﬁnd investors willing to buy low-strike binary curve ﬂoors.
Dealers had a bit more success ﬁnding investors willing to buy low-strike lin-
ear curve ﬂoors but often had to move the strike up a bit in order to entice
investors to buy such a thing. For example, if a dealer had bought a binary
436

<!-- Page 460 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
curve ﬂoor on 2s10s struck at −20 bps through the issuance of a non-inversion
note, it might be able to ﬁnd an investor willing to buy a linear ﬂoor on 2s10s
struck at +20 bps. Although this left the dealer with some strike mismatch
as well as a mismatch between binary and linear options, at least it enabled
the dealer to get out of some of the correlation risk it had obtained.
As investors became more familiar with curve options, dealers tried to
sell a variety of low-strike curve ﬂoors. But the real demand for the product
was in high-strike curve caps. There were many investors in 2006 and 2007
who were expecting the curve to steepen dramatically. As 2s10s hovered
around 0 bps in 2006, many investors were interested in buying, for example,
curve caps on 2s10s struck at +50 bps or +100 bps that expire in a year or
more. These options were very far out-of-the-money (both on a spot basis
and forward basis) and were therefore cheap. Some investors, most notably
hedge funds, would view those options as cheap “lottery tickets” (and indeed
some of these investments did pay oﬀin the years to follow when the curve
steepened dramatically). Demand for these high-strike curve caps was strong.
In summary, in 2005 and 2006 dealers found themselves in the position of
being long a lot of low-strike curve ﬂoors and being short a lot of high-strike
curve caps. The curve ﬂoors they were long were often near-the-money, while
the curve caps they were short were out-of-the-money. Sitting on such strike
mismatch was not a great position for dealers to be in. But in most cases,
this was the best that dealers could do in order to get out of some of the
correlation risk they were exposed to by issuing non-inversion notes.
9.3.5
The Pricing of Curve Options
The pricing of curve options is complicated due to the correlation of the two
underlying points of the curve.26 As more dealers started pricing curve op-
tions in 2005, it was common to see a wide variation in prices shown by dif-
ferent dealers. This called into question the types of methodologies that
diﬀerent dealers used to price these options.
Let’s consider again a linear curve option on 2s10s and deﬁne S =
10-year CMS −2-year CMS. As discussed in Sawyer (2005), dealers used
essentially one of the following three methodologies (presented in order of
increasing complexity and sophistication) to price curve options:
• Methodology 1: Regard S as a primitive random variable in and of itself
(i.e., do not regard S as the diﬀerence between two random variables
26Actually, it’s more complicated than that. Recall that in Section 6.5 we discussed
that a convexity adjustment to the forward swap rate is required if a counterparty is
paying a CMS rate. Similarly, the forwards used to price options on CMS rates should
also be convexity adjusted. We will ignore this fact throughout this chapter.
437

<!-- Page 461 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
but rather as a single random variable). Make distributional assump-
tions about the variable S and look at the volatility of S.
• Methodology 2: Recognize that 10-year CMS and 2-year CMS are each
random variables, make distributional assumptions on each and use
implied volatilities for each, and incorporate a “ﬂat” (i.e., non–time
varying) correlation parameter to achieve a distribution for their dif-
ference, S. Under this methodology, the correlation between 10-year
CMS and 2-year CMS is assumed to remain constant through time.
• Methodology 3: Again recognize that 10-year CMS and 2-year CMS are
each random variables and make distributional assumptions on each
and use implied volatilities for each but now allow for a term structure
of correlation (i.e., the correlation parameter varies over time).
The ﬁrst methodology is not particularly robust, and it does not even
consider the correlation between 10-year CMS and 2-year CMS.27 The sec-
ond methodology is much more general but does not allow for a time-varying
correlation component, whereas the third methodology does. The diﬀerence
between Methodologies 2 and 3 can be seen in Figure 9.10. Under Method-
ology 2, implied correlations remain constant as the expiry of the option
varies. Under Methodology 3, implied correlations are assumed to change
and increase as time to expiration increases.28
Although Methodologies 2 and 3 are both fairly robust, the pricing of
curve options using each methodology creates inconsistencies that cannot be
reconciled. For example, suppose that we are pricing up a short-dated linear
curve option. If we are using a ﬂat correlation assumption as per Methodol-
ogy 2, this means that we will come up with a price for the curve option that
is lower than someone who is employing a time-varying correlation model
as per Methodology 3.29 But if we are pricing up a long-dated linear curve
option, then using a ﬂat correlation assumption as per Methodology 2 means
27Note that if employing the ﬁrst methodology, it would be a bad idea to assume that
S follows a lognormal distribution; S can take on signiﬁcantly negative values, whereas a
lognormally distributed variable is always positive.
28The fact that the implied correlation curve under Methodology 3 is upward sloping
is related to the fact that implied volatilities for curve options typically decline as expiries
increase, as shown in Figure 9.9. Recall that as implied volatilities for curve options go
up, this must imply that implied correlations go down, and vice versa. Thus, since the
implied volatility curve for curve options is downward sloping, that means the implied
correlation curve must be upward sloping everything else the same.
29Under Methodology 2, short-dated curve options are priced using a higher implied
correlation than under Methodology 3. Thus, the implied volatility used under Method-
ology 2 must be lower, which means that the option price must be lower, assuming the
option has positive vega.
438

<!-- Page 462 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.10: Comparison of Method 2 and Method 3
we will come up with a higher price for the option than someone using a time-
varying implied correlation parameter.
As the demand for non-inversion notes grew tremendously in 2005–2006,
this meant that the supply of curve options that dealers got long increased
dramatically. This tended to depress the prices of curve options, which meant
that the implied volatilities of curve options came down and implied corre-
lations went up. During this time, implied correlations were trading high,
certainly compared to their long-run historical averages. That is, the levels
of implied correlations obtained from curve option prices were signiﬁcantly
higher than what was empirically observed in the market over a long period
of time.
Correlation Skews
Recall that skew refers to the fact that options with diﬀerent strikes have
diﬀerent implied volatilities. Correlation skew refers to the fact that when
one computes the implied correlation from a variety of curve options with
diﬀerent strikes, implied correlation varies in accordance with a change in
strike. For example, consider the curve caps and ﬂoors in Table 9.4 that all
expire in one year.
Each of these options has a diﬀerent strike and may trade at a diﬀerent
implied vol and therefore trade at a diﬀerent implied correlation. What was
439

<!-- Page 463 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Option Type
Payoﬀ
Strike
Curve Cap
max(S −200 bps, 0)
+200
Curve Cap
max(S −100 bps, 0)
+100
Curve Cap
max(S −50 bps, 0)
+ 50
Curve Floor
max(0 bps −S, 0)
0
Curve Floor
max(−20 bps −S, 0)
−20
Table 9.4: A Variety of Curve Options and Their Strikes
typically observed in the market in 2005–2006 was that high-strike curve
options traded at a lower implied correlation than did at-the-money ones.
The intuition for this is as follows: high-strike curve caps come into-the-
money when the curve is steep. Historically, steep yield curve environments
have been associated with periods of high volatility, and vice versa. During
this time we observed that implied curve vol for high-strike curve caps was
high, which meant that implied correlation was low. We also observed that
very low-strike curve options, which come in-the-money when the curve is
signiﬁcantly inverted often traded at relatively high implied vols and thus
relatively low implied correlations as well.30
But regardless of these perceived dynamics, it is ultimately the supply and
demand of curve options of diﬀerent strikes that impact the implied volatil-
ities and therefore the implied correlations at which these options trade.
As we have discussed, the popularity of non-inversion notes created a large
30If an options trader sells a high-strike curve cap that is out-of-the-money today, it
will have relatively low gamma. As the option approaches the money, its gamma will
increase and thus the trader will be forced to delta hedge his short gamma position. For
this option to approach the money means that the curve steepens out, and historically
the realized volatility of the curve as well as implied vols tend to be high in a steep curve
environment. Thus, the option trader will be forced to delta hedge in a situation in which
he expects realized vol to be high. It is an understanding of this dynamic that, in part,
has option traders selling high-strike curve options at high implied vols and therefore at
low implied correlations.
Consider now an option trader who sells a very low-strike curve ﬂoor that is out-of-the-
money today. It, too, will have relatively low gamma. As the option approaches the money
(meaning the curve is signiﬁcantly inverted), its gamma will increase and thus the trader
will need to delta hedge his short gamma position. But this will happen in an inverted
curve environment, and many traders believed the realized and implied vol of the curve
would be high in such a curve environment Thus, the option trader will be forced to delta
hedge in a situation in which he expects realized vol to be high. Collectively, these hedging
dynamics suggest that a trader will raise the implied vol that he is willing to sell both
high-strike and very low-strike curve options.
440

<!-- Page 464 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
supply of low-strike (binary) curve ﬂoors for dealers. While there was some
interest from investors to buy these low-strike curve ﬂoors, investors were
primarily interested in buying high-strike (mostly linear) curve caps, think-
ing that despite the ﬂattening of the curve in 2005 and 2006, the curve
would eventually resteepen. The buying of linear curve caps was a popular
means of positioning for this eventual resteepening of the curve. Thus, deal-
ers often found themselves in the position of being long low-strike binary
curve ﬂoors through their selling of non-inversion notes and being short
high-strike linear curve caps due to the demand for these options from
investors. Thus, the forces of supply and demand created a situation in
which the implied volatility of low-strike curve options traded at lower lev-
els than for high-strike curve options. This translates into saying that low-
strike curve options traded at a higher implied correlation than high-strike
curve options. Very low-strike curve options (which come into-the-money
when the curve is signiﬁcantly inverted) also traded at relatively low im-
plied correlations during this time. There weren’t many non-inversion notes
printed with very low strikes, and thus very low-strike curve options were
in limited supply. These phenomena are demonstrated in Figure 9.11, which
shows the implied correlation skew for a set of curve options with diﬀerent
strikes.
Figure 9.11: Typical Correlation Skew on 2s10s Curve Options with an
Expiry of Two Years in 2005
441

<!-- Page 465 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
9.3.6
A Couple of Trades
Let’s look now at two trades involving curve options that were very popular
in 2007.
Trade 1
In spring 2007 the swap curve had a rather unusual shape to it, as shown
in Figure 9.12. This is what the curve looked like in the months preceding
the ﬁnancial crisis and reﬂected the debate in the market about how serious
the housing crunch was going to be. Some were saying that the Fed was
going to have to cut rates a lot in the coming months (and they were right),
while others said that the eﬀects of the housing crunch were overblown and
would not aﬀect the economy in any major way. As this debate raged on,
the result was a swap curve that was severely inverted in the short end and
was subsequently upward sloping thereafter. This unusual kink lent itself to
some interesting trades that could be put together by taking advantage of
the forward curve.
Using a proprietary swap model, we can derive 3-month LIBOR forwards
by bootstrapping this curve and can then determine the swap curve one year
Figure 9.12: The Swap Curve in Spring 2007
442

<!-- Page 466 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.13: 3-Month LIBOR Forwards Derived from Bootstrapping the
Curve in Figure 9.12
forward using techniques we have developed in Chapter 3. These results
are shown in Figures 9.13 and 9.14. Using data from Figure 9.14, we can
determine 1s5s spot and 1s5s one year forward, as displayed in Table 9.5. As
indicated in this table, although spot 1s5s was inverted 23.3 bps at this time,
the forwards were suggesting that 1s5s would be +20.9 bps steep in one year.
This “expected” steepening of 23.3 + 20.9 = 44.2 bps over the course of the
year was a signiﬁcant amount of carry. Anyone who put on steepeners would
experience signiﬁcant negative carry. Those who did the reverse, and put
on ﬂatteners, would experience signiﬁcant positive carry.31 Although people
were
aware of the possibility of the housing crunch further deteriorating,
31In Chapter 8, we discussed the notion that if we put on a steepener when the curve
was expected to steepen, we would earn negative carry over a holding period, whereas if
we put on a ﬂattener we would earn positive carry. Intuitively, if we want to go with the
forwards, we have to pay to do so, whereas if we are willing to go against the forwards,
we will be paid to do so.
443

<!-- Page 467 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.14: The Swap Curve in Spring 2007 and the Curve One Year Forward
Spot
One Year Forward

5.091%

5.339%

1s5s:

+20.9 bps
Table 9.5: 1s5s Spot and 1s5s, One Year Forward in Spring 2007
thereby prompting the Fed to ease, they found the large positive carry from
ﬂatteners very appealing. Recognizing that they could get stopped out (i.e.,
hit a stop-loss) if they put on a ﬂattener using swaps and the trade went
against them, many avoided this trade.
Curve ﬂoors presented a nice opportunity for investors who were inclined
to take a view against the forwards, taking the view that the curve would not
444

<!-- Page 468 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
steepen out nearly as much as the 44.2 bps that the market was looking for
over the next year. Consider a linear curve ﬂoor on 1s5s struck at-the-money
forward that expires in one year. This ﬂoor pays out 1 bp of the notional for
each bp that 1s5s is ﬂatter than the strike at expiry. That is, the payout on
this option in one year is
max[Strike −(5-year CMS −1-year CMS), 0] × Notional.
(9.7)
What is the at-the-money forward strike on this option? It’s +20.9 bps, the
market’s expectation of where 1s5s will be in a year.32 Suppose that at the
time, this option could be purchased for 12 bps.
This was considered by many to be an attractive opportunity. This is
particularly true in terms of the “carry and roll-down” on the trade. Suppose
we buy this option today for 12 bps of the notional and hold the position
until maturity. Assuming an unchanged spot swap curve, the payout from
this option would be
max[+20.9 bps −(5.106% −5.339%), 0] = 44.2 bps,
(9.8)
for a net proﬁt (excluding NPV considerations) of 44.2 bps −12 bps = 32.2
bps, a nice trade.
What actually happened? What actually happened was not nearly as
pretty as this carry and roll-down analysis. Over the course of the next year,
the ﬁnancial crisis worsened and the Fed cut short-term rates and the curve
steepened out signiﬁcantly. At the time of expiration, the swap curve was as
depicted in Figure 9.15. As this ﬁgure shows, 1s5s was 3.283% −2.362% =
+92.1 bps at expiration. Thus, the option ﬁnished very far out-of-the-money.
So an investor who bought this option would have lost the premium paid at
trade inception and would have received no payout at expiration.
Although this trade did not perform well, it was in many ways a much
safer trade than if someone had put on a 1s5s ﬂattener outright. For example,
suppose an investor wanted to risk $100,000 per bp on the ﬂattener. That
is, the investor was interested in putting on a trade in which every basis
point change in 1s5s results in a payout change of $100,000. If the investor
bought the curve ﬂoor, this means that the investor would buy a notional of
$1bn. The premium of 12 bps of the notional would have been paid with no
subsequent payout from the option, amounting to a loss of $1.2mm. Alter-
natively, suppose the investor put on an outright forward ﬂattener by paying
32Technically, the at-the-money forward strike on this option is not +20.9 bps. That
diﬀerential was computed using the diﬀerence in the two relevant forward swap rates. As
we discussed in Chapter 6, there is a convexity adjustment that must be made to swap
rates that are being paid out as underlying variables. Once again, we will ignore such
adjustments for our purposes throughout this chapter.
445

<!-- Page 469 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.15: The Swap Curve in Spring 2008
on a 1x2 and receiving on a 1x6 and chose notionals such that he was risking
$100,000 per bp. This trade would have been put on at a rate diﬀerential
of +20.9 bps, the diﬀerence between the two forward starting swap rates.
Assuming this trade was held for a year, 1s5s ﬁnishes at +92.1 bps, resulting
in a loss of +92.1 −20.9 = 71.2 bps, or approximately $7.12mm.33
Trade 2
The following trade opportunity was available in 2007 and exploited the
shape of both the forward curve and the vol curve for curve options. Re-
fer now to Figure 9.16, the top half of which shows the vol curve for curve
33Most investors would have probably hit a predetermined stop-loss on the trade prior
to one year going by, given the large move in the curve, and unwound the trade somewhere
along the way. The nice thing about the version of the trade using the curve option is
that it gives one a well-deﬁned downside equal to the premium paid for the option.
446

<!-- Page 470 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.16: The Vol Curve for Curve Options (top half ) and the 2s10s
Curve, Spot and Forward (bottom half )
options. As we have discussed previously, short-dated curve options typi-
cally trade at a higher implied vol than do long-dated options, and this
diﬀerential is rather pronounced. The bottom half of this ﬁgure shows 2s10s,
both spot and forward, at this time. For example, 2s10s spot was around 73
447

<!-- Page 471 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
bps, a year forward was around 100 bps, and two years forward was around
80 bps.34
Consider now an investor who is presented with the vol and swap curves
in this ﬁgure and has the view that neither curve is going to change over the
horizon of a year. Suppose the investor purchases a linear curve cap on 2s10s
that expires in two years and is struck at-the-money forward. This cap pays
out 1 bp of the notional for each bp that 2s10s is steeper than the strike at
expiry. That is, the payout on this option in two years is
max[(10-year CMS −2-year CMS) −Strike, 0] × Notional.
(9.9)
Assume this option can be purchased by the investor for 33 bps of the no-
tional. Suppose now the investor holds on to the option for one year (at which
time the option has one year remaining in its life). Consider the change in
the value of the option over this time frame under the assumption that the
swap curve and vol curve are both unchanged in one year. The change in
the value of the option is displayed in Table 9.6.
What’s going on here? The investor buys the option for 33 bps, and a
year later it is worth 37.5 bps. We usually think of the value of options going
down as time to expiry draws near and the market doesn’t move due to the
time decay of the option, but the value of this option is actually going up.
“These go to eleven.”35 The time decay of the option is certainly a factor
34It is worth repeating that it would be most appropriate to look at convexity-adjusted
forward rates in the analysis of curve options. We will continue to ignore such adjustments
for our purposes here.
35In the movie This Is Spinal Tap, musician Nigel Tufnel (played by Christopher Guest)
is being interviewed by Marty DiBergi (played by Rob Reiner). Nigel is showing his am-
pliﬁer to Marty and points out that the volume knobs all go to a highest setting of 11 (as
compared to most ampliﬁers, whose volume knobs only go up to 10). Nigel then claims
that this numerical schema means that his amp is louder (“It’s one louder”). When Marty
suggests that the volume knobs could be renumbered so that 10 is loudest, Nigel, who is
clearly confused, replies after a bit, “These go to eleven.” The excerpt from the movie is
as follows:
Nigel: . . . the numbers all go to eleven. Look . . . right across the board.
Marty: Ahh . . . oh, I see. . . .
Nigel: Eleven . . . eleven . . . eleven. . . .
Marty: . . . and most of these amps go up to ten. . . .

Nigel: Well, it’s one louder, isn’t it? It’s not ten. You see, most . . . most

up . . . all the way up. . . .
Marty: Yeah.. . .
