here, and according to Table 9.6 the value of that time decay is 7.6 bps after
one year, assuming the vol curve and swap curve do not change. That is,
assuming the vol and swap curves do not change over the course of the next
year, the value of the option would decline 7.6 bps due to theta decay.
But there are two other factors at work here that counteract the theta
bleed. First, recall from Figure 9.16 that 2s10s, two years forward is about 80
bps, but one year forward it’s about 100 bps. That is, if the curve does not
change at all over the course of a year, the underlying 2s10s curve will steepen
about 20 bps. That is a good thing for the holder of a curve cap. Given the
curve cap in question, in one year the option will have one year remaining
in its life, and according to Table 9.6, the option will increase in value by
9.6 bps due to this approximately 20 bps steepening of the curve.36 Second,
when ﬁrst purchased the option has two years until expiry, and thus after
one year it has only one year until expiration. But according to Figure 9.16
the implied vol used to price one-year curve options is signiﬁcantly higher
than that used to price two-year curve options. According to Table 9.6, the
change in the value of the option when it has one year remaining due to using
the higher implied vol is +2.5 bps. As shown in Table 9.6, the net eﬀect of
these forces is that despite the loss of one year in the life of the option, the
value of the option actually increases 4.5 bps.
Nigel: Nowhere. Exactly. What we do is if we need that extra . . . push over
the cliﬀ. . . you know what we do?
Marty: Put it up to eleven.
Nigel: Eleven. Exactly. One louder.
Marty: Why don’t you just make ten louder and make ten be the top . . .
number . . . and make that a little louder?
Nigel: . . . these go to eleven.
From This Is Spinal Tap, released on March 2, 1984. Used by permission of Studio Canal.
36Remember this is an option, and its delta in general will be less than one. Therefore,
although the curve steepens 20 bps, this does not mean that the value of the option will
increase 20 bps. It will increase by a lesser amount.
449

<!-- Page 473 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
According to this “carry and roll-down” analysis, buying this option and
holding it for a year is a pretty attractive proposition if neither the vol curve
nor the swap curve changes shape. Of course, most investors don’t literally
believe that these assumptions will hold true. Most will probably buy the
curve option because they believe the curve will steepen. But if the investor
is wrong, this analysis indicates the option should perform pretty well for the
investor in case the market doesn’t change much over the course of the year.37
9.3.7
Delta Hedging Curve Options
Recall that as more and more non-inversion notes were printed, dealers were
getting longer and longer low-strike binary curve ﬂoors. As there was little
demand from investors for these types of options, dealers most often sat on
them. They had bought the options at relatively low (i.e., attractive) levels of
implied volatility and would hedge out the risks associated with the positions
as best as they could. The risk of prime importance was delta, which in the
case of a curve option means the change in the value of the option with
respect to a change in the steepness or ﬂatness of the underlying curve.38
Let’s consider a particular example. Deﬁne S = 30-year CMS −10-year
CMS. Consider a binary curve ﬂoor that pays oﬀa ﬁxed coupon 7% when S
is less than 0 and 0% otherwise. When we think about delta hedging a curve
option, it may be easier just to think of S as the sole underlying variable itself
and not focus on the fact that it’s actually the diﬀerence between two random
variables. The payoﬀat expiry of this option is shown in Figure 9.7. When S
is positive, this option pays out zero, and when it’s negative, the option pays
out a 7% coupon. This payoﬀproﬁle looks very similar to the binary options
we examine in Appendix A, except of course that the underlying random
variable S can take on negative values in addition to positive ones. When
S gets bigger, that means the curve is steeper, and when it’s smaller, that
means the curve is ﬂatter. And when S is negative, that means the curve is
inverted.
In Appendix A, we show that the delta proﬁle of binary options is un-
usual. In the case of a binary put on a stock, when the underlying is high and
the option is out-of-the-money, as the underlying goes down and approaches
37This type of analysis does not take into account a number of factors that could
decrease the value of the options. For example, if curve option vol comes down and/or
the (forward) swap curve ﬂattens over the next year, the value of the curve option will
decrease.
38Although the most appropriate delta hedge might well consist of forward starting
swaps, some dealers would instead use spot starting swaps for delta hedging curve options
inasmuch as they have slightly better liquidity. For ease of exposition, we will consider
the use of spot starting swaps when we speak of delta hedging curve options.
450

<!-- Page 474 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
the strike, a trader who is long the option and delta hedging will be buy-
ing stock. And provided the stock doesn’t cross below the strike and starts
going back up, a delta hedger will sell some of the stock. So, in summary,
provided the underlying remains above the strike, a delta hedger who is long
the binary put will buy stock as the underlying goes down and will sell stock
as the underlying goes up. But if the underlying should fall below the strike,
then there is a dramatic change in the delta hedging activities of the trader.
Once the underlying crosses below the strike, things work in reverse. With
the underlying below the strike, a further decline in the underlying will cause
a delta hedger to sell some of the underlying. And if the underlying should
rise (but still remains below the strike), the delta hedger will buy stock. As
we discussed in the Appendix A, the seemingly perverse change in the delta
hedging activities are due to the fact that the binary put option has positive
gamma when the option is out-of-the-money and negative gamma when the
option is in-the-money.
The same logic applies to a binary curve ﬂoor. Suppose we are long an
out-of-the-money binary curve ﬂoor and are delta hedging the position. Then
our delta hedging activities will be as follows:
1. Provided that the curve doesn’t ever ﬂatten too much and the option
remains out-of-the-money, then we will put on ﬂatteners when the
curve steepens and put on steepeners when the curve ﬂattens.
2. If the curve should ever ﬂatten enough such that the option is in-the-
money, then we will put on ﬂatteners when the curve ﬂattens and put
on steepeners when the curve steepens (provided, of course, that the
curve doesn’t steepen so much such that the option is again out-of-
the-money, in which case we revert to the above case).
Assuming that the size of the option position is large enough, the delta
hedging of the position can have signiﬁcant implications on curve dynamics.
Provided the curve is steep and the option is out-of-the-money, the delta
hedging activities of someone who is long this option will actually tend to be
a stabilizing force on the curve. That’s because as the curve ﬂattens, a delta
hedger who comes in and puts on steepeners will cause the curve to steepen
(on the margin) in the face of the ﬂattening.39 And as the curve steepens,
delta hedgers will come in and put on ﬂatteners, which on the margin will
cause the curve to ﬂatten. This was exactly the dynamic that contributed to
39If an investor puts on a steepener in swaps, then he receives in the short-tenor swap
and pays in the long-tenor swap. This will, on the margin, put downward pressure on the
short-tenor swap rate and upward pressure on the long-tenor swap rate, which means that
the delta hedger’s activities will put pressure on the curve to steepen, counteracting the
ﬂattening that was prevailing.
451

<!-- Page 475 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.17: Delta Hedging Proﬁle of a Binary Curve Floor Struck at 0
the low realized vol curve dynamics in 2006.40 In aggregate, the delta hedging
activities of the many dealers who were long these out-of-the-money binary
put options were a signiﬁcant force. As the curve would steepen, they would
come in en masse and put on ﬂatteners, which stabilized the curve. And when
the curve would ﬂatten, they would come in and put on steepeners, which
again stabilized the curve. These stabilizing forces would be at work, pro-
vided the curve did not ﬂatten so much that the options went in-the-money.
However, if the curve were to ﬂatten enough such that the binary ﬂoors
were in-the-money, the delta hedging activities of someone who is long the
40This analysis implicitly assumes that only dealers delta hedged curve options, while
buy-side participants who traded them did not. This assumption was indeed generally
the case. Note that if both buyers and sellers of curve options were to delta hedge, their
hedging would cancel one another out and therefore net hedging activities would have no
eﬀect on the curve.
452

<!-- Page 476 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
option change dramatically and actually become a destabilizing force for
the curve. When the binary ﬂoor is in-the-money, as the curve starts to
ﬂatten, a delta hedger who is long the ﬂoor will actually put on ﬂatteners,
not steepeners. On the margin, as the curve is ﬂattening and a delta hedger
puts on ﬂatteners himself, this will serve to cause the curve to ﬂatten some
more. Conversely, provided the curve is ﬂat enough such that the binary
ﬂoors are in-the-money, a steepening curve will be met by a delta hedger
who needs to put on steepeners himself. This, too, will serve to cause the
curve to steepen further in the face of steepening.
The delta hedging proﬁle for a binary curve ﬂoor is summarized in Fig-
ure 9.17. In this particular case we have assumed that the binary ﬂoor is
struck at zero. When the curve is positively sloped, the delta hedging activi-
ties of someone who is long the option tend to stabilize the curve. But when
the curve is inverted, the delta hedging activities of someone who is long the
option tend to destabilize the curve.
9.3.8
So Why Did 30-Year Swap Spreads Go Negative — and
What Does That Have to Do with Curve Options?
Many thought it an impossibility. Some risked huge sums that it would never
happen, and even when it did happen, they entered into further positions
that expressed the view that it would soon correct itself. It did correct itself
brieﬂy, but then it happened again. And it got worse and worse and worse,
and it didn’t go away for a long time thereafter.
As we discussed in Chapter 1, 30-year swap spreads went negative for the
ﬁrst time on October 23, 2008, shortly following the collapse of Lehman. This
marked the ﬁrst time that a benchmark swap spread in the dollar market
had ever gone negative.41 There were a number of factors that contributed
to this phenomenon, not the least of which was the delta hedging needs of
dealer desks on the back of the curve options they had created.
As is often the case in ﬁnancial markets, there may be a variety of factors
at work that dictate market moves. Oftentimes the interplay of these factors
is complicated, and ﬁguring out which factor or factors will dominate is a
nontrivial task. In the case of 30-year swap spreads in fall 2008, it seems as
though there were many factors at work that all conspired to tighten spreads
signiﬁcantly.
Perhaps the most important thing to keep in mind is that the market, in
the weeks following the collapse of Lehman, was extremely illiquid. In many
cases, people were only trading if they needed to, and even a trade of modest
41Swap spreads had previously gone negative in other foreign currency swap markets,
for example, in the sterling market.
453

<!-- Page 477 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.18: The 2s10s Swap Curve Leading Up and Into the Crisis
size could have a sizable impact on the market. As shown in Figure 9.18, as
the crisis ﬁrst started to manifest itself in the summer of 2007, 2s10s started
to steepen as the market started to rally, led by the front end of the curve.
When Lehman Brothers fell in 2008, many of its counterparties enter-
tained the possibility of entering into replacement trades with other dealers.
This would mean that the counterparties would terminate their existing
swap trades with Lehman and go out to the market and replace these trades
at levels prevailing in the market at the time. Any losses associated with
terminating existing swaps with Lehman would be taken up in bankruptcy
courts.42
42Lehman Brothers Holdings Inc. ﬁled for Chapter 11 under the United States
Bankruptcy Code on September 15, 2008. A few weeks later, Lehman Brothers Special
Financing Inc. (LBSF) ﬁled on October 3, 2008, and two other Lehman entities, Lehman
Brothers Financial Products Inc. and Lehman Brothers Derivative Products Inc., ﬁled
on October 5, 2008. The initial ﬁling of the holding company in September was enough
for many counterparties to examine their ability to terminate their trades with Lehman,
regardless of which particular entity that they faced in their swap transactions (the ability
of counterparties to terminate their Lehman swap transactions upon the Chapter 11 ﬁling
of the holding company was somewhat complicated and depended on which particular
Lehman entity a counterparty faced in its swap transactions). Under the standard terms
of the ISDA that Lehman had on with many of its counterparties, the ﬁling of Chapter
454

<!-- Page 478 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
In particular, pension funds who have long-dated liabilities had received
on many long-dated swaps to hedge. When Lehman fell, it was commonly
believed that many pension funds went out into the market to put on re-
placement trades. And as they received ﬁxed in long-dated swaps, primarily
in the 30-year sector, this pushed long-dated swap spreads down. In addition,
in the midst of the crisis, many ﬁnancial ﬁrms were deleveraging, selling a va-
riety of assets, including long-dated Treasuries. The outright selling of long-
dated Treasuries pushed the yields of these treasuries up versus long-dated
swaps, which again served to narrow spreads. Some leveraged funds actually
had on trades in which they had simultaneously bought long-dated Trea-
suries and paid ﬁxed on long-dated swaps (i.e., asset swaps). In order to
deleverage, these funds sold these Treasuries and essentially received ﬁxed
on swaps, which also tended to put downward pressure on long-dated swap
spreads. At the same time, as the crisis raged on, many in the market ex-
pected that the government would need to radically increase the supply of
treasuries to fund the crisis, which also served to narrow swap spreads.43
But yet another major driver of 30-year swap spreads into negative territory
had to do with the delta hedging by dealer desks of non-inversion notes on
10s30s that they had underwritten.
Figure 9.19 shows what was going on with 10s30s in swaps as the crisis
mounted. Whereas 2s10s steepened signiﬁcantly in the weeks following the
Lehman bankruptcy, 10s30s actually ﬂattened signiﬁcantly due to the market
forces we just discussed. And on October 8, 2008, the 10s30s curve was
essentially ﬂat. Now comes the tricky part.
11 constituted an Event of Default, which gave Lehman’s counterparties the right, but
not the obligation, to terminate their swap transactions. Upon termination, a termination
amount based on the mark-to-market of the swaps in question would either be made to
or owed by Lehman’s counterparties who elected termination. See Stromfeld et al. (2009)
for more details.
In one interesting case, a counterparty of LBSF decided not to terminate its swap upon
the latter’s Chapter 11 ﬁling. The swap was out-of-the-money to the counterparty, and
if it had decided to terminate, it presumably would have been required to make a termi-
nation payment to LBSF. Not only did the counterparty not terminate the transaction,
but (relying on a provision in the ISDA Master Agreement that it claimed released either
party from making payments in the event of the other party’s default or potential default)
it also withheld performance (i.e., it decided not to pay coupons in the swap). However the
court ruled that the counterparty could not rely on this provision as a basis for withholding
performance. Moreover, the court ruled that the decision by the counterparty not to termi-
nate the swap “promptly upon a debtor counterparty’s bankruptcy ﬁling” may be deemed
a waiver of this right. This decision suggests that, under the provisions of bankruptcy, a
non-defaulting counterparty does not have the right to “play the market” once an Event
of Default has occurred. See Davis Polk and Wardwell (2009) for more details.
43In Section 2.2.1, we discussed that an increase or decrease in the supply of Treasuries
(or even a change in the expectation of such) may aﬀect swap spreads. An expected in-
crease in the supply of Treasuries tends to lower swap spreads.
455

<!-- Page 479 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.19: The 10s30s Swap Curve Leading Up and Into the Crisis
Dealers had gotten long tons of low-strike binary curve ﬂoors on 10s30s
through the non-inversion notes that they had underwritten. Many of these
were struck at zero. Prior to the crisis when 10s30s was steeper, these options
were out-of-the-money, and as the curve started to ﬂatten, these options were
approaching being at-the-money. As 10s30s continued to ﬂatten and eventu-
ally inverted, many of these options became in-the-money.
As we discussed in Section 9.3.7, when the 10s30s curve inverted and
many of the binary curve ﬂoors were now in-the-money, exotic desks who
were long these options and delta hedging faced a switch in their gamma
proﬁle. With the 10s30s curve inverted, these binary curve ﬂoors now had
negative gamma. That meant that as the curve continued to ﬂatten (i.e.,
become more inverted), exotic desks were forced to put on 10s30s ﬂatteners.
That is, these desks were forced to pay in 10s and receive in 30s. Swap desks
across the street were being asked by exotic desks who managed the risk
in these 10s30s binary curves to receive in 10s and pay in 30s in order to
fulﬁll these delta hedging requirements. The pace of this became relentless.
In particular, as swap desks were asked to receive in 10s and pay in 30s, the
10s30s curve continued to invert, and the 30-year swap spread at which swap
dealers were willing to pay went lower and lower and lower, as shown in
456

<!-- Page 480 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S
Figure 9.20: 30-Year Swap Spreads Leading Up and Into the Crisis
Figure 9.20. Whereas the 10s30s curve in swaps was essentially ﬂat on Oc-
tober 8, 2008, a few days later on October 13 the curve was 24 bps inverted.
Exotic desks who (robotically) needed to delta hedge were undeterred when
30-year spreads were approaching zero; they needed to continue to put on
ﬂatteners as the curve continued to ﬂatten, regardless of the levels at which
they were getting done. They were like the Terminator: they couldn’t be
bargained with or reasoned with; they felt no pity or remorse. They would
absolutely not stop until they were hedged.
Eventually, 30-year spreads were pushed all the way to zero—and then
through zero into negative territory on October 23, 2008. On that date 30-
year swap spreads closed at −3 bps. The impossible had happened. Some
investors rushed in to buy spreads in negative territory, thinking that this
phenomenon would soon correct itself. Some said that they wanted to buy
30-year spreads just so that they could tell their children and grandchildren
that they were there when 30-year spreads went negative and that they did
not pass up on the opportunity of a lifetime. But those who did probably got
burned badly. Thereafter 30-year spreads continued to collapse, and within
a few weeks they closed at −53.75 bps. And although 30-year spreads sub-
sequently rebounded into positive territory for a brief time some weeks later
(and the 10s30s curve vacillated between being inverted or not), 30-year
spreads once again turned negative and remained in negative territory for
years thereafter.
457

<!-- Page 481 -->
M O R E R E C EN T P R O D U C T I N N OVAT I O N S

1. We have previously looked at a few diﬀerent variations of knockout swaps.
In Chapter 6 we examined a period-by-period knockout swap in which each
period of the trade can be knocked out irrespective of what happens in other
periods. In Problem Set 6, we considered a full knockout swap that was a
5-year trade that could be knocked out after a year, depending on where the
4-year swap rate was. Notice that this example considered the special case
in which the reference rate (which was the 4-year swap rate prevailing in a
year) was the same as the remaining maturity of the trade at the time at
which the trade could be knocked out.

Appendix A
Refresher in Option Pricing
Many things in this appendix underly the options-related material covered
throughout the text. If you are not familiar with the material discussed here,
I suggest you become intimately familiar with it. This appendix provides a
brief review of the fundamentals of option pricing. We’ll begin with some
basic deﬁnitions and concepts and establish boundary conditions that op-
tion prices must obey. We’ll review the binomial option pricing model and
introduce a concept known as risk-neutral valuation, a major paradigm in
the pricing of options. We’ll review the basics of the Black-Scholes model
and examine several quantities that are commonly used to measure how op-
tion prices change when other variables change. We will discuss some exotic
options and the concept of a package. Again, this material is intended to be
both brief and a review. If you need more detail, I refer you to Hull (2008)
for a more complete treatment.
A.1
The Basics
We will make the following assumptions throughout:
• There are no arbitrage opportunities.
• Markets are frictionless (i.e., there are no transaction costs).
• All individuals can borrow or lend at the same risk-free rate, r, which
is assumed to be positive and constant through time.

<!-- Page 487 -->

Table A.1: Some Sample Trading Strategies
An arbitrage is a trading strategy that enables us to lock in a riskless
proﬁt. To be a bit more formal, an arbitrage is a strategy in which we have
nonnegative cash ﬂows today, nonnegative cash ﬂows in all states of na-
ture in the future, and strictly positive cash ﬂow either today or in at least
one state of nature in the future.
To gain some intuition behind this deﬁnition, suppose that there are two
states of nature in the future, called heads and tails, and consider the in-
dependent trading strategies in Table A.1. Which of these four strategies
represent an arbitrage opportunity?1
Notice that under the assumption of no arbitrage, if two assets have
exactly the same payoﬀin each state of the world in the future, then they
must have the same price today. If they did not have the same price, then
we could buy the cheap one and sell the expensive one, resulting in positive
cash ﬂow today. Our cash ﬂows in the future would be zero, as any cash
ﬂow emanating from the asset we are short would be exactly oﬀset by our
1Strategies A and B represent arbitrage opportunities, since each strategy has non-
negative cash ﬂows both today and at all states of nature in the future, and each has
strictly positive cash ﬂows either today or in some state of nature in the future. Strategy
C, although it looks like a “good deal,” is strictly speaking not an arbitrage since it has a
negative cash ﬂow today. It could be the case that Strategy C lends itself to an arbitrage.
For example, suppose that one is able to borrow $0.05 today with a promise to pay back
$0.06 in either state in the future. If this is the case, then combining Strategy C with this
loan we can form a trading strategy, call it C′, consisting of zero cash ﬂow today and a
payout of $0.94 in each state in the future, which is surely an arbitrage. So given the fact
that Strategy C is so seemingly an arbitrage itself, why don’t we just call it one? There is
no telling how long the time diﬀerence is between now and the future, or how high interest
rates are. For instance, suppose that borrowing $0.05 today necessitated an investor to
pay back $1.10 in the future. In this case, not only is Strategy C not an arbitrage—it is
a really bad deal. Strategy D is not an arbitrage. Although it has nonnegative cash ﬂows
both today and at all states of nature in the future, it does not have strictly positive cash
ﬂows either today or in at least one state in the future.
464

<!-- Page 488 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
long. This strategy would yield an arbitrage, since there would be positive
cash ﬂows today and no cash ﬂows in the future. Thus, in order to avoid
arbitrage, it must be that the two assets have the same price today.
How reasonable are these assumptions? It depends. It’s true that ar-
bitrage opportunities come about on occasion, but most ﬁnancial markets
are such that any systematic arbitrages would never last long before prices
moved back in line. The assumption of zero transaction costs is not very
reasonable since most market participants pay bid/oﬀer when they want to
transact, but we’ll make this assumption for simplicity.2 The third assump-
tion of nondiﬀerentiated borrowing and lending rates is not terribly realistic.
In fact, during the ﬁnancial crisis that ﬁrst manifested itself in 2007, certain
institutions were having a very diﬃcult time borrowing at all. The seem-
ingly innocuous assumption that the risk-free rate be positive even became
suspect in the aftermath of the ﬁnancial crisis, when the yields on certain
short-term U.S. Treasury bills were negative.3 Nonetheless, we will go ahead
with these assumptions, bearing in mind their shortcomings.
A derivative or derivative security is a contract or asset whose value de-
pends on some underlying asset. An option is a particular type of derivative.
All options are derivatives, but not all derivatives are options.4 There are
two types of basic options. The owner of a call option has the right, but not
the obligation, to buy an underlying asset within some prespeciﬁed time at
some prespeciﬁed price. The owner of a put option has the right, but not the
obligation, to sell an underlying asset within some prespeciﬁed time at some
prespeciﬁed price. The prespeciﬁed price is known as the strike or exercise
price. If the owner of the option may exercise only at maturity, the option
is said to be European. If exercisable at any time at or before maturity, the
option is said to be American.
The variables that determine the value of an option include the current
underlying asset price S, the strike price X, the time remaining to expiration
T, the volatility of the underlying asset σ, the risk-free rate of interest r, and
any dividends d paid by the underlying asset during the life of the option.5
2For the pricing of options with transaction costs, see, for example, Leland (1985).
3The negative yields on these bills were a function of the high demand for these
securities. As investor demand for short-dated bills kept pushing prices up and eventually
above par, the yields on these bills went negative. The interpretation that investors were
willing to take a small loss for the certainty of getting most of their money back has some
merits. It is important to note that for many interest rate derivatives the risk-free rate
employed in option pricing is not based on Treasury rates but rather on LIBOR rates. As
discussed in Chapters 1 and 3, LIBOR is an important benchmark in the swaps market.
4A futures contract, for example, is a derivative that is not an option.
5In some cases, the risk-free rate will be quoted assuming continuous compounding,
and at other times it will be quoted assuming simple compounding. Let rc and r denote
the risk-free rate quoted assuming continuous compounding and simple compounding,
respectively. The present value of a dollar received in a year must be the same, regardless
465

c

S

σ

T

X

Table A.2: Some Symbols Used in This Appendix
This and other notations are summarized in Table A.2. Unless stated other-
wise, we will assume that the underlying asset does not pay any dividends
during the life of the option.
The value of an option is a function of these underlying variables. For
example, in the case of a call option whose value today is denoted c, it
would be most appropriate to represent this as c(S, X, T, σ, r, d). This is,
however, a lot of notation to carry around, and most often we will just write
c and bear in mind the dependence of c on these variables. Other times, in
order to highlight the dependence of c on one or more of these variables in
particular, we may employ notation such as c(X), in this case indicating the
dependence of c on the strike X. However, it is important to remember that
despite highlighting the dependency of the option price on one particular
variable, all these variables drive the price of the option.
As discussed in Hull (2008), the eﬀect on option prices of an increase in
any of these variables (while holding all other variables ﬁxed) is shown in
Table A.3. Most of these results are pretty straightforward. We will skip over
most of them but will highlight a few that are not entirely obvious:
• An increase in volatility increases both call and put prices. This result
follows from the limited downside the holder of a call or a put has. An
increase in volatility increases the spread of the distribution of the ter-
of the method of quoting interest. Thus, we must have
e−rc =
1
1 + r ,
or rc = ln(1 + r).

minal asset price. Thus, there is a greater chance the option will ﬁnish
deeper in-the-money, meaning that the underlying asset price will be
above (below) the strike price for a call (put). Although an increase
in volatility also means that there is a greater chance that the option
will ﬁnish deeper out-of-the-money, meaning that the underlying asset
price is below (above) the strike price for a call (put), the holder of the
option can lose at most his premium.
• Options always increase in value as time to expiration increases, right?
Wrong. This is true for American options but is not always true for Eu-
ropean
options.
Consider
two
American
options
on
the
same
underlying with the same strike price. One expires at time t1 and the
other at time t2 where t1 < t2. If the American option that expires
at t2 ever traded at a price less than the American that expires at t1,
we could buy the long-dated option and sell the short-dated option,
pocket money today, and exercise the long-dated option to cover our
short if the short were ever exercised against us.6 But then this would
be an arbitrage opportunity. Inasmuch as we assume there are no ar-
bitrage opportunities, it must then be that the longer-dated American
is worth more than the shorter-dated American.
This argument doesn’t hold for Europeans, however. In fact, some-
times European options with shorter times to expiry are worth more
6Note that this is not to say that exercising our long-dated American call option if
the short-dated American call is exercised is our optimal strategy. All we are saying is it’s
enough to cover our short, guaranteeing nonnegative cash ﬂows at all times in the future.
Indeed, if the underlying asset pays no dividends, it is deﬁnitely not in our best interest
to exercise the American option early, as we will soon see.
467

<!-- Page 491 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
than otherwise similar options with longer times to expiry. As an ex-
treme example, consider a European put option on an underlying that
is at zero. This is as good as life gets for the owner of a put option.
In this case, the option holder wants the option to expire as quickly as
possible so that he can get the strike price X as soon as possible. The
sooner expiry, the higher the present value of X that the put holder
will receive.7
• What about increasing the risk-free rate? Why does that increase the
value of calls but decrease the value of puts? Let’s just go with the
ﬂow and accept this for now. We will return to this concept in a bit.
A.2
Boundaries on Option Prices
Before we discuss the pricing of options, let’s start by discussing certain
restrictions that option prices must obey in order to avoid arbitrage. It is
important to note that the restrictions that follow do not depend whatsoever
on any distributional assumptions on the underlying asset.
An American option must be worth at least as much as its European
counterpart:8
c ≤C
p ≤P.
(A.1)
An American option lets us do everything that a European option does—and
more. We could always choose to never exercise our American option early
and thereby mimic the payoﬀpattern of the European. Thus, Americans
must be worth at least as much as Europeans. To be a bit more formal, sup-
pose that the European was worth more than the American. Then we could
buy the American and sell the European today, generating positive cash
ﬂow. There are two states of nature we need to consider in the future: one in
which the European is exercised and one in which it is not. If the European
is exercised at time T (the only time at which it may be exercised), then
exercise the American at that time. The proceeds from the American will
exactly oﬀset our short, resulting in zero cash ﬂows. If the European is not
exercised against us, then we are guaranteed nonnegative cash ﬂows in this
7As a second example, consider a European call option on a stock that expires in
a month. If the company is planning to issue a liquidating dividend, say, the day after
expiry, this option is probably going to be worth more than an otherwise similar European
call option that expires in, say, two months.
8It is implicitly understood that both options are written on the same underlying,
have the same strike, and have the same time to expiration T.
468

<!-- Page 492 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
state of the world. This would imply arbitrage, and thus the European can
be worth no more than the American.9
It is important to note that it is sometimes optimal to exercise an Amer-
ican put option early (i.e., prior to its expiration), which means that the
second inequality in Equations A.1 may hold with equality. We’ll talk more
about this later.
The next thing to note for European and American call options is
0 ≤c ≤S
0 ≤C ≤S.
(A.2)
Call options can’t be worth less than zero. Right? If we ever come up with a
call option price that is less than zero, we probably messed up somewhere.10
The other side of the restriction merits a bit more discussion. It says that a
call option can never be worth more than the underlying. A call option gives
us the right to buy the underlying, but we have pay the strike price for it.
Since we have to pay something to get the underlying when we exercise the
call, it can’t be worth more than underlying itself.11 If we ever found a call
option that was worth more than the underlying, we’d sell the call and buy
9The technique or strategy just employed to show the arbitrage bounds in Equa-
tion A.1 is one that we will employ often, known more formally as proof by contradiction.
In order to show that a desired result holds true, we ﬁrst start out by assuming it as false
(e.g., in this case, we assumed that c > C) and show that this would lead to arbitrage,
which we know cannot happen. Thus we conclude that our original assumption (in this
case, that c > C) was false, and therefore conclude that the opposite must be true (in this
case, that c ≤C).
10One time when I was reviewing a homework problem that asked to solve for the price
of a call option, a student insisted that the price was −$5. I said I would gladly buy the
call from him and pointed out that his price of −$5 meant that when I bought the call
from him, he would pay me $5 today to take it. After a moment or two of further thought,
he rescinded his oﬀer.
11What about a call option that has a strike of zero? In this case, owning the call is
the same as owning the underlying, and Equations A.2 hold with equality. Intuitively, if
we own a call option that has a strike of zero, then we will always exercise the call option,
since doing so gives us the underlying without having to pay anything for it, which is
the same as just owning the underlying. To be a bit more formal, denote by c(X = 0)
a call option with a strike of zero. Let’s show that c(X = 0) ≥S. Suppose this was not
true. That is, suppose c(X = 0) < S. Then buy the call with a strike of zero and sell the
underlying, which is positive cash ﬂow today. At expiration, let’s exercise the call option
we are long. Note that since the strike is zero, we will have to pay nothing in order to
exercise. Upon exercise, we will obtain the underlying that we will use to close out our
short position in the underlying. Thus, we have locked in zero cash ﬂows in the future and
positive cash ﬂows today. This would be an arbitrage. Thus, it must be that c(X = 0) ≥S.
At the same time, we also know from the ﬁrst line in Equation A.2 that c(X = 0) ≤S
(this condition is true for all calls and therefore must be true for a call with a strike of
zero). So we have found that c(X = 0) ≥S and that c(X = 0) ≤S, from which we
conclude that it must be that c(X = 0) = S.
469

<!-- Page 493 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
the underlying (positive cash ﬂow today), and if the call were ever exercised
against us, we’d deliver the underlying and get the strike price (positive cash
ﬂow again). If we could follow this strategy, we could make some money. But
who’s going to take the other side of this trade?
For puts, we have the following restrictions:
0 ≤p ≤X
0 ≤P ≤X.
(A.3)
If a put were worth more than its strike, we could sell the put and lend X
(which is positive cash ﬂow today). If the put is ever exercised against us,
reclaim the X that we lent (plus some interest) in order to pay the strike,
and we’ll get the underlying. If the put is not exercised against us, the X that
we lent will return XerT at expiry.12 Thus, we would have nonnegative cash
ﬂows at all points in the future, which would be an arbitrage from whence
the restriction follows.
A Lower Bound on Call Prices and Early Exercise of American Calls
Whereas Equations A.2 and A.3 place some meaningful upper bounds (and
trivial lower bounds) on the values of puts and calls, we can place some
interesting lower bounds on their values as well. For European calls on a
non-dividend paying asset,
c ≥max[0, S −Xe−rT ].
(A.4)
In order to show that this result is true, consider the strategy in Table A.4
that consists of selling the underlying, buying the call, and buying risk-
free bonds with a face of X. Notice that the cash ﬂows at expiry from this
strategy are nonnegative. In the state of nature in which ST > X, this
strategy results in cash ﬂow of zero. And in the state in which ST ≤X, the
quantity X −ST is nonnegative which means that the cash ﬂow generated
in this state is nonnegative. Thus, in order to avoid arbitrage, we must have
S −Xe−rT −c ≤0, from which the result follows.13
From Equation A.4 and the fact that C ≥c it follows immediately that
C ≥max[0, S −Xe−rT ].
(A.5)
12Throughout this section we will assume that interest is continuously compounded.
Thus, an amount X that earns interest at a continuously compounded rate r will grow to
XerT at time T.
13If S −Xe−rT −c > 0, then this strategy would result in positive cash ﬂow today and
nonnegative cash ﬂows in all states of nature in the future, which would be an arbitrage.
Thus, it must be that S −Xe−rT −c ≤0. Since c ≥S −Xe−rT and c ≥0, it follows that
c ≥max[0, S −Xe−rT ].

Table A.4: Arbitrage Strategy that Shows c ≥S −Xe−rT
And since r > 0, it follows that
S −Xe−rT > S −X,
and so we can conclude that C > S −X. The fact that C > S −X is a pretty
important result, because it tells us that (in the absence of dividends) it is
never optimal to exercise an American call early. Therefore, an American
call is worth exactly the same as a European call on an asset that pays
no dividends. What if the asset pays dividends?14 So suppose we own an
American call option on a non-dividend-paying asset, and we are absolutely
convinced that the underlying is going to go down precipitously. Should we
exercise the American call quickly, take delivery of the underlying asset, and
sell it in the market before the underlying goes down?15
A Lower Bound on Put Prices and Early Exercise of American Puts
There is an analogous restriction to Equation A.4 for European puts on a
non-dividend paying asset:
p ≥max[0, Xe−rT −S].
(A.6)
In order to show this holds, consider the strategy in Table A.5 consisting
of buying the asset, buying the put, and selling risk-free bonds with a face
of X. The cash ﬂows at expiry from this strategy are nonnegative, and so
14In this case, early exercise of an American call option may be optimal. Consider, for
example, a company that is about to issue a liquidating dividend. In this situation, the
owner of an American call option on the stock of this company may ﬁnd it in her best
interest to exercise early, prior to the liquidating dividend.
15This would not be our optimal strategy. If we do this, we would get S −X, but
if we instead sell the call, we will get C, and as we showed, it must be the case that
C > S −X. So if we don’t want the call option, then we should sell it in the market
instead of exercising early.

we must have
Xe−rT −S −p ≤0
to avoid arbitrage. Since we also know p ≥0, the result in Equation A.6
follows.
Since P ≥p, it follows from Equation A.6 that
P ≥max[0, Xe−rT −S].
(A.7)
And since we can always exercise an American put early, it must be worth
at least as much as what we can get for immediate exercise, X −S. That is,
P ≥X −S.
So what does the fact that P ≥X−S tell us about early exercise of American
puts? Very little, unfortunately. Note that the inequality is weak. If it were
a strict inequality, then we would be able to say that an American put
option is always worth more than its exercise value. But this isn’t the case.
Sometimes it turns out that American puts should be exercised early in
which case P = X −S.
A Further Look at Early Exercise of Options on Non-Dividend Paying Assets
So how come we never exercise American calls on non-dividend-paying as-
sets early, but sometimes we exercise American puts early? Let’s look at
the diﬀerence between the proceeds from early exercise of an American call,
S −X, and the value of keeping the American call alive, C. This diﬀer-
ence represents the value of early exercise of the American call. While we
don’t (yet) have an exact value for the American call option, we can use a
472

<!-- Page 496 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
lower bound on the call that we found in Equation A.5, namely, that C ≥
S −Xe−rT , to make the following realization:
Value of Early Exercise of American Call = (S −X) −C
≤(S −X) −(S −Xe−rT )
= −X(1 −e−rT )
< 0.
(A.8)
What is this telling us? We have just found that the value of early exercise
of an American call option, as measured by the diﬀerence between what we
get if we exercise, S −X, and what we get if we hold on to the call, C, is
always negative. This shows us deﬁnitively that early exercise of an American
call is a bad thing. The holder of an American call option who exercises early
forgoes (among other things) the interest that can be earned on the strike
price. That is, if the American call is exercised today, it is necessary to pay
the strike of X today. If instead the holder of the American call does not
exercise today but decides she will wait until time T to exercise, this implies
that she will pay out X at time T. But in order to fund a position of X at
time T, she only needs to have Xe−rT on hand today. She can invest this
amount today in risk-free bonds and it will grow to an amount equal to X at
time T. Thus, by exercising early, she pays out X today, whereas by delaying
exercise, she need only outlay Xe−rT . The diﬀerence between what she pays
out if she exercises today, X, and the amount she needs to spend to buy
risk-free bonds that will pay oﬀa face amount of X at time T, Xe−rT , is
−X(1 −e−rT ),
which, as shown in Equation A.8, is a negative number.
Now let’s use a lower bound on an American put given in Equation A.7
to look at the value of early exercise of an American put:
Value of Early Exercise of American Put = (X −S) −P
≤(X −S) −(Xe−rT −S)
= X(1 −e−rT ).
(A.9)
So what does this tell us? Not much, really. For American puts, the
value of early exercise is no greater than X(1 −e−rT ). But this quantity
is a positive number. Although this places an upper bound on the value
of early exercise, it does not, however, preclude there being positive value
associated with early exercise. If the value of early exercise is positive, then
early exercise is a good thing; if it’s negative, then it’s a bad thing. Since this
473

upper bound is positive, all we can conclude is that sometimes early exercise
of American puts may be the right thing to do. In fact, we shouldn’t have to
think too long in order to come up with a simple example that demonstrates
this point.16
European options are very commonly found in the swaps market, but
Americans are somewhat uncommon. However, Bermudan options (which
can be exercised on a multiple discrete number of dates before expiry) are
fairly common. In Chapters 5 and 6 we consider the early exercise of Bermu-
dan options and show by way of example that there may be times that these
options are optimally exercised early.
A.3
European Put-Call Parity
European put-call parity is a fundamental result that, while very simple, is a
helpful tool. For European puts and calls on the same non-dividend-paying
underlying, the same strike, and the same expiry, we have the following
relationship:
c = p + S −Xe−rT .
(A.10)
The arbitrage argument that gets us there is shown in Table A.6.
Buying the put and the asset, selling the call, and selling risk-free bonds
with a face of X results in a strategy that has zero cash ﬂow at all times in
the future. Therefore, it must be that c −p −S + Xe−r(T −t) = 0 to avoid
arbitrage.
16What should we do if we own an American put and the value of the underlying asset
is zero?
474

<!-- Page 498 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.1: One-Period Binomial Tree
A.4
Binomial Pricing
So far we haven’t really said too much: we deﬁned a bunch of things, showed
a few boundary conditions, and showed European put-call parity. That’s all
ﬁne and well, but the real question is, How much are options worth? Our
ﬁrst crack at tackling this question is by developing the binomial pricing
model.
Consider the following example. Figure A.1 depicts an asset that costs
$32 today (time t = 0). At time t = 1, the asset price will be either $48 with
a 75% probability or $16 with a 25% probability. How do we know these
probabilities? Who really cares? If it makes you happy, pretend we ﬂip a fair
coin twice: if it comes up heads twice the asset will be worth $16; otherwise,
it’s worth $48. Also, assume that the (simple) risk-free rate over the period
is 33 1
3% (in other words, a $1 investment in the risk-free asset at time t = 0
will return $1.33 at time t = 1).
Now consider a European call option on this asset with an exercise price
of $40 that expires at t = 1. Figure A.2 shows the payoﬀof the call option
at time t = 1. At the upper node, the call is in-the-money and the payoﬀis
475

<!-- Page 499 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.2: Payoﬀof a Call Option
max[$48 −$40, 0] = $8, and at the lower node the call is out-of-the-money
and the payoﬀis max[$16 −$40, 0] = $0. How much is this call worth today
at time t = 0? Well, that’s what we are trying to ﬁgure out. First, try to
answer the following questions:
• What’s the expected terminal value of the asset?
• What’s the expected return of the asset?
• What’s the expected payoﬀof the call?
You can look down below at the footnote to see the answers to these three
questions.17 Now that you’ve answered the above three questions, how about
these three:
• What’s the price of the call?
• How will investors’ risk preferences inﬂuence the price of the call?
17The answers are: 0.75 × $48 + 0.25 × $16 = $40; (40 −32)/32 = 25%; and 0.75 × $8 +
0.25 × $0 = $6.
476

<!-- Page 500 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
• How will the probabilities of an up or down movement aﬀect the price
of the call?
I suspect that you might ﬁgure that the price of the call is equal to the
expected payoﬀof the call, which is $6, discounted by the risk-free rate,
which is $6/1.33 = $4.50. This seems straightforward enough.
The other two questions may seem a bit more subtle. The ﬁrst time I was
posed with them, I argued that the more risk-averse investors were, the lower
the price of the call, since the call option essentially oﬀers a risky payoﬀof
either $8 or $0. And I also thought that the higher the probability that the
payoﬀof the call would be $8, the greater the value of the call option. Seems
reasonable, doesn’t it? But it’s all wrong, horribly wrong.
To see why, consider a hedge portfolio consisting of h units of the under-
lying asset and borrowing B such that
h × 48 −B × 1.33 = 8
h × 16 −B × 1.33 = 0.
We can solve for h and B that satisfy these two equations and ﬁnd h = 0.25
and B = 3. Entering into this hedge portfolio requires an initial investment
or outlay of cash equal to $5 (buy 0.25 shares at $32 a share, which costs $8,
and partially ﬁnance the purchase by borrowing $3).18 And of course this
hedge portfolio was designed so that at t = 1 the portfolio could be unwound,
and the proceeds would be equal in value to the payoﬀof the call in either
state. To verify this, note that the 0.25 shares will be worth 0.25×$48 = $12
in the upper state or 0.25×$16 = $4 in the lower state, we will have to repay
the borrowings plus interest of $3(1.33) = $4, and therefore we will be left
with either $8 or $0 in the upper or lower nodes, respectively.
But this means that the call must be worth $5 today. By the assumed
lack of arbitrage, since we found a hedge portfolio that replicates the payoﬀ
of the call, the call option and the hedge portfolio must be worth the same
today. Thus, the call is worth $5, not $4.50, which was the number we came
up with by (incorrectly) discounting the expected payoﬀof the call by the
risk-free rate.
Notice that investor risk preferences never entered into this discussion
anywhere. Any investor, regardless of his or her risk preferences, could set up
the same hedge portfolio and replicate the call payoﬀs. That is, the values of
h and B do not depend at all on how risk averse an investor is, and therefore
option prices do not depend on risk preferences.
In addition, note that the up and down probabilities of 75% and 25%
never entered into the discussion. Thus, the call price does not depend on
18We are making the assumption that one can buy fractional shares of the underlying.
477

<!-- Page 501 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.3: One-Period Binomial Tree
these probabilities whatsoever.19 More important, this means that in order
to price options, we don’t have to worry about trying to ﬁgure out what
these probabilities are.
There was nothing special about this particular numerical example. We
can, and will, do this in general. Consider an asset whose current price is S
and will be either uS or dS at the end of one period, where u > 1 and d < 1
and d < 1 + r < u, where r is the risk-free rate over the period assuming
simple interest. The evolution of the asset price is shown in Figure A.3. Why
do we need to require that d < 1+r < u?20 Denote the up and down payoﬀs
of the call by cu and cd, as depicted in Figure A.4.
We wish to set up a hedge portfolio with parameters h and B such that
huS −BR = cu
hdS −BR = cd,
(A.11)
19It is true, however, that changing these probabilities may change today’s asset price,
which would aﬀect the value of the call. But taking today’s asset price as given, the up
and down probabilities don’t matter at all.
20If 1 + r < d < u, then there would be an arbitrage by buying the risky asset and
selling bonds. And if d < u < 1 + r, there would be an arbitrage by buying bonds and
selling the risky asset.
478

<!-- Page 502 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.4: Payoﬀs from Call Option
where R = 1 + r. We can solve for h and B in terms of the other given
variables and ﬁnd
h = cu −cd
(u −d)S
(A.12)
B = dcu −ucd
(u −d)R .
(A.13)
By the assumed lack of arbitrage, the value of the call must equal the value
of the hedge portfolio today:
c = hS −B,
(A.14)
where h and B are as given in Equations A.12 and A.13, respectively.21
21The value of the hedge portfolio today is the amount of cash we have to take out of
our pocket to set up the hedge portfolio. We are buying h shares of the underlying at a
cost of S per share, for a total cost of hS. However, we are simultaneously borrowing B
today to partially ﬁnance this purchase, which means our total cash outlay is hS −B.
479

<!-- Page 503 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Equation A.14 is the value of the call option that we derived under an
assumed lack of arbitrage. A few lines of algebra show that the expression
in Equation A.14 can be rewritten as
c =
1
1 + r [pncu + (1 −pn)cd] ,
(A.15)
where
pn = R −d
u −d .
(A.16)
Equation A.15 is completely equivalent to Equation A.14, but the appeal
of Equation A.15 is that it looks as though we are computing the expected
payoﬀof the call and discounting back at the risk-free rate. This valuation
expression is intuitively pleasing and has come to be known as risk-neutral
valuation.22 The “probabilities” pn and 1−pn do indeed have the properties
that they are between 0 and 1, and of course they sum to 1, but they are
not really probabilities. They are, however, often referred to as risk-neutral
probabilities. Risk-neutrality is an artiﬁcal construct but one that makes
option pricing intuitive. The risk-neutral expression in Equation A.15 may
sometimes be denoted by notation like

ˆE(cT )

,
(A.17)
c =
1
1 + r
where the “ ˆ” denotes that expectations are taken using the risk-neutral
probabilities pn and 1 −pn. We can appeal to risk-neutrality to price up the
call in the numerical example we considered earlier. In this case, we have
pn = 4/3 −0.5
1.5 −0.5 = 5/6,
and the value of the call is
c =
1
1.33 [$8 × (5/6) + $0 × (1/6)] ,
which is equal to $5, just as we found above.
Let’s go back to Equation A.14, the value of the call option obtained by
setting up a hedge portfolio. It is easy to show from Equations A.12 and A.13
that 0 ≤h ≤1 and B ≥0, which shows that a call option can be thought of
as a levered position in the underlying asset. That is, a call option is equal to
22The term risk-neutral comes from the fact that someone who is risk-neutral is indiﬀer-
ent between a random outcome and its expected payoﬀ. If someone were risk-neutral, they
would value a risky asset by computing its expected payoﬀin the future and discounting
at the risk-free rate.
480

<!-- Page 504 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
a (fractional) long position in the underlying asset that is partially ﬁnanced
by borrowing. Similarly, a put option can be thought of as a (fractional)
short position in the underlying asset, the proceeds of which are lent out.23
Also note that in both Equations A.14 and A.15 there was nothing par-
ticularly important about the fact that the options valued were calls. The
same approach can be taken to value any derivative, v, that has payoﬀs of
vu in the upper state and vd in the lower state. In this case, appealing to
risk-neutrality gives the value of the derivative as
1
1 + r [pnvu + (1 −pn)vd] ,
(A.18)
where pn is as speciﬁed in Equation A.16.
A.4.1
Multiperiod Extensions
The binomial pricing model generalizes to multiperiods. As a concrete ex-
ample, consider the following two-period model in which an asset’s possible
prices are set out in Figure A.5. Assume again that the risk-free rate is
33 1
3% each period. What is the price today of a European call option on the
asset with a strike of $60 that expires in two periods at t = 2? The way to
solve for the price of the call is to work “backwards” in the tree. At time
t = 1, we will either be at the upper node or the lower node. If we are at the
upper node, we know that one period later at t = 2 the asset will be worth ei-
ther $72 or $24. We can use our one-period binomial methodology to ﬁnd the
value of the call at the upper node at time t = 1. Using Equation A.15, it can
be veriﬁed that the value is (3/4) × [(5/6) × 12 + (1/6) × 0] = $7.5. Sim-
ilarly, if we are at the lower node at time t = 1, the call will be worth $0
at time t = 2, regardless of which outcome subsequently occurs. Thus, if
we ﬁnd ourselves at the lower node at time t = 1, the value of the call is
surely $0, which we can also verify using Equation A.15 if there is any doubt
in our minds whatsoever. Moving back in the tree to time t = 0, we can
ﬁnd the value today of the call that will be worth either $7.5 in the upper
node or $0 in the lower node at time t = 1. The value of the call today is
(3/4) × [(5/6) × 7.5 + (1/6) × 0] = $4.6875. Figure A.6 lays this out. The
23Given these interpretations, we can now go back to answer (at least from the per-
spective of the binomial model) one of the questions posed in Section A.1: why do calls
increase in value as the risk-free rate increases? A call is equivalent to a levered position
in the underlying ﬁnanced by borrowing. As the risk-free rate increases, the cost of bor-
rowing goes up. That is, the cost of the equivalent levered position goes up—that is, it is
harder to ﬁnance—and therefore the call is worth more. A similar argument shows that
puts decline in value as the risk-free rate increases.
481

<!-- Page 505 -->
Figure A.5: Two-Period Binomial Tree
Figure A.6: Valuing the Call Going Backwards

<!-- Page 506 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
approach demonstrated in this simple two-period example easily generalizes
to multiperiods of any dimension. See Hull (2008) for more details.
A.5
The Black-Scholes Formula
A multiperiod binomial model is a powerful paradigm used to price a variety
of options. It is a discrete model, meaning that the underlying asset price
moves discretely, once per period. In comparison, the Black-Scholes model
considers a continuous distribution, in which the asset price can move at any
instant in time.
In particular the Black-Scholes model assumes that the underlying asset
price is lognormally distributed. We say that a random variable Y is lognor-
mally distributed with parameters μ and σ2 if Y = eX, where X is normally
distributed with mean μ and variance σ2. The variable X is said to be nor-
mally distributed with mean μ and variance σ2, denoted X ∼N(μ, σ2), if
the probability density function for X is given by
n(x) =
1
√
2πσ e−(x−μ)2/2σ2.
(A.19)
The cumulative distribution function for X ∼N(μ, σ2) is given by
N(d) =
 d
1
√
2πσ e−(x−μ)2/2σ2dx.
(A.20)
−∞
It can be shown that E(X) = μ and var(X) = σ2. For the case in which
μ = 0 and σ2 = 1, we say that X is a standard normal random variable. Note
that if Z is a standard normal random variable, then the random variable
X = aZ + b ∼N(b, a2).
A random variable Y that is lognormally distributed with parameters μ
and σ2 can be shown to have a probability density function given by
f(y) =
1
√
2πσy e−(ln(y)−μ)2/2σ2, for y > 0.
(A.21)
It can be shown that
E(Y ) = eμ+σ2/2
(A.22)
and
var(Y ) = e2μ+σ2(eσ2 −1).
(A.23)

Note that the random variable Y can take on any positive value, whereas
the variable X can take on any value. A picture of the probability density
function of various lognormally distributed random variables is shown in
Figure A.7.
Black-Scholes assumes that the underlying asset price at time T is log-
normally distributed. In particular, let’s assume

Thus, ln(ST /S) is normally distributed with mean (μ−σ2/2)T and variance
σ2T. Lognormal distributions are pretty reasonable models of the distribu-
tion of many asset prices, like equities. As we discussed in Chapter 5, log-
normality is often a questionable assumption for the distribution of interest
rates.
Black and Scholes (1973) showed that if the underlying asset price is
lognormally distributed and pays no dividends, then the value of a call option
on the underlying asset struck at X that matures at time T is given by
c = SN(d1) −Xe−rT N(d2),
(A.28)
where
d1 = ln (S/X) + (r + σ2/2)T
σ
√
T
(A.29)
d2 = d1 −σ
√
T,
(A.30)
where N(·) is the cumulative normal distribution function as given in Equa-
tion A.20. Equation A.28 is known as the Black-Scholes formula. One of the
(somewhat remarkable) things to notice about the Black-Scholes formula is
that it does not include the parameter μ despite it appearing in the speciﬁ-
cation of the underlying asset price dynamics as speciﬁed in Equation A.24.
The beauty of this is that we do not need to know the value of the parameter
μ in order to price the option.
We are not going to concern ourselves with the actual derivation of the
Black-Scholes formula. There are a number of ways to derive it. In their orig-
inal paper, Black and Scholes (1973) took an approach somewhat analogous
(although signiﬁcantly more complicated mathematically) to setting up a
hedge portfolio as we did with the binomial model.24 They showed that such
restrictions and the assumed lack of arbitrage dictate that the price of the
call is as in Equation A.28.
Since that time, others have shown that the Black-Scholes formula can be
priced under the assumption of risk-neutrality. That is, it can be shown that
the value of the call as in Equation A.28 is consistent with assuming that
the underlying asset price is lognormally distributed as per Equation A.24
and is the solution to computing the following expectation:
c = e−rT ˆE[max(ST −X, 0)],
(A.31)
24Actually, if you want to get technical, they set up a portfolio consisting of a position
in the call option and a position in stock and derived conditions on the portfolio such
that it was risk-free. These conditions placed restrictions on the call price, the solution to
which was shown to be the Black-Scholes formula.
485

<!-- Page 509 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
where ˆE means that expectations are taken assuming risk-neutrality. So
what does that mean? This amounts to replacing the “μ” by an “r” in
Equation A.27:
ln(ST /S) ∼N

.
(A.32)
2
This is pretty important. The parameter μ is asset speciﬁc. That is, each
underlying asset has its own expected return. How would you go about ﬁg-
uring out what this is? Fortunately, you don’t have to. Under risk-neutrality,
all assets can be valued assuming they earn an expected return equal to the
risk-free rate.
Using put-call parity, it is easy to show using Equation A.28 that accord-
ing to Black-Scholes the value of a European put on the underlying asset is
p = Xe−rT N(−d2) −SN(−d1),
with d1 and d2 as deﬁned in Equations A.29 and A.30, respectively.
Figures A.8 and A.9 show the Black-Scholes value of a call option and a
put option as a function of the underlying asset price.

d1 = ln (S/X) + (r −d + σ2/2)T

Black’s Formula
An application of the Black-Scholes formula on a dividend-paying asset in
Equation A.33 can be used to value a European call option on a forward con-
tract or a futures contract. Black (1976) showed that the value of a European
call option on a forward is given by
c = e−rT [FN(d1) −XN(d2)] ,
(A.36)
where
d1 = ln (F/X) + σ2T/2
σ
√
T

√

487

<!-- Page 511 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
where F represents the forward price. This simple extension of Black-Scholes
is known as Black’s formula. Black’s formula is often used to value LIBOR
caps and ﬂoors as well as swaptions, as discussed in Chapters 4 and 5.
A.6
Option Sensitivities
Second in importance only to the value of options themselves, the question
of how option prices will change if the underlying asset price changes, if
volatility changes, or as time passes is of paramount importance to an option
trader. Think about it: if we’re running an options book, we’re probably
going to want to know what happens to the value of our book if implied
volatility increases. If we’re net short options, this could hurt us. If we’re net
long options, an increase in implied volatility will likely mean an increase in
the value of our option portfolio.25
The buzzwords that we’ll be throwing around in this section are the so-
called greeks: delta, gamma, vega, and theta, to name a few. Each of these
provides a measurement of the change in the value of an option, given a
change in an underlying variable on which the option price depends (while
holding the other variables constant). It is important to note that the compu-
tation of these option sensitivities depends crucially on the particular model
we employ. For example, if we are using the Black-Scholes model in which the
underlying asset is assumed to be lognormally distributed, options sensitiv-
ities may be quite diﬀerent from those computed using a diﬀerent model.26
Depending on the particular option model being employed, it may be rather
easy and convenient to compute the various option sensitivities analytically.
If not, we can always resort to numerical approximations, as we will discuss.
A.6.1
Delta
The ﬁrst option sensitivity that we’ll discuss (which is probably also the one
that is most near and dear to an option trader’s heart) is the change in the
option price with respect to a change in the underlying asset price. This
is known as the delta of the option. The reason that option traders are so
concerned with delta is because once an option is traded, movement in the
underlying can potentially change the value of the position dramatically.
Denote by V (S, X, r, T, σ) the value of any option as a function of the un-
derlying asset price, strike, risk-free rate, time to expiry, and implied volatil-
25Not all options increase (decrease) in value as implied volatility increases (decreases),
as we will discuss later in this appendix.
26For example, in Chapter 5 we discuss the normal model as an alternative to Black-
Scholes for the pricing of interest rate options.
488

<!-- Page 512 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
ity. For ease of notion, we will often write V as a function of just one of these
variables, with the implicit understanding that the option value actually de-
pends on all of the parameters. The delta of the option is given by
Δ = ∂V
∂S .
(A.37)
For any price of the underlying asset, S, delta can be approximated by:
Δ ≈ΔV
ΔS = V (S + ϵ) −V (S)
ϵ
,
(A.38)
where V (S) denotes the value of the option when the underlying is at S and
ϵ is a small positive number. When should we use Equation A.37 to calcu-
late delta versus using Equation A.38? Typically, people use Equation A.37
whenever it is analytically easy to compute that partial derivative, and if
not, then they resort to the approximation in Equation A.38. Equation A.38
makes it easy to calculate the value of delta by computing the value of the
option when the underlying is equal to S + ϵ and again when the underlying
is equal to S. The diﬀerence of these two values divided by ϵ gives a close
approximation to the true delta when ϵ is very small.
For European calls and puts on a non-dividend-paying asset, delta is
easily determined analytically using the Black-Scholes formula:
Δc = ∂c
∂S = N(d1) > 0
(A.39)
Δp = ∂p
∂S = N(d1) −1 < 0.
(A.40)
Figures A.10 and A.11 show the delta of a call option and a put option,
respectively, according to the Black-Scholes formula as a function of the
underlying asset price.
But what does this really mean? When an option trader buys or sells an
option, probably the ﬁrst thing that he is worried about is his delta. Once
that option is traded (and assuming the trader doesn’t or can’t immediately
get out of the position), if the underlying moves around, it is going to aﬀect
the value of his option, often in a big way. Yes, while it is true that the
trader’s position has exposure to other things, too, such as movements in
implied volatility, the thing that is often most likely to move in the near
term and aﬀect the value of his position is the underlying.
Delta hedging refers to trading in the underlying in conjunction with a po-
sition in options such that the changes in the net value of the portfolio are re-
duced as the underlying moves.27 When delta hedging, it is often necessary to
27Again, this is not to suggest that other variables that drive the value of the option,
such as implied volatility, are not moving. But delta hedging refers to immunizing changes
in an option position due solely to movements in the underlying.
489

<!-- Page 513 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.10: Delta of Call Option as a Function of Underlying Asset When
X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1 Month
Figure A.11: Delta of Put Option as a Function of Underlying Asset When
X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1 Month
readjust the amount of the underlying held in the portfolio since the delta of
an option is not a constant but, rather, moves as a function of the price of the
underlying and other variables that determine the price of the option. This
process of adjusting the delta hedge is referred to as dynamic delta hedging.
490

<!-- Page 514 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
To continue with this thought a bit more, suppose that an option trader
sells an option and wants to delta hedge the position by trading in the
underlying. The value of the position will be
−V + nS,
(A.41)
where n is the number of shares of the underlying held in the portfolio. A
positive value of n indicates the trader is long the underlying, and a negative
value indicates he is short. The change in the value of this portfolio with
regard to a change in the price of the underlying is found by taking the
partial derivative of the position in Equation A.41 and is
−∂V
∂S + n,
(A.42)
since of course ∂S/∂S = 1. Oftentimes when delta hedging, an option trader
is interested in maintaining a delta-neutral position, which is to say that the
change in the value of his portfolio in Equation A.41 is zero for a small
change in the underlying. In order to make this position delta neutral, we
must set the expression in Equation A.42 equal to zero. Thus, in order to
set up a delta-neutral hedge for an option we have sold, we set the position
in the underlying
n = ∂V
∂S .
(A.43)
By deﬁnition ∂V/∂S is Δ, the delta of the option. Thus, in order to maintain
a delta-neutral position, we must set n equal to Δ. Inasmuch as the delta of
an option is not a constant, an option trader must continuously adjust the
amount of his hedge if he wants to maintain a delta-neutral position.
Note that if an option trader is instead long an option and wants to delta
hedge by trading in n shares of the underlying, the value of his position is
+V + nS.
(A.44)
In order to maintain a delta-neutral position against this long option posi-
tion, the trader will want to maintain a position in the underlying
n = −∂V
∂S .
(A.45)
The only diﬀerence between the formulation in Equation A.44 in which it is
assumed the option trader is long the option and in Equation A.41 in which
the option trader is short the option is the sign in front of the V, indicating the
appropriate position. Once again, a positive quantity for n in Equations A.44
491

<!-- Page 515 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
and A.45 indicates a long position, whereas a negative quantity indicates a
short position.28
As an example, consider now the particular case of an option trader who
sells a call option and wants to maintain a delta-neutral position. Assuming
the trader employs the Black-Scholes model, then the trader will, in accor-
dance with Equations A.39 and A.43, purchase n = N(d1) shares of the
underlying. Inasmuch as the quantity N(d1) is positive, this means that the
option trader who sold the call will buy the underlying to delta hedge. As a
second example, consider the case of an option trader who buys a put option
and wants to maintain a delta-neutral position. From Equation A.45, we see
that the trader should set n = −∂V/∂S. Under the Black-Scholes model, this
means that n = −(N(d1) −1), which is positive by virtue of Equation A.40.
This means that an option trader who delta hedges a long position in a put
option will buy the underlying.29
A.6.2
Gamma
Gamma is diﬃcult to conceptualize. Maybe it’s because it’s a second deriva-
tive. I don’t know. Gamma measures the change in the delta of a derivative
that occurs when the underlying asset price changes. More formally, gamma
is given by
Γ = ∂2V
∂S2 = ∂Δ
∂S .
(A.46)
Gamma can be approximated by making use of the delta of an option as
follows:
Γ ≈Δ(S + ϵ) −Δ(S)
ϵ
,
(A.47)
where Δ(S) represents the delta of the derivative when the underlying asset
price is S and ϵ is a small positive number.
For European calls and puts on a non-dividend-paying asset, gamma as
per Equation A.46 is easy to determine analytically from the Black-Scholes
formula:
28Be careful in Equation A.45. The negative sign in this equation does not imply
necessarily that n is negative; if ∂V/∂S is negative, then indeed n would be positive.
29There is nothing mysterious here. If we are long a put option, then as the underlying
goes down, the value of our put goes up. If we want to remain delta neutral, we will have
to be long some of the underlying. As the underlying goes down, losses in the value of the
hedge will oﬀset gains in the value of the option.
492

<!-- Page 516 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.12: Gamma of a Call Option as a Function of Underlying Asset
When X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1 Month
Γc = ∂Δc
∂S = n(d1)

> 0
(A.48)

Γp = ∂Δp
∂S = n(d1)
