(A.49)
where n(·) is the standard normal density function. Figure A.12 shows the
gamma of a call option according to the Black-Scholes formula. As seen in
this ﬁgure, gamma is largest when the underlying is near-the-money (mean-
ing the underlying asset price is close to the strike) and falls oﬀprecipitously
if the option is either deep in-the-money or deep out-of-the-money. If the op-
tion is deep in-the-money, then an option trader who is long the option and
delta hedging is short nearly a full share of the underlying to remain delta-
neutral. As the underlying moves around slightly, the option remains deep
in-the-money, and the trader will not need to adjust the hedge much, mean-
ing gamma is low. Similarly, if the option is deep out-of-the-money, an option
trader who is long the option and delta hedging will be short almost none of
the underlying. A small change in the underlying means the option will still
be deep out-of-the-money and will not necessitate changing the delta hedge
much, again meaning that gamma is low. It is when the underlying is around
the strike that a change in the underlying implies the largest changes in the
delta, which is to say that the gamma of the option is largest.
Consider now Figure A.13 that shows an option with positive gamma.
Suppose we are long this option and are delta hedging it. The current value
493

<!-- Page 517 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.13: An Option with Positive Gamma
of the underlying asset is S. In order to be delta neutral, our delta hedge
is to short a number of units of the underlying asset equal to the slope of
the tangent line to the option at S.30 Let’s suppose that we are going to dy-
namically delta hedge this option in the following manner: if the underlying
price moves by an amount ϵ where ϵ > 0, then we are going to readjust the
amount of underlying we are short in order to once again make our portfolio
delta neutral. Suppose now that the underlying trades up to S + ϵ. Since
the slope of the tangent line to the option price is steeper (i.e., the option
price is now more sensitive to changes in the underlying), this means that the
delta of the option is greater, and we will sell some more of the underlying
at S + ϵ. If the underlying subsequently falls back down to S, now the delta
30How do we know that our delta hedge is going to be short the underlying just by
looking at this picture? From the picture it is clear that as the underlying goes up, the
value of the option goes up (and as the underlying goes down, the value of the option goes
down). Thus, in order to be delta neutral we will need to be short some of the underlying,
as an increase in the underlying will cause our short to fall in value (and a decrease in the
underlying will cause our short to rise in value). More formally, from Equation A.45, we
know that our position in the underlying required to be delta neutral against our long in
the option is n = −∂V/∂S. Since ∂V/∂S, the slope of the tangent line at S, is positive,
this means that n is negative.
494

<!-- Page 518 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
has gone back down to where it was originally, and we need to pare back on
the amount of the underlying we are short. Whereas we had just sold some
underlying when it was trading at S + ϵ, we now need to buy some back at
S. If the underlying trades down to S −ϵ, now the delta of the option (as
indicated by the ﬂattest of the three tangent lines) is even smaller, and we
need to buy some more underlying, which we will do at the current price of
S −ϵ. And if the underlying trades back up to S, we will sell some more of
the underlying when we adjust our delta hedge.
But look at what is happening here in terms of our position in the
underlying. When we ﬁrst buy the option, we start out shorting some of
the underlying in order to have a delta-neutral position. When the underly-
ing trades up, we sell some more. When it comes down, we buy some back
at a cheaper price. When it comes down some more, we buy some more back
at an even cheaper price. And when it subsequently goes back up, we wind
up selling some at a more expensive price than we had just bought it. In
other words, we are in a situation in which we keep buying low and selling
high, which is a very good thing. The more that the underlying keeps going
up and down, the more we can keep proﬁting by trading in the underlying,
buying it at lower prices than at which we sold it, and selling it at higher
prices than at which we bought it.31
This is the beauty of gamma. Being long gamma (which means the
gamma of a position is positive) allows us to beneﬁt from trading in the
underlying if we are dynamically delta hedging.32 Of course, this beneﬁt
comes at a price in the case of calls and puts: we have to shell out the pur-
chase price of the option upfront in order to buy the option, which creates a
long gamma position for us. If the actual volatility in the underlying is suﬃ-
ciently large enough over the life of the option, then we can earn a substantial
proﬁt from buying the option and delta hedging. On the other hand, if the
31Even if we buy the option and set up a delta-neutral hedge by shorting some of the
underlying, and the underlying subsequently moves only in one direction, the gamma of
the option will beneﬁt us. For example, suppose we buy the option depicted in Figure A.13
and the underlying subsequently moves from S to S + ϵ and sits there. In this case the
value of the option will increase to a greater extent than will the associated loss in the
hedge; the value of the call will go up to V (S +ϵ) and therefore beneﬁt from the curvature
of the price function, whereas the hedge will only go down linearly.
32When we are long gamma and delta hedging, our delta hedging activities can actually
serve to stabilize the price movements in the underlying, assuming theses trading activities
in the underlying are signiﬁcant enough. As the price of the underlying goes up and we
sell it to delta hedge, this tends, all else equal, to bring the price of the underlying back
down. And as the price of the underlying goes down and we buy it in order to delta hedge,
this exerts upward pressure on the price of the underlying. We will talk further about this
potential stabilizing (or destabilizing, if we are short gamma) dynamic later on in this
appendix when we consider binary options.
495

<!-- Page 519 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
actual volatility of the underlying is low, we could lose money by buying the
option and delta hedging.33
If we are short gamma, meaning the gamma of our position is negative,
then the situation is reversed. As the underlying trades up, we need to buy
some of the underlying in order to remain delta neutral, and as the under-
lying trades down, we need to sell some of the underlying to remain delta
neutral.34 Thus, if we are short gamma and are delta hedging, we tend to
buy the underlying when it is high and sell it when it is low, which is not an
advantageous thing to be doing in and of itself.35 Inasmuch as we acquired
the short gamma position by selling an option, we have taken in a premium
upfront that oﬀsets our potential losses from delta hedging. The actual proﬁt
or loss from selling an option and delta hedging will depend on the magni-
tude of actual price movements in the underlying and the frequency with
which a trader readjusts his delta hedge.
The dynamics just described explain why many traders buy and sell
short-dated options. While it is true that some short-dated option traders
trade these options to take a view on the direction of the underlying and
never delta hedge, many others are interested in trading these options for
33The actual proﬁt or loss from buying an option and delta hedging depends very
much on the particular delta hedging strategy employed by the option trader. An option
trader must make a decision both on the frequency and the minimum price movements
in the underlying upon which he will adjust his delta hedge. Some option traders regard
the process of making these choices as much of an art as a science. Question 10 in the
problem set at the end of Chapter 5 allows us to consider this a bit further.
34Why is this happening? Let’s consider a couple of examples to build some intuition.
First, suppose we are short a call option, and we are delta hedging to remain delta neutral.
We are short gamma, as per Equation A.48. In accordance with Equation A.43, we are
going to set our delta hedge to n = ∂c/∂S = Δc, which is positive by virtue of Equa-
tion A.39. Thus, our delta hedge against our short call is to buy shares of the underlying.
If the underlying trades up, the delta of the call option increases, and we need to buy
more shares to remain delta neutral. If the underlying trades down, the delta of the option
decreases, and we need to sell some shares of the underlying to remain delta neutral.
As a second example, suppose we are short a put option, and we are delta hedging to
remain delta neutral. We are short gamma, as per Equation A.49. We are going to set
our delta hedge to n = ∂p/∂S = Δp in order to remain delta neutral. Note that this is
a negative number according to Equation A.40. This means that when we sell the put,
in order to set up a delta-neutral hedge we will sell shares. Now as the underlying trades
up, the delta of the put increases (the delta is negative and becomes less negative, getting
closer to zero), and we need to reduce our short by buying some shares to remain delta
neutral. If the underlying trades down, the delta of the put decreases (the delta, which was
negative, becomes more negative), and we have to sell some more shares of the underlying
to remain delta neutral.
35In the case of an option position with negative gamma, the delta hedging activities
of an option trader can actually serve to exacerbate movements in the underlying. As the
underlying goes up and the option trader buys some of it, this will tend to push the price
up further. And as the underlying goes down and the trader sells some to delta hedge,
this serves, on the margin, to push the price down even further.
496

<!-- Page 520 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
their gamma proﬁle. An option trader who thinks that the actual (also called
realized) volatility in the underlying will be signiﬁcant enough so that po-
tential proﬁts from delta hedging will outweigh the cost of an option will be
inclined to buy the option. One who expects low realized volatility in the
underlying may be inclined to sell short-dated options, taking in premium,
and hope for minimal costs associated with delta hedging the option. Of-
tentimes (but not always), sell-side option traders (i.e., dealers) who trade
short-dated options with buy-side clients (who often don’t delta hedge but
are rather trading the options to take a directional view on the underlying)
will delta hedge their option positions.36
A.6.3
Vega
The vega of an option is the change in the value of the option given a change
in implied volatility. Formally, vega is given by
∂V
∂σ .
(A.50)
Vega can be approximated as follows:
V (σ + ϵ) −V (σ)
ϵ
,
(A.51)
where V (σ) represents the value of the derivative when the implied volatility
is σ. For European calls and puts on a non-dividend-paying asset, vega is
found from the Black-Scholes formula using Equation A.50:
∂c
∂σ = Sn(d1)
√
T > 0
(A.52)
∂p
∂σ = Sn(d1)
√
T > 0.
(A.53)
36For those who ﬁnd gamma a hard concept to get one’s hands around, it may be
easier to guess if an option trader is long or short gamma, given his demeanor. If the
market for the underlying is moving around a lot, and you see an option trader who is
running a delta-neutral book who looks like he’s on the verge of a nervous breakdown,
it’s a pretty good bet that he’s short gamma. It’s hard to miss: that irascible look with
eyes glued to the screen and teeth clenched as the underlying continues to move. Ask that
guy a question, and you’re likely to get a response like Charlie Sheen’s character Bud Fox
gave to Marv, played by John C. McGinley, in the movie Wall Street when Marv asked
what was up and Bud screamed at him and told him to do his own homework. On the
other hand, ﬁnd an option trader who is also running a delta-neutral book but who has a
care-free look on his face when the market for the underlying is moving and barely looks
on the screens to see what the market is doing; and when he does look, you see a slight
self-assured grin. That guy is most likely long gamma.
497

<!-- Page 521 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.14: Vega of a Call Option as a Function of Underlying Asset When
X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1 Month
Figure A.14 shows the vega of a call option according to the Black-Scholes
formula. If we multiply the vega by 0.01, this will tell us how much the value
of the option will increase given a 1% increase in implied volatility. The vega
of an option tends to be largest when the underlying is near the strike and
falls oﬀas we move away from the strike.
Whereas gamma is usually a measure that is associated with short-dated
options, vega is a term that is usually associated with long-dated options.
In the rates markets, for example, the terms “short-dated options” and
“gamma” are often used interchangeably, and the terms “long-dated options”
and “vega” are also synonymous, with the demarcation between the two usu-
ally at an expiry of one year. The reason for this is that whereas short-dated
options can have large gamma, vega is typically quite small; an increase in
implied volatility is not going to change the value of a short-dated option all
that much. That is, option traders tend to trade short-dated options for their
gamma proﬁle, not their vega proﬁle, and trade long-dated options for their
vega proﬁle, not their gamma proﬁle. Option traders often buy short-dated
options because of their signiﬁcant gamma and the associated beneﬁts from
employing certain delta hedging strategies of these positions, which is why
these options have come to be known as simply “gamma.” An option trader
who wants to take a view on implied volatility would be much more likely
to take a position in a long-dated option that can have signiﬁcant exposure
to changes in implied volatility. It’s for this reason that trading long-dated
options has become known as trading “vega.”
498

<!-- Page 522 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
A.6.4
Theta
The change in the value of an option given an increase in time to expiration
is given by

of an option is given by
Θ = −∂V

The theta of an option can be approximated as follows:
Θ ≈−V (T + ϵ) −V (T)

Figure A.15 shows the theta of a call option according to the Black-

represents time decay. As time goes on, your option is running out. That is
usually a bad thing. Can you think of an example of an option that has
positive theta?37
37A very deep in-the-money put can have a positive theta. As an extreme example,
consider a put on an underlying asset that has gone to zero. This is as good as it gets for
the holder of a put option. In this case, the holder of a put option wants to exercise the
option as soon as possible.

A.7
Binary Options
Binary options, a type of exotic option, make their appearance in the swaps
market relatively frequently. As such, we will spend a considerable amount
of time discussing them. Binary options, also known as digital options, pro-
vide the holder with either nothing or a prespeciﬁed payment at expiration.
This prespeciﬁed payment may consist of either a cash sum or a number of
units of the underlying asset. The former type is known as a cash-or-nothing
option. To take a concrete example, consider a cash-or-nothing call option
in which the holder receives a payment of K if the underlying asset price is
at least X at expiration and nothing otherwise. That is, the payoﬀfrom the
option is:
Payoﬀ=
 K
if ST ≥X
0
if ST < X.
This payoﬀpattern is shown in Figure A.16.
Under the Black-Scholes assumptions value of the binary cash-or-nothing
call option, cCN, is38
38Theoretically, in the Black-Scholes model, there is no diﬀerence in price between a
binary call option that pays K if the underlying asset price ﬁnishes at exactly the strike
X at expiry and one that pays zero in this case (since the Black-Scholes model attributes
zero probability to any one asset price obtaining). Practically speaking, option traders
500

<!-- Page 524 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.16: Payoﬀof Cash-or-Nothing Binary Call Option
cCN = Ke−rT N(d2).
(A.57)
Similarly, with a cash-or-nothing put option, the holder receives a pay-
ment of K if the underlying asset price is at or below X at expiration and
nothing otherwise. That is, the payoﬀfrom the option is:
Payoﬀ=
 K
if ST ≤X
0
if ST > X.
(A.58)
This payoﬀpattern is shown in Figure A.17. Under the Black-Scholes as-
sumptions, the value of the binary cash-or-nothing put option, pCN, is
pCN = Ke−rT [1 −N(d2)] .
(A.59)

are very concerned as to whether or not they are paying K if the asset price ﬁnishes on
the strike, often charging diﬀerent premia for the two, particularly if the strike price X is
believed to be a “focal” point upon which it is thought the asset price may land.

A.7.1
Delta of Binary Options
Binary options have some pretty strange properties, which we will examine.
Let’s start out by looking at the delta of binary options. As we discussed in
Section A.6.1, the delta of an option, Δ, is the change in the option price with
respect to a change in the underlying. Delta is typically the ﬁrst sensitivity
parameter that an option trader will look at, as changes in the underlying
can happen quickly and signiﬁcantly impact the value of the option. The
delta of binary cash-or-nothing calls, denoted Δ (cCN), and binary cash-or-
nothing puts, Δ (pCN), can be determined analytically from Equations A.57
and A.59, respectively:
∂S
= Ke−rT n(d2)
Δ (cCN) = ∂cCN
σS
√
T
∂S
= −Ke−rT n(d2)
Δ (pCN) = ∂pCN
σS
√
T
.
The delta of a cash-or-nothing binary put is shown in Figure A.19. This
ﬁgure looks a little weird compared to the delta of an ordinary put option as
shown in Figure A.11. For an ordinary put option, the delta is always nega-
tive and monotonic; it is close to zero when the put is out-of-the-money and
becomes more and more negative as the put goes further in-the-money. This
means that if an option trader is long an ordinary put option and is delta
Figure A.19: Delta of a Cash-or-Nothing Binary Put Option as a Function
of Underlying Asset When K = 1mm, X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1
Month
503

<!-- Page 527 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
hedging the option, he will be long very little of the underlying when the
option is deep out-of-the-money but will buy more and more of the under-
lying as the underlying price goes down and the option goes deeper in-the-
money.
The situation is quite diﬀerent with a binary put option. Consider the
position of an option trader who is long a binary put and is delta hedging. If
the put is deep out-of-the-money, the option trader will be long very little of
the underlying. As the underlying price goes down, the trader will initially
buy more and more of the underlying, similarly as he would if he were delta
hedging an ordinary put option. However, as the binary put goes in-the-
money, the delta proﬁle of the option changes course dramatically, and the
option trader actually starts selling some of his position in the underlying.
As the underlying price continues to go down, and the binary put goes
deeper and deeper in-the-money, the option trader will continue to sell the
underlying until he again holds very little of the underlying as a hedge.
What’s the rationale behind this seemingly schizophrenic delta hedging
activity? When the underlying price is high and the binary put is deep out-
of-the-money, the option trader will need to hold very little of the underlying,
since any move downwards in the underlying doesn’t increase the value of
the option that much. As the underlying price goes down and approaches
being at-the-money, the value of the binary put starts to go up signiﬁcantly,
and in order to remain delta neutral, the trader will buy more and more of
the underlying (as the underlying goes down, the value of the shares of the
underlying that the trader has purchased to delta hedge goes down, which
oﬀsets the increase in the value of the option). However, as the underlying
continues to go down and the binary put option is in-the-money, there is no
further upside in terms of the potential payout from the option. Although a
further decline in the underlying price will further buﬀer against the possi-
bility of the option ﬁnishing out-of-the-money, the payout from the option is
ﬁxed. As the underlying continues to fall, the value of the option isn’t rising
that much more, but the shares of the underlying that the trader is long
to delta hedge continue to fall in value. This is markedly diﬀerent from an
ordinary put, in which any decreases in the underlying through the strike
continue to increase the value of the put signiﬁcantly. In order to remain
delta neutral, the trader who is long this binary put option must actually
start to sell some of the shares that he had been buying as the underlying
declines below the strike.
The characteristics of the delta hedging strategy for the binary put option
that was just described can be summarized by looking at its gamma proﬁle
as displayed in Figure A.20. In this ﬁgure, we have not only displayed the
gamma of the option but we have also depicted the delta hedging activities
504

<!-- Page 528 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.20: Gamma of Cash-or-Nothing Binary Put as a Function of Under-
lying Asset, with Description of Delta Hedging Activities for a Long Position
in the Option When K = 1, X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 3 Month
of a trader who is long this option. When the underlying is above the strike
and the option is out-of-the-money, the holder of the binary put is long
gamma (meaning gamma is positive), and when the underlying is low and
the option is in-the-money, the holder of the binary put is short gamma
(meaning gamma is negative). When the option is out-of-the-money and its
gamma is positive, a trader who is long the option and delta hedging winds
up buying the underlying as it falls and selling the underlying as it rises. And
when the option is in-the-money and its gamma is negative, a trader who
is long the option and delta hedging winds up selling the underlying when
it falls and buying the underlying as it rises. It is the change in the sign of
the gamma of this option that is responsible for the erratic delta hedging
pattern associated with it.
Potential Stabilizing or Destabilizing of the Underlying Through
Delta Hedging
One important implication of the delta hedging activities of binary options
is the potential stabilizing or destabilizing forces that such hedging can have
on the price movements of the underlying. It is important to note that these
phenomena would only potentially manifest themselves when these delta
505

<!-- Page 529 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
hedging activities were large relative to other trading activities in the under-
lying itself. Whether or not delta hedging activities are stabilizing or desta-
bilizing depends on whether the trader delta hedging is long or short the
option and whether or not the option is in-the-money or out-of-the money.39
Consider, for example, a trader who is long a binary put option that is
out-of-the-money and is delta hedging it. As the underlying goes down, the
trader will buy the underlying, which on the margin will tend to drive the
price of the underlying up. And as the underlying goes up, the trader will
sell the underlying, which on the margin will tend to drive the price of the
underlying down. Thus, the delta hedging activities of a trader who is long
an out-of-the-money binary put tend to be a stabilizing force on the value
of the underlying, just as the delta hedging activities by a trader who is
long ordinary options can result in stabilizing the price of the underlying.
If the delta hedging activities of such binary options are signiﬁcant enough,
this can be an overwhelming force in the market, resulting in the underlying
being rather stable and trading in a tight range.
By comparison, if a trader is long a binary put that is in-the-money, the
delta hedging activities of these options can result in tremendous destabi-
lization of the underlying. If the binary put is in-the-money, then as the
underlying price goes down, the trader who is long the option and delta
hedging will sell some of the underlying, which will further serve to push the
underlying down. And while the binary put is in-the-money, if the under-
lying price goes up, the trader will start buying some of the underlying,
which will further serve to push the underlying up. Thus, the delta hedging
activities for in-the-money binary puts can actually serve, at the margin, to
destabilize the price of the underlying. Again, if the delta hedging activities
of these options are large compared to outright trading in the underlying
from other traders in the market, this can create destabilizing forces in the
underlying, resulting in erratic price moves.40
Delta Hedging Binary Options as Expiry Approaches
The delta of a binary option is relatively well behaved for a signiﬁcant period
of time before maturity. However, when the time to expiry is short, the delta
39We assume here that only one of the two counterparties involved in the option trade
will delta hedge. The other is assumed to hold the option to maturity without delta
hedging. This is often the case when a dealer buys or sells an option to a buy-side account:
the dealer often delta hedges the position and the client does not. If both counterparties
delta hedge, then the delta hedging activities of each counterparty could oﬀset one another.
40This is a concept explored in Chapter 9, where we look at binary curve options. The
delta hedging activities of in-the-money binary curve ﬂoors at the height of the ﬁnan-
cial crisis are widely regarded as having contributed to USD 30-year swap spreads going
negative for the ﬁrst time in history.
506

<!-- Page 530 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
of a binary option is unstable if the option is near-the-money. Remember,
the delta of the option measures the change of the value of the option given
a change in the underlying. When the binary option is either deep in-the-
money or deep out-of-the-money, the delta is small. Consider now a binary
call option. If the binary is deep out-of-the-money, a slight increase in the
underlying is going to help bring the option closer to the money. As with an
ordinary option, this will increase the value of the option only slightly, and
thus the delta is small. If the option is deep in-the-money, a slight increase
in the underlying doesn’t do much either. It looked like the owner was going
to get K (the ﬁxed binary payment of the option) before the move, and it
still looks like he’ll get K, with a slightly greater probability. Thus, for a
deep in-the-money binary call option a slight change in the underlying does
not change the value of the option much, and the delta is again small. It
is when the option is near-the-money that the delta is greatest, as a slight
ﬂuctuation in the underlying most signiﬁcantly changes the probability of
receiving K instead of 0.
Figure A.21 shows the delta for a binary call option as a function of the
underlying for several diﬀerent expiries. As this ﬁgure shows, when time to
expiration is short, like a day, the delta is out of control if we are near-
the-money. In this case, a slight ﬂuctuation in the underlying can mean the
diﬀerence of getting 0 or getting K, which has been set to $1mm. This is
Figure A.21: Delta of a Cash-or-Nothing Binary Call as a Function of Under-
lying Asset for Various Expiries When K = 1mm, X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%
507

<!-- Page 531 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
a fundamentally diﬀerent situation from a regular call option, which pays
out a dollar for each dollar the asset price ﬁnishes above the strike. Think
about it: suppose we are short this binary call, it expires tomorrow, and the
underlying asset is currently at $49.99. If the underlying ﬁnishes at $49.99,
we owe nothing, but if it ﬁnishes at $50.00 we owe $1mm. Imagine we are
short this option with one day to go and the underlying is near-the-money.
How are we going to hedge this? We’re not. It’s a complete nightmare. Our
delta explodes if we are near-the-money; the amount of the underlying that
we would need to buy or sell as the underlying oscillates near the strike is
enormous. Transaction costs associated with trading the underlying—not to
mention the eﬀect that one may have on the price of the underlying itself
through trying to trade such large size in the underlying in order to hedge—
would be signiﬁcant.41
A.7.2
Vega of Binary Options
Let’s look now at the vega of binary options. As we discussed in Section A.6.3,
the vega of an option tells us how much the value of the option will change
when implied volatility changes. This is an item of primary importance to an
option trader, whose job is often to manage vega risk and/or take views on
movements in implied volatility in the options markets.
Under the Black-Scholes assumptions, the vega of a cash-or-nothing
binary call is as follows:
∂σ
= ∂

Ke−rT N(d2)

∂cCN
∂σ
= −Ke−rT n(d2)
ln(S/Xe−rT )
T

T
+ 0.5
√
σ2√
= −Ke−rT n(d2)d1
σ .
Similarly, it can be shown that under the Black-Scholes assumptions the
vega of a cash-or-nothing binary put is
∂pCN
∂σ
= Ke−rT N(d2)d1
σ .
41Indeed, an option trader who is short a binary call that is near-the-money may try
to prevent the option from ﬁnishing in-the-money by selling the underlying itself, which
exerts downward pressure on the price (the legality of this is somewhat of a gray area,
depending in part on the particular marketplace in which the underlying trades). If the
option trader sells enough of the underlying, he may be able to drive the price down to
prevent the option from ﬁnishing in-the-money. Of course, other market forces may be at
play that could make this diﬃcult.
508

<!-- Page 532 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
Figure A.22: Vega of a Cash-or-Nothing Binary Call as a Function of Under-
lying Asset When K = 1, X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 20%, T = 1 Month
If looking at the computation of the analytical expression for the vega of
the option is unsettling, one could always resort to approximating the vega
computationally, as in Equation A.51.
Figure A.22 shows the vega of a cash-or-nothing call as a function of the
underlying asset. This vega proﬁle is very unusual as compared to ordinary
options. When the option is in-the-money, the holder of the option is short
vol, meaning that an increase in volatility actually decreases the value of
the option. When the option is out-of-the-money, the holder of the option
is typically long vol, meaning that an increase in implied volatility increases
the value of the option.42 What’s going on here? Once the option is in-the-
money, that’s it: life is not going to get any better. The maximum payout
from the option is K. There is no further upside. When the option is in-
the-money, the holder wants nothing more than for volatility to go to zero,
for the underlying to stop moving around, so that he can get that money
K. When the option is far enough out-of-the-money, the holder is long vol
because he needs the underlying to move enough so that the option ﬁnishes
in-the-money. So an option trader who trades in this option may start out
with a certain vol position, only to ﬁnd that his vol position completely ﬂips
around as the underlying moves.
42As seen in Figure A.22, the sign of the vega does not change when the underlying is
equal to the strike, but rather slightly below the strike.
509

<!-- Page 533 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
A.8
Packages
Packages refer to options that are formed from a combination of other op-
tions, cash, and the underlying asset itself. The value of a package must
be equal to the sum of the prices of the component parts, as dictated by
arbitrage.
Being able to recognize an option as a package of other building blocks
is a very important skill to master. It usually requires staring at a picture of
the payout of the option and trying to ﬁgure out how to break the picture
down into more simple components. Oftentimes while doing so, one makes
some wrong turns and has to go back and try again. But you do enough
of these and you get the hang of it. We use this technique, for example,
deconstructing the knockout swap in Chapter 6.
Example
An asset-or-nothing option provides the holder with either nothing or a num-
ber of units of the underlying asset. Consider an asset-or-nothing call that
provides the holder with one unit of the underlying asset if the underlying
Figure A.23: Payoﬀof an Asset-or-Nothing Call
510

<!-- Page 534 -->
A P P EN D I X A : R EF R E S H ER I N O P T I O N P R I C I N G
is above the strike and zero otherwise. The payoﬀfrom the option is:
Payoﬀ=
 ST
if ST ≥X
0
if ST < X.
This payoﬀpattern is shown in Figure A.23. If you stare at Figures A.16
and A.23 long enough, perhaps you will recognize that the payoﬀof an
asset-or-nothing call is the same as the payoﬀof a portfolio consisting of an
ordinary call option on the underlying struck at X and a cash-or-nothing
binary call struck at X with a payoﬀK set equal to X.43 Since both al-
ternatives oﬀer the same payoﬀs, they must be worth the same today.44
Thus,
CAN = c(X) + cCN(X)
= SN(d1) −Xe−rT N(d2) + Xe−rT N(d2)
= SN(d1).
2
43Let’s show this is the case, by looking at the payoﬀof the portfolio at expiration. If
ST < X, then both the call option and the cash-or-nothing binary call option pay nothing,
in which case the portfolio pays nothing. If, however, ST ≥X, then the call option pays
out ST −X and the binary pays out X, for a total of ST −X + X = ST . Thus, this
portfolio has the same payout as the asset-or-nothing binary call.
44If they were not worth the same, you could arbitrage this situation by buying the
cheaper one and selling the more expensive one, which gives you positive cash ﬂow today
and zero cash ﬂow in the future.
511

3%.

4. Consider a company that is a clothing manufacturer who is having ﬁ-
nancial trouble. Their stock does not pay any dividends, and the price is

the end of one year. In an eﬀort to raise cash today, the company is running
the following promotion: with the purchase of every suit, a customer will
receive the choice of a) $8 cash today or b) a contract that speciﬁes that if
the company’s stock price at the end of one year is above its current level
of $100, the customer will receive $20 one year from today. If, however, the
stock price is below $100, the customer will receive nothing.
Assume that the company’s stock is actively traded and that the risk-free
rate is 8%. Assuming you buy a suit from the company, which would you
choose (i.e., which ﬁnancial asset do you choose, not which suit)?
5. Consider a stock whose current price is $20. The distribution of the secu-
rity is lognormal. Assume that the continuously compounded risk-free rate

Consider a derivative contract written on the stock. This contract will pay
$1 if the price of the stock is at least $25 in one year. This contract is worth
$0.34 today. What is the value today of a contract that will pay oﬀ$1 if the
price of the stock is less than $25 in one year?
6. A European put option with one month until expiration is selling for $2.50.
The stock pays no dividends. The current stock price is $47, the strike is $50,
and the continuously compounded risk-free rate is 6%. Is there an arbitrage
available?
7. Let c1, c2, and c3 be the prices of European call options with strikes

X2 −X1. All options are on the same stock and have the same maturity.
Show that c2 is less than or equal to (c1 + c3)/2.
8. Consider an option on a stock. Suppose the stock price is currently $30
and assume that the stock price is lognormally distributed. Assume that the
exercise price is $29, the continuously compounded risk-free rate is 5%, the
volatility of the stock price is 25%, and the time to maturity of the option
is four months. Find the price of the option if it is a) a European call, b) an
American call, or c) a European put. Show that put-call parity holds.

price of the put is $40, and the underlying stock price is currently $32.

The continuously compounded risk-free rate is 2%. Is there an arbitrage

not necessarily increase the value of European options. For example, we
considered the case of a European call option on a stock that expires in a
month. If the company is planning to issue a liquidating dividend, say, the
day after expiry, this option is probably going to be worth more than an
otherwise similar European call option that expires in, say, two months. We
also considered the case of a European put option on a stock whose current
price was zero. In this example an increase in time to expiration only serves
to delay the time until you obtain the strike and thereby decreases the value
of the put.
Consider now a non-dividend-paying asset and denote by c(X, t, T) and
p(X, t, T) the value at time t of a call and put option, respectively, on the
underlying asset that has a strike of X and expires at time T.
a) Consider the two quantities c(Xe−r(T −t), 0, t) and c(X, 0, T). Can you
deﬁnitively say that one of these must be worth at least as much as the
other? If so, show why. If not, explain why not.
b) Consider the two quantities p(Xe−r(T −t), 0, t) and p(X, 0, T). Can you

other? If so, show why. If not, explain why not.
c) How do you interpret these ﬁndings?

tant valuation formulas were given in Equations A.14 and A.15, respectively.

16. A chooser option is a European option that matures at time T, but the
buyer does not have to determine whether the option is a put or a call at
the time of purchase. The buyer has until time t to make this choice, where
t < T. Assume that the call and the put both have the same strike price
and time to expiry, and we will assume that the underlying asset pays no
dividends.
516

<!-- Page 540 -->
S U P P LEM EN TA L P R O B LEM S E T
The value of a chooser option at any time after the choice has been made
(i.e., between times t and T) is just the value of a European call or put,
depending on the choice made by the holder.
Deﬁne c(X, t, T) as the value of a European call option at time t with a strike
of X that expires at time T. Notice that the time remaining until maturity
is T −t. Deﬁne p(X, t, T) analogously.
At time t, the owner of the chooser will rationally choose whichever of the
call or put has greater value. Thus, at time t, the value of the chooser is
max [c(X, t, T), p(X, t, T)] .
(A.60)
a) Show that the value of the chooser today is given by
c(X, 0, T) + p(Xe−r(T −t), 0, t).
(A.61)
Hint: Start by using put-call parity, which says that
p(X, t, T) = c(X, t, T) −S + Xe−r(T −t),
to substitute into the expression for the put in Equation A.60.
b) “Go the other way around” and substitute in for the value of a call in
Equation A.60 using put-call parity as given in the form
c(X, t, T) = p(X, t, T) + S −Xe−r(T −t)
and show in this manner that the value of the chooser option must be equal
to
p(X, 0, T) + c(Xe−r(T −t), 0, t).
(A.62)
c) We have now derived two expressions for the value today of the chooser
option, one as in Equation A.61 and the other as in Equation A.62. Show
that these two formulations are equivalent.
d) A straddle is a position that is long both a call and a put with the same
strike and the same expiry. The value of a straddle that matures at time T
is
c(X, 0, T) + p(X, 0, T).
(A.63)

e) Consider a chooser option on a non-dividend-paying stock with a ﬁnal

<!-- Page 542 -->
Appendix B
A Brief Review of Some Fixed
Income Topics
The purpose of this appendix is to provide a very brief review of the concept
of duration as a primary measure of risk for bonds. For simplicity, we will
consider here only bonds that are assumed to pay one time per year, and in
addition we will only consider the valuation and analysis of bonds on their
settlement date. For a more general discussion, see, for example, Fabozzi
(1993).
B.1
Present Value
Perhaps the most fundamental concept of valuation of ﬁxed income securities
is the notion of present value. If a cash ﬂow c is to be received in t periods,
then the present value of that cash ﬂow is equal to
PV =
c
[1 + zc(t)]t ,
(B.1)
where zc(t) represents an appropriate discount rate, or zero coupon rate, for
cash ﬂow received at time t. A concept related to a zero coupon rate is a
discount factor. The discount factor for cash ﬂow received at time t, denoted
DF(0, t), is given by
DF(0, t) =
1
[1 + zc(t)]t .
(B.2)
It is important to note the dependence of the particular zero coupon rate on
the time at which the cash is received. Cash ﬂows received at diﬀerent times

<!-- Page 543 -->
A P P EN D I X B : A B R I EF R EV I E W O F S O M E F I X ED I N C O M E T O P I C S
will be discounted using diﬀerent zero coupon rates. For example, suppose
we have an n-year bond that pays an annual coupon equal to c, then its
price today, P, is given by
P =
c
[1 + zc(1)]1 +
c
[1 + zc(2)]2 + . . . +
c + F
[1 + zc(n)]n ,
(B.3)
where F is the face value of the bond. Given the price of a bond, P, we can
deﬁne the yield to maturity, y, of the bond as that number that solves the
following equation:
P =
c
(1 + y)1 +
c
(1 + y)2 + . . . +
c + F
(1 + y)n .
(B.4)
Note that the yield to maturity is the yield used to discount each cash ﬂow
of the bond, regardless of when it is received. It is important to note that
yield to maturity is a market convention; it much easier to analyze a bond
and make a comparison of bonds on the basis of a single number, yield
to maturity, than it is to discuss a whole sequence of zero coupon rates.
However, yield to maturity has no true economic meaning. Individual cash
ﬂows should rightfully be discounted at their individual zero coupon rates,
not the artiﬁcial construct known as yield to maturity.
B.2
Duration
Duration is the primary metric used in ﬁxed income to measure the price
sensitivity of a bond to changes in interest rates. We will look at three diﬀer-
ent measures of duration. Macaulay duration is a weighted average time until
the cash ﬂows of a bond are received. This measure is of limited practical use.
Modiﬁed duration is often used to measure price sensitivity when the cash
ﬂows of a bond are known with certainty. When the cash ﬂows of a bond are
uncertain due to options that have been embedded in the bond, a popular
measure of interest rate sensitivity used is referred to as eﬀective duration.
B.2.1
Macaulay Duration
Deﬁne
PVCFi =
CFi
(1 + y)i ,
where CFi denotes the cash ﬂow of the bond at time i. Note that
n
P =
i=1
PVCFi.
520

<!-- Page 544 -->
A P P EN D I X B : A B R I EF R EV I E W O F S O M E F I X ED I N C O M E T O P I C S
Macaulay duration is deﬁned by
n
i=1 i × PVCFi
P
.
Macaulay duration is equal to the weighted average time to receiving cash
ﬂow of the bond, where the weights are equal to present value of each cash
ﬂow (computed using its yield to maturity y) as a percentage of the bond’s
price. Macaulay duration is of limited use in the analysis of bonds. There is
a related measure, however, that is widely used.
B.2.2
Modiﬁed Duration
The modiﬁed duration of a bond, Dm, is deﬁned by
Dm = Macaulay duration
1 + y
.
The modiﬁed duration of a bond, Dm, has the property that
dP = −Dm × P × dy,
(B.5)
where dy represents a small change in the yield to maturity of the bond and
dP represents the change in the price of the bond. Thus, given the modiﬁed
duration of a bond, its price, and a small change in yield, Equation B.5 gives
us a way to compute the change in the price of the bond. It is very important
to note that the modiﬁed duration of a bond is not a constant but rather
changes as a function of the yield to maturity. As a result, the formula in
Equation B.5 is accurate only for a small change in yield. The diﬀerence
between the actual change in a bond’s price and the change in price as given
in Equation B.5 is measured by the convexity of the bond. The convexity
can be quantiﬁed, and the interested reader is referred to Fabozzi (1993).
Example
Figure B.1 shows the price of a 30-year bond with an annual coupon of 6%
as a function of its yield to maturity. The price is computed using Equa-
tion B.4 and is represented by the curve in the ﬁgure. Given an initial yield
to maturity of 6%, the modiﬁed duration of the bond has been calculated
and is equal to 14.59. For a given change in yield, Equation B.5 is used to
estimate the new price of the bond. These estimates are represented by the
straight line in the graph. Note that the diﬀerence between the actual price
of the bond for a given change in yield and the estimate of its price based
on its modiﬁed duration is a positive number. The convexity of the bond
quantiﬁes this diﬀerence.
2
521

<!-- Page 545 -->
A P P EN D I X B : A B R I EF R EV I E W O F S O M E F I X ED I N C O M E T O P I C S
Figure B.1: A Bond with Positive Convexity and Its Price Estimates Based
on Its Modiﬁed Duration
B.2.3
Effective Duration
The bonds that we have considered thus far have known cash ﬂows. Some
bonds that we consider have embedded options, and as a result the coupons
(or principal) paid by such bonds are not certain but depend on those op-
tions. It is often desirable to come up with a measure of these bonds’ sen-
sitivities to changes in rates. The use of modiﬁed duration is inappropriate
since modiﬁed duration assumes that the coupons are known with certainty.
For bonds with embedded options, an often used metric for price sensi-
tivity is known as eﬀective duration. The eﬀective duration of the bond is
given by
P−Δx −P+Δx
2 × Δx × P0
,
(B.6)
where P−Δx and P+Δx are the values of the bond when the yield curve
is shifted down or up by an amount Δx, respectively, and P0 represents
the value of the bond when the yield curve is not shifted. Notice that the
values P−Δx and P+Δx incorporate changes to the value of the bond due to
any embedded options. Furthermore, note that this measure of interest rate
sensitivity does not rely on a single yield to maturity concept but rather on
a parallel shift in the underlying yield curve up or down.
522

<!-- Page 546 -->
Appendix C
A Closer Look at Day Count and
Payment Conventions in Swaps
The standard settlement date, or eﬀective date, on USD spot starting swaps
is two New York and London Business Days after the trade date, often
denoted “T + 2.”1 Unless otherwise indicated, the standard convention is
that the ﬁxed side pays semiannually on a 30/360 day count basis and the
ﬂoating side is 3-month LIBOR that pays quarterly on an Act/360 basis.
Payments are never made on “bad days,” deﬁned as weekends or holidays
in New York or London during which time banks or foreign exchange markets
are closed. If a payment were to fall on a bad day, the actual payment date
will depend on whether payments are made on a preceding, following, or
modiﬁed following basis. If payments are made on a preceding day basis,
then if a payment were due on a bad day, it would instead be paid on the
ﬁrst preceding good business day. If payments are made on a following day
basis, then if a payment were due on a bad day, it would instead be paid
on the next good business day. Finally, if payments are made on a modiﬁed
following basis, then if a payment were due on a bad day, it would instead
be paid on the next good business day unless that next good business day
happens to fall in the subsequent month, in which case we revert to making
the payment on the ﬁrst preceding good business day.
Preceding, following, or modiﬁed following answer when payments should
occur. But if a payment date is moved in accordance with one of these
conventions, should the payment amount be adjusted as well? Exactly how
much should be paid on a payment date is determined by whether payments
1Forward starting swaps have eﬀective dates that are beyond T + 2.

Following with Adjustment
are made on an adjusted or unadjusted basis. If a payment date falls on a
bad day, then the actual date on which the payment occurs is changed, and
this results in changing the number of days in the period. If payments are
made with adjustment, then the number of accrual days in the period will
change to correspond with the change in the payment date. If payments are
made with no adjustment, then the number of accrual days in the period
will not change despite the change in the payment date.
The standard in the market is that swap payments are made modiﬁed
following with adjustment on both the ﬁxed and ﬂoating sides. Let’s take a
look at a 2-year swap executed on April 28, 2003, which is a Monday. The
eﬀective date of the swap, following the standard T + 2 rule, is Wednesday,
April 30, 2003. The actual payment dates of the swap are shown in Table C.1.
Note that there are a total of 719 days that accrue on the ﬁxed side and
730 accrual days on the ﬂoating side. Although this may seem “unfair” to the
ﬂoating rate payer who has to pay out accruals for an additional 11 days, the
ﬁxed rate takes that into account, and there is no arbitrage opportunity here.
The ﬁxed rate is set such that the present value of the ﬁxed cash ﬂows equals
the present value of the ﬂoating cash ﬂows and incorporates the disparity in
the number of accrual days on each side of the swap.
The ﬁxed side pays semiannually, and if there were no bad days, this
would imply payments would occur each October 30 and April 30. Note,
however, that one of the ﬁxed payments occurs on October 29, 2004. The
524

reason for this is that October 30, 2004 is a Saturday. The next good business
day is November 1, 2004, but inasmuch as that takes us into the next month,
under modiﬁed following we revert back to the ﬁrst preceding good business
day, which is Friday, October 29. Since payments are adjusted, the ﬁxed rate
accrues for only 179 days this period, the number of 30/360 days between
April 30, 2004 and October 29, 2004. The ﬁnal period on the ﬁxed side
faces a similar plight: April 30, 2005 is a Saturday, and since the next good
business day is in May, we revert back to Friday, April 29, 2005 for the last
payment date in the swap under modiﬁed following. Note that the number
of bond days in this period from October 29, 2004 to April 29, 2005 is 180. If
the ﬁxed side were unadjusted, each semiannual period would accrue exactly
180 days despite the change in actual payment dates.
The same conventions apply to the ﬂoating leg of the swap and aﬀect
the quarterly periods ending in October 2004 and April 2005 similarly. In
addition, the quarterly period ending on January 30, 2005 is a Sunday, and
thus the payment is made on Monday, January 31, 2005 under the modiﬁed
following convention.
Now let’s take a look at a 2-year swap that is also executed on Monday,
April 28, 2003 and also has an eﬀective date of April 30, 2003, but assume
that payments on the ﬁxed side are made on a following basis with adjust-
ment. Assume the ﬂoating payments are still made on a modiﬁed following
basis with adjustment. The actual payment dates of this swap are shown in
Table C.2.
525

<!-- Page 549 -->
A P P EN D I X C : DAY CO U N T A N D PAY M EN T C O N V EN T I O N S I N S WA P S
The ﬂoating payments in this swap are identical to the ones in Table C.1,
but the ﬁxed payments diﬀer somewhat. In the third semiannual period, the
ﬁxed payment is now made on Monday, November 1, 2004; the scheduled pay-
ment date of October 30, 2004 was a bad day, and under the following con-
vention we roll to the next good business day. Since payments are still made
with adjustment, this means that we accrue the swap rate for the total num-
ber of bond days between April 30, 2004 and November 1, 2004 which is 181.
We also need to be careful with the last semiannual period in the swap. April
30, 2005 is a Saturday, and thus we need to roll forward to the next good
business day. Sunday is obviously out, but we need be careful: Monday, May
2, 2005 is May Day, a London holiday on which banks are closed. Thus the
next good business day is Tuesday, May 3, 2005. Since payments are made
with adjustment, the swap rate is accrued for the number of bond days be-
tween November 1, 2004 and May 3, 2005 which is 182. If the ﬁxed side were
accruing on an unadjusted basis, each period would accrue for exactly 180
days despite the change in payment dates.
The upshot here is that both 2-year swaps (i.e., the one in which the
ﬁxed side pays modiﬁed following with adjustment and the one in which the
ﬁxed side pays following with adjustment) are not the same. Both have of
course the same exact cash ﬂows on the ﬂoating side, but their ﬁxed sides
diﬀer due to the diﬀerent payment conventions employed. The ﬁrst swap has
a total of 719 bond days accruing on the ﬁxed side, whereas the second has
a total of 723 days. The ﬁxed rates in these two swaps cannot be the same.
If the ﬁxed rates were the same, we would always be better oﬀpaying in
the ﬁrst one and receiving in the second one at the same ﬁxed rate and
accrue the additional 4 days. Which swap should have the higher rate?
As of 3:00 p.m. EST on April 28, 2003, the ﬁxed rate in the ﬁrst of the
2-year swaps in which both sides pay modiﬁed following with adjustment
was 1.81643%. At the very same time, the ﬁxed rate in the second of the two
swaps in which the ﬁxed side pays following with adjustment was 1.80671%,
a diﬀerence of about 0.97 bps. This is a fairly substantial diﬀerence: let’s say
we entered into $1bn notional of this 2-year swap and agreed to pay ﬁxed
on a following basis, but paid at the wrong rate of 1.81643%. We would be
paying a rate that is too high and would have lost around $190,000 on a
mark-to-market basis for our mistake.2
Back in the 1990s, some proprietary swap models failed to take these
diﬀerences into account. At the time, there were some traders in the mar-
ket who would try to “pick oﬀ” (i.e., take advantage of) other traders whose
models did not account for these adjustments properly. For example, suppose
2To ﬁgure out how much we would lose, put PMT = 0.97/2 bp, n = 4, and i =
1.80671%/2 and get PV = 1.9 bps upfront, or $190,000 on a notional of $1bn.
526

<!-- Page 550 -->
A P P EN D I X C : DAY CO U N T A N D PAY M EN T C O N V EN T I O N S I N S WA P S
we were aware of the diﬀerences in how these two swaps should be priced but
our counterparty was not. We could enter into a $1bn 2-year swap with them
in which we receive ﬁxed using standard market conventions of modiﬁed fol-
lowing on both sides. Suppose we receive ﬁxed in this swap at 1.81643%.
After the deal had been executed, we could contact our counterparty about
our (seemingly innocuous) request to change the ﬁxed side of the swap to
following with adjustment, instead of modiﬁed following with adjustment,
but keep the ﬁxed rate the same. Our counterparty might very well agree to
the change, since his model fails to price the diﬀerence correctly. In reality,
when changing the ﬁxed leg to following with adjustment, the ﬁxed rate in
the swap should decline to 1.80671%. Thus, we would make nearly $200,000
by picking oﬀour counterparty for nearly 1 bp in the rate. Of course, such
behavior would be considered a huge breach of etiquette by many in the mar-
ket. That being said, the conventional wisdom in the market is that certain
traders attempted to exploit opportunities like this during that time.
It is important to note that signiﬁcant diﬀerences in swap rates as a
function of payment conventions are exceedingly rare. Such diﬀerences tend
to manifest themselves in swaps whose payment dates roll near the end of
months, such as in the example considered here. Typically, the diﬀerence in
a swap by changing the payment conventions is a small rounding error. But
swaps that have payment dates near the end of a month should be priced
with some extra caution, as hopefully this example demonstrates.
527

<!-- Page 552 -->
Appendix D
A Quick Look at Mortgages
This is a very brief introduction to mortgages and mortgage-backed secu-
rities. It is intended only to provide a basis for understanding the market
dynamics prevalent in the summer of 2003 in which mortgage investors were
forced to pay ﬁxed in swaps in order to manage the duration of their port-
folios during the massive sell-oﬀin the bond market. For more information
on mortgage securities, see Sundaresan (2009) or Fabozzi (2005).
A mortgage is a collateralized loan in which the borrower, a homeowner,
obtains cash from a lender (typically a bank or mortgage originator) in order
to help ﬁnance the purchase of the home. The home itself acts as collateral
on the loan: in the event of default, the lender takes possession of the house
and property.1
The standard mortgage in the United States is a ﬁxed rate, 30-year loan in
which the borrower pays equal payments on a monthly basis for a term of 30
years. It is important to note that a mortgage is a fully self-amortizing loan:
the magnitude of each monthly payment is enough to pay oﬀinterest due
on the loan as well as some principal. Moreover, the monthly payments are
“level,” meaning that the payments are the same each month for the 30-year
life of the loan. As the life of the mortgage goes by, the outstanding balance
on the mortgage decreases at an increasing rate, as a declining proportion
1One assessment of the quality of a mortgage when regarded as an asset of the lender
is known as the loan to value, or LTV, which is the ratio of the loan size to the value of
the property. The smaller the LTV, the higher the credit quality of the mortgage.

Table D.1: Principal and Interest Payments on a $100,000 30-Year 6% Mort-
gage
of the ﬁxed monthly payment needs to be applied to paying oﬀinterest and
is instead applied to paying oﬀprincipal.
Consider, for example, a 30-year loan of $100,000 on which the annual
mortgage rate is 6%. One can easily calculate on a ﬁnancial calculator that
the monthly mortgage payment is $599.55. Alternatively, the monthly mort-
gage payment may be calculated using the formula
P ×
y/m
1 −(1 + y/m)−mn ,
where P is the face of the loan, y is the mortgage rate, m is the number of
payments per year, and n is the number of years of the loan.2
In the ﬁrst month, the interest that needs to be paid on the loan is
$100, 000 × 6%/12 = $500, and the remaining $99.55 portion of the monthly
payment reduces the outstanding principal balance accordingly. By the last
month of the mortgage, the outstanding principal balance is only $596.57.
The ﬁnal payment pays oﬀinterest due of $2.98, and the remainder of the
monthly payment is enough to pay oﬀthe balance. Table D.1 demonstrates
what happens to the principal balance and interest paid over the life of the
loan.
2For our purposes here, we will sometimes round calculations of the monthly mortgage
payment to the nearest cent. This will introduce a slight rounding error in the principal
balance that can safely be ignored.
530

<!-- Page 554 -->
A P P EN D I X D : A Q U I C K L O O K AT M O R T G AG E S
Figure D.1: Principal and Interest Payments Over the Life of a Mortgage
Over the life of the loan, the interest component of the monthly payment
steadily declines as more and more principal is paid oﬀwith each passing
month. Notice that the sum of the interest paid on the outstanding balance
and the principal paid down (the sum of the third and fourth columns,
respectively, in Table D.1) is always a constant $599.55. Figure D.1 shows
graphically how the division of principal and interest varies over the life of
the loan.
One of the most salient features of most mortgages is that the borrower
has the right to prepay the mortgage at any time over the life of the loan.3
That is, the borrower may at any time pay oﬀthe outstanding principal
balance. The borrower may choose to do so for any of a number of reasons:
his job may necessitate that he move to another city and sell his house, he
may decide to sell his current home to buy a bigger one or a smaller one
and so on. But perhaps the most interesting reason that a homeowner may
prepay his existing mortgage is due to the possible economic incentive to
reﬁnance his loan, which will arise if mortgage rates go lower.
3Mortgages are typically nontransferable, meaning that if a homeowner moves from
his house, he cannot apply his existing mortgage to his new home, nor can he in any way
assign it to the new owner of his old house. An exception is Federal Housing Administration
(FHA) mortgages, which may, under certain conditions, be assumed by another qualiﬁed
borrower upon sale of the home.
531

<!-- Page 555 -->
A P P EN D I X D : A Q U I C K L O O K AT M O R T G AG E S
For example, suppose that after the homeowner takes out the $100,000
30-year mortgage at 6%, mortgage rates drop to 5.75%. Instead of making
payments of $599.55 as per the loan at 6%, the homeowner could immedi-
ately prepay the loan and instead take out a new 30-year mortgage at a rate
of 5.75%, which works out to be a monthly payment of $583.57. That means
that over the life of the loan the borrower will save ($599.55 −$583.57) ×
360 = $5,752.80 in nominal interest expense if he were to immediately reﬁ-
nance.4 The borrower may or may not choose to reﬁnance: by reﬁnancing,
he will incur transaction costs (legal and other closing costs) that will dimin-
ish his economic incentive to reﬁnance. Or perhaps the borrower is not that
savvy and does not think to reﬁnance in a lower rate environment. Other
times a borrower who met the underwriting standards necessary to qualify
for his original loan may no longer qualify for a new loan by the time rates
fall (if, e.g., the value of the home, which serves as collateral for the loan,
has fallen too signiﬁcantly relative to the size of the loan). But one thing
is true: the lower rates go after he takes out his initial loan, the greater his
incentive to prepay the mortgage and reﬁnance at a lower rate.
Let’s take a look at this mortgage now from the perspective of the lend-
ing institution. The lending institution owns a (risky) asset that pays ﬁxed
coupons each month, and has the possibility of being prepaid as rates fall.5
In some sense, then, this asset resembles a callable bond: as rates fall, the
asset may be “called away” by the “issuer,” who in this case is of course the
homeowner. Let’s assume that as rates do fall, the probability that the loan is
prepaid increases.6 Then as rates fall, the probability that the lender receives
his principal back early increases. Conversely, as rates rise, the probability
4Note that in a 30-year 6% loan the borrower makes total payments of $215,838.19,
of which $100,000 goes to paying back principal and the remaining $115,838.19 goes to
paying oﬀinterest expense. In the 30-year 5.75% loan, the borrower makes total pay-
ments of $210,086.23, of which $100,000 goes to paying back principal and the remaining
$110,086.23 goes to paying oﬀinterest expense. This simplistic example ignores consid-
erations such as the timing of cash ﬂows, transaction costs to reﬁnance, and so on, but
nonetheless demonstrates the point that as mortgage rates go lower, the borrower will
have an incentive to reﬁnance.
5The asset is risky not only in terms of the uncertainty of the cash ﬂows that the loan
may generate but also in terms of the possibility of the borrower defaulting.
6This may not necessarily be the case. The analysis of the possible prepayment of
mortgages is a very complicated area in ﬁnance that takes into consideration many vari-
ables, including the credit (FICO) score of a borrower, the loan size, current LTV, and
geography. Another variable that is often considered is burnout, which refers to the phe-
nomenon in which borrowers who have previously not taken advantage of a lower rate
environment to reﬁnance are not likely to do so in a subsequent lower rate environment. If
this is the case, then a low rate environment may not necessarily increase the probability
of prepayment.
532

<!-- Page 556 -->
A P P EN D I X D : A Q U I C K L O O K AT M O R T G AG E S
that the lender’s asset will remain outstanding until the ﬁnal maturity of
the loan increases.
Oftentimes, lenders such as banks and mortgage originators do not wish
to hold the mortgages they originate on their books. These institutions
make money through the origination of loans, the process by which they
lend money to new borrowers. If they sell their mortgages after they are
originated, they will have more balance sheet available to make additional
loans.7 This works out well because there are investors in the market who
are interested in buying their mortgages, usually on a securitized basis. Buy-
ing mortgages loan by loan on an unsecuritized basis would prove diﬃ-
cult for most potential investors, as investors generally don’t have the re-
sources to dedicate to analyzing individual loans, which contain credit risk
due to the nature of the individual borrowers. Some of the GSEs—namely,
Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, as well as Ginnie Mae (as a direct arm of the
government)—help to facilitate the process of making mortgages available
to investors by
• Buying individual mortgages from lending institutions;
• Bundling individual mortgages with similar characteristics into a larger
grouping known as a pool; 8
• Providing a guarantee on the mortgages in the pool, thereby protecting
investors who buy the mortgages from the possibility of default of
individual loans in the pool;
• Securitizing the pool and making it available for sale to investors on a
liquid basis.
Investors who buy these securitized mortgage pools, known as agency
mortgage-backed securities, typically earn a higher yield than they would on
comparable duration Treasuries, even though they have the government’s
imprimatur against default risk. The reason for the higher yield is the pre-
payment risk that the investor faces: as the market rallies, borrowers in the
pool are more likely to prepay their loans, and therefore the weighted average
life of the security decreases. Conversely, as the market sells oﬀ, borrowers
7There are times, however, when these institutions choose to maintain a portion of the
loans they originate on their balance sheets. If, for example, a bank believes other asset
classes it normally invests in are less attractive than usual and the commercial lending
activities of the bank are for some reason curtailed, the bank may ﬁnd it advantageous to
retain a portion of the mortgages it originates in its own portfolio.
8The reason that the agencies bundle mortgages with similar characteristics (e.g.,
coupon, maturity, loan size) is that it makes for a pool that has uniformly similar char-
acteristics and is thus easier for investors to analyze on a consistent basis.
533

<!-- Page 557 -->
A P P EN D I X D : A Q U I C K L O O K AT M O R T G AG E S
Figure D.2: The Negative Convexity of Mortgages (lower curve) Versus a
Security with Positive Convexity (higher curve)
in the pool are less likely to prepay their loans, and therefore the weighted
average life of the security increases. This does imply the following very im-
portant characteristic of MBSs: as the market rallies, the duration of MBSs
decreases, and as the market sells oﬀ, the duration of MBSs increases.
Now let’s take a step back for a minute: suppose we are a ﬁxed income
investor, and we expect the market to rally. Given our view, we would prefer
to own assets that have a long duration rather than a short duration. The
reason for this is that longer duration assets, by deﬁnition, have greater sen-
sitivity to changes in interest rates. And if rates are going down, we want a
lot of price sensitivity to beneﬁt from greater price appreciation of the assets
in our portfolio. If we instead own short duration assets, these would have
limited price sensitivity, and their value would not increase as dramatically
in a rally. On the other hand, if we expect that the market will sell oﬀ, we
would prefer to hold short duration assets in our portfolio. A sell-oﬀin the
market will diminish the value of our ﬁxed rate bonds. Those with a short
duration will depreciate in value less than those with longer durations.
Unfortunately, this is anything but the experience we will have if we own
MBSs. As the market rallies (and we wished that we owned long duration
assets to maximize price appreciation in our ﬁxed income portfolio), bor-
rowers in the pool will begin to prepay their loans as the economic incentive
to do so increases. This will shorten the duration of the MBSs we own, with
534

<!-- Page 558 -->
A P P EN D I X D : A Q U I C K L O O K AT M O R T G AG E S
principal being returned to the bondholder at par instead of allowing us to
continue to own a bond that is now paying a premium coupon. Conversely,
as the market sells oﬀ(and we are hoping to own short duration assets to
minimize the price depreciation of our portfolio), the incentive for borrowers
to prepay will decrease, and thus the expectation that prepayments will
occur will decline. This will cause the duration of MBSs to increase. In sum,
this is a bad thing: as the market rallies, and we want our mortgages to stick
around, they disappear, and when the market sells oﬀ, and we want our
mortgages to disappear, they stick around. More formally, this phenomenon
is characteristic of the negative convexity inherent in MBSs. The eﬀect of
negative convexity on the price performance of MBSs is demonstrated in
Figure D.2.
This negative convexity is the manifestation of the options that the MBS
bondholder is inherently short to the homeowners. Of course, investors who
buy MBSs are compensated for enduring this negative convexity: the coupons
paid by MBSs issued at par are higher than on comparable duration Trea-
suries issued at par. The excess yield of MBSs more than compensates for
small market moves and undercompensates for larger moves.
Let’s now refer back to the market dynamics of the summer of 2003 in
which the market sold oﬀdramatically, nearly 150 bps in about six weeks.
Investors who held large portfolios of mortgages found that the durations
of their portfolios were increasing (or put another way, their mortgage port-
folios were extending). In order to manage the extension of their mortgage
portfolios, such investors found it necessary to enter into trades to oﬀset
the increasing duration of their portfolios. One way they could do this was
by selling Treasuries, and another way they could do this was by paying
ﬁxed in swaps.9 As large mortgage investors increased their hedging activity
in both the Treasury and especially the swaps market, this contributed to
and exacerbated the huge sell-oﬀand spread widening that was discussed in
Section 2.2.1.
9Note that as the market is selling oﬀ(with yields rising and bond prices falling),
investors who need to hedge their mortgages are being put into the position of having to
sell Treasuries at lower prices or pay a higher ﬁxed rate in swaps than they could have
before the sell-oﬀ. Either of these alternatives are disadvantageous and are characteristic
of having to hedge the negative convexity implicit in mortgage securities.
535

<!-- Page 560 -->
Appendix E
The Normal Model
Here is a heuristic sketch of the derivation of the value of swaptions under
the assumption of a normal distribution, as compared to the standard Black-
Scholes assumption of lognormality. We employ risk-neutrality. In particular,
let us consider pricing a T into n payer swaption struck at X, under the
assumption that the underlying swap rate R ∼N(F, σ2
NT), where F denotes
the n-year swap rate, T years forward. In this formulation, we refer to σN
as normal vol. For ease of notation, deﬁne τ = σN

Equation E.23 shows why we often approximate normal vol with the quantity
F × σLN: the term in parentheses is often close to one, provided that σLN
and T are small. However, when σLN is large and/or time to expiration
T is large, the quantity in Equation E.23 provides a meaningfully better
approximation.

The relationship between lognormal implied vol, σLN, and normal implied
vol, σN, for swaptions with an arbitrary strike can be determined with a
numerical procedure that we will outline below.1 Before we begin, recall
that under the normal model the value of a T into n receiver swaption with
strike X is equal to

strike of the swaption. Denote these functions by σLN(X) and σN(X), respectively. Both
Zhou (2003) and Sadr (2009) suggest that
σN (X) ≈σLN(X) ×

Table E.1: Option Sensitivities Under the Normal Model
Recall that under the lognormal model the value of a T into n receiver
swaption with strike X is given in Equation E.11 and is
A × [XN(−d2) −FN(−d1)] ,
(E.25)
where d1 and d2 are as deﬁned in Equations E.12 and E.13, respectively.
We assume that the normal model holds (in which case σN does not vary
as we allow the strike of swaptions of a particular expiry and tail to vary)
and seek to derive restrictions on σLN. For a given expiry T, a tail n, and a
given strike X:
1. Price up the receiver swaption under the normal model for a given level
of normal implied vol, σN, using Equation E.24. Denote by priceN the
price we obtain.
2. Set the value of a T into n receiver swaption struck at X under the
lognormal model in Equation E.25 equal to the price we just solved for
in Step 1. That is, set
A × [XN(−d2) −FN(−d1)] = priceN.
(E.26)
3. Determine the level of lognormal implied vol, σLN, that satisﬁes Equa-
tion E.26.
Following this procedure for a variety of strikes X allows us to (numerically)
deduce the relationship between σN and σLN under the assumption of the
normal model. This process was used to create Figure 5.15.2
2This procedure could be modiﬁed to utilize payer swaptions instead of receiver swap-
tions or even swaption straddles. The point here is to require that the value of an option
is the same regardless of which model we use, then to derive restrictions on the implied
vols that must be used in each model.

Solutions to Selected Problems
Chapter 1
1. a) The interpolated 4-year Treasury yield is computed using the 3-year
and 5-year Treasury yields. The 4-year interpolated yield is
0.980% +
4 −3

× (1.877% −0.980%) = 1.4285%.
5 −3
Thus, the bid side 4-year swap rate is 1.4285% + 0.2475% = 1.676%, and
the oﬀer side 4-year swap rate is 1.4285% + 0.2525% = 1.681%.
2. a) The 5-year oﬀer side rate semi bond is equal to the bid side of the 5-year
Treasury yield plus the oﬀer side of the 5-year spread, which is 1.877% +
0.2125% = 2.0895%. This would typically be rounded to 2.090%.
3. a) The 5-year bid side swap rate is 5.25% semi bond. First, we will convert
this rate to a quarterly bond basis and then to a quarterly money basis:

1 + 0.0525
2/4
−1
= 5.216%
4 ×
2
5.216% × 360/365 = 5.145%.
