The 5-year oﬀer side swap rate is 5.27% semi bond. First, we will convert
this rate to a quarterly bond basis and then to a quarterly money basis:

1 + 0.0527
2/4
−1
4 ×
= 5.236%
2
5.236% × 360/365 = 5.164%.
Note that since the ﬂoating side of the swap remains 3-month LIBOR, there
is no need to adjust for any basis swap despite the fact that the ﬁxed side is
paying quarterly.
b) The 5-year bid side swap rate is 5.25% semi bond. First, we will convert
this rate to a monthly bond basis and then to a monthly money basis:

1 + 0.0525
2/12
−1
12 ×
= 5.193%
2
5.193% × 360/365 = 5.122%.
The 5-year oﬀer side swap rate is 5.27% semi bond. First, we will convert
this rate to a monthly bond basis and then to a monthly money basis:

1 + 0.0527
2/12
−1
12 ×
= 5.213%
2
5.213% × 360/365 = 5.142%.
c) The ﬂoating side is now 1-month LIBOR, and thus we need to incorporate
the value of the basis swap. When the dealer is paying ﬁxed and receiving
1-month LIBOR, we must use the oﬀer side of the basis swap, which is +3 bps.
Since the dealer is willing to pay 5.122% monthly money versus 3-month
LIBOR, this implies that the dealer would be willing to pay 5.122%−0.03%=
5.092% monthly money versus 1-month LIBOR. Figure S.1 shows this cal-
culation.
When the dealer is receiving ﬁxed and paying 1-month LIBOR, we must use
the bid side of the basis swap, which is +1 bp. Since the dealer is willing
to receive 5.142% monthly money versus 3-month LIBOR, this implies that
the dealer would be willing to receive 5.142% −0.01% = 5.132% monthly
money versus 1-month LIBOR.
546

<!-- Page 570 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Figure S.1: Computing the Bid Side Swap Rate Versus 1-Month LIBOR
Chapter 2
1. The corporation can issue a ﬂoater at par and pay coupons of 3-month
LIBOR + 66, or it can issue a ﬁxed rate bond and pay coupons equal to
T5 + 96 and enter into a 5-year swap to create synthetic ﬂoating funding. If
the corporation enters into the swap, it will receive ﬁxed from the dealer at
the bid side. Since mid-market swap spreads in ﬁve years are 34 bps, this
means that the corporation will receive a ﬁxed rate of T5+32 versus LIBOR.
Issuing debt at T5 + 96 and receiving ﬁxed at T5 + 32 versus LIBOR nets
out to paying LIBOR + 64. Figure S.2 may be helpful.
Thus, the corporation would be better oﬀissuing the ﬁxed rate bond and
swapping to ﬂoating, saving 2 bps on the coupon. By the way, if we wanted
to be very careful, we would recognize that 64 bps paid semi bond is not
the same as 64 bps paid quarterly money. The equivalent number of bps
paid quarterly money will be less than 64 (albeit a fraction of a bp less as
determined by a proprietary swap model). This means that the corporation
would obtain synthetic ﬂoating rate funding at a better level than LIBOR
+ 64. This realization does not change our answer; the corporation is still
better oﬀissuing ﬁxed and swapping to ﬂoating.
547

<!-- Page 571 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Figure S.2: Synthetic Floating Rate Funding Obtained by Corporation
3. a) Instead of paying ﬁxed at the mid-market rate of 5.60%, the dealer has
inadvertently paid at a rate of 5.65%. This mistake is against the dealer. We
should use the PV Coupon 01 number in order to determine the loss to the
dealer. The value of this mistake is
$43,321 × 5 × 0.5 = $108,302.
In the preceding equation, the factor of 5 is used since the mistake was 5 bps,
and the PV Coupon 01 number supplied in Table 2.3 is for a 1 bp change.
The factor of 0.5 in this equation accounts for the fact that the notional is
$50mm, not $100mm as used in the calculations in Table 2.3.
b) Since the dealer is paying ﬁxed and the market has rallied, the swap is in
favor of the client and against the dealer. Since we are dealing with a change
in the swap curve for a swap that has already been executed, it would be
most appropriate to use the PV01 numbers in Table 2.3. The loss to the
dealer is
$40,237 × 5 × 0.5 = $100,592.
Notice we have used the PV01 number assuming the ﬁrst LIBOR has already
set; a rally in the market would not change the initial LIBOR set in the trade.
Notice that the answer to this question is slightly diﬀerent to the answer
obtained in part a) due to the (appropriately) diﬀerent PV01 methodology
employed.
548

<!-- Page 572 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Chapter 3
2. a) As shown in the top half of Figure S.3, the 2x5 swap rate is 7.071%. As
a handcheck to this calculation, consider receiving in a 5-year swap and pay-
ing in a 2-year swap. The carry over the ﬁrst two years is 6.60% −6.00% =
60 bps. The future value of this carry (using the 2-year swap rate as dis-
count rate) is 123.6 bps. Amortizing this over the back three years (using
the 5-year swap rate as discount rate) gives an annuity of 46.8 bps. Adding
this annuity to the 5-year swap rate gives 6.60% + 0.468% = 7.068%, three
tenths of a bp away from the true answer.
b) The value of the ﬂoating side of a swap including ﬁctitious principal is
par on any reset date. But today is not a reset date in the swap. The ﬁrst
reset date is in two years. Thus, we know that in two years the value of the
ﬂoating side will be par. Therefore, the value of the ﬂoating side today is the
present value of $100mm to be received in two years:
$100mm
[1 + zc(2)]2 =
$100mm
(1.06012)2 = $88,979,417.
As shown in the bottom half of Figure S.3, this can also be veriﬁed by
modeling the swap with the assumption that the counterparties exchange
principal at maturity in ﬁve years. We ﬁnd that the value of the ﬁctitious
ﬂoating rate bond is $88,979,417.
3. a) As it stands today, the client is receiving 5.80% when the 5-year swap
rate is 6.60%. As shown in the top half of Figure S.4, the mark-to-market
on the trade is $3,339,836 against the client (the trade is modeled from the
perspective of the client). As a handcheck to this calculation, it is as if the
client is paying an annuity of 6.60% −5.80% = 80 bps for ﬁve years. Put
PMT = 80 bps, n = 5, and i = 6.60% and get PV = 331.6 bps of the no-
tional, or $3,315,590.
b) The client is currently receiving ﬁxed. In order to unwind the trade, it is
as if the client will be paying ﬁxed to the dealer. Thus, it is the oﬀer side
5-year swap rate that is applicable in the pricing of the unwind. The 5-year
mid-market swap rate is 6.60%. The oﬀer side 5-year swap rate is 1 bp from
there, or 6.61%. The pricing of the unwind using a 5-year swap rate of 6.61%
is shown in the bottom half of Figure S.4. The diﬀerence between the two
NPVs, $3,381,267 −$3,339,836 = $41,431, represents the proﬁtability that
the dealer makes on the unwind if it deals at the oﬀer side rate.
549

<!-- Page 573 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Figure S.3: The 2x5 Swap Rate (top half ) and with Fictitious Principal
(bottom half )
6. a) The 2-year swap rate is given as 6%, and in problem 2 we found that
the 2x5 swap rate is 7.071%.
b) The ﬁxed rate in the 2-year swap will need to be increased, and the
ﬁxed rate in the 2x5 swap will need to be decreased. As we start increasing
the ﬁxed rate in the 2-year swap, the value of this trade moves in favor of
the investor. As we start decreasing the ﬁxed rate in the 2x5 swap, the value
of this trade moves against the investor. In a calculation not shown here,
550

<!-- Page 574 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Figure S.4: Mark-to-Market on Existing Swap with 5.80% Coupon (top half )
and Unwind Value of Swap with Bid/Oﬀer Taken into Account (bottom half )
when we set the rates on the two trades equal to 6.60%, the NPV of one
trade exactly oﬀsets the other trade. In particular, with a rate of 6.60%,
the 2x5 swap has a negative mark-to-market of $1,102,058 for the investor,
and the 2-year swap has a positive mark-to-market of $1,102,058 for the
investor.
c) The rate that we solved for in part b) was the 5-year swap rate. Receiving
ﬁxed on $100mm 2-year swaps and simultaneously receiving ﬁxed on $100mm
551

<!-- Page 575 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
2x5 swaps is essentially the same thing as receiving ﬁxed on $100mm 5-year
swaps. Changing the ﬁxed coupons as we did in part b) shows that this is
indeed the case.
Chapter 4
1. a) The value of the cap is 266.1 bps of the notional, or around $2,661,000,
as shown in Figure S.5.
b) The average vol is found by constraining each of the base vols to be the
same and solving for the vol that makes the value of the cap 266.1 bps, the
same value as determined in part a). As shown in Figure S.6, the average
vol is 14.66%.
c) The value of the ﬂoor is 224.3 bps, or around $2,243,000, as shown in
Figure S.7.
d) From cap-ﬂoor parity, a 5-year swap in which a counterparty pays a ﬁxed
rate of 6.50% is equal to a long position in a 6.50% cap and a short position
in a 6.50% ﬂoor. Thus, the value of the swap is $2,660,619 −$2,243,140 =
$417,479 in favor of the ﬁxed rate payer.
We can verify this result from the bootstrap method as shown in Figure S.8.
The value of the swap to the ﬁxed rate payer is $27,553,645 −$27,136,166 =
$417,479, as expected.
2. The dealer is paying min(L, 8%). Notice that min(L, 8%) = L−max(0, L−
8%). Thus, the dealer is paying LIBOR less the payout on an 8% cap. The
ﬁxed rate in the swap will be the par swap rate reduced by the amortized
value of the cap.
We ﬁrst need to value an 8% cap. The cap is worth 100.1 bps, as shown in
the top half of Figure S.9. In order to solve for the ﬁxed rate in the swap, we
put in an upfront amount to account for the value of the cap that beneﬁts
the dealer. As shown in the bottom half of Figure S.9, the ﬁxed rate in the
swap is 6.36%.
552

c) To determine the appropriate cancelable swap rate, proceed as follows:
this position consists of a swap in which the swap dealer pays ﬁxed and
receives LIBOR −25 for ﬁve years, and the swap dealer owns an option to
receive the same ﬁxed rate and pay LIBOR −25 for three years, starting in
two years. We can model up the swap easily using our swap model, reducing
each of the forwards by the spread of 25 bps.
We have to be a little careful computing the value of the swaption. The em-
bedded swaption gives the swap dealer the right to receive a ﬁxed rate, x, and
pay LIBOR −25. Unfortunately, our swaption model only values swaptions

e) In this trade, the swap dealer pays a ﬁxed rate of 7.75% in the ﬁrst two
years, a ﬁxed rate of x in the last three years, and is long a 2 into 3 receiver
swaption struck at x. The level of x is found by iterating so that the trade
has zero NPV today. We ﬁnd a rate of 7.456%, as shown in Figure S.17.
Notice that the ﬁxed rate is relatively high in the ﬁrst two years and is lower
in the back years. Such a trade is referred to as a step-down structure.

it is using the normal when we (inconsistently) assume ﬂat skew for both. Of
course, the assumption of ﬂat skew in the normal model implies downward
lognormal skew. Under the assumption of ﬂat normal skew, if we had instead
used the lower level of lognormal implied vol for this high-strike option that
the normal model necessitates, we would come out with the same lower can-
celable swap rate of 7.593% that we solved for in part d).
7. a) As shown in Figure S.21, the extendible swap rate is 7.638%.
b) This is the same rate we solved for in problem 4 c) for a 4nc1. This is no
coincidence; the two trades are identical, a direct result of swaption parity,
as we will now show.
Recall that by swaption parity we know that being long a 1 into 3 7.638%
receiver and short a 1 into 3 7.638% payer is equivalent to receiving ﬁxed at
7.638% in a 3-year swap, one year forward. This implies
Long 1 into 3 payer struck at 7.638%
= Long 1 into 3 receiver struck at 7.638% +
Paying ﬁxed at 7.638% in a 1x4.
(S.1)
We will use Equation S.1 to substitute into one of the lines below.
567

<!-- Page 594 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
Now note that from the perspective of the dealer the extendible swap is
equivalent to
Pay ﬁxed for 1 year at 7.638% + Long 1 into 3 payer struck at 7.638%
= Pay ﬁxed for 1 year at 7.638% + Long 1 into 3 receiver struck at 7.638%
+ Pay ﬁxed at 7.638% in a 1x4
= Pay ﬁxed for 1 year at 7.638%
+ Pay ﬁxed at 7.638% in a 1x4
+ Long 1 into 3 receiver struck at 7.638%
= Pay ﬁxed for 4 years at 7.638% + Long 1 into 3 receiver struck at 7.638%
= 4nc1 with a coupon of 7.638%.
Thus, this extendible swap is equivalent to a cancelable swap that has the
same coupon and has
• A ﬁnal maturity date that matches the ﬁnal maturity of the extendible
if it is extended and
• A call date that matches the date upon which the extendible may be
extended.
Note that in the ﬁrst equation above we have made use of Equation S.1.
In the second equation above, we have just rearranged terms. And in the
second to last equation, we made the realization that if we pay ﬁxed in a
spot starting swap for one year at a rate x and we also pay ﬁxed in a 3-year
swap, one year forward at the same rate x, that is the same thing as just
paying ﬁxed in a spot starting 4-year swap at a rate x.

1. a) The 5-year Prime-LIBOR basis swap is −270 mid-market. Since the
dealer is willing to pay 5 bps from mid-market, this implies that the dealer
is willing to pay Prime −275 versus receiving LIBOR ﬂat. Since the dealer
will actually be receiving LIBOR + 15 from the issuer, he will be willing to
pay Prime −275 + 15, or Prime −260. Thus the value of C is 2.60%.
b) The dealer makes 5 bps running. We should be a bit careful since pay-
ments are made quarterly. Put PMT = 5/4 = 1.25 bps, n = 20, and i =
7.00%/4 = 1.75% and solve for PV = 20.9 bps of the notional, or $209,400.
If we wanted to be a bit more careful, we would recognize that the 5 bps
proﬁtability is earned on an Act/360 basis. Notice that 5 bps Act/360 is
approximately 5 bps × 365/360 = 5.1 bps 30/360. Recalculating the present
value with this adjustment gives a proﬁtability of $212,320.
2. a) Model a swap in which the dealer receives LIBOR −10 bps from
the issuer and pays LIBOR + 10 bps. These ﬂows alone are negative NPV
to the dealer. The dealer will be buying the cap to compensate for this
shortfall.

Figure S.23: Valuing the LIBOR Flows and Solving for the Strike on the Cap
As shown in the top half of Figure S.23, the dealer receives LIBOR −10
bps and pays LIBOR + 10 bps, creating an NPV of −$827,142. In order to
break even, we solve for the strike on the cap such that the cap is worth
$827,142. As shown in the bottom half of Figure S.23, the strike on such a
cap is 9.008%.
Thus, the coupon cap should be set to 9.008% + 10 bps = 9.108%.

Chapter 9
1. a) First, consider the case in which the 4-year swap rate, one year for-
ward and 1-year LIBOR, one year forward are negatively correlated. Then
as 1-year LIBOR, one year forward goes down (in which case the chances of
the swap knocking out increase), the 4-year swap rate, one year forward will
tend to go up. As the 4-year swap rate, one year forward goes up, the value
of the residual 4-year swap in a year decreases to the ﬁxed rate receiver.
Thus, the swap will tend to knock out in situations in which the remaining
4-year swap is decreasing in value to the client.
Now consider the case in which the 4-year swap rate, one year forward and
1-year LIBOR, one year forward are positively correlated. Then as 1-year
LIBOR, one year forward goes down (in which case the chances of the swap
knocking out increase), the 4-year swap rate, one year forward will tend to
go down as well. But as the 4-year swap rate, one year forward goes down,
the value of the residual 4-year swap in a year increases to the ﬁxed rate
receiver. Thus, the swap will tend to knock out in situations in which the
remaining 4-year swap is increasing in value to the client.
In summary, when the correlation is negative, the knockout feature will tend
to knock out the swap in situations in which it may be advantageous from the
point of view of the ﬁxed rate receiver. But when the correlation is positive,
the knockout feature will tend to knock out the swap in situations in which
it is disadvantageous from the point of view of the ﬁxed rate receiver. Thus,
everything else the same, positive correlation hurts the ﬁxed rate receiver
and he is compensated for this by receiving a higher ﬁxed coupon.
In the USD swaps market, it is often empirically observed that short-term
LIBOR forwards are positively correlated with longer-term forward swap
rates. Given this fact, knockout swaps such as the one that we have con-
sidered here are typically priced assuming positive correlation between the
forward LIBOR rate that determines whether or not the swap knocks out
579

<!-- Page 603 -->
S O L U T I O N S T O S ELE C T ED P R O B LEM S
and the residual forward swap rate. Making the assumption of positive cor-
relation serves to increase the ﬁxed rate that can be paid in the trade.
b) Yes, this is possible. The 4-year swap rate, one year forward shares, in
its construction, a LIBOR forward in common with 1-year LIBOR, one year
forward. This fact in isolation points to positive correlation between the two
rates. However, the 4-year swap rate, one year forward is constructed with an
additional three forward LIBOR rates. It is possible that these three other
forward rates are negatively correlated with the front forward in such a way
that the 1x5 and 1x2 rates are uncorrelated.
In fact, we was such a thing (in which near term LIBOR forwards were neg-
atively correlated with longer term LIBOR forwards) during certain periods
of the ﬁnancial crisis. There were times during the ﬁnancial crisis when we
saw long-term rates (for example, the 10-year swap rate) decrease signiﬁ-
cantly. At the same time, we saw short-term LIBOR rates rise as ﬁnancial
institutions were less willing to lend to one another. This was a time during
which short-term LIBOR forwards were negatively correlated with long-term
LIBOR forwards.
Appendix A
1. a) The value of the European call is 12.5, as shown in Figure S.27. In this
ﬁgure, h and B represent the amount of shares and borrowing, respectively,
in the replicating portfolio. The quantities c1u and c1d represent the value
of the call at time t = 1 in the upper node and lower node, respectively, and
c is the value of the call today.
b) The value of the European put is 3, as shown in Figure S.28. This ﬁgure
also shows the appropriate replicating portfolio.
c) From put-call parity, p = c−S +PV(X). Thus, the put should equal 12.5
−32 + 40/(1.33)2 = 3, which it does.
d) The value of the American call is the same as the value of the European
call, since there is no value to early exercise.
e) If at time t = 1 we reach the lower node, the unexercised put is worth
14, although immediate exercise provides a payoﬀof 24. Thus, early exercise
is optimal at t = 1 at the lower node (but not at the upper node). The
value of the American put at time t = 0 if it is not exercised is therefore

equal to (3/4) × [(5/6) × 2 + (1/6) × 24] = 4.25. However, if at time t = 0,
the put is exercised immediately, the payoﬀis 8, which exceeds the value if
unexercised. The value of the American put is therefore 8.
2. At time t = 1, the holder of the compound option will choose to exercise
and deliver the call option if and only if the value of the call option is less
than $20. Figure S.29 shows the value of the call option at each possible
node at time t = 1. Thus, the holder will only exercise if we reach the
bottom node at time t = 1. In this case, the value exercising is the diﬀerence
between the $20 received by exercising and the $5.625 value of the delivered
call, or $14.375. Thus, the value at time t = 0 of the compound option is
(3/4) × [0 × (5/6) + 14.375 × (1/6)] = 1.797.
4. Referring to Figure S.30, we ﬁnd that u = 1.2, d = 0.2, and since r = 0.08,
we therefore have pn = 0.88 from Equation A.16. The value of the derivative
contract is therefore (1/1.08) × (0.88 × 20 + 0.12 × 0) = 16.30. Therefore,
choose b).
